recipes.name: LB (Lysogeny Broth), Miller
recipes.summary: General-purpose rich medium for routine growth of E. coli and other non-fastidious bacteria; the default cloning/plasmid workhorse.

components.name: Tryptone
components.note: Pancreatic digest of casein (Bacto Tryptone or equivalent).

components.name: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
components.note: 10 g/L divided by MW 58.44 = 171 mM. This high-salt level is what defines the Miller formulation.

recipes.adjustPHWith: 5 N NaOH (approx. 0.2 mL/L); HCl to lower
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min, liquid cycle
recipes.storage: Room temperature after autoclaving, or 4 C; keep sealed and dark
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months as dehydrated powder; sterile broth several months at room temperature if unopened and not cloudy
recipes.hazards: pH is adjusted with 5 N NaOH (approx. 0.2 mL/L); HCl to lower — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Total dehydrated powder equivalent is 25 g/L (Sigma L3522).
For plates add agar to 1.5% w/v (15 g/L). For top/overlay agar use 0.7% w/v (7 g/L).

Miller = 10 g/L NaCl; Lennox = 5 g/L; Luria/low-salt = 0.5 g/L. Only the NaCl differs, and the versions are not interchangeable - salt level changes growth and the behaviour of salt-sensitive selections.
pH adjustment is optional for most routine E. coli work - unadjusted LB sits near pH 6.8-7.0.
Add heat-labile antibiotics only after the medium has cooled below ~50 C.
Do not use high-salt Miller LB when selecting with salt-sensitive antibiotics (zeocin, hygromycin, phleomycin) - use the Luria/low-salt version.
Expected yield quoted by the Barrick Lab for E. coli in this medium is on the order of 5 x 10^9 cells/mL.
(2026-07-21): NaCl 58.44 g/mol confirmed on PubChem; 10 g/L = 171 mM as stated.

sources.title: Barrick Lab protocol wiki - Media Recipes: LB (Miller) 10 g tryptone, 5 g yeast extract, 10 g NaCl per L
American Society for Microbiology - Luria Broth (LB) and Luria Agar (LA) Media and Their Uses protocol
PubChem CID 5234 - Sodium chloride, MW 58.44 g/mol
Sigma-Aldrich L3522, LB Broth (Miller) product page - composition and preparation
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - LB (Luria-Bertani) liquid medium (Sambrook & Russell, Molecular Cloning 3rd ed.)
Protocols Online - Lysogeny broth (Miller/Lennox/Luria variants)

recipes.name: LB (Lysogeny Broth), Lennox
recipes.summary: Reduced-salt LB; standard for phage P1 work, salt-sensitive antibiotics, and many protein-expression protocols.

components.name: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
components.note: 5 g/L divided by MW 58.44 = 85.6 mM (commonly quoted as 86 mM) - half the Miller level.

recipes.adjustPHWith: 5 N NaOH / HCl
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min, liquid cycle
recipes.storage: Room temperature or 4 C, dark
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months as powder; several months sterile at room temperature
recipes.hazards: pH is adjusted with 5 N NaOH / HCl — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Total dehydrated powder equivalent is 20 g/L (Sigma L3022).
For plates add agar to 1.5% w/v (15 g/L); for overlay/top agar 0.7% w/v (7 g/L).

Lennox's original 1955 formulation also contained 1 g/L glucose; virtually all modern commercial 'LB Lennox' powders omit it. Add 1 g/L D-glucose only if you specifically want the historical formulation.
Preferred over Miller for zeocin, hygromycin B and phleomycin selection, which lose activity at high ionic strength.
The Barrick Lab wiki makes the same point in the opposite direction: the 5 g/L and 10 g/L NaCl formulations are both called LB and are not interchangeable, so record which one an experiment used.
(2026-07-21): NaCl 58.44 g/mol confirmed on PubChem; 5 g/L = 85.6 mM as stated.

sources.title: Barrick Lab protocol wiki - Media Recipes (notes the 0.5% w/v NaCl LB formulation alongside the 1% Miller version)
PubChem CID 5234 - Sodium chloride, MW 58.44 g/mol
Sigma-Aldrich L3022, LB Broth (Lennox) product page - composition and preparation
Lennox ES (1955) Transduction of linked genetic characters of the host by bacteriophage P1. Virology 1:190-206
Protocols Online - Lysogeny broth (Miller/Lennox/Luria variants)

recipes.name: LB (Lysogeny Broth), Luria / low-salt
recipes.summary: Very low salt LB used when the selection agent (zeocin, phleomycin, hygromycin) is inhibited by NaCl.

components.name: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
components.note: 0.5 g/L divided by MW 58.44 = 8.56 mM.

recipes.adjustPHWith: 5 N NaOH / HCl (use pH 7.5 if the medium is for zeocin selection)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min, liquid cycle
recipes.storage: Room temperature or 4 C, dark
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months as powder
recipes.hazards: No hazard from the medium itself, but this recipe exists to be used with zeocin/phleomycin-class selection agents. Zeocin is a copper-chelating glycopeptide that cleaves DNA: treat it as a mutagen/cytotoxin, wear gloves, avoid aerosols, keep it and any plates containing it in the dark at the recommended storage temperature, and dispose of it as chemical waste. Concentrated NaOH used for the pH 7.5 adjustment is corrosive.
notes: ATTRIBUTION: the 0.5 g/L NaCl figure above is the classic 'LB Luria' formulation, NOT Invitrogen/Thermo's 'Low Salt LB'. Invitrogen's Low Salt LB for Zeocin is 10 g/L tryptone, 5 g/L yeast extract, 5 g/L NaCl (i.e. Lennox-level salt), pH adjusted to 7.5 with NaOH.

The actual zeocin requirement is a threshold, not a specific recipe: salt below 110 mM AND pH 7.5. Miller LB (171 mM) fails it; Lennox LB (85.6 mM) already passes it; this Luria formulation (8.56 mM) passes it comfortably. Any of the low-salt options works provided you set the pH.

For zeocin selection, pH matters as much as salt: adjust to pH 7.5 (not the 7.0 listed in finalPH above, which is the generic Luria-LB value) before autoclaving, since zeocin activity falls sharply below pH 7. Cool to below 55 C before adding zeocin; the supplier's stated working range in E. coli is 25-50 ug/mL, and the medium and plates should then be kept dark because zeocin is light sensitive.
For plates add agar to 1.5% w/v (15 g/L).
Growth yields are somewhat lower than Miller LB for some strains.
(2026-07-21): NaCl 58.44 g/mol confirmed on PubChem; 0.5 g/L = 8.56 mM as stated.

sources.title: PubChem CID 5234 - Sodium chloride, MW 58.44 g/mol (basis of the mM conversions above)

Invitrogen/Thermo Fisher - Zeocin Selection Reagent User Guide (MAN0000019): Low Salt LB = 10 g tryptone, 5 g NaCl, 5 g yeast extract in 950 mL, pH adjusted to 7.5 with 1 N NaOH then made to 1 L; requires salt below 110 mM and pH 7.5; zeocin 25-50 ug/mL for E. coli
Protocols Online - Lysogeny broth (Miller/Lennox/Luria variants)

recipes.name: LB Agar (Miller) - plates
recipes.summary: Standard solid LB for pouring selective and non-selective plates.

components.name: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
components.note: 171 mM; Miller-level salt.

components.name: Agar
components.note: 15 g/L. Use 1.5% for standard plates; 1.2% gives a softer plate that is easier to replica-plate; 2% for plates incubated at 42 C or for swarming-prone strains.

recipes.adjustPHWith: 5 N NaOH / HCl (adjust before adding agar)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min
recipes.storage: Poured plates inverted at 4 C in a sealed bag; bring to room temperature before use
recipes.shelfLife: Poured plates 1-3 months at 4 C (antibiotic plates shorter: ampicillin ~1 month, others 2-3 months)
recipes.hazards: Molten agar is a scald hazard; wear heat-resistant gloves. pH is adjusted with 5 N NaOH / HCl (adjust before adding agar) — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Total dehydrated powder equivalent is 40 g/L (Miller LB agar at 1.5% agar).

Published agar loadings for LB plates span 12-16 g/L: Addgene's plate protocol uses a 37 g/L premix containing 12 g/L agar, the Barrick Lab uses 16 g/L, and 15 g/L is the usual default. Pick one and stay with it - gel stiffness affects colony morphology and replica plating.
Cool to 50-55 C before adding antibiotics, IPTG or X-gal, then pour.
A 1 L batch pours roughly 40 standard 90-100 mm plates at ~25 mL each, or up to ~50 at 20 mL each. Small 60 mm plates take 5-10 mL.
Swirl gently when mixing after autoclaving - vigorous mixing traps bubbles that solidify on the plate surface.
Do not re-autoclave agar repeatedly; hydrolysis weakens the gel.
For sacB (sucrose) counter-selection, leave the NaCl out and put 5-10% w/v sucrose in its place; sucrose can be autoclaved with the rest of the medium.

sources.title: Addgene protocol - Pouring LB Agar Plates (premix 37 g/L: 5 g yeast extract, 10 g casein peptone, 10 g NaCl, 12 g agar; antibiotic added after cooling)
Barrick Lab protocol wiki - Media Recipes: LB (Miller) agar, and the sucrose substitution for sacB counter-selection
American Society for Microbiology - Luria Broth (LB) and Luria Agar (LA) Media and Their Uses protocol
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - LB medium recipe (Sambrook & Russell, Molecular Cloning 3rd ed.)

recipes.name: LB Agar (Lennox) - plates
recipes.summary: Solid Lennox LB for plating when reduced NaCl is preferred (phage work, salt-sensitive selection).

components.name: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
components.note: 85.6 mM; Lennox-level salt.

components.name: Agar
components.note: 15 g/L for standard plates.

recipes.adjustPHWith: 5 N NaOH / HCl
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min
recipes.storage: Poured plates inverted at 4 C, sealed
recipes.shelfLife: 1-3 months at 4 C
recipes.hazards: Molten agar scald hazard. pH is adjusted with 5 N NaOH / HCl — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Total dehydrated powder equivalent is 35 g/L.
Use as the bottom layer for phage plaque assays with a 0.7% top agar overlay of the same base.
Cool to 50-55 C before adding antibiotics, IPTG or X-gal.
As for Miller agar, 12-16 g/L agar all appear in published protocols; 15 g/L is the default here.

sources.title: Barrick Lab protocol wiki - Media Recipes: LB agar, plus the 7-8 g/L soft/top agar variant used for phage work
PubChem CID 5234 - Sodium chloride, MW 58.44 g/mol
Sigma-Aldrich L2897, LB Agar (Lennox) product page
Protocols Online - Lysogeny broth

recipes.name: LB Top Agar (soft agar overlay, 0.7%)
recipes.summary: Semi-solid LB overlay poured over LB plates for phage plaque assays, transposon/library plating and lawn formation.

components.name: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
components.note: Lennox-level salt (85.6 mM) is the usual base for phage overlays; use 10 g/L if you want a Miller base.

components.name: Agar
components.note: 7 g/L. This is the key difference from plates (1.5%). Published overlays range 0.4-0.8%; the Barrick Lab specifies 7-8 g/L and 0.7% is the most common single value.

recipes.adjustPHWith: 5 N NaOH / HCl
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min
recipes.storage: Solidified bottles at room temperature; remelt in a microwave or boiling water bath and hold at 45-50 C in a water bath before use
recipes.shelfLife: Several months as sealed sterile bottles; remelt no more than a few times
recipes.hazards: Molten agar scald hazard; remelting in a microwave can superheat and boil over. pH is adjusted with 5 N NaOH / HCl — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Hold at 45-48 C before mixing with cells - hotter than ~50 C kills the indicator bacteria, cooler and it sets in the tube.
Use ~3 mL overlay per 90-100 mm plate, 7-8 mL per 150 mm plate.
For lambda and many coliphages add MgSO4 to 10 mM and/or CaCl2 to 2-5 mM after autoclaving to aid adsorption. MgSO4*7H2O MW 246.48, CaCl2*2H2O MW 147.01 (PubChem).
Agarose (0.6-0.7%) or Noble agar can substitute where agar impurities inhibit the phage.
For a nutritionally leaner overlay that gives larger plaques, omit the yeast extract (tryptone top agar).

sources.title: Barrick Lab protocol wiki - Media Recipes: soft/top agar for phage studies uses 7-8 g agar per litre of LB
PubChem CID 24843 - Magnesium sulfate heptahydrate, MW 246.48 g/mol; PubChem CID 24844 - calcium chloride dihydrate, MW 147.01 g/mol
Kropinski AM et al. (2009) Enumeration of Bacteriophages by Double Agar Overlay Plaque Assay. Methods Mol Biol 501:69-76
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - LB medium (base for top agar; Sambrook & Russell)

recipes.name: Tryptone Soft Agar (motility / swim agar, 0.3%)
recipes.summary: Very low-percentage tryptone agar in which motile bacteria swim through the gel, producing expanding chemotactic rings.

components.name: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
components.note: 85.6 mM. Some chemotaxis protocols use 8 g/L NaCl.

components.name: Agar
components.note: 3 g/L. Swimming assays use up to 0.3%; classic tryptone chemotaxis plates are often made at 0.35%. Swarm agar 0.5-0.7%; twitching assays 1.0%. The percentage is the single most important variable in this assay.

recipes.adjustPHWith: NaOH / HCl
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15 min (a longer cycle, ~45 min for a 1 L batch, is used by some labs to get a fully homogeneous low-percentage agar suspension)
recipes.storage: Pour fresh; use plates the same day or next day
recipes.shelfLife: 1-2 days - drying changes the effective agar concentration and ruins reproducibility
recipes.hazards: Molten agar scald hazard. pH is adjusted with NaOH / HCl — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Pour thick plates, ~25 mL per 90 mm plate, and let them set on a level bench for a few hours; thicker plates hold moisture and give more reproducible rings.
Inoculate by stabbing into the centre of the agar with a toothpick or pipette tip, do not spread on the surface, and do not stab all the way through to the plastic.
Incubate upright, not inverted - condensation dripping onto a soft plate destroys the assay.
Deliberately contains no yeast extract - a shallow amino acid gradient is what drives the visible chemotactic ring.
Batch-to-batch agar variation strongly affects ring diameter; compare only strains run on plates from the same pour.
Defined alternatives exist for non-enteric organisms: the O'Toole lab swim plate for Pseudomonas is 0.3% agar in M8 salts with 0.2% glucose, 0.5% casamino acids and 1 mM MgSO4.

The tryptone/NaCl composition traces to Adler's 1966 chemotaxis work (paywalled); the 0.3% agar, stab-inoculation and thick-plate practice above are supported by the freely available O'Toole lab protocol.

sources.title: Ha DG, Kuchma SL, O'Toole GA (2014) Plate-Based Assay for Swimming Motility in Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Methods Mol Biol 1149:59-65 - author manuscript free in PMC (0.3% agar, ~25 mL thick plates, stab inoculation, incubate upright)
PubChem CID 5234 - Sodium chloride, MW 58.44 g/mol
Adler J (1966) Chemotaxis in Bacteria. Science 153:708-716

recipes.name: SOB (Super Optimal Broth)
recipes.summary: Magnesium-rich rich broth used to grow cells for competent-cell preparation; the base for SOC.

components.name: Tryptone
components.note: 2% w/v

components.name: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
components.note: 0.5 g/L divided by MW 58.44 = 8.56 mM (usually quoted as '10 mM NaCl')

components.name: Potassium chloride (KCl)
components.note: 0.186 g/L divided by MW 74.55 = 2.49 mM, i.e. the standard 2.5 mM. Equivalent to adding 2.5 mL of 1 M KCl per litre.

components.name: Magnesium chloride (MgCl2)
components.note: Add 5 mL of sterile 2 M MgCl2 per litre AFTER autoclaving (5 mL x 2 M / 1 L = 10 mM). Make the 2 M stock from MgCl2*6H2O, MW 203.30 (40.66 g per 100 mL), or from anhydrous MgCl2, MW 95.21 (19.04 g per 100 mL). Sambrook's '19 g in 100 mL' refers to the anhydrous salt.

recipes.adjustPHWith: 5 N NaOH (protocols specify anywhere from pH 6.8 to 7.5; the Barrick Lab uses 7.5)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min; add the MgCl2 as a separately sterilized stock afterwards
recipes.storage: 4 C after the Mg addition
recipes.shelfLife: Base medium several months sterile; complete SOB best used within 1-2 months
recipes.hazards: pH is adjusted with 5 N NaOH (protocols specify anywhere from pH 6.8 to 7.5; the Barrick Lab uses 7.5) — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Add Mg only after autoclaving - autoclaving Mg with phosphate/peptone causes precipitation.

A common alternative Mg regime is 10 mM MgCl2 PLUS 10 mM MgSO4 (add 10 mL of a sterile 1 M MgCl2 / 1 M MgSO4 mix per litre); this is what NEB uses. The Barrick Lab instead uses 20 mM MgSO4 (2.4 g/L anhydrous, MW 120.37) added before autoclaving, with the medium set to pH 7.5.
SOB + 20 mM glucose = SOC.
Do not substitute LB for SOB in chemically competent cell prep - transformation efficiency drops substantially.
(2026-07-21): MgCl2 anhydrous 95.21 and MgCl2*6H2O 203.30 both confirmed on PubChem, so the two stock weights above are internally consistent; KCl 74.55 confirmed; NaCl 58.44 confirmed.

sources.title: Barrick Lab protocol wiki - Media Recipes: SOB/SOC (20 g/L tryptone, 5 g/L yeast extract, 0.5 g/L NaCl, 0.186 g/L KCl, magnesium salt, pH 7.5)
PubChem CID 24644 - Magnesium chloride hexahydrate, MW 203.30 g/mol (and CID 5360315, anhydrous MgCl2, MW 95.21)
PubChem CID 4873 - Potassium chloride, MW 74.55 g/mol
New England Biolabs - SOC Outgrowth Medium (B9020) composition, showing the 10 mM MgCl2 + 10 mM MgSO4 magnesium regime used with an SOB base
Hanahan D (1983) Studies on transformation of Escherichia coli with plasmids. J Mol Biol 166:557-580

recipes.name: SOC (Super Optimal broth with Catabolite repression)
recipes.summary: SOB plus 20 mM glucose; the standard recovery/outgrowth medium after bacterial transformation or electroporation.

components.name: Tryptone
components.note: 2% w/v

components.name: Potassium chloride (KCl)
components.note: 2.49 mM (quoted as 2.5 mM); MW 74.55

components.name: Magnesium chloride (MgCl2)
components.note: 5 mL of sterile 2 M MgCl2 per litre, added after autoclaving. MgCl2*6H2O MW 203.30; anhydrous MgCl2 MW 95.21.

components.name: D-Glucose (dextrose, anhydrous)
components.note: 20 mL of a 0.22 um filter-sterilized 1 M glucose stock per litre, added after autoclaving and cooling. 1 M = 180.16 g/L (18% w/v), well below glucose's solubility. Equivalent and easier: 40 mL of sterile 10% w/v glucose into 960 mL of SOB, which gives 0.4% w/v = 22 mM. D-glucose monohydrate is MW 198.17, so weigh 10% more if using it.

recipes.adjustPHWith: 5 N NaOH (the Barrick Lab SOB/SOC base is set to pH 7.5)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave the base 121 C, 15-20 min; add filter-sterilized glucose and sterile MgCl2 afterwards
recipes.storage: 4 C; aliquot into small sterile tubes to avoid repeated opening
recipes.shelfLife: Commercial sealed SOC is dated about a year at 4 C; lab-made SOC is best used within a few months and discarded immediately if cloudy
recipes.hazards: pH is adjusted with 5 N NaOH (the Barrick Lab SOB/SOC base is set to pH 7.5) — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Never autoclave the glucose with the peptones - Maillard browning destroys sugar and produces growth-inhibitory products. Filter it in separately (glucose stocks can alternatively be autoclaved alone, as the Barrick Lab does).
NEB's commercial SOC Outgrowth Medium is 2% peptone, 0.5% yeast extract, 10 mM NaCl, 2.5 mM KCl, 10 mM MgCl2, 10 mM MgSO4, 20 mM glucose - i.e. it carries both magnesium salts.
Warm to 37 C before adding to transformed cells; cold SOC lowers recovery.
Typical use: 250-950 uL per transformation, shaking 37 C for 1 h before plating.
SOC is extremely rich and contaminates easily once glucose is in - store as many small aliquots rather than one large bottle.
(2026-07-21): D-glucose 180.16 (PubChem CID 5793) and glucose monohydrate 198.17 confirmed; 20 mL of 1 M per litre = 20 mM checks out.

sources.title: Barrick Lab protocol wiki - Media Recipes: SOC = SOB plus 40 mL sterile 10% w/v glucose per 960 mL (about 20 mM), added once the medium has cooled to ~50 C
PubChem CID 5793 - D-Glucose, MW 180.16 g/mol
New England Biolabs - SOC Outgrowth Medium (B9020) composition
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - SOC Medium recipe (Sambrook & Russell, Molecular Cloning)
Hanahan D (1983) J Mol Biol 166:557-580

recipes.name: 2xYT (Double Yeast-Tryptone)
recipes.summary: Enriched broth with roughly double the nutrients of LB; standard for M13/filamentous phage propagation, phage display and higher-density E. coli culture.
recipes.adjustPHWith: 5 N NaOH
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 20 min (15 psi), liquid cycle
recipes.storage: Room temperature or 4 C, dark
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months as powder; several months sterile
recipes.hazards: pH is adjusted with 5 N NaOH — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Total dehydrated equivalent 31 g/L.
Composition verified against the freely published QIAGEN FAQ (16 g tryptone, 10 g yeast extract, 5 g NaCl per litre), which in turn cites Current Protocols in Molecular Biology 1.1.3.
For plates add agar to 1.5% w/v (15 g/L); for M13 top agar use 0.7% w/v.
Preferred over LB for M13, fd and phagemid work, and generally gives higher biomass than LB before the culture stalls.
Not phosphate-buffered - cultures still acidify at high density. Use Terrific Broth if you need buffering.

sources.title: QIAGEN FAQ 213 - recipe for 2x YT: 16 g tryptone, 10 g yeast extract, 5 g NaCl per litre
PubChem CID 5234 - Sodium chloride, MW 58.44 g/mol
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - YT medium recipe (Sambrook & Russell)

recipes.name: Terrific Broth (TB)
recipes.summary: Phosphate-buffered, glycerol-containing rich medium for very high cell density - the standard for plasmid preps and recombinant protein expression.

components.name: Glycerol
components.note: 4 mL per litre. Primary carbon source; non-fermentable, so it avoids acetate overflow. Glycerol MW 92.09, density 1.26 g/mL, so 4 mL is about 5 g.

components.name: Potassium phosphate monobasic (KH2PO4)
components.note: 0.017 M x 136.09 = 2.31 g/L, matching the published Tartof-Hobbs weight. Prepared as part of a separately autoclaved 10x phosphate stock (0.17 M KH2PO4).

components.name: Potassium phosphate dibasic (K2HPO4)
components.note: 0.072 M x 174.18 = 12.54 g/L, matching the published weight. Part of the 10x stock (0.72 M K2HPO4). If you only have K2HPO4*3H2O, MW 228.22, use 16.43 g/L.

recipes.adjustPHWith: none normally required - the phosphate pair sets pH ~7.2
recipes.sterilization: autoclave the tryptone/yeast extract/glycerol in 90% of the final volume at 121 C for 20 min; autoclave the 10x phosphate stock separately and add after both have cooled below 60 C
recipes.storage: 4 C; room temperature acceptable for short periods
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months as powder; complete medium best within 1-2 months at 4 C
notes: Make the 10x phosphate stock as 2.31 g KH2PO4 + 12.54 g K2HPO4 in 90 mL water, adjust to 100 mL, autoclave. Add 100 mL per litre of finished medium.

Autoclaving phosphate together with the peptones causes precipitation - keep them separate. This is the single most common TB preparation error, and the same rule the Barrick Lab applies to its minimal-agar recipes.

Commercial 'Terrific Broth, modified' powders (e.g. Sigma T9179) use a different phosphate load: 12 g/L tryptone, 24 g/L yeast extract, 9.4 g/L K2HPO4, 2.2 g/L KH2PO4, plus 4-8 mL/L glycerol added by the user. Both are widely used; the Tartof-Hobbs values above are the original.
TB routinely reaches OD600 of 10-20 in shake flasks - use baffled flasks and no more than 20-25% fill volume or the culture becomes oxygen-limited.
Autoinduction media (e.g. ZYM-5052) are built on a TB-like base.
(2026-07-21): KH2PO4 136.09 (PubChem lists 136.086) and K2HPO4 174.18 confirmed as the anhydrous salts named in 'form'; molarity x MW reproduces 2.31 and 12.54 g/L exactly.

PROVENANCE: the primary reference, Tartof & Hobbs (1987) in the Bethesda Research Labs 'Focus' newsletter, is not available online and the Sambrook reprint is paywalled - but the formulation itself is not source-limited. The CC-BY-SA OpenWetWare entries give the identical 10x phosphate stock (0.17 M KH2PO4 = 23.1 g/L and 0.72 M K2HPO4 = 125.4 g/L, added at 100 mL per litre once both parts are below 60 C), which is exactly where the 2.31 and 12.54 g/L final figures come from. The free Sigma sheet independently corroborates the tryptone/yeast extract/glycerol part, but lists the modified phosphate load rather than these values.

sources.title: PubChem CID 516951 - monobasic potassium phosphate, MW 136.09 g/mol; PubChem CID 24450 - dibasic potassium phosphate, MW 174.18 g/mol
PubChem CID 753 - Glycerol, MW 92.09 g/mol
Sigma-Aldrich T9179, Terrific Broth (modified) powder - composition of the commercial variant
OpenWetWare - Terrific Broth entry (community wiki reproduction of the Tartof-Hobbs formulation)

Tartof KD & Hobbs CA (1987) Improved media for growing plasmid and cosmid clones. Bethesda Research Labs Focus 9:12 - original source of the 2.31/12.54 g/L phosphate pair; reproduced as the Terrific Broth recipe in Sambrook & Russell, Molecular Cloning (Cold Spring Harbor Protocols)

recipes.name: Nutrient Broth
recipes.summary: Simple general-purpose broth for cultivating non-fastidious organisms; the reference medium in many standard methods and teaching labs.

components.name: Peptone
components.note: Enzymatic digest of gelatin/meat; Difco 'Peptone'.

recipes.adjustPHWith: NaOH / HCl; Difco specification is pH 6.8 +/- 0.2 at 25 C
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15 min
recipes.storage: Dehydrated powder in a tightly closed container at 15-30 C; prepared medium 2-8 C
recipes.shelfLife: Powder until the labelled expiry; prepared broth a few weeks at 2-8 C
recipes.hazards: Beef extract is animal-derived; check institutional rules if TSE/BSE-free media are required. pH is adjusted with NaOH / HCl; Difco specification is pH 6.8 +/- 0.2 at 25 C — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Total dehydrated powder is 8 g/L for this (Difco/US) formulation.

IMPORTANT VARIANT: the Oxoid/European 'nutrient' base is a different medium - Lab-Lemco beef extract 1 g/L, yeast extract 2 g/L, peptone 5 g/L and NaCl 5 g/L, at pH 7.4 +/- 0.2 (see the Oxoid CM0003 nutrient agar specification). It contains salt and yeast extract and is specified a full 0.6 pH units higher. Check which formulation your method calls for before quoting 'nutrient broth' results.
For nutrient agar add agar to 1.5% w/v (15 g/L) - see the separate nutrient agar recipe.
Note the Difco pH is deliberately slightly acidic (6.8), unlike LB at 7.0.
Much less rich than LB; do not expect comparable cell densities.

sources.title: BD Difco & BBL Manual, 2nd ed. - Nutrient Broth (beef extract 3 g, peptone 5 g per litre; pH 6.8 +/- 0.2)
Thermo Scientific Oxoid Nutrient Agar CM0003 quality specification - the alternative Lab-Lemco/yeast extract/peptone/NaCl base at pH 7.4 +/- 0.2
BD Difco Nutrient Broth 234000 product information

recipes.name: Nutrient Agar
recipes.summary: Solid general-purpose medium for isolation, colony counts and maintenance of non-fastidious bacteria.

components.name: Agar
components.note: 15 g/L for plates. For nutrient soft agar / overlays use 0.7% w/v; for slants 1.5-2%.

recipes.adjustPHWith: NaOH / HCl; Difco specification pH 6.8 +/- 0.2 at 25 C (2.3% solution)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15 min
recipes.storage: Poured plates inverted at 2-8 C, sealed and away from direct light; dehydrated powder 15-30 C, tightly closed
recipes.shelfLife: Poured plates 1-2 months at 2-8 C; discard if shrunken, cracked or discoloured
recipes.hazards: Molten agar scald hazard; beef extract is animal-derived. pH is adjusted with NaOH / HCl; Difco specification pH 6.8 +/- 0.2 at 25 C (2.3% solution) — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Total dehydrated powder is 23 g/L for the Difco formulation.

IMPORTANT VARIANT: Oxoid Nutrient Agar CM0003 is a different formulation - Lab-Lemco beef extract 1 g/L, yeast extract 2 g/L, peptone 5 g/L, NaCl 5 g/L, agar 15 g/L (28 g/L total powder), pH 7.4 +/- 0.2. Do not mix the two specifications in one method.
Heat with frequent agitation and boil for 1 minute to dissolve the agar completely before autoclaving.
Cool to about 50 C before pouring.
Widely used for standard plate counts and as a base for blood agar (add 5-7% defibrinated blood at 45-50 C).

sources.title: Thermo Scientific Oxoid Nutrient Agar CM0003 quality specification (Lab-Lemco 1, yeast extract 2, peptone 5, NaCl 5, agar 15 g/L; 28 g/L; pH 7.4 +/- 0.2; autoclave 121 C 15 min, cool to 50 C, pour)
BD Difco Nutrient Agar 213000 product page - beef extract 3 g, peptone 5 g, agar 15 g per litre; pH 6.8 +/- 0.2
Oxoid prepared Nutrient Agar plate/slant specification (storage 2-12 C away from direct light; discard damaged or discoloured media)

recipes.name: M9 Salts, 5x concentrate
recipes.summary: Concentrated buffered salt/nitrogen base that is diluted to make M9 minimal medium.

components.name: Sodium phosphate dibasic (Na2HPO4)
components.note: Anhydrous Na2HPO4, MW 141.96: 33.9 g/L = 0.239 M in the 5x, 47.8 mM at 1x. Hydrate equivalents for the same molar amount: Na2HPO4*2H2O (MW 177.99) 42.5 g/L; Na2HPO4*7H2O (MW 268.07) 64.0 g/L; Na2HPO4*12H2O (MW 358.14) 85.5 g/L. Using the wrong hydrate is the classic M9 mistake - Sambrook's 5x lists 64 g of the heptahydrate, which is the same thing as 33.9 g anhydrous.

components.name: Potassium phosphate monobasic (KH2PO4)
components.note: Anhydrous KH2PO4, MW 136.09: 15 g/L = 0.110 M in the 5x, 22.0 mM at 1x.

components.name: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
components.note: MW 58.44: 2.5 g/L = 42.8 mM in the 5x, 8.56 mM at 1x.

components.name: Ammonium chloride (NH4Cl)
components.note: MW 53.49: 5 g/L = 93.5 mM in the 5x, 18.7 mM at 1x. Sole nitrogen source; substitute 15NH4Cl for isotopic labelling.

recipes.adjustPHWith: not normally adjusted; the phosphate pair gives ~pH 7.0-7.4 at 1x
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15 min (15 psi), liquid cycle - dispense into 200 mL aliquots first
recipes.storage: Room temperature or 4 C; discard if any precipitate or turbidity appears
recipes.shelfLife: 6-12 months sterile
notes: Dissolve fully before autoclaving; phosphates dissolve slowly in cold water, so warm and stir.
This concentrate contains NO carbon source, magnesium, calcium or trace elements - all are added at the 1x stage.
200 mL of this 5x makes 1 L of 1x M9.

TWO LEGITIMATE STRENGTHS EXIST. The 33.9 g/L anhydrous Na2HPO4 version above matches Sambrook (64 g/L of the heptahydrate) and Sigma M6030. The Barrick Lab and many other labs use a slightly weaker medium: 6 g/L Na2HPO4, 3 g/L KH2PO4, 0.5 g/L NaCl, 1 g/L NH4Cl at 1x, i.e. a 5x of 30/15/2.5/5 g/L. Both buffer near pH 7; just do not mix figures between the two.
(2026-07-21): Na2HPO4 141.96, KH2PO4 136.09, NaCl 58.44 and NH4Cl 53.49 all confirmed on PubChem, as were the 2H2O/7H2O/12H2O sodium phosphate masses (177.99 / 268.07 / 358.14).

sources.title: Barrick Lab protocol wiki - Media Recipes: M9 minimal medium at 1x (6 g Na2HPO4 anhydrous, 3 g KH2PO4, 0.5 g NaCl, 1 g NH4Cl per litre)
PubChem CID 24203 - Sodium phosphate dibasic, MW 141.96 g/mol (hydrates: CID 6451167 dihydrate 177.99, CID 6096963 heptahydrate 268.07, CID 61456 dodecahydrate 358.14)
PubChem CID 25517 - Ammonium chloride, MW 53.49 g/mol
Sigma-Aldrich M6030, M9 Minimal Salts 5x - composition (Na2HPO4 33.9, KH2PO4 15, NaCl 2.5, NH4Cl 5 g/L)
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - M9 minimal medium (standard) (Sambrook & Russell, Molecular Cloning)

recipes.name: M9 Minimal Medium (1x, complete with glucose)
recipes.summary: Defined minimal medium for E. coli: used for auxotroph selection, isotope labelling, metabolic and physiology experiments.

components.name: M9 salts
components.note: 200 mL of sterile 5x M9 salts per litre. See the m9-salts-5x recipe.

components.name: Magnesium sulfate (MgSO4)
components.note: 2 mL of sterile 1 M MgSO4 per litre. Make the 1 M stock from MgSO4*7H2O, MW 246.48 (24.65 g per 100 mL); anhydrous MgSO4 is MW 120.37 (12.04 g per 100 mL). Some labs (e.g. Barrick) use 1 mM instead of 2 mM.

components.name: Calcium chloride (CaCl2)
components.note: 0.1 mL of sterile 1 M CaCl2 per litre (or, less error-prone, 1 mL of 0.1 M). Make the 1 M stock from CaCl2*2H2O, MW 147.01 (14.70 g per 100 mL); anhydrous CaCl2 is MW 110.98 (11.10 g per 100 mL).

components.name: D-Glucose (dextrose, anhydrous)
components.note: 20 mL of 0.22 um filter-sterilized 20% (w/v) glucose per litre = 4 g/L = 22.2 mM. Glucose MW (anhydrous) 180.16; monohydrate 198.17. Lower carbon loads are common (Barrick uses 0.1% w/v); glycerol 0.2-0.4% v/v or another carbon source can be substituted.

components.name: Thiamine hydrochloride (vitamin B1)
components.note: OPTIONAL. 1 mL of a filter-sterilized 1 mg/mL stock per litre (about 3 uM). Required for thiamine auxotrophs (e.g. many BL21 and B-strain derivatives) and commonly added as insurance. Thiamine hydrochloride MW 337.3.

components.name: Casamino acids
components.note: OPTIONAL - only for 'M9 rich' / supplemented M9. 2 g/L. Adding this makes the medium no longer strictly minimal and defeats amino acid auxotroph selection. Vitamin-free acid-hydrolysed casein; acid hydrolysis destroys tryptophan and largely destroys cysteine, so supplement those separately if needed.

recipes.solvent: sterile deionized water (bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: usually not adjusted; the phosphate buffer holds ~pH 7.0-7.4
recipes.sterilization: assemble aseptically from separately sterilized components - autoclave the 5x salts, MgSO4 and CaCl2; 0.22 um filter the glucose and thiamine
recipes.storage: 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: Assemble fresh where possible; complete medium 2-4 weeks at 4 C
notes: Add components in this order to avoid precipitation: water, then 5x salts, then glucose, then MgSO4, then CaCl2. Adding concentrated CaCl2 or MgSO4 directly into concentrated phosphate produces an insoluble phosphate precipitate.
Never autoclave glucose with the phosphate salts.

For M9 minimal plates, autoclave the agar (15-16 g/L) separately from the phosphate salts, cool both to ~55 C, then combine and add the sterile supplements - the Barrick Lab flags agar-plus-phosphate and phosphate-plus-sugar autoclaving as producers of growth-inhibitory compounds.
Doubling time on M9/glucose is roughly 50-60 min for E. coli K-12 at 37 C, versus ~20-25 min in LB. Plan overnight cultures accordingly.
For 15N or 13C labelling replace NH4Cl and/or glucose with the labelled forms in the 5x salts and carbon stock respectively.
Some protocols add trace metals (Fe, Zn, Mn, Cu, Co, Mo) for prolonged or high-density growth; base M9 has none beyond what is carried in as contaminants.

(2026-07-21): MgSO4*7H2O 246.48, anhydrous MgSO4 120.37, CaCl2*2H2O 147.01, anhydrous CaCl2 110.98, D-glucose 180.16, glucose monohydrate 198.17 and thiamine HCl 337.3 all confirmed on PubChem; every stock weight above follows from those MWs.

recipes.name: MacConkey Agar
aka: MacConkey agar with crystal violet and neutral red
recipes.summary: Selective and differential agar: bile salts and crystal violet suppress Gram-positives while lactose plus neutral red distinguishes lactose fermenters (pink) from non-fermenters (colourless).

components.name: Lactose, monohydrate
components.note: The differential substrate. alpha-Lactose monohydrate MW 360.31; anhydrous lactose MW 342.30 - the 10 g/L figure is quoted for the monohydrate as supplied.

components.name: Bile salts mixture
components.note: Selective agent against Gram-positives. Oxoid 'Bile Salts No. 3' or Difco bile salts mixture.
components.hazard: Irritant powder; avoid raising dust

components.name: Neutral red
components.note: 30 mg/L. pH indicator - turns red/pink below about pH 6.8 as acid accumulates from lactose fermentation. Neutral red (hydrochloride) MW 288.77.
components.hazard: Dye; harmful if swallowed, stains; avoid inhalation of the powder

components.name: Crystal violet
components.note: 1 mg/L. Inhibits Gram-positive organisms, especially enterococci and staphylococci. Weigh from a dilute stock - 1 mg per litre cannot be weighed accurately on a normal balance. Crystal violet (chloride) MW 408.0.
components.hazard: Harmful if swallowed; suspected carcinogen (IARC-listed for gentian violet); stains skin and clothing

components.name: Agar
components.note: 13.5 g/L in the Difco formulation. Oxoid MacConkey No. 3 uses 1.5% (15 g/L).

recipes.adjustPHWith: NaOH / HCl; specification pH 7.1 +/- 0.2 at 25 C
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15 min - do NOT over-autoclave
recipes.storage: Poured plates inverted at 2-8 C in a sealed bag, protected from light
recipes.shelfLife: Poured plates 1-2 months at 2-8 C; dehydrated powder until labelled expiry
recipes.hazards: Contains crystal violet (harmful, suspected carcinogen, staining) and neutral red; wear gloves and avoid raising dust when weighing. Buying the dehydrated blend is safer and far more reproducible than weighing the dyes yourself. Molten agar is also a scald hazard. pH is adjusted with NaOH / HCl; specification pH 7.1 +/- 0.2 at 25 C — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Total dehydrated powder is 50 g/L (components sum to 50.03 g).
In practice, use the commercial dehydrated blend: the 1 mg/L crystal violet cannot be weighed reliably from scratch.
Boil with frequent agitation for 1 minute to fully dissolve before autoclaving.
Exceeding 121 C / 15 min degrades the bile salts and lactose and softens the gel.
Do not remelt and repour - the selective agents deteriorate.

Result interpretation: pink to red colonies, often with a bile-precipitate halo = strong lactose fermenter (e.g. E. coli); colourless/transparent = non-fermenter (e.g. Salmonella, Shigella, Pseudomonas).

Variants exist: MacConkey Agar Base (no lactose, add your own sugar); MacConkey Agar without crystal violet (permits enterococci and staphylococci); MacConkey with sorbitol (SMAC) for E. coli O157:H7.

DYE-LEVEL WARNING: Oxoid MacConkey Agar No. 3 (CM0115) is 20 g/L peptone, 10 g/L lactose, 1.5 g/L bile salts No. 3, 5 g/L NaCl, 0.03 g/L neutral red, 0.001 g/L crystal violet, 15 g/L agar, pH 7.1 +/- 0.2 - i.e. the same 0.03 g/L neutral red as the Difco formula above. The 0.075 g/L neutral red figure belongs to the DIFFERENT Oxoid product 'MacConkey Agar' CM0007 (20 g/L peptone, 5 g/L bile salts, 12 g/L agar, NO crystal violet, pH 7.4 +/- 0.2). Do not carry 0.075 g/L over to a No. 3 preparation - it is 2.5x the correct dye level.
Not intended for E. coli K-12 lab strain maintenance - use LB. This is a clinical/environmental isolation medium.

(2026-07-21): lactose monohydrate 360.31 and anhydrous lactose 342.30, neutral red 288.77 and crystal violet 408.0 all confirmed on PubChem. These MWs are informational - all components here are dosed by mass, not molarity.

recipes.name: SM Buffer with Gelatin
recipes.summary: Standard bacteriophage dilution and storage buffer; gelatin coats surfaces and stabilises lambda particles during storage.

components.name: NaCl
components.note: 0.1 M = 5.84 g/L; the classic published recipe rounds this to 5.8 g/L (0.0992 M).

components.name: Magnesium sulfate heptahydrate
components.note: 0.008 M x 246.48 = 1.97 g/L, i.e. the 2 g/L of the published recipe. Anhydrous MgSO4 (MW 120.37, PubChem CID 24083) equivalent is 0.96 g/L.

components.name: Tris-Cl, pH 7.5
components.note: 50 mL of 1 M Tris-Cl pH 7.5 per litre.

components.name: Gelatin
components.note: 0.1 g/L, added as 5 mL of a 2% (w/v) gelatin stock per litre.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve NaCl and MgSO4*7H2O in 800 mL, add Tris-Cl and gelatin, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: pH is set by the 1 M Tris-Cl pH 7.5 stock; no further adjustment normally needed
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 20 min at 121 C (15 psi), liquid cycle
recipes.storage: Room temperature, in 50 mL sterile aliquots
recipes.shelfLife: Indefinite at room temperature if sterile; discard each aliquot after use
notes: Composition per litre: 5.8 g NaCl, 2 g MgSO4*7H2O, 50 mL 1 M Tris-Cl pH 7.5, 5 mL 2% (w/v) gelatin, water to 1 L.
NaCl 58.44 and MgSO4*7H2O 246.47 and is updated to the PubChem value.
VARIANT TRIS LEVEL: some published and commercial SM formulations use 20-25 mM Tris-HCl rather than 50 mM, with NaCl, MgSO4 and gelatin unchanged. Both buffer adequately at pH 7.5.
Discard each aliquot after use to minimise the chance of contaminating the whole batch.
Gelatin can be omitted for most tailed phages; it matters most for lambda and for very dilute suspensions where adsorption to plastic or glass costs titre.
Do not freeze phage in SM buffer at -20 C without a cryoprotectant; hold at 4 C for months to years, or add glycerol and go to -80 C.

PubChem - Sodium chloride, CID 5234, MW 58.44
PubChem - Magnesium sulfate heptahydrate, CID 24843, MW 246.48

Sambrook & Russell, Molecular Cloning: A Laboratory Manual, 3rd ed., Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory Press

recipes.name: SM Buffer (without gelatin)
recipes.summary: Gelatin-free SM buffer for phage dilution, elution and storage; preferred when gelatin would interfere with downstream steps (protein gels, mass spec, DNA prep).

components.name: NaCl
components.note: 0.1 M = 5.84 g/L; published recipe rounds to 5.8 g/L.

components.name: Magnesium sulfate heptahydrate
components.note: 0.008 M x 246.48 = 1.97 g/L, i.e. the published 2 g/L.

components.name: Tris-Cl, pH 7.5
components.note: 50 mL of 1 M Tris-Cl pH 7.5 per litre.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve salts in 800 mL, add Tris-Cl, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: pH is set by the 1 M Tris-Cl pH 7.5 stock
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 20 min at 121 C (15 psi), liquid cycle
recipes.storage: Room temperature, in sterile aliquots
recipes.shelfLife: Indefinite at room temperature if sterile
notes: Identical to SM buffer with gelatin, minus the gelatin.
This is the version to use for phage going into TEM, SDS-PAGE, proteomics or genomic DNA extraction.
Commercial 'SM buffer' is usually sold as 50 mM Tris-HCl pH 7.5 / 100 mM NaCl / 8 mM MgSO4 / 0.01% gelatin, chemically the same as the classic recipe; the 20-25 mM Tris variant also circulates.
NaCl 58.44 (CID 5234) and MgSO4*7H2O 246.48 (CID 24843) confirmed on PubChem.

recipes.name: Phage Buffer (SEA-PHAGES)
recipes.summary: General-purpose phage dilution/storage buffer used throughout the SEA-PHAGES program; includes Ca2+, which many non-lambda phages need for adsorption.

components.name: Tris-HCl, pH 7.5
components.note: 10 mL of 1 M Tris-HCl pH 7.5 per litre.

components.name: Magnesium sulfate
components.note: 10 mL of 1 M MgSO4 per litre. Weighed directly as MgSO4*7H2O (MW 246.48) this is 2.46 g/L.

components.name: NaCl
components.note: 0.0685 M x 58.44 = 4.00 g/L, matching the published 4 g/L.

components.name: Calcium chloride
components.note: 1 mL of sterile 1 M CaCl2 per litre, added AFTER autoclaving and cooling.

recipes.adjustPHWith: pH is set by the 1 M Tris-HCl pH 7.5 stock
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min; add sterile CaCl2 after cooling
recipes.storage: Room temperature or 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: 6-12 months; discard if cloudy
recipes.hazards: pH is adjusted with pH is set by the 1 M Tris-HCl pH 7.5 stock — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Composition: 10 mM Tris pH 7.5, 10 mM MgSO4, 68.5 mM NaCl (4 g/L), 1 mM CaCl2.
MATH CHECK 2026-07-21: 4 g/L NaCl / 58.44 = 68.4 mM, so the stated 68.5 mM is correct to within rounding.
ADD CaCl2 AFTER AUTOCLAVING. Autoclaving Ca2+ with phosphate or carbonate, or at high concentration, gives visible precipitate.
A 10% (v/v) glycerol version of this buffer is used by SEA-PHAGES for freezer archiving of phage stocks.
Ca2+ is required for adsorption by many phages, including most mycobacteriophages - that is the main practical difference from SM buffer.

recipes.name: TM Buffer (Tris-Magnesium)
recipes.summary: Minimal Tris/Mg2+ buffer for diluting, resuspending and concentrating phage when the extra salt of SM buffer is undesirable.

components.name: Magnesium sulfate
components.note: Or weigh MgSO4*7H2O directly: MW 246.48 (PubChem CID 24843), so 10 mM = 2.46 g/L.

recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl / NaOH (pH normally set by the Tris-HCl stock)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 6-12 months
recipes.hazards: pH is adjusted with HCl / NaOH (pH normally set by the Tris-HCl stock) — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: MARKED UNCERTAIN: 'TM buffer' is not standardised in the phage literature. The most common phage-context definition, and the one matching a commercial TMG specification minus gelatin, is 10 mM Tris-HCl + 10 mM MgSO4.

OPEN SOURCE GAP: the exact composition here rests on a manufacturer product specification; no open protocol repository defines a single canonical 'TM buffer'. Confirm against your own source protocol.
Published variants you may meet: 50 mM Tris-HCl pH 7.2-7.5 + 10 mM MgSO4; 40 mM Tris-HCl pH 7.5 + 10 mM MgCl2 + 50 mM NaCl; 10 mM Tris pH 8.0 + 10 mM MgCl2.
ALWAYS check which TM your source protocol means - the Tris concentration varies fivefold between variants and MgCl2 is sometimes substituted for MgSO4.
If your protocol calls for MgCl2 instead: MgCl2*6H2O MW 203.30, anhydrous MgCl2 MW 95.21 - a more than twofold difference, so read the bottle.

recipes.name: TMG Buffer (Tris-Magnesium-Gelatin)
recipes.summary: TM buffer plus 0.01% gelatin; a low-ionic-strength phage diluent for serial dilutions where phage loss to tube walls is a concern.

components.name: Magnesium sulfate
components.note: Or MgSO4*7H2O, MW 246.48 (PubChem CID 24843), at 2.46 g/L.

components.name: Gelatin
components.note: 0.1 g/L; add as 5 mL of 2% (w/v) gelatin stock per litre.

recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl / NaOH (pH normally set by the Tris-HCl stock)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min (gelatin dissolves during autoclaving)
recipes.storage: Room temperature; 4 C also fine
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months; discard if cloudy or if any growth appears
recipes.hazards: pH is adjusted with HCl / NaOH (pH normally set by the Tris-HCl stock) — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Matches the commercial TMG specification: 10 mM Tris-HCl, 10 mM MgSO4, 0.01% gelatin, pH 7.2.
OPEN SOURCE GAP: composition is supported by a manufacturer product specification rather than an open protocol repository.
Some protocols specify pH 7.4-7.5 rather than 7.2; anywhere in that range is acceptable for phage handling.
Gelatin gels slightly at 4 C - warm to room temperature and mix before use.
Omit gelatin (i.e. use TM) for material going to protein gels, TEM or mass spectrometry.

sources.title: Sigma-Aldrich - TMG buffer solution, BioUltra, pH 7.2 (product 89552)
PubChem - Magnesium sulfate heptahydrate, CID 24843, MW 246.48
PubChem - Tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane, CID 6503, MW 121.14

recipes.name: Lambda Diluent (Tris/MgSO4)
recipes.summary: Simple Tris/Mg2+ diluent used for serial dilution and plating of bacteriophage lambda.

components.name: Magnesium sulfate
components.note: Or MgSO4*7H2O, MW 246.48 (PubChem CID 24843), at 2.46 g/L.

recipes.adjustPHWith: pH set by the Tris-HCl stock
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 6-12 months
recipes.hazards: pH is adjusted with pH set by the Tris-HCl stock — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: MARKED UNCERTAIN: 'lambda diluent' is used loosely. Many labs use SM buffer and call it lambda diluent; others use this simpler 10 mM Tris / 10 mM MgSO4 formulation; some use plain 10 mM MgSO4.
OPEN SOURCE GAP: no open protocol repository fixes a single canonical 'lambda diluent'; verify against your own lab's lambda protocol.
For serial dilutions down to very low titre, prefer SM buffer with gelatin or TMG - the gelatin materially reduces phage loss to plastic.
10 mM MgSO4 alone is an adequate diluent for lambda over short handling times and is what several classic lambda plating protocols specify.
Some open plaque-assay protocols simply dilute in the growth medium (e.g. LB) instead of a defined diluent; that is acceptable for short handling times.

Chlamydomonas Resource Center - Moderate Scale Lambda Phage DNA Prep (lambda diluent, lambda top agar, SM buffer)
PubChem - Magnesium sulfate heptahydrate, CID 24843, MW 246.48
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - SM buffer (pdb.rec8111)

recipes.name: LB Top Agar, 0.7% (soft agar overlay)
recipes.summary: Molten 0.7% agar overlay for double-agar-overlay plaque assays; low agar lets progeny phage diffuse and form visible plaques.

components.name: NaCl
components.note: Lennox salt level, matching the classic top-agar recipe. Use 10 g/L if you are matching Miller LB bottom plates.

components.name: Agar (bacteriological)
components.note: 7 g/L.

recipes.adjustPHWith: 5 N NaOH (a few hundred microlitres per litre)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 20 min at 121 C (15 psi)
recipes.storage: Room temperature in a tightly closed bottle. Remelt and hold at 45-50 C before use.
recipes.shelfLife: Several months as a sealed solid; do not remelt more than a few times
recipes.hazards: Molten agar at 50 C and freshly autoclaved bottles cause burns; superheated agar can boil over when removed from the autoclave. 5 N NaOH is corrosive - wear eye protection and gloves when adjusting pH.
notes: Composition per 100 mL: 0.7 g agar, 1 g tryptone, 0.5 g yeast extract, 0.5 g NaCl, water to 100 mL.

AGAR PERCENTAGE VARIES BY LAB: 0.7% is the classic figure; 0.8% is also standard open practice (Barrick Lab uses LB-Miller + 0.8% agar). 0.6% gives larger, easier-to-count plaques for slow-diffusing phages; 0.4-0.5% agarose is used for very large phages.

HOLD TEMPERATURE: equilibrate to 45-50 C before adding host cells. Above ~55 C you damage the host lawn; below ~42 C the agar sets in the tube. Some protocols park aliquots in a 55-60 C block and add cells immediately before pouring - workable, but do not let cells sit at that temperature.
Typical assay: 3 mL molten top agar + 100 uL overnight host culture + 100 uL phage dilution, brief vortex, pour onto a pre-warmed, dry bottom agar plate.
Many phages need divalent cations - supplement to 1-10 mM CaCl2 and/or 10 mM MgSO4 from sterile 1 M stocks added after cooling to 50 C.
Use agarose instead of agar if agar sulfation or impurities inhibit your phage (common with some marine and Pseudomonas phages), and always for plaque lifts.

Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - Top Agar (pdb.rec107999)
Kropinski AM et al. Enumeration of bacteriophages by double agar overlay plaque assay. Methods Mol Biol. 2009;501:69-76

recipes.name: LB Agar Bottom Plates (1.5%) for plaque assays
recipes.summary: Firm 1.5% agar base plates poured under the soft-agar overlay in double-agar plaque assays.

components.name: NaCl
components.note: Miller LB. Use 5 g/L for Lennox LB to match the 0.7% top agar salt level.

recipes.adjustPHWith: 5 N NaOH (approx. 0.2 mL per litre)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 20 min at 121 C (15 psi), liquid cycle
recipes.storage: Poured plates at 4 C, inverted, in sleeves; dry before use
recipes.shelfLife: 1-2 months at 4 C
recipes.hazards: Molten agar burns. 5 N NaOH is corrosive - wear gloves and eye protection.
notes: Miller LB is 10 g/L tryptone, 5 g/L yeast extract, 10 g/L NaCl; Lennox uses 5 g/L NaCl. Match the salt level of your top agar if plaque morphology matters.
Pour ~25 mL per 90-100 mm plate for phage work - thicker than usual, so plates do not dry out during long incubations.
PLATES MUST BE DRY before overlaying, or the top agar slides and plaques smear. Dry lids-off in a laminar flow hood 15-30 min, or overnight at room temperature.
Pre-warm plates to 37 C before pouring the overlay so the top agar does not set unevenly.
Supplement with CaCl2/MgSO4 from sterile 1 M stocks (added after cooling to ~50 C) if your phage requires divalent cations.

sources.title: Barrick Lab - LB: Lysogeny Broth / Luria-Bertani (Miller) Agar recipe
Barrick Lab - Media Recipes (LB: 10 g tryptone, 5 g yeast extract, 10 g NaCl per litre)
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - LB (pdb.rec8141)
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - LB Agar Plates (pdb.rec108355)

recipes.name: NZCYM Broth
recipes.summary: Rich, Mg2+-containing medium that is the standard for growing E. coli hosts and propagating bacteriophage lambda to high titre.

components.name: Magnesium sulfate heptahydrate
components.note: 2 g/L of the heptahydrate (MW 246.48) = 8.1 mM Mg2+. Anhydrous MgSO4 equivalent is 0.98 g/L (MW 120.37, PubChem CID 24083).

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in 950 mL, adjust pH, then bring to 1 L)
recipes.adjustPHWith: 5 N NaOH (approx. 0.2 mL per litre)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 20 min at 121 C (15 psi), liquid cycle
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months if sterile
recipes.hazards: 5 N NaOH is corrosive - wear gloves and eye protection when adjusting pH.
notes: MATH CHECK 2026-07-21: 2 g/L / 246.48 g/mol = 8.11 mM, so the stated 8.1 mM is correct.

TWO FORMULATIONS EXIST. The classic molecular-cloning NZCYM is the one given here. The DSMZ medium 544 version is identical plus 2 g/L maltose - check which one your protocol means, because the maltose changes LamB induction in lambda work.
'NZY' is the same medium without casamino acids; 'NZM' is without yeast extract and casamino acids.
For plates add 15 g/L agar; for top agarose add 7 g/L agarose (see the NZCYM top agarose entry).
The MgSO4 is functionally important, not just nutritional - lambda requires Mg2+ for particle stability and adsorption.
5 N NaOH is corrosive; add it dropwise with stirring.

sources.title: DSMZ Medium 544 - NZCYM medium (casein hydrolysate 10 g, NaCl 5 g, casamino acids 1 g, yeast extract 5 g, MgSO4*7H2O 2 g, maltose 2 g per litre)
PubChem - Magnesium sulfate heptahydrate, CID 24843, MW 246.48
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - NZCYM (pdb.rec8132)
Sambrook & Russell, Molecular Cloning: A Laboratory Manual, 3rd ed., Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory Press

recipes.name: NZCYM Top Agarose, 0.7%
recipes.summary: Soft agarose overlay in NZCYM for lambda plaque assays and plaque lifts; agarose rather than agar because it is cleaner for downstream nucleic acid work.

components.name: Agarose
components.note: 7 g/L.

recipes.adjustPHWith: 5 N NaOH
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 20 min at 121 C (15 psi)
recipes.storage: Room temperature as a sealed solid; remelt and hold at 45-50 C
recipes.shelfLife: Several months
recipes.hazards: Molten agarose burns; remelt carefully to avoid superheating and boil-over. 5 N NaOH is corrosive.
notes: Use AGAROSE not agar when you will do plaque lifts or hybridisation - agar contains sulfated polysaccharides that inhibit downstream enzymatic steps.
Hold at 45-50 C. Use ~3 mL per 90 mm plate, ~7 mL per 150 mm plate.
Standard lambda plating: adsorb phage to host cells resuspended in 10 mM MgSO4 for 15-20 min at 37 C, then add molten top agarose and pour.
For plates rather than overlay, use 15 g/L agar in NZCYM instead.
MATH CHECK 2026-07-21: 2 g/L MgSO4*7H2O / 246.48 = 8.11 mM, consistent with the NZCYM broth entry.

sources.title: DSMZ Medium 544 - NZCYM medium composition
Kauffman KM, Polz MF. Tube-free agar overlays: rapid plaque assays with fewer steps and materials. protocols.io, dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.j8ncrve
Chlamydomonas Resource Center - Lambda Phage DNA Prep (NZY top agarose 0.7%, NZY plates 15 g agar)
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - NZCYM (pdb.rec8132)

recipes.name: CaCl2 Stock, 1 M
recipes.summary: Sterile divalent cation stock; Ca2+ is required for adsorption by many phages and is added to media, top agar and phage buffer.

components.name: Calcium chloride dihydrate
components.note: 147.01 g/L. Anhydrous CaCl2 (MW 110.98, PubChem CID 5284359) would be 110.98 g/L; the hexahydrate CaCl2*6H2O (MW 219.07, PubChem CID 6093252) would be 219.07 g/L.

recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter (preferred) or autoclave 121 C, 15 min
recipes.storage: Room temperature or 4 C, in small sterile aliquots
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year; discard if any precipitate forms
recipes.hazards: CaCl2 dissolution is strongly exothermic - the solution gets hot. Add the salt to water slowly with stirring, in a heat-tolerant vessel. The solid is irritating to eyes and skin.
notes: Dihydrate 147.01 (CID 24844) and anhydrous 110.98 (CID 5284359) confirmed on PubChem.07.
HYDRATE FORM MATTERS: anhydrous 110.98, dihydrate 147.01, hexahydrate 219.07 g/mol. Weighing the anhydrous mass from a dihydrate bottle gives only 0.755 M.
Anhydrous CaCl2 is hygroscopic and picks up water on the shelf; the dihydrate is the more reliable choice for accurate molarity.
ADD Ca2+ AFTER AUTOCLAVING media and buffers. Autoclaving with phosphate or carbonate gives calcium precipitates.
Typical working concentrations for phage: 1-10 mM final in top agar, plates, lysate cultures and phage buffer.
An equivalent published 2.5 M stock is 11 g CaCl2*6H2O in 20 mL (11 / 219.07 / 0.020 L = 2.51 M), 0.22 um filtered, stored in 1 mL aliquots at 4 C.

sources.title: PubChem - Calcium chloride dihydrate, CID 24844, MW 147.01
PubChem - Calcium chloride (anhydrous), CID 5284359, MW 110.98
PubChem - Calcium chloride hexahydrate, CID 6093252, MW 219.07
SEA-PHAGES Reagent Preparation Guidelines (lists a separately prepared 100 mM CaCl2 stock among the components for media and phage buffer)
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - CaCl2 stock solution (pdb.rec8531)

recipes.name: MgSO4 Stock, 1 M
recipes.summary: Sterile Mg2+ stock used to supplement media, top agar and phage buffers, and to resuspend host cells before lambda adsorption.

components.name: Magnesium sulfate, anhydrous
components.note: 120.37 g/L (the published recipe is 12 g in 100 mL). If your bottle is MgSO4*7H2O (MW 246.48, PubChem CID 24843) weigh 246.48 g/L instead - roughly double the mass.

recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min, or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year; discard if cloudy
notes: Anhydrous MgSO4 120.37 (CID 24083) confirmed; heptahydrate updated from 246.47 to the PubChem value 246.48.

HYDRATE FORM MATTERS: anhydrous MgSO4 = 120.37 g/mol; heptahydrate MgSO4*7H2O (Epsom salt, the common lab form) = 246.48 g/mol. The common failure is weighing the ANHYDROUS mass (120.37 g/L) out of a bottle that is actually the HEPTAHYDRATE: that delivers only 120.37/246.48 = 0.49 M, i.e. HALF the intended concentration. The converse error (weighing 246.48 g/L of genuinely anhydrous salt) gives ~2 M, i.e. double.
Rewritten above.

The published recipe is 12 g per 100 mL. That equals 1 M only for the ANHYDROUS salt (12 / 120.37 / 0.1 L = 1.00 M), so anhydrous is what is meant. Most lab bottles are the heptahydrate - check the label before weighing.
1 M MgSO4 can precipitate on cooling after autoclaving; warm and mix before use.
Standard use: resuspend host cells in 10 mM MgSO4 before adsorbing lambda; supplement top agar to 10 mM.

sources.title: PubChem - Magnesium sulfate (anhydrous), CID 24083, MW 120.37
PubChem - Magnesium sulfate heptahydrate, CID 24843, MW 246.48
Barrick Lab - Media Recipes (1 M MgSO4 stock; 10% w/v MgSO4*7H2O stock)
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - MgSO4 (Magnesium sulfate) (pdb.rec8110)

recipes.name: Tris-Cl Stock, 1 M, pH 7.5
recipes.summary: Buffer stock used to make SM buffer, phage buffer, TM/TMG and most phage handling solutions.

components.name: Tris base
components.hazard: Concentrated HCl used for titration is corrosive and gives off fumes - work in a fume hood.
components.note: 121.14 g/L, then titrate to pH 7.5 with concentrated HCl (~65 mL of 12 N HCl per litre). Titrate to a meter - this volume is a starting estimate, not a fixed amount.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~800 mL, adjust pH, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: concentrated HCl
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 20 min, or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year
recipes.hazards: Concentrated HCl is corrosive and gives off fumes - titrate in a fume hood with gloves and eye protection. Tris dust is an irritant.
notes: Tris free base 121.14, formula C4H11NO3, confirmed on PubChem (CID 6503). Do not use 157.59 (Tris-HCl salt, PubChem CID 93573) unless you are actually weighing out the hydrochloride.
5. The standard titration table gives 70 mL for pH 7.4, 60 mL for pH 7.6 and 42 mL for pH 8.0, so pH 7.5 needs ~65 mL. 70 mL overshoots pH 7.5. Corrected to ~65 mL.

TRIS pH IS STRONGLY TEMPERATURE DEPENDENT (approx. -0.028 pH units per degree C). Adjust at the temperature the buffer will be used at, and let the solution cool to room temperature before the final adjustment.
For SM buffer and phage buffer the target is pH 7.5; TMG specifies pH 7.2 and some TM variants pH 7.4.
Autoclaved Tris often yellows slightly - normal, and it does not affect phage work.

sources.title: PubChem - Tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane, C4H11NO3, CID 6503, MW 121.14
PubChem - Tromethamine hydrochloride (Tris-HCl salt, for contrast: MW 157.59), CID 93573
Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory Press - Stock Solutions (free sample chapter, Tris-Cl titration table)

recipes.name: Gelatin Stock, 2% (w/v)
recipes.summary: Concentrated gelatin stock used to supply the 0.01% gelatin in SM and TMG buffers.
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min (this also dissolves the gelatin)
recipes.storage: 4 C (will set to a gel) or room temperature in small sterile aliquots
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months; discard at any sign of microbial growth
notes: DILUTION CHECK: 5 mL of this 2% stock per litre gives 0.1 g/L = 0.01% (w/v) final gelatin in SM buffer and TMG - a 200-fold dilution.
Gelatin does not dissolve well in cold water - sprinkle onto the water, let it swell 15-30 min, then autoclave to dissolve fully.
Gelatin is an animal protein and therefore a nutrient source, which makes this stock a prime contamination target. Aliquot small and discard aggressively.
Omit gelatin entirely for phage preps destined for protein gels, TEM or mass spectrometry.

sources.title: OpenWetWare - SM Buffer Preparation (0.01% gelatin from a 2% stock)
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - SM buffer with gelatin (pdb.rec466)

recipes.name: PEG/NaCl Phage Precipitation Solution, 5x (20% PEG-8000 / 2.5 M NaCl)
recipes.summary: Concentrates phage from lysates by polyethylene glycol precipitation; added at one quarter volume to give 1x (4% PEG / 0.5 M NaCl) final.

components.name: Polyethylene glycol 8000
components.note: 200 g/L, i.e. 100 g per 500 mL.

components.name: NaCl
components.note: 2.5 M x 58.44 = 146.1 g/L. The commonly published 75 g per 500 mL is 2.57 M - close enough for precipitation.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve NaCl first in ~80% of final volume, then add PEG in small portions with vigorous stirring, then bring to final volume)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min (swirl afterwards to redissolve), or 0.22 um filter while warm
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 6-12 months
recipes.hazards: PEG powder is a dust/inhalation nuisance - weigh carefully, ideally in a balance enclosure. The solution is very viscous, so pipette slowly and allow for hold-up volume.
notes: MATH CHECK 2026-07-21: 2.5 M NaCl = 146.1 g/L; adding 1 volume of 5x to 4 volumes of lysate gives 4% PEG and 0.5 M NaCl final, which is standard.
PEG DISSOLVES SLOWLY. Add it in small portions to the NaCl solution with vigorous stirring, or stir several hours at room temperature; gentle warming to 37-50 C helps.
Standard use: 1 volume of 5x into 4 volumes of clarified lysate, mix, hold 1 h to overnight at 4 C, then centrifuge 10,000-11,000 x g for 20-30 min at 4 C.
CLARIFY THE LYSATE FIRST (centrifuge and/or 0.45 um filter) - PEG precipitates cell debris just as well as phage.
Drain the pellet thoroughly and respin briefly to remove residual PEG; carry-over PEG inhibits restriction enzymes and PCR downstream.
PEG-6000 is used interchangeably at the same 20% w/v; PEG-8000 is the more common choice for phage.
Resuspend the pellet gently in SM buffer or phage buffer - vortexing shears tailed phage.

Bacteriophage Propagation and Quantification via the Phage on Tap Method - protocols.io

Antibody Design Labs - Small-Scale Preparation of Bacteriophage by PEG Precipitation (20% PEG-8000 / 2.5 M NaCl; 100 g PEG-8000 + 75 g NaCl to 500 mL)
Sambrook & Russell, Molecular Cloning: A Laboratory Manual, 3rd ed., Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory Press

recipes.name: Phage Glycerol Freezer Stock (25% v/v final)
recipes.summary: Cryoprotected phage stock for long-term archiving at -80 C; equal volumes of phage suspension and sterile 50% glycerol give 25% glycerol final.

components.name: Phage lysate or purified phage in SM buffer / phage buffer
components.note: Use a high-titre stock: at least 1e8 pfu/mL, ideally 1e10.

components.name: Glycerol, sterile aqueous
components.note: Equal volume of sterile 50% (v/v) glycerol. To make the 50% stock, mix 50 mL glycerol + 50 mL water and autoclave.

recipes.solvent: none - the phage suspension is the diluent
recipes.sterilization: the glycerol solution is autoclaved 121 C, 20 min; do not autoclave after adding phage
recipes.storage: 80 C in screw-cap cryovials
recipes.shelfLife: Years at -80 C; re-titre every 1-2 years
notes: FINAL GLYCEROL CONCENTRATION: 25% (v/v) here. Published open practice spans roughly 10-50% v/v final - SEA-PHAGES archives in phage buffer with 10% glycerol, the Barrick Lab adds glycerol to 20% for -80 C phage lysates, and 50% is a common cryopreservation reference condition.
USE SCREW-CAP CRYOVIALS. Snap-cap tubes can pop open at -80 C.
ALWAYS KEEP A NON-FROZEN BACKUP: most tailed phages survive for years at 4 C in SM buffer, and for some phages that is more reliable than freezing.
GLYCEROL IS NOT UNIVERSALLY PROTECTIVE FOR PHAGE - it reduces titre for some phages. Titre before and after freezing to check that your specific phage tolerates it.
Avoid freeze-thaw cycles - aliquot into single-use volumes.
20 C storage is markedly worse than -80 C for phage; use -80 C or liquid nitrogen vapour phase.
Mix gently by inversion; do not vortex tailed phage.

sources.title: Barrick Lab - Phage Lysate Preparation (glycerol to 20% for -80 C storage; chloroform for 4 C storage)
SEA-PHAGES Phage Discovery Guide - phage buffer with 10% glycerol for freezer archiving
Huang et al. Comparative evaluation of long-term preservation methods for morphologically distinct bacteriophages. Microbiology Spectrum (ASM) 2025;13(7)
Polymer-Mediated Cryopreservation of Bacteriophages. Biomacromolecules, 2021 (50 wt% glycerol at -80 C as reference condition)

recipes.name: Chloroform Treatment of Phage Lysates (use note)
recipes.summary: Adding a small volume of chloroform to a phage lysate kills residual host bacteria and releases phage from unlysed cells - but it destroys lipid-containing phages.

components.name: Chloroform
components.hazard: Chloroform is a suspected human carcinogen (IARC Group 2B), a CNS depressant and a hepatotoxin. Handle only in a fume hood with laminated or doubled nitrile gloves; single nitrile gloves are permeated within minutes. Dispose of as halogenated organic waste.
components.note: Typical range 0.5-2% (v/v). Some plate-lysate protocols use a few drops per tube (e.g. 50-100 uL per mL-scale lysate), or a 1:10 v/v wash.

recipes.solvent: not applicable - added directly to the phage lysate
recipes.sterilization: none - chloroform IS the sterilising agent here
recipes.storage: Lysates held over a chloroform layer keep at 4 C for months. Store in glass or polypropylene, never polystyrene.
recipes.shelfLife: Chloroform-treated lysates: months to years at 4 C
recipes.hazards: Chloroform (CHCl3, MW 119.37, PubChem CID 6212) is a suspected carcinogen (IARC Group 2B), hepatotoxic and nephrotoxic, and a CNS depressant. Use in a fume hood only. It dissolves polystyrene and many plastics - use glass or polypropylene. Collect as halogenated organic waste; never pour it down the drain.
notes: CRITICAL: DO NOT USE CHLOROFORM ON LIPID-CONTAINING PHAGES. Phages with lipid envelopes or internal lipid membranes (Cystoviridae such as phi6, Corticoviridae such as PM2, Tectiviridae such as PRD1) are inactivated by lipid solvents. Lambda and most tailed phages (Caudoviricetes) tolerate it.

ALWAYS TEST FIRST on a small aliquot: titre before and after chloroform. If the titre drops, skip the step and use 0.22 um filtration instead. Even non-lipid phages can lose titre through capsid protein denaturation.
Typical use: add chloroform to 0.5-2% (v/v), vortex or shake, hold at 4 C for 10-30 min, then centrifuge briefly and take the upper aqueous phase.
Chloroform is denser than water and forms the LOWER phase. Take the phage from the upper aqueous layer.
Residual chloroform inhibits some downstream enzymes and is toxic to host cells at plating - let traces evaporate, or back-extract, before sensitive assays.
DO NOT use chloroform for phage preparations intended for therapy, animal work or any clinical application - residual solvent is unacceptable there. Use filtration and chromatography instead.
Chloroform is a reasonable alternative to filtration when you need to avoid the phage losses that 0.22 um membranes cause with large or filamentous phages.

sources.title: Barrick Lab - Phage Lysate Preparation (chloroform added to lysates; fume hood, gloves, coat and eye protection)
Bonilla N et al. Phage on tap - a quick and efficient protocol for the preparation of bacteriophage laboratory stocks. PeerJ 4:e2261 (2016)
PubChem - Chloroform, CID 6212 (MW 119.37; hazard and toxicity data)
Vidaver AK et al. Bacteriophage phi6: a lipid-containing virus of Pseudomonas phaseolicola (organic-solvent sensitivity of lipid-containing phages)
The Impact of Chloroform on Viability of Temperate Bacteriophage Lambda and Survival of its Host, Escherichia coli. J Biomed Res Environ Sci

recipes.name: Maltose Stock, 20% (w/v)
recipes.summary: Sugar supplement added to host cultures to induce LamB, the outer membrane maltoporin that is the receptor for bacteriophage lambda.

components.name: Maltose monohydrate
components.note: 200 g/L. Anhydrous maltose MW = 342.30 (PubChem CID 439186); the monohydrate is 360.31 (PubChem CID 23724983, C12H24O12). Because 20% is a mass/volume spec the hydrate form does not change the mass weighed, but it does change the molarity (20% monohydrate = 0.555 M; 20% anhydrous = 0.584 M).

recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter - DO NOT AUTOCLAVE
recipes.storage: 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: 3-6 months; discard if cloudy or if crystals appear
notes: Anhydrous maltose 342.30 (C12H22O11, CID 439186) and monohydrate 360.31 (C12H24O12, CID 23724983) both looked up directly on PubChem. An earlier pass had raised the monohydrate to 360.32 by adding a rounded 18.02 for water; the correct figure is 360.31.
DO NOT AUTOCLAVE sugar solutions with media - autoclaving caramelises maltose and can generate growth-inhibitory Maillard products. Filter sterilise and add to cooled medium.
DILUTION CHECK: 10 mL of 20% stock per litre gives 0.2% (w/v) final, the standard level for inducing LamB in the host before lambda infection.
Only relevant for lambda and other LamB-dependent phages. Most phages use a different receptor and need no maltose.
LamB is a maltoporin and is maltose-inducible; growth on maltose raises LamB levels and improves lambda adsorption relative to glucose-grown cells.
Commonly paired with 10 mM MgSO4 in the growth medium for lambda plating hosts.

recipes.name: Ampicillin stock solution, 100 mg/mL (1000x)
recipes.summary: 1000x selection stock for beta-lactamase (bla / AmpR) plasmids in E. coli; working concentration 100 ug/mL.

components.name: Ampicillin sodium salt
components.form: Ampicillin sodium salt (sodium ampicillin), CAS 69-52-3, C16H18N3NaO4S, MW 371.4 g/mol
components.note: MW and formula verified on PubChem: C16H18N3NaO4S, 371.4 g/mol. 100 mg/mL = 100 g/L = 0.269 M. Use the SODIUM SALT, which is freely water-soluble; the free acid (ampicillin anhydrous, C16H19N3O4S, MW 349.4, PubChem CID 6249) dissolves poorly in water and needs base or another solvent.
components.hazard: Beta-lactam. Potent contact sensitiser / allergen - wear gloves and avoid inhaling powder. Do not handle if penicillin-allergic.

recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: 20 C in single-use aliquots (protect from repeated freeze-thaw); 4 C only for a couple of weeks of active use
recipes.shelfLife: 4-6 months at -20 C (some suppliers quote up to 1 year); about 2 weeks at 4 C
recipes.hazards: Beta-lactam antibiotic; potent allergen/sensitiser. Avoid skin contact and dust inhalation.
notes: Working concentration in E. coli: 100 ug/mL (1:1000 dilution of this stock), the value given by both Addgene and the Barrick Lab. 50 ug/mL is sometimes used for low-copy plasmids.

The Barrick Lab protocol page gives 4-6 months at -20 C and about 2 weeks at 4 C, and explicitly flags that ampicillin degrades quickly both in stock solution and in plates. The shorter figures are used here.
Add to molten agar only after it has cooled - ampicillin is heat-labile and is destroyed by autoclaving.
Secreted beta-lactamase degrades the ampicillin around large AmpR colonies, so satellite colonies appear on plates older than roughly a day. Carbenicillin avoids this.
Prewash a new 0.22 or 0.45 um filter with water before filtering to flush out filter extractables.

sources.title: PubChem CID 23663979 - Ampicillin sodium (C16H18N3NaO4S, MW 371.4)
PubChem CID 6249 - Ampicillin free acid (C16H19N3O4S, MW 349.4)

Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - Ampicillin Stock Solution (1000x), pdb.rec108101
Sigma-Aldrich A9518 - Ampicillin sodium salt (CAS 69-52-3)

recipes.name: Carbenicillin stock solution, 100 mg/mL (1000x)
recipes.summary: More stable beta-lactam substitute for ampicillin on AmpR plasmids; greatly reduces satellite colonies.

components.name: Carbenicillin disodium salt
components.form: Carbenicillin disodium salt (alpha-carboxybenzylpenicillin disodium), CAS 4800-94-6, C17H16N2Na2O6S, MW 422.4 g/mol
components.note: MW and formula verified on PubChem: C17H16N2Na2O6S, 422.4 g/mol. 100 mg/mL = 100 g/L = 0.237 M. The disodium salt is freely water-soluble.
components.hazard: Beta-lactam; allergen/sensitiser as for ampicillin.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (bring to final volume); 50% ethanol is used by some labs, in which case filtration can be skipped
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter for the aqueous stock; not required if the stock is made in 50% ethanol
recipes.storage: 20 C in aliquots
recipes.shelfLife: 6-12 months at -20 C; about 1 month at 4 C
recipes.hazards: Beta-lactam antibiotic; potent allergen/sensitiser.
notes: Working concentration in E. coli: 100 ug/mL (1:1000), per both Addgene and the Barrick Lab. Some labs use 50 ug/mL.
Interchangeable with ampicillin for selecting bla (AmpR) plasmids. Carbenicillin is a poorer beta-lactamase substrate, so plates stay selective longer and give far fewer satellite colonies.
The Barrick Lab prepares its 100 mg/mL carbenicillin stock in 50% ethanol and does not filter it; an aqueous filter-sterilised stock works equally well and is what is specified above.
Heat-labile: add to molten agar after cooling, never before autoclaving.

sources.title: PubChem CID 20933 - Carbenicillin disodium (C17H16N2Na2O6S, MW 422.4)
Barrick Lab - Antibiotic Stock Solutions (carbenicillin 100 mg/mL in 50% EtOH, 100 ug/mL working, -20 C, no filtration)
Addgene - Molecular Biology Reference: Antibiotics (carbenicillin 100 mg/mL stock, 100 ug/mL working; usable in place of ampicillin)
Sigma-Aldrich - Carbenicillin disodium salt substance record (CAS 4800-94-6)

recipes.name: Kanamycin stock solution, 50 mg/mL (1000x)
recipes.summary: 1000x selection stock for aminoglycoside phosphotransferase (nptII / KanR) plasmids in E. coli; working concentration 50 ug/mL.

components.name: Kanamycin sulfate
components.form: Kanamycin A monosulfate, CAS 25389-94-0, C18H38N4O15S (i.e. C18H36N4O11 - H2SO4), MW 582.6 g/mol
components.note: MW and formula verified on PubChem: C18H38N4O15S, 582.6 g/mol for the monosulfate. Kanamycin A free base is C18H36N4O11, MW 484.5 (PubChem CID 6032). 50 mg/mL = 50 g/L = 0.0858 M of the sulfate salt. Commercial kanamycin sulfate is sold on a potency basis (often >=750 ug base per mg); for routine plasmid selection the salt mass is weighed directly without potency correction.
components.hazard: Aminoglycoside. Avoid inhalation; ototoxic/nephrotoxic on systemic exposure.

recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: 20 C in aliquots (4 C acceptable for short periods)
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year at -20 C
recipes.hazards: Aminoglycoside antibiotic; avoid dust inhalation.
notes: Working concentration in E. coli: 50 ug/mL (1:1000 dilution). Addgene and the Barrick Lab both list 50 mg/mL stock and 50 ug/mL working.
Kanamycin is relatively heat-stable but is still added to molten agar after cooling, by convention.
Aminoglycoside uptake falls at high ionic strength, so kanamycin selection is weaker on high-salt LB; low-salt LB (Lennox, 5 g/L NaCl) gives cleaner selection.
Some labs use 30 ug/mL on plates and 50 ug/mL in liquid culture.
The Barrick Lab notes possible cross-selection between kanamycin and gentamicin resistance markers - do not assume the two are independent.
