recipes.name: Chloramphenicol stock solution, 25 mg/mL (1000x, ethanol)
recipes.summary: 1000x ethanolic selection stock for chloramphenicol acetyltransferase (cat / CmR) plasmids; working concentration 25 ug/mL.

components.name: Chloramphenicol
components.form: Chloramphenicol free base, CAS 56-75-7, C11H12Cl2N2O5, MW 323.13 g/mol
components.note: MW and formula verified on PubChem: C11H12Cl2N2O5, 323.13 g/mol. 25 mg/mL = 25 g/L = 77.4 mM. Chloramphenicol is only sparingly water-soluble (a few mg/mL), so the stock is made in ethanol rather than water.
components.hazard: Suspected carcinogen; associated with idiosyncratic aplastic anaemia. Weigh the powder in a fume hood and wear gloves.

recipes.solvent: 100% ethanol (bring to final volume); 70% or 95% ethanol also works
recipes.sterilization: none - an ethanolic stock is effectively self-sterilising. If you do filter, use a solvent-resistant membrane (PTFE or nylon); cellulose-ester membranes are attacked by ethanol
recipes.storage: 20 C in aliquots, protected from light
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months to 1 year at -20 C
recipes.hazards: Chloramphenicol is a suspected carcinogen and is linked to aplastic anaemia. Weigh in a fume hood; wear gloves. Ethanol is flammable - keep away from ignition sources.
notes: Working concentration in E. coli: 25 ug/mL (1:1000 of this stock). Addgene lists exactly this pairing (25 mg/mL in ethanol, 25 ug/mL working). The Barrick Lab uses 20 mg/mL and 20 ug/mL, and 34 ug/mL is another widely used value; the accepted range spans roughly 10-34 ug/mL. Check the convention for your plasmid and strain.
Because the stock is ethanolic, keep the added volume below about 1% of the medium volume - 1 mL per litre at 1000x is well inside that.
Add to molten agar after cooling. Chloramphenicol itself is comparatively heat-stable, but ethanol flashes off in very hot agar.
For a 34 mg/mL stock, dissolve 340 mg in ethanol to 10 mL final volume and use at 1:1000 for 34 ug/mL.

sources.title: PubChem CID 5959 - Chloramphenicol (C11H12Cl2N2O5, MW 323.13)
Addgene - Molecular Biology Reference: Antibiotics (chloramphenicol 25 mg/mL in ethanol, 25 ug/mL working)
Barrick Lab - Antibiotic Stock Solutions (chloramphenicol 20 mg/mL in EtOH, 20 ug/mL working, -20 C, no filtration needed)

recipes.name: Tetracycline stock solution, 10 mg/mL (1000x, 70% ethanol)
recipes.summary: 1000x selection stock for tetracycline-resistance (tetA / TcR) plasmids; working concentration 10 ug/mL.

components.name: Tetracycline hydrochloride
components.form: Tetracycline hydrochloride, CAS 64-75-5, C22H25ClN2O8 (written as C22H24N2O8 - HCl), MW 480.9 g/mol
components.note: MW and formula verified on PubChem: C22H25ClN2O8, 480.9 g/mol for the hydrochloride. Tetracycline free base is C22H24N2O8, MW 444.4 (PubChem CID 54675776) and is much less soluble; the HCl salt is the standard laboratory reagent. 10 mg/mL = 10 g/L = 20.8 mM.
components.hazard: Harmful if swallowed; strongly light-sensitive; stains. Avoid dust inhalation.

recipes.solvent: 70% ethanol (bring to final volume); 50% or 100% ethanol also used. Water is acceptable only for stocks used within days
recipes.sterilization: none if made in ethanol (self-sterilising); 0.22 um filter if made in water
recipes.storage: 20 C in aliquots, wrapped in foil - tetracycline is strongly light-sensitive
recipes.shelfLife: Treat as short-lived: the Barrick Lab flags tetracycline stock stability at under 2 weeks. Foil-wrapped ethanolic stocks held at -20 C are often used for a few months, but make fresh stock if selection looks weak. Aqueous stocks: about 2 weeks at 4 C in the dark
recipes.hazards: Light-sensitive; photodegradation products (including anhydrotetracycline) are biologically active. Harmful if swallowed. Ethanol is flammable.
notes: Working concentration in E. coli: 10 ug/mL (1:1000). Addgene and the Barrick Lab both give 10 mg/mL stock and 10 ug/mL working. 12.5 and 15 ug/mL are also common.
The Barrick Lab protocol page reports tetracycline stock stability of less than 2 weeks, so the entry now leads with the short figure and treats multi-month storage as best-case rather than expected.
Tetracycline chelates divalent cations - Mg2+ and Ca2+ in rich media reduce its potency. Avoid magnesium-supplemented media when selecting with tetracycline.
Protect plates and cultures from light; keep tetracycline plates foil-wrapped at 4 C and use within about 2-4 weeks.
Add to molten agar after cooling.
Ethanolic stocks are markedly more stable than aqueous ones - use 70% ethanol for anything kept longer than a few days.

sources.title: PubChem CID 54704426 - Tetracycline hydrochloride (C22H25ClN2O8, MW 480.9)
PubChem CID 54675776 - Tetracycline free base (C22H24N2O8, MW 444.4)
Barrick Lab - Antibiotic Stock Solutions (tetracycline 10 mg/mL in 70% EtOH, 10 ug/mL working, -20 C, light sensitive, stability under 2 weeks)
Addgene - Molecular Biology Reference: Antibiotics (tetracycline 10 mg/mL stock, 10 ug/mL working)
Sigma-Aldrich T7660 - Tetracycline hydrochloride (CAS 64-75-5)

recipes.name: Streptomycin stock solution, 50 mg/mL (1000x)
recipes.summary: 1000x aqueous stock for streptomycin selection (aadA / strA-strB markers, or rpsL counter-selection); working concentration 50 ug/mL.

components.name: Streptomycin sulfate
components.form: Streptomycin sulfate salt, CAS 3810-74-0, 2 C21H39N7O12 - 3 H2SO4 (empirical formula C42H84N14O36S3), MW 1457.4 g/mol for the 2:3 formula unit
components.note: Salt statement. PubChem gives the streptomycin sulfate record as C42H84N14O36S3, MW 1457.4 - that is 2 streptomycin to 3 sulfuric acid. The commonly quoted 728.7 g/mol is exactly half of this, i.e. the same material expressed per streptomycin unit (1 streptomycin : 1.5 H2SO4). Both describe the same substance; check which convention your bottle label uses before doing molar calculations. 50 mg/mL = 50 g/L = 68.6 mM in streptomycin units (34.3 mM of the 2:3 formula unit).
components.hazard: Aminoglycoside; ototoxic/nephrotoxic on systemic exposure. Avoid dust inhalation.

recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: 20 C in aliquots
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year at -20 C
recipes.hazards: Aminoglycoside antibiotic; avoid dust inhalation.
notes: Working concentration in E. coli: 50 ug/mL is a common laboratory default, but the useful range is wide. The Barrick Lab uses a 100 mg/mL stock at 100 ug/mL working; published values span roughly 25-100 ug/mL depending on strain and marker. 100 ug/mL is typical when selecting spontaneous rpsL (StrR) mutants.
Streptomycin is heat-labile - add to molten agar after cooling.
Aminoglycoside uptake depends on the proton-motive force, so streptomycin is much less effective under anaerobic conditions and on very rich or high-salt media.
Frequently paired with spectinomycin, since aadA confers resistance to both.

sources.title: PubChem CID 19648 - Streptomycin sulfate (C42H84N14O36S3, MW 1457.4; 2 streptomycin : 3 H2SO4)
Barrick Lab - Antibiotic Stock Solutions (streptomycin 100 mg/mL in water, 100 ug/mL working, -20 C, filter sterilise)
Sigma-Aldrich - Streptomycin sulfate salt substance record (CAS 3810-74-0)

recipes.name: Spectinomycin stock solution, 50 mg/mL (1000x)
recipes.summary: 1000x aqueous stock for spectinomycin selection (aadA / SpecR); working concentration 50 ug/mL.

components.name: Spectinomycin dihydrochloride pentahydrate
components.form: Spectinomycin dihydrochloride pentahydrate, CAS 22189-32-8, C14H24N2O7 - 2 HCl - 5 H2O (PubChem writes the hydrate as C14H36Cl2N2O12), MW 495.3 g/mol
components.note: MW and hydrate form verified on PubChem: 495.3 g/mol for the dihydrochloride pentahydrate. Spectinomycin free base is C14H24N2O7, MW 332.35 (PubChem CID 15541), so about 67% of the pentahydrate salt mass is spectinomycin base - scale accordingly if a protocol specifies base equivalents. 50 mg/mL = 50 g/L = 101 mM of the salt.
components.hazard: Aminocyclitol antibiotic. Avoid dust inhalation.

recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: 20 C in aliquots, protected from light
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year at -20 C
recipes.hazards: Avoid dust inhalation; standard antibiotic handling precautions.
notes: Working concentration in E. coli: 50 ug/mL (1:1000), which is the pairing Addgene lists. The Barrick Lab uses 60 mg/mL at 60 ug/mL, and doubles it to about 120 ug/mL for cloning selection. 100 ug/mL is common where background growth is a problem, and 25 ug/mL when combined with a second antibiotic.
Add to molten agar after cooling.
AadA confers resistance to both spectinomycin and streptomycin; the two are often used together.
Spectinomycin selection is noticeably weaker on high-salt media - low-salt LB gives cleaner selection.

sources.title: PubChem CID 30971 - Spectinomycin dihydrochloride pentahydrate (MW 495.3)
PubChem CID 15541 - Spectinomycin free base (C14H24N2O7, MW 332.35)
Addgene - Molecular Biology Reference: Antibiotics (spectinomycin 50 mg/mL stock, 50 ug/mL working)

recipes.name: Gentamicin stock solution, 10 mg/mL (1000x)
recipes.summary: 1000x aqueous stock for gentamicin selection (aacC1 / GmR); working concentration 10-20 ug/mL in E. coli.

components.name: Gentamicin sulfate
components.form: Gentamicin sulfate, CAS 1405-41-0 - a MIXTURE of the sulfate salts of gentamicins C1, C1a, C2, C2a and C2b. No single molecular weight applies to the material sold for laboratory use.
components.note: Deliberately NOT given a mw_gmol. PubChem CID 9855350 records one defined gentamicin sulfate stoichiometry (C60H125N15O25S, MW 1488.8), but the reagent on the shelf is a multi-component mixture, so molarity is not meaningful. Vendors sell it on a potency basis (commonly around 590-700 ug gentamicin base per mg salt). Weigh by salt mass for routine selection; correct using the lot potency printed on the bottle only if your protocol specifies potency units. 10 mg/mL = 10 g/L.
components.hazard: Aminoglycoside; ototoxic/nephrotoxic on systemic exposure. Avoid dust inhalation.

recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: 20 C in aliquots (4 C acceptable; gentamicin is comparatively stable)
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year at -20 C
recipes.hazards: Aminoglycoside antibiotic; avoid dust inhalation.
notes: MARKED UNCERTAIN for two reasons: (1) gentamicin sulfate has no single molecular weight - it is a defined mixture and vendor potency varies between lots, so molar calculations are not possible; (2) the published E. coli working concentration varies substantially between sources.

Working concentration in E. coli: the Barrick Lab uses a 20 mg/mL stock at 20 ug/mL working, and 15-25 ug/mL is widely used; some tables give 10 ug/mL. Gram-negative work outside E. coli commonly uses 20-30 ug/mL. Confirm against your own plasmid and strain before relying on the low end.
If you follow the Barrick Lab values, make the stock at 20 mg/mL instead and still add it 1:1000.
The Barrick Lab flags possible cross-talk between gentamicin and kanamycin resistance - do not assume the two markers select independently.
Add to molten agar after cooling.
Aminoglycoside uptake is proton-motive-force dependent - selection is weak under anaerobic conditions.

sources.title: PubChem CID 9855350 - Gentamicin sulfate (one defined stoichiometry; the lab reagent is a C1/C1a/C2/C2a/C2b mixture)
Barrick Lab - Antibiotic Stock Solutions (gentamicin 20 mg/mL in water, 20 ug/mL working, -20 C; kanamycin cross-talk note)
Sigma-Aldrich G1264 - Gentamicin sulfate (CAS 1405-41-0; sold on a potency basis)

recipes.name: Erythromycin stock solution, 100 mg/mL (ethanol)
recipes.summary: Ethanolic macrolide stock, mainly for erm-marked Gram-positive work; E. coli requires much higher concentrations because of outer-membrane exclusion and efflux.

components.name: Erythromycin
components.form: Erythromycin A free base, CAS 114-07-8, C37H67NO13, MW 733.9 g/mol
components.note: MW and formula verified on PubChem: C37H67NO13, 733.9 g/mol. 100 mg/mL = 100 g/L = 136 mM. The free base is essentially insoluble in water and must be dissolved in ethanol, methanol or DMSO. Erythromycin estolate, stearate and ethylsuccinate are different salts/esters with different molecular weights and are NOT interchangeable for laboratory selection.
components.hazard: Harmful if swallowed. Ethanol is flammable.

recipes.solvent: 100% ethanol (bring to final volume); DMSO or methanol also acceptable
recipes.sterilization: none - an ethanolic solution is self-sterilising. If filtration is unavoidable, use a solvent-resistant (PTFE or nylon) membrane
recipes.storage: 20 C in aliquots, protected from light
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months to 1 year at -20 C
recipes.hazards: Macrolide antibiotic; harmful if swallowed. Ethanol is flammable - keep away from ignition sources.
notes: MARKED UNCERTAIN: the working concentration of erythromycin in E. coli is genuinely not standardised. E. coli is intrinsically resistant because the outer membrane excludes macrolides and AcrAB-TolC pumps them out, so reported selection concentrations span roughly 100-500 ug/mL. The Barrick Lab uses a 250 mg/mL ethanolic stock at 250 ug/mL working for E. coli; 150 ug/mL is another frequently cited value for erm plasmid selection. Verify with a kill curve for your strain.

For Gram-positive hosts (Bacillus, Staphylococcus, Lactococcus, Streptococcus) erm selection is typically 5-10 ug/mL, and MLS-resistance selection often uses about 1 ug/mL erythromycin with 25 ug/mL lincomycin.

Ethanol load: at 250 ug/mL from this 100 mg/mL stock you add 2.5 mL per litre, i.e. 0.25% ethanol - tolerated. Do not exceed about 1% ethanol in the medium. If you prefer a 250 mg/mL stock, only 1 mL per litre is needed.
Add to molten agar after cooling.
Erythromycin is acid-labile; do not use it in acidified media.

Many erm resistance genes are INDUCIBLE rather than constitutive. Plating untreated cells straight onto a high erythromycin concentration can kill them before resistance is expressed. If transformants fail to appear, allow a longer outgrowth or pre-induce with a sub-inhibitory concentration before applying full selection.

sources.title: PubChem CID 12560 - Erythromycin (C37H67NO13, MW 733.9)
Barrick Lab - Antibiotic Stock Solutions (erythromycin 250 mg/mL in EtOH, 250 ug/mL working for E. coli, -20 C, no filtration needed)
Sigma-Aldrich E6376 - Erythromycin (CAS 114-07-8)

Ge B. et al., Sci Rep 7:11017 (2017) - Effects of low concentrations of erythromycin, penicillin and virginiamycin on bacterial resistance development in vitro. NOTE: tested Campylobacter and Enterococcus, not E. coli.

recipes.name: IPTG stock solution, 1 M
recipes.summary: Gratuitous, non-metabolisable lac operon inducer for T7/lac and tac/trc expression systems and for blue-white screening.

components.name: IPTG
components.form: Isopropyl beta-D-1-thiogalactopyranoside, CAS 367-93-1, C9H18O5S, MW 238.30 g/mol (dioxane-free grade preferred)
components.note: MW and formula verified on PubChem: C9H18O5S, 238.30 g/mol. Math check: 1 M x 238.30 g/mol = 238.30 g/L, i.e. 2.383 g in a final volume of 10 mL. Highly water-soluble.
components.hazard: Low toxicity at bench scale; avoid raising dust.

recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: 20 C in 1 mL single-use aliquots; 4 C for up to about 1 month in active use
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year at -20 C
recipes.hazards: Not classified as hazardous at bench scale; standard gloves and eye protection.
notes: Working concentrations from this 1 M stock (1:1000 gives 1 mM): protein induction in BL21(DE3) is typically 0.1-1.0 mM, with 0.4-0.5 mM the most common default. Lower IPTG (0.05-0.1 mM) combined with a lower temperature favours soluble protein.
Blue-white screening: 0.1 mM final in the plate, or spread 100 uL of 0.1 M IPTG on the plate surface and let it absorb for at least 30 min before plating.
A 20% (w/v) stock is 200 g/L, which is 200 / 238.30 = 0.839 M, not 0.8 M. Prepare it by mass (2 g in a final volume of 10 mL) rather than back-calculating from a rounded molarity.
IPTG is not metabolised by E. coli, so induction is sustained - unlike lactose or arabinose.
Do not autoclave. Filter-sterilise only.

sources.title: PubChem CID 656894 - IPTG (C9H18O5S, MW 238.30)
protocols.io - Efficient Preparation of Competent Cells and Recombinant Protein Expression in E. coli BL21(DE3) Using IPTG Induction
Addgene Blog - Plasmids 101: Inducible promoters (lac/IPTG, pBAD/arabinose, Tet systems; mechanism)
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - IPTG solution, pdb.rec8091
Sigma-Aldrich I5502 - Isopropyl beta-D-1-thiogalactopyranoside (CAS 367-93-1)

recipes.name: X-gal stock solution, 20 mg/mL (2% w/v in DMF)
recipes.summary: Chromogenic beta-galactosidase substrate for blue-white screening of lacZ-alpha recombinants; hydrolysis releases an indolyl product that oxidises to an insoluble blue dye.

components.name: X-gal
components.note: MW and formula verified on PubChem: C14H15BrClNO6, 408.63 g/mol. 20 mg/mL = 20 g/L = 2% (w/v) = 48.9 mM. X-gal is not water-soluble and must be dissolved in N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) or DMSO.
components.hazard: Handle in glass or polypropylene only - DMF dissolves polystyrene.

components.name: N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF)
components.form: N,N-dimethylformamide, anhydrous, CAS 68-12-2, C3H7NO, MW 73.09 g/mol
components.note: The solvent, listed as a component so its hazard travels with the recipe. Verified on PubChem: C3H7NO, 73.09 g/mol.
components.hazard: Reproductive toxicant (suspected of damaging the unborn child), skin-absorbed, flammable. Handle only in a fume hood with nitrile gloves.

recipes.sterilization: none - a DMF solution is self-sterilising; do not filter, as DMF destroys most filter membranes
recipes.storage: 20 C, wrapped in aluminium foil (light-sensitive), in a glass or polypropylene tube
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year at -20 C protected from light
recipes.hazards: DMF is a reproductive toxin and is readily absorbed through skin. Prepare in a fume hood wearing nitrile gloves; keep away from ignition sources. DMF dissolves polystyrene - never use standard polystyrene tubes or pipettes.
notes: Working concentration: 40 ug/mL final in LB agar (2 mL of this 20 mg/mL stock per litre of medium).

Surface-spread alternative, more economical and the more common bench practice: spread 40 uL of the 20 mg/mL stock over a standard 90 mm plate and let it absorb for at least 30 min before plating cells.
Stocks at 25 and 40 mg/mL are also sold or specified elsewhere; adjust the volume added to reach 40 ug/mL final.
Use with IPTG (0.1 mM final) to induce lacZ. Blue colour needs 16-20 h at 37 C and deepens further if plates are held at 4 C for a few hours.
DMSO can substitute for DMF and is less toxic, but X-gal is somewhat less soluble in it.

sources.title: PubChem CID 65181 - X-Gal (C14H15BrClNO6, MW 408.63)
PubChem CID 6228 - N,N-Dimethylformamide (C3H7NO, MW 73.09; GHS reproductive toxicity classification)
Addgene Blog - Plasmids 101: Blue-white screening (lacZ-deltaM15 host requirement)

Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - X-gal solution, pdb.rec8092

recipes.name: L-arabinose stock solution, 20% (w/v)
recipes.summary: Inducer of the araBAD (pBAD) promoter; supports graded, titratable induction in arabinose-catabolism-deficient hosts.

components.name: L-(+)-Arabinose
components.note: MW and formula verified on PubChem: C5H10O5, 150.13 g/mol. Math check: 20% (w/v) = 200 g/L; 200 / 150.13 = 1.33 M. Use the L-enantiomer - D-arabinose does not induce araBAD. Very freely water-soluble.
components.hazard: Non-hazardous sugar.

recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter (do not autoclave - sugars caramelise and can form growth-inhibitory products)
recipes.storage: 4 C, or -20 C in aliquots for long-term
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months at 4 C; 1 year at -20 C
notes: Working concentration: 0.2% (w/v) final = 13.3 mM gives full induction; add 10 mL of this 20% stock per litre of medium (1:100).

For titrating expression level, the Invitrogen pBAD manual specifies testing 0.00002%, 0.0002%, 0.002%, 0.02% and 0.2% - make the intermediate points by serial 10-fold dilution of the 20% stock in sterile water.

Graded rather than all-or-none induction requires a host defective in arabinose catabolism and transport, e.g. TOP10 or LMG194. In wild-type E. coli the arabinose is consumed and the population induces heterogeneously.
Glucose represses pBAD via catabolite repression - use glycerol (0.2%) rather than glucose as the carbon source when inducing.
A 10% or 2% stock is often more convenient for small-scale titrations; scale the mass accordingly.

sources.title: PubChem CID 439195 - L-Arabinose (C5H10O5, MW 150.13)
Addgene Blog - Plasmids 101: Inducible promoters (AraC/pBAD induction mechanism)
Invitrogen/Thermo Fisher - pBAD/His and pBAD/Myc-His User Manual (20% arabinose stock; 0.00002%-0.2% induction series; TOP10/LMG194 hosts)
Schleif R. FEMS Microbiol Rev 34:779 (2010) - AraC and regulation of the L-arabinose operon in E. coli

recipes.name: Anhydrotetracycline (aTc) stock solution, 400 ug/mL (1000x)
recipes.summary: Non-antibiotic tetracycline analogue that induces TetR-repressed promoters (pTet/tetO) at concentrations far below any growth effect.

components.name: Anhydrotetracycline hydrochloride
components.form: Anhydrotetracycline hydrochloride, CAS 13803-65-1, C22H23ClN2O7 (written as C22H22N2O7 - HCl), MW 462.9 g/mol
components.note: MW and formula verified on PubChem: C22H23ClN2O7, 462.9 g/mol. Math check: 400 ug/mL = 0.4 g/L; 0.4 / 462.9 = 8.64e-4 M = 864 uM. This is close to saturation in water - do not attempt a more concentrated aqueous stock.
components.hazard: Light-sensitive; tetracycline-family compound. Avoid dust inhalation.

recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: 20 C in 1 mL single-use aliquots, protected from light
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year at -20 C if not subjected to repeated freeze-thaw
recipes.hazards: Strongly light-sensitive. Avoid dust inhalation; handle as a tetracycline-class compound.
notes: OPEN SOURCE GAP: PubChem covers the chemistry (MW, formula, salt form) under public domain, but the 400 ug/mL aqueous 1000x recipe and the induction-plateau data below currently have only closed-access support (Cold Spring Harbor Protocols; Nucleic Acids Research). Treat the practice numbers as provisional until confirmed against an open protocol.
Working concentration: 400 ng/mL from a 1:1000 dilution of this stock, per the Cold Spring Harbor 1000x recipe.

Most TetR/pTet systems are fully induced near 100 ng/mL (216 nM); above roughly 100 ng/mL the induction factor changes little, and 100 ng/mL is commonly cited as the highest concentration causing no growth retardation. Titrate over 0-200 ng/mL for graded expression.
Water is the recommended solvent here even though ethanol and DMSO are more usual for tetracyclines, because aTc decays more slowly in water at this near-saturating concentration.
ATc binds TetR far more tightly than tetracycline does but has essentially no antibacterial activity, which is why it is preferred as an inducer.
Protect cultures and plates from light; aTc photodegrades quickly. Add it fresh at the time of induction.

Addgene Blog - Plasmids 101: Inducible promoters (TetR/tet system mechanism)

Rodriguez-Garcia A. et al., Nucleic Acids Res 33:e87 (2005) - tetracycline-inducible promoters; induction factor changes little above 100 ng/mL aTc. NOTE: measured in Streptomyces, not E. coli.

recipes.name: Antibiotic supplementation of LB medium - working concentrations (stock to final)
recipes.summary: Calculator sheet: how much of each antibiotic stock to add per volume of LB broth or molten LB agar for standard E. coli selection. Every row below is 1:1000 except erythromycin, which is 1:400 from the 100 mg/mL stock.

components.name: Ampicillin
components.note: 1:1000. Most common AmpR selection; Addgene and Barrick Lab both give this pairing.

components.name: Carbenicillin
components.note: 1:1000. Substitute for ampicillin; far fewer satellite colonies.

components.name: Kanamycin
components.note: 1:1000. Addgene and Barrick Lab agree on 50/50.

components.name: Chloramphenicol
components.note: 1:1000. Ethanolic stock. 10-34 ug/mL all in common use; Barrick Lab uses 20 ug/mL.

components.name: Tetracycline
components.note: 1:1000. Ethanolic stock. Keep plates in the dark.

components.name: Streptomycin
components.note: 1:1000. Range 25-100 ug/mL; Barrick Lab uses 100 ug/mL.

components.name: Spectinomycin
components.note: 1:1000. Addgene gives 50/50; Barrick Lab uses 60 ug/mL, and about 120 ug/mL for cloning selection.

components.name: Gentamicin
components.note: To 20/20 to match the Barrick Lab table, which is the open reference used here. Older tables give 10 ug/mL; the practical range is 10-25 ug/mL - verify for your system.

components.name: Erythromycin
components.note: To 250 ug/mL to match the Barrick Lab value for E. coli (2.5 mL of a 100 mg/mL stock per litre = 0.25% ethanol). E. coli is intrinsically macrolide-resistant. Gram-positive hosts need only 5-10 ug/mL. Highly variable - verify empirically.

recipes.solvent: LB broth or molten LB agar (the antibiotic stock volume is negligible and is not subtracted from the medium volume)
recipes.sterilization: none - the medium is autoclaved first, then the sterile antibiotic stock is added aseptically
recipes.storage: LB + antibiotic broth: 4 C, use within about 1 month. Plates: 4 C, inverted, in a sealed bag
recipes.shelfLife: Do not store plates longer than about 1 month at any temperature, and inspect for contamination before use (Addgene). Ampicillin and carbenicillin plates degrade fastest; tetracycline plates should be used within 2-4 weeks and kept in the dark
recipes.hazards: See the individual antibiotic recipes. Chloramphenicol (suspected carcinogen) and the beta-lactams (potent allergens) deserve particular care.
notes: CRITICAL: cool autoclaved agar before adding antibiotic. Addgene specifies 60 C, at which agar is still molten but most antibiotics survive; many protocols use 50-55 C, which is safer for the heat-labile beta-lactams and streptomycin. Anything hotter destroys them.
Addgene's plate-pouring protocol caps storage at about 1 month at any temperature, and that is the figure used here.
At 1000x, add 1 mL of stock per litre of medium (1 uL per mL). Swirl thoroughly but gently to avoid bubbles before pouring.
A standard 90 mm plate takes about 25 mL, so 1 L of agar pours roughly 40 plates.
When selecting with two antibiotics at once, many labs halve each concentration to reduce combined toxicity.
These are E. coli values. Other organisms differ substantially - do not transfer them to Agrobacterium, Pseudomonas, Bacillus and so on without checking.
Uses the 10 mg/mL gentamicin stock in this pack (10 µL per mL of medium for 10 µg/mL).

recipes.name: Blue-white screening supplement for LB agar (IPTG + X-gal)
recipes.summary: IPTG and X-gal additions to antibiotic LB agar for lacZ-alpha complementation screening of recombinant clones.

components.name: IPTG
components.note: 1:10000 dilution of the 1 M stock, i.e. 100 uL per litre of medium. Induces lacZ expression.

components.name: X-gal
components.note: 1:500 dilution, i.e. 2 mL of the 20 mg/mL DMF stock per litre of medium.
components.hazard: Dissolved in DMF, a reproductive toxin - add in a fume hood wearing nitrile gloves.

components.name: Ampicillin (or the appropriate plasmid marker)
components.note: Blue-white screening is always layered on top of normal plasmid selection.

recipes.solvent: molten LB agar cooled to 50-60 C
recipes.sterilization: none - added aseptically to already-autoclaved medium
recipes.storage: 4 C, inverted, in the dark (foil-wrapped or in a light-proof bag)
recipes.shelfLife: About 1 month at 4 C protected from light
recipes.hazards: The X-gal stock is dissolved in DMF, a reproductive toxin that is absorbed through skin - handle in a fume hood with nitrile gloves. DMF also dissolves polystyrene.
notes: Surface-spread alternative, which uses far less X-gal and is the more common bench practice: onto a pre-poured antibiotic LB plate, spread 40 uL of 20 mg/mL X-gal plus 100 uL of 0.1 M IPTG, and let it absorb for at least 30 min at 37 C before plating cells.
Requires a lacZ-deltaM15 host - Addgene lists XL1-Blue, DH5-alpha, DH10B, JM109, STBL4, JM110 and TOP10 - together with a vector carrying lacZ-alpha with the multiple cloning site inside it.
Blue colour needs 16-20 h at 37 C. If colonies are pale, move plates to 4 C for 2-4 h - the indigo dye intensifies in the cold.
Faint-blue colonies can still be recombinant (small in-frame inserts); pick clearly white colonies and confirm by colony PCR or restriction digest.
IPTG can be omitted with lacI-negative hosts, but including it gives more reliable colour.

sources.title: Addgene Blog - Plasmids 101: Blue-white screening (lacZ-deltaM15 host requirement; XL1-Blue, DH5-alpha, DH10B, JM109, STBL4, JM110, TOP10)

recipes.name: TAE Electrophoresis Buffer, 50x
recipes.summary: Concentrated stock for agarose gel running buffer; dilute 1:50 to 1x (40 mM Tris-acetate, 1 mM EDTA).

components.name: Tris base
components.form: Tris base (free base, tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane / tromethamine), C4H11NO3, MW 121.14, CAS 77-86-1
components.note: 242 g/L / 121.14 g/mol = 2.00 M in the 50x stock, giving 40 mM Tris in 1x. Do not substitute Tris-HCl salt (MW 157.59) at this mass.

components.name: Glacial acetic acid
components.note: 57.1 mL per litre of 50x. Density 1.049 g/mL, MW 60.05, so 57.1 mL = 59.9 g = 0.997 mol -> ~1.0 M acetate in 50x = 20 mM in 1x.
components.hazard: Corrosive, flammable. Dispense in a fume hood; add acid to water, never the reverse.

components.name: EDTA (from 0.5 M Na2EDTA, pH 8.0 stock)
components.note: = 100 mL of 0.5 M EDTA pH 8.0 per litre of 50x; yields 1 mM EDTA in 1x.

recipes.sterilization: none (autoclaving optional; buffer is not required to be sterile)
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 6-12 months; discard if precipitate or microbial growth appears
recipes.hazards: Glacial acetic acid is corrosive and flammable.
notes: Amounts per litre of 50x: 242 g Tris base, 57.1 mL glacial acetic acid, 100 mL 0.5 M EDTA pH 8.0, water to 1 L. The Barrick Lab reagent-recipe page and OpenWetWare list the identical amounts, so this is confirmed by open sources as well as by Sambrook/CSH.
pH is not titrated - the mixture lands near 8.5 (1x measures ~8.3-8.5). Do not adjust.

MW/molarity audit: 242 g/L Tris = 2.00 M (verified against PubChem CID 6503, MW 121.14); 57.1 mL/L acetic acid = ~1.0 M, not 2 M. Some wiki copies of this recipe state '2 M acetic acid' in the 50x stock - that is arithmetically impossible at 57.1 mL/L and is a transcription error in those pages.
50x TAE is close to saturation; if it precipitates in the cold, warm and stir before diluting, or make 25x instead.
TAE has lower buffering capacity than TBE and exhausts on long or high-voltage runs (the anode compartment acidifies). Recirculate or replace buffer for runs longer than ~2 h.
Choose TAE over TBE when the DNA will be gel-extracted: borate carries over and interferes with silica-column binding and with several downstream enzymes.

sources.title: Barrick Lab - Protocols: Reagent Recipes (50x TAE)
OpenWetWare - TAE
PubChem CID 6503 - Tromethamine (Tris base), C4H11NO3, MW 121.14
PubChem CID 176 - Acetic acid, C2H4O2, MW 60.05
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - TAE (recipe pdb.rec8644)

recipes.name: TAE Electrophoresis Buffer, 1x (working)
recipes.summary: Working agarose gel running buffer and gel-casting buffer; the standard buffer when DNA will be recovered from the gel.

components.name: 50x TAE stock
components.note: 20 mL of 50x per litre of 1x (equivalently 400 mL into a 20 L carboy, as the Barrick Lab page describes for bulk tank buffer).

recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: Prepare fresh or use within ~1 month; discard if cloudy
notes: Final composition: 40 mM Tris, 20 mM acetate, 1 mM EDTA, pH ~8.3-8.5.
Cast the gel in the same buffer that fills the tank - mismatched ionic strength gives distorted, smiling bands.
Low ionic strength means DNA migrates faster than in TBE and large fragments (>12 kb) resolve better, at the cost of buffering capacity.
Addgene's gel electrophoresis protocol uses 1x TAE for both the gel and the tank and runs at 80-150 V until the dye front is ~75-80% down the gel (about 1-1.5 h).

sources.title: Barrick Lab - Protocols: Reagent Recipes (TAE working dilution)
Addgene - Protocol: How to Run an Agarose Gel
OpenWetWare - TAE (1x working dilution)

recipes.name: TBE Electrophoresis Buffer, 10x
recipes.summary: Concentrated stock for agarose/polyacrylamide running buffer; dilute 1:10 to 1x (89 mM Tris-borate, 2 mM EDTA).

components.name: Tris base
components.form: Tris base (free base, tromethamine), C4H11NO3, MW 121.14, CAS 77-86-1
components.note: 0.89 M x 121.14 = 107.8 g/L; the canonical recipe rounds this to 108 g/L.

components.name: Boric acid
components.form: Boric acid, H3BO3 (PubChem formula BH3O3), MW 61.83, CAS 10043-35-3
components.note: 0.89 M x 61.83 = 55.0 g/L; the canonical recipe states 55 g/L. PubChem CID 7628 currently reports the MW as 61.84; 61.83 and 61.84 differ only in rounding of the boron atomic weight and change the mass by 0.01 g/L.
components.hazard: EU-classified reproductive toxicant, Repr. 1B, H360FD (may damage fertility; may damage the unborn child), signal word Danger. Weigh with dust control or in a fume hood.

components.name: EDTA (from 0.5 M Na2EDTA, pH 8.0 stock)
components.note: = 40 mL of 0.5 M EDTA pH 8.0 per litre of 10x; yields 2 mM EDTA in 1x. From powder the equivalent is 7.44 g/L Na2EDTA*2H2O (0.02 M x 372.24).

recipes.sterilization: none (autoclaving optional)
recipes.storage: Room temperature, in glass or PETG; keep out of the cold room
recipes.shelfLife: 3-6 months; discard once crystals form and will not redissolve
recipes.hazards: Boric acid is a reproductive toxicant (H360FD). Handle the powder with care.
notes: Amounts per litre of 10x: 108 g Tris base, 55 g boric acid, 40 mL 0.5 M EDTA pH 8.0. pH lands near 8.3 without titration.
10x TBE throws borate-EDTA crystals on storage. Many labs keep a 5x stock instead, which stays in solution - see the separate 5x recipe.
If autoclaving, loosen the cap; do not autoclave a tightly sealed bottle.

Prefer TBE for fragments below ~1 kb, for sharp bands, and for long or high-voltage runs. Avoid TBE when the DNA will be gel-purified or used directly in enzymatic reactions, because borate inhibits ligase and polymerases and interferes with silica-column binding.

recipes.name: TBE Electrophoresis Buffer, 5x (storage-stable stock)
recipes.summary: The storage-stable TBE stock - it does not throw crystals the way 10x does; dilute to 0.5x or 1x for use.

components.name: Tris base
components.form: Tris base (free base, tromethamine), MW 121.14, CAS 77-86-1

components.name: Boric acid
components.note: 27.5 g/L / 61.83 = 0.445 M - equimolar with the Tris, as it should be.
components.hazard: Reproductive toxicant, H360FD, signal word Danger (PubChem GHS).

components.name: EDTA (from 0.5 M Na2EDTA, pH 8.0 stock)
components.note: = 20 mL of 0.5 M EDTA pH 8.0 per litre of 5x.

recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months
recipes.hazards: Boric acid is a reproductive toxicant.
notes: Amounts per litre of 5x: 54 g Tris base, 27.5 g boric acid, 20 mL 0.5 M EDTA pH 8.0. The Barrick Lab reagent-recipe page lists exactly these amounts, which is an open confirmation of the Sambrook/CSH figures.
The reason to keep 5x rather than 10x is simply that the solutes stay dissolved during storage (paraphrased from the CSH note; wording is ours).

Typical working strengths: 0.5x (45 mM Tris-borate, 1 mM EDTA) for agarose, 1x (89 mM Tris-borate, 2 mM EDTA) for polyacrylamide and urea gels. The Barrick page dilutes 100 mL of 5x to 500 mL for 1x, or to 1 L for 0.5x.
Dilute shortly before use.

recipes.name: TBE Electrophoresis Buffer, 1x (working)
recipes.summary: Working running buffer for agarose and polyacrylamide gels; best resolution for small DNA fragments.

components.name: 10x TBE stock
components.note: 100 mL of 10x per litre. Equivalently 200 mL of 5x per litre.

recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: Use within ~1 month
notes: Final composition: 89 mM Tris, 89 mM borate, 2 mM EDTA, pH ~8.3.
0.5x TBE (45 mM Tris-borate, 1 mM EDTA) is the classic agarose working strength and runs cooler; 1x is standard for PAGE.
Cast the gel in the same buffer strength used in the tank.

sources.title: Barrick Lab - Protocols: Reagent Recipes (TBE working dilutions, 1x and 0.5x)

recipes.name: 1 M Tris-HCl, pH 7.5
recipes.summary: Workhorse buffer stock for TE, restriction/ligation buffers, and lysis buffers at near-neutral pH.

components.name: Tris base
components.form: Tris base (free base, tromethamine), C4H11NO3, MW 121.14, CAS 77-86-1
components.note: 121.14 g per litre. Do NOT substitute Tris-HCl salt (MW 157.59, PubChem CID 93573) at this mass - it would give a lower molarity and the wrong starting pH.

components.name: Concentrated HCl (~12 M / 37%)
components.note: Titrant only, not a fixed amount. Roughly 65 mL per litre reaches pH 7.5 (CSH tabulates 70 mL for pH 7.4 and 60 mL for pH 7.6).
components.hazard: Concentrated HCl is corrosive and gives off irritating fumes - use a fume hood.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~800 mL, titrate, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: concentrated HCl
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 20 min (15 psi, liquid cycle) or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year (sterile, unopened aliquots)
recipes.hazards: Concentrated HCl is corrosive.
notes: Tris pH is strongly temperature dependent: dpH/dT is about -0.028 units per degree C. Titrate at the temperature the buffer will be used at, and always let the solution return to room temperature before taking the final reading.
Alternative without concentrated acid: mix 1 M Tris base (121.14 g/L) with 1 M Tris-HCl salt (157.59 g/L) until the pH reads 7.5.

Use a Tris-compatible electrode. The usual failure is at the reference junction rather than the glass membrane: silver leaching from an Ag/AgCl reference precipitates in the junction in amine and protein-containing solutions, producing a drifting liquid-junction potential worth up to several tenths of a pH unit. A calomel or double-junction reference avoids this. Do not assume a fixed direction for the error and 'correct' for it mentally - the sign is not reliably predictable. (This supersedes an earlier note in this file that claimed such electrodes read high in amine buffers.)
A 1 M Tris solution that looks yellow indicates poor-quality Tris; discard it.
Tris buffers poorly below pH 7.2 - use phosphate or HEPES there instead.

sources.title: Barrick Lab - Protocols: Reagent Recipes (Tris-HCl, 1 M: 121 g Tris base in 800 mL, titrate with HCl, to 1 L)
PubChem CID 6503 - Tromethamine (Tris base), MW 121.14
PubChem CID 93573 - Tromethamine hydrochloride (Tris-HCl), C4H12ClNO3, MW 157.59
NEB - pH vs Temperature for Tris Buffer
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - Tris-Cl (recipe pdb.rec8063)

recipes.name: 1 M Tris-HCl, pH 8.0
recipes.summary: Standard stock for TE buffer, DNA elution buffers, and most cloning buffers.

components.name: Tris base
components.form: Tris base (free base, tromethamine), C4H11NO3, MW 121.14, CAS 77-86-1
components.note: 121.14 g per litre (recipes commonly round to 121.1 g or 121 g).

components.name: Concentrated HCl (~12 M / 37%)
components.note: Titrant only. CSH tabulates ~42 mL per litre to reach pH 8.0.
components.hazard: Corrosive; add in a fume hood.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~800 mL, titrate, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: concentrated HCl
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 20 min (15 psi, liquid cycle) or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year (sterile aliquots)
recipes.hazards: Concentrated HCl is corrosive.
notes: Sequence: dissolve 121.1 g Tris base in ~800 mL water, add ~42 mL concentrated HCl, let it cool to room temperature, make the final pH adjustment, bring to 1 L, aliquot and autoclave.

Temperature dependence: dpH/dT is about -0.028 per degree C, so a buffer set to 8.0 at 25 C reads roughly 8.5-8.6 in a 4-5 C cold room.58 value from a manufacturer table; the coefficient-based range is what is actually verifiable here.)
All-salt alternative: 1 M Tris-HCl salt is MW 157.59 (CAS 1185-53-1, PubChem CID 93573). Mixing Tris base with Tris-HCl salt to the target pH avoids handling concentrated acid.

sources.title: Barrick Lab - Protocols: Reagent Recipes (Tris-HCl, 1 M, pH 8.0)
PubChem CID 6503 - Tromethamine (Tris base), MW 121.14
PubChem CID 93573 - Tromethamine hydrochloride, MW 157.59
NEB - pH vs Temperature for Tris Buffer
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - Tris-HCl (recipe pdb.rec8747)

recipes.name: 0.5 M EDTA, pH 8.0
recipes.summary: Chelator stock used in TE, TAE, TBE, and loading dyes; sequesters Mg2+ to inhibit nucleases.

components.name: EDTA disodium salt dihydrate
components.form: Na2EDTA*2H2O (ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt dihydrate), C10H18N2Na2O10, MW 372.24, CAS 6381-92-6
components.note: 0.5 M x 372.24 = 186.12 g per litre. Do NOT use free-acid EDTA (MW 292.24, PubChem CID 6049) or the tetrasodium salt at this mass.

components.name: NaOH
components.note: Titrant only - about 20 g of NaOH pellets, or ~50 mL of 10 M NaOH, per litre to bring the slurry to pH 8.0 (both figures are 0.5 mol NaOH, and they agree with the Barrick Lab recipe).
components.hazard: NaOH is corrosive; the solution heats strongly as pellets dissolve. Eye protection required.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~700-800 mL, titrate, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: NaOH (pellets or 10 M)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 20 min (15 psi, liquid cycle)
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year or more
recipes.hazards: NaOH is corrosive - wear eye protection; the solution warms during dissolution.
notes: Na2EDTA*2H2O will not dissolve until the pH is raised toward 8.0. The powder sits as a white slurry and then clears suddenly as the last carboxylates are deprotonated - keep adding NaOH with vigorous stirring.
Amounts: 186.1 g Na2EDTA*2H2O into ~700-800 mL water, adjust to pH 8.0 with NaOH, make up to 1 L, aliquot, autoclave.
If your bottle is EDTA free acid (MW 292.24) the mass for 0.5 M is 146.12 g/L, and substantially more NaOH is needed to reach pH 8.0.
Do not overshoot pH 8.0 - back-titrating with HCl introduces chloride you did not intend.

sources.title: Barrick Lab - Protocols: Reagent Recipes (EDTA 0.5 M pH 8.0: 186.1 g Na2EDTA*2H2O, ~50 mL 10 M NaOH, to 1 L)
PubChem CID 636371 - Edetate disodium dihydrate, C10H18N2Na2O10, MW 372.24
PubChem CID 6049 - Edetic acid (EDTA free acid), C10H16N2O8, MW 292.24
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - EDTA (recipe pdb.rec8030)

recipes.name: TE Buffer, pH 8.0 (1x)
recipes.summary: Standard buffer for dissolving and storing DNA; EDTA chelates Mg2+ to block nuclease activity.

components.name: Tris-HCl (from 1 M Tris-HCl pH 8.0 stock)
components.note: 10 mL of 1 M Tris-HCl pH 8.0 per litre.

components.name: EDTA (from 0.5 M Na2EDTA pH 8.0 stock)
components.note: 2 mL of 0.5 M EDTA pH 8.0 per litre.

recipes.adjustPHWith: set by the component stocks; verify and adjust with HCl/NaOH only if the reading is off
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 20 min (15 psi, liquid cycle) or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: Room temperature; 4 C for long-term DNA storage
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year sterile
recipes.hazards: pH is adjusted with set by the component stocks; verify and adjust with HCl/NaOH only if the reading is off — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: pH 8.0 TE is the default for genomic and plasmid DNA - the mildly alkaline pH suppresses acid-catalysed depurination.

1 mM EDTA can inhibit downstream reactions that need Mg2+ (PCR set up with a large template volume, some restriction digests). Use low-TE (10 mM Tris, 0.1 mM EDTA) or nuclease-free water in those cases.
10x TE (100 mM Tris-Cl, 10 mM EDTA, pH 8.0) is the same recipe with both stocks scaled tenfold; dilute before use.
For RNA work do not build Tris buffers in DEPC-treated water - DEPC reacts with the primary amine of Tris. Use certified nuclease-free water.

sources.title: OpenWetWare - TE (10 mM Tris-HCl at the desired pH, 1 mM EDTA; role of EDTA in chelating divalent metals)
OpenWetWare - CTR:Recipes (TE: 10 mL 1 M Tris pH 8.0 + 2 mL 0.5 M EDTA per litre)
Barrick Lab - Protocols: Reagent Recipes (component 1 M Tris-HCl and 0.5 M EDTA stocks)
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - TE buffer, 10X (recipe pdb.rec8018)

recipes.name: TE Buffer, pH 7.5 (1x)
recipes.summary: TE variant at pH 7.5, used where a near-neutral buffer is preferred (e.g. hybridization, some enzyme storage).

components.name: Tris-HCl (from 1 M Tris-HCl pH 7.5 stock)
components.note: 10 mL of 1 M Tris-HCl pH 7.5 per litre.

components.name: EDTA (from 0.5 M Na2EDTA pH 8.0 stock)
components.note: 2 mL of 0.5 M EDTA pH 8.0 per litre. The EDTA stock is always made at pH 8.0 regardless of the final TE pH, because that is the pH at which the disodium salt dissolves.

recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl / NaOH if verification is off
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 20 min (15 psi, liquid cycle) or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: Room temperature; 4 C for DNA storage
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year sterile
recipes.hazards: pH is adjusted with HCl / NaOH if verification is off — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Identical to TE pH 8.0 except for the pH of the Tris stock. For routine long-term DNA storage prefer pH 8.0.
Check the pH after mixing - the pH 8.0 EDTA contributes a small upward nudge at only 1 mM, but verify rather than assume.
OpenWetWare's TE page states the general form (10 mM Tris-HCl at the pH you want, 1 mM EDTA), which is what makes the 7.5 variant legitimate rather than a typo.

sources.title: OpenWetWare - TE (TE is 10 mM Tris-HCl at the desired pH plus 1 mM EDTA)
Barrick Lab - Protocols: Reagent Recipes (1 M Tris-HCl and 0.5 M EDTA component stocks)
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - Tris-HCl (recipe pdb.rec8747)

recipes.name: 3 M Sodium Acetate, pH 5.2
recipes.summary: Salt stock for ethanol/isopropanol precipitation of DNA and RNA (use at 1/10 volume, 0.3 M final).

components.name: Sodium acetate trihydrate
components.form: CH3COONa*3H2O (sodium acetate trihydrate), C2H3NaO2*3H2O, MW 136.08, CAS 6131-90-4
components.note: 3 M x 136.08 = 408.2 g per litre. With ANHYDROUS sodium acetate (MW 82.03, CAS 127-09-3, PubChem CID 517045) the mass is 246.1 g/L instead - swapping masses between the hydrate and the anhydrous salt is the classic error with this recipe.

components.name: Glacial acetic acid
components.note: Titrant only - add dropwise to bring the solution to pH 5.2.
components.hazard: Corrosive and flammable; use in a fume hood.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~800 mL, titrate, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: glacial acetic acid
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 20 min (15 psi, liquid cycle) or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year or more
recipes.hazards: Glacial acetic acid is corrosive and flammable.
notes: Sequence: dissolve 408.2 g sodium acetate trihydrate in ~800 mL water, titrate to pH 5.2 with glacial acetic acid, make up to 1 L, aliquot, autoclave.
Standard use: add 1/10 volume of the 3 M stock to the nucleic acid sample (0.3 M final), then 2-2.5 volumes of cold 100% ethanol, or ~0.7 volume isopropanol.
pH 5.2 is the usual choice for DNA. Where carryover of low pH into a downstream reaction matters, use 3 M sodium acetate at pH 7.0 or ammonium acetate instead.
For RNA, make the stock with nuclease-free water and dedicate the bottle to RNA.
Ammonium acetate (2.5-7.5 M) is a common alternative that carries less dNTP through the precipitation, but it must not precede a T4 polynucleotide kinase reaction (ammonium ions inhibit the kinase).

sources.title: PubChem CID 23665404 - Sodium acetate trihydrate, MW 136.08
PubChem CID 517045 - Sodium acetate (anhydrous), C2H3NaO2, MW 82.03
protocols.io - Isopropanol DNA Precipitation (uses 3 M sodium acetate pH 5.2 at 1/10 volume), doi:10.17504/protocols.io.bxg7pjzn
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - Sodium Acetate (3 M, pH 5.2), pdb.rec085761

recipes.name: Agarose Gel, 0.8% (w/v)
recipes.summary: Low-percentage analytical gel for resolving large DNA - genomic DNA checks, linearized plasmids, roughly 0.8-12 kb.

components.name: Agarose (molecular biology / electrophoresis grade)
components.note: 0.8 g per 100 mL of 1x running buffer.

components.name: 1x TAE or 1x TBE running buffer
components.note: Shown as a dilution of 50x TAE. For TBE, dilute 10x TBE 1:10 (or 5x TBE 1:10 for 0.5x). Cast the gel in the same buffer that goes in the tank.

recipes.solvent: 1x TAE or 1x TBE (the buffer is the solvent - never plain water)
recipes.sterilization: none (microwaving to dissolve effectively pasteurizes it)
recipes.storage: Cast and use the same day; a poured gel keeps overnight at 4 C wrapped or submerged in buffer
recipes.shelfLife: Use within 24 h of casting
recipes.hazards: Molten agarose is above 90 C and superheats in a microwave, so it can boil over violently. Loosen the cap, heat in short bursts, swirl between bursts, and use heat-resistant gloves.
notes: Resolution: NEB's table gives 0.8-12 kb for 0.7% and 0.5-10 kb for 1.0%, so 0.8% sits at roughly 0.7-11 kb. Addgene's protocol frames 0.7-2% as the everyday working range.
Weigh agarose into the flask, add buffer, swirl, then microwave in 30-45 s bursts with swirling until no lens-shaped particles remain when the flask is held to the light.
Cool to ~55-60 C before adding stain and pouring, or the tray warps and the stain degrades.
Stain options at that point: SYBR Safe at 1x (1 uL of 10,000x per 10 mL) or ethidium bromide to 0.2-0.5 ug/mL final.
Run at 5-10 V/cm of inter-electrode distance; for genomic DNA and other very large fragments drop to 1-2 V/cm.

sources.title: Addgene - Protocol: How to Run an Agarose Gel (0.7-2% range, 1x TAE, EtBr 0.2-0.5 ug/mL, 80-150 V)
OpenWetWare - Endy:Agarose gel
NEB - Agarose Gel Resolution guideline (percent vs resolvable size range)
LibreTexts - Common Agarose Gel Questions and Tips

recipes.name: Agarose Gel, 1% (w/v)
recipes.summary: The default all-purpose analytical gel; resolves roughly 0.5-10 kb linear DNA.

components.name: Agarose (molecular biology / electrophoresis grade)
components.note: 1.0 g per 100 mL of 1x running buffer.

components.name: 1x TAE or 1x TBE running buffer
components.note: Shown as a dilution of 50x TAE; substitute 10x TBE at 1:10 if running TBE.

recipes.solvent: 1x TAE or 1x TBE (the buffer is the solvent)
recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: Use same day
recipes.hazards: Superheated molten agarose - risk of boil-over and burns.
notes: Resolution range per NEB's table: ~0.5-10 kb linear DNA.
Typical mini-gel: 0.5 g agarose in 50 mL of 1x TAE.
A 4-6 mm gel is ideal; thicker gels need more stain and image poorly.
Do not overload wells - overloaded lanes smile and streak.
Add stain at ~55 C: 5 uL of 10,000x SYBR Safe per 50 mL, or ethidium bromide to 0.2-0.5 ug/mL.

sources.title: Addgene - Protocol: How to Run an Agarose Gel
NEB - Agarose Gel Resolution guideline
LibreTexts - Common Agarose Gel Questions and Tips

recipes.name: Agarose Gel, 1.5% (w/v)
recipes.summary: Mid-high percentage gel for PCR products and smaller fragments; resolves roughly 0.2-3 kb.

components.name: Agarose (molecular biology / electrophoresis grade)
components.note: 1.5 g per 100 mL of 1x running buffer.

components.name: 1x TAE or 1x TBE running buffer
components.note: TBE gives noticeably sharper bands at this percentage; choose TAE if the band will be excised and purified.

recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: Use same day
recipes.hazards: Superheated molten agarose - risk of boil-over and burns.
notes: Resolution range per NEB's table: ~0.2-3 kb linear DNA.
Higher-percentage gels take longer to dissolve and set faster - pour promptly once the flask has cooled to ~55-60 C.
Run a little slower (4-6 V/cm) than a 1% gel to limit joule heating and band distortion.

sources.title: NEB - Agarose Gel Resolution guideline
Addgene - Protocol: How to Run an Agarose Gel
LibreTexts - Common Agarose Gel Questions and Tips

recipes.name: Agarose Gel, 2% (w/v)
recipes.summary: High-percentage gel for small fragments - genotyping, colony PCR, and short amplicons (~50 bp-2 kb).

components.name: Agarose (molecular biology / electrophoresis grade)
components.note: 2.0 g per 100 mL of 1x running buffer.

components.name: 1x TBE (preferred) or 1x TAE running buffer
components.note: Shown as a dilution of 10x TBE. TBE is strongly preferred at 2% for resolving small fragments.

recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: Use same day
recipes.hazards: Superheated molten agarose - risk of boil-over and burns.
notes: Resolution range per NEB's table: ~0.05-2 kb linear DNA.
2% gels are brittle and dissolve slowly - allow for evaporation during microwaving and top the flask back up to weight before pouring.
Below ~100 bp, move to 3-4% agarose, a high-resolution sieving agarose, or polyacrylamide.

Tracking dye positions shift strongly with percentage: at 2% agarose xylene cyanol FF sits near 750-800 bp and bromophenol blue near 150 bp, whereas at 1% they are near 4 kb and 300-500 bp respectively. At 2% the xylene cyanol band can sit right on top of your fragment, so a bromophenol-blue-only loading dye is often the better choice.
(Correction retained from an earlier pass: the ~750 bp figure belongs to the 2% gel, not the 1% gel.)

recipes.name: 6x DNA Gel-Loading Dye (glycerol, two-dye formulation)
aka: bromophenol blue / xylene cyanol loading dye
recipes.summary: Classic two-dye loading buffer; glycerol sinks the sample into the well and the dyes track the run front.

components.name: Glycerol
components.note: 30 mL per 100 mL. Glycerol is viscous - pipette slowly or weigh it (density ~1.26 g/mL; C3H8O3, MW 92.09).

components.name: Bromophenol blue (sodium salt)
components.form: Bromophenol blue sodium salt, C19H9Br4NaO5S, MW 691.9, CAS 34725-61-6
components.note: 0.25 g per 100 mL. The free acid form is MW 669.96 - the difference is immaterial at this w/v usage but matters if you ever compute molarity.

components.name: Xylene cyanol FF
components.form: Xylene cyanole FF (sodium salt), C25H27N2NaO6S2, MW 538.6, CAS 2650-17-1
components.note: 0.25 g per 100 mL. MW verified against PubChem; the 538.60 figure in circulation is correct for the sodium salt.

recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: 4 C (room temperature is acceptable for short periods)
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year or more
notes: Use at 1x final: 1 volume of 6x dye to 5 volumes of sample (e.g. 2 uL dye + 10 uL sample; Addgene's protocol uses 5 uL per 25 uL, the same ratio).

Dye migration in a 1% agarose gel: bromophenol blue tracks roughly 300-500 bp and xylene cyanol FF roughly 4-5 kb. Both shift with agarose percentage and with buffer, so confirm before concluding that a band is a dye front.9 kb figure for TBE; the range is what is defensible.)
This formulation contains no EDTA. If the sample comes from a Mg2+-containing reaction and will sit before loading, use the EDTA-containing 6x dye instead.
A sucrose version (40% w/v sucrose with the same two dyes) is denser and works equally well.

The Barrick Lab keeps a related 6x buffer with 60% glycerol, ~0.04% bromophenol blue (0.015 g in 40 mL) and 3x TAE - adding buffer salts to the dye helps mobility consistency for samples loaded straight from PCR mixes.

recipes.name: 6x DNA Gel-Loading Dye with EDTA (Thermo R0611 formulation)
recipes.summary: Commercial-equivalent 6x loading dye containing EDTA to quench Mg2+-dependent nucleases and stop enzymatic reactions.

components.name: Glycerol
components.note: 60 mL per 100 mL. Very viscous - warm slightly and pipette slowly, or weigh it (density ~1.26 g/mL).

components.name: Tris-HCl (from 1 M Tris-HCl pH 7.6 stock)
components.note: 1 mL of 1 M Tris-HCl pH 7.6 per 100 mL. Make the pH 7.6 stock the same way as pH 7.5/8.0 (121.14 g/L Tris base; CSH tabulates ~60 mL conc. HCl per litre for pH 7.6).

components.name: EDTA (from 0.5 M Na2EDTA pH 8.0 stock)
components.note: 12 mL of 0.5 M EDTA pH 8.0 per 100 mL. 60 mM in the 6x gives 10 mM EDTA at 1x - enough to chelate the Mg2+ in a typical digest.

components.name: Bromophenol blue (sodium salt)
components.form: Bromophenol blue sodium salt, C19H9Br4NaO5S, MW 691.9, CAS 34725-61-6
components.note: 30 mg per 100 mL.

components.name: Xylene cyanol FF
components.form: Xylene cyanole FF (sodium salt), C25H27N2NaO6S2, MW 538.6, CAS 2650-17-1
components.note: 30 mg per 100 mL.

recipes.adjustPHWith: set by the Tris-HCl stock; verify
recipes.sterilization: none (0.22 um filtration optional; 60% glycerol filters very slowly)
recipes.storage: Room temperature or 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year or more
recipes.hazards: pH is adjusted with set by the Tris-HCl stock; verify — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Composition as published by the manufacturer for 6X DNA Loading Dye #R0611: 10 mM Tris-HCl pH 7.6, 0.03% bromophenol blue, 0.03% xylene cyanol FF, 60% glycerol, 60 mM EDTA.
Use at 1x: 1 volume of dye to 5 volumes of sample.
The 60% glycerol makes samples sink cleanly and is more forgiving than 30% when loading deep wells under buffer.
The EDTA stops restriction digests and other Mg2+-dependent reactions on contact, which is convenient when loading straight from the thermal block.
The dye colour is much fainter than the 0.25% formulation; that is deliberate, so faint bands are not obscured.

OpenSourceGap: this exact formulation is a commercial product and is documented only in the vendor's freely published technical literature - there is no open-repository primary source for it. The component MWs, however, are all confirmed against PubChem.

recipes.name: Ethidium Bromide Stock, 10 mg/mL
recipes.summary: Concentrated intercalating DNA stain stock; diluted 1:20,000 into gels or staining baths.

components.name: Ethidium bromide
components.note: 10 g/L is the same as 10 mg/mL, which is 25.4 mM (10 / 394.3). Dissolve with magnetic stirring for several hours.
components.hazard: PubChem GHS aggregate: signal word Danger, H302 (harmful if swallowed), H330 (fatal if inhaled), H341 (suspected of causing genetic defects). Weighing the powder is the highest-risk step - buy the ready-made 10 mg/mL solution if at all possible.

recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: 4 C (room temperature is also acceptable), wrapped in foil or in an amber bottle; light-sensitive
recipes.shelfLife: Several years if protected from light
recipes.hazards: Suspected germ-cell mutagen (H341), harmful if swallowed (H302), fatal if inhaled (H330) - signal word Danger, per the GHS classification aggregated on PubChem. Wear nitrile gloves and change them often, plus lab coat and eye protection. Handle the powder only in a fume hood. Never pour EtBr solutions down the drain: collect as regulated chemical waste or pass through activated-charcoal destaining media per your institution's EHS policy.
notes: If you must weigh the powder: fume hood, decontaminate the balance afterwards, and treat weigh boats as hazardous waste. Most labs buy the 10 mg/mL aqueous solution specifically to avoid this step.
Do not autoclave EtBr solutions - autoclaving does not destroy the compound and can aerosolize it.
SYBR Safe, GelRed and GelGreen are lower-hazard alternatives and are the better default for teaching labs and new setups.

Hazard wording in this entry was tightened to the actual GHS statements rather than the informal 'powerful mutagen, moderately toxic' phrasing used previously - note in particular the inhalation hazard, which the old wording understated.

sources.title: PubChem CID 14710 - Ethidium bromide, C21H20BrN3, MW 394.3, GHS classification (H302, H330, H341; Danger)
Sigma-Aldrich - Ethidium bromide technical article (staining and handling)
Bio-Rad - Ethidium Bromide Solution, 10 mg/mL, product literature

recipes.name: Ethidium Bromide Working Solution, 0.5 ug/mL
recipes.summary: Working dilution for casting into agarose gels or for post-electrophoresis staining baths.

components.name: Ethidium bromide (from 10 mg/mL stock)
components.note: 1:20,000 dilution = 5 uL of 10 mg/mL stock per 100 mL; 2.5 uL for a 50 mL mini-gel. Addgene's protocol uses 0.2-0.5 ug/mL (about 2-5 uL of a 10 mg/mL stock per 100 mL), so 0.5 ug/mL is the upper end of normal practice, not a minimum.
components.hazard: Mutagen - gloves, and treat gel, buffer and tips as EtBr waste.

recipes.solvent: 1x TAE or 1x TBE (for in-gel staining, added to molten agarose cooled to ~55 C) or water/running buffer for a post-run bath
recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: Staining baths can be reused for weeks in a covered, foil-wrapped tray at room temperature; label the tray clearly as EtBr
recipes.shelfLife: Reuse the bath until sensitivity drops, typically several weeks
recipes.hazards: Mutagen (GHS H341; also H302/H330 for the concentrate). Everything the solution touches - gel, buffer, gloves, tips, trays - becomes EtBr waste. Never discard to drain.
notes: Do not exceed 0.5 ug/mL in the gel, the running buffer, or the bath; higher concentrations raise background and retard DNA migration further.
In-gel staining: add to molten agarose at ~55 C, swirl, pour. DNA stains as it runs.

Post-staining: soak the finished gel in 0.5 ug/mL for 15-30 min with gentle agitation, then destain in water for 15-30 min. Post-staining is more sensitive for small fragments because EtBr, being positively charged, migrates toward the cathode during a run and depletes from the leading part of the gel.
At 0.5 ug/mL, intercalated EtBr slows linear DNA by roughly 15%. Never size a stained sample against a ladder run in an unstained gel.
Visualize at ~300 nm (excitation maxima near 300 and 520 nm, emission near 600 nm). Wear a UV face shield, and keep exposure short if the DNA is destined for cloning - UV nicks DNA.

sources.title: Addgene - Protocol: How to Run an Agarose Gel (EtBr final 0.2-0.5 ug/mL)
PubChem CID 14710 - Ethidium bromide (GHS classification, physical data)

recipes.name: SYBR Safe DNA Gel Stain, 1x (in-gel working solution)
recipes.summary: Reduced-mutagenicity alternative to ethidium bromide, cast directly into the agarose gel at 1x.

components.name: SYBR Safe DNA Gel Stain (10,000x concentrate in DMSO)
components.note: 1:10,000 = 10 uL of concentrate per 100 mL of gel (3 uL per 30 mL, 5 uL per 50 mL). A 1:20,000 dilution still visualizes all but very faint bands.

recipes.solvent: 1x TAE or 1x TBE agarose gel solution
recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: Concentrate: room temperature (2-25 C) protected from light. DMSO freezes below ~18 C, so if it has solidified, thaw completely and mix before pipetting.
recipes.shelfLife: Per manufacturer lot expiry, typically 1 year or more; protect from light
recipes.hazards: Formulated for reduced mutagenicity relative to ethidium bromide, but it is still a DNA-binding dye - wear gloves and dispose of per institutional policy. The concentrate is in DMSO, which promotes skin absorption of anything dissolved in it.
notes: Two equivalent approaches: dilute the concentrate 1:10,000 into the buffer first, then add agarose powder and melt; or add the concentrate to molten agarose cooled to ~55 C and swirl thoroughly. The first gives more even distribution.
Worked example: 3 uL of 10,000x concentrate into 30 mL of 1x TBE, mix, then add the agarose.
A pale orange tint in the molten agarose is normal.

Excitation maxima near 280 and 502 nm, emission near 530 nm when bound to DNA. A blue-light transilluminator (~470-500 nm) gives the best sensitivity and avoids UV damage to the DNA; a 300 nm UV transilluminator works but is less sensitive.
DNA appears as bright green bands on a dark background.
Post-electrophoresis staining also works: soak the gel in a 1x solution in running buffer for ~30 min in the dark.

OpenSourceGap: SYBR Safe is a proprietary formulation whose composition is not published, so the primary documentation is the vendor's free manual; the 1:10,000 usage is independently reproduced in open protocols.io agarose-gel protocols.

recipes.name: DEPC-Treated Water (RNase-free water)
recipes.summary: Water treated with diethylpyrocarbonate to inactivate RNases, then autoclaved to destroy residual DEPC; for RNA work.

components.name: Diethylpyrocarbonate (DEPC)
components.form: Diethyl pyrocarbonate (diethyl dicarbonate), C6H10O5, MW 162.14, CAS 1609-47-8
components.note: 1 mL of fresh DEPC per litre of water. Use a fresh bottle - DEPC hydrolyses on storage and old stock is ineffective.
components.hazard: PubChem GHS aggregate: signal word Warning, H302 (harmful if swallowed), H315 (skin irritation), H319 (serious eye irritation), H335 (may cause respiratory irritation). Strong lachrymator and acylating agent; reacts with water to release CO2, so pressure can build in a closed bottle. Dispense in a fume hood only.

recipes.sterilization: REQUIRED: autoclave 121 C, 20 min (15 psi, liquid cycle) after the DEPC incubation, to hydrolyse residual DEPC to ethanol and CO2
recipes.storage: Room temperature in a dedicated RNase-free bottle; aliquot into single-use volumes
recipes.shelfLife: 6-12 months in sealed sterile aliquots; discard any aliquot that has been pipetted into
recipes.hazards: DEPC is a potent acylating agent, an irritant to skin, eyes and airway, and a lachrymator (GHS H302, H315, H319, H335; signal word Warning per PubChem). Handle the neat liquid only in a fume hood with gloves and eye protection. Bottles must be loosely capped during incubation and autoclaving - CO2 evolution can burst a sealed bottle.Treat it as an irritant and acylating agent and keep the fume-hood handling either way.
notes: Amounts and timing: 0.1% v/v DEPC (1 mL per litre), shake to disperse, hold at 37 C for at least 12 h, then autoclave on a liquid cycle for 20 min.

DEPC reacts with primary amines, so it cannot be used to treat Tris (or HEPES, or any amine-containing solution). Make Tris solutions with water that has already been DEPC-treated and autoclaved, or with certified nuclease-free water.
The autoclave step is not optional: residual DEPC carboxymethylates adenine residues in RNA and will interfere with reverse transcription and in vitro translation.

Current guidance from RNA reagent suppliers is that certified nuclease-free water is preferable to lab-made DEPC water - it removes both the DEPC exposure and the residual-DEPC artefacts. Keep DEPC treatment for cases where no certified alternative exists.
General RNase-free technique: dedicated pipettes and filter tips, frequent glove changes, and glassware baked at 180-250 C for ~4 h rather than DEPC-treated.

recipes.name: PBS (Phosphate-Buffered Saline), 10x, pH 7.4
recipes.summary: 10x concentrated phosphate-buffered saline stock; dilute 1:10 for washes, dilutions and blocking buffers.

components.name: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
components.note: MW 58.44 (PubChem CID 5234, NaCl); 80 g/L = 1.369 M in the 10x stock, i.e. 137 mM at 1x.

components.name: Potassium chloride (KCl)
components.note: MW 74.55 (PubChem CID 4873, KCl); 2.0 g/L = 26.8 mM in the 10x = 2.7 mM at 1x.

components.name: Sodium phosphate dibasic, ANHYDROUS (Na2HPO4)
components.note: Anhydrous Na2HPO4, MW 141.96 (PubChem CID 24203); 14.4 g/L = 101 mM in the 10x = 10 mM at 1x. Hydrate equivalents at the same molarity: dihydrate Na2HPO4*2H2O MW 177.99 (CID 6451167) = 18.1 g/L; heptahydrate Na2HPO4*7H2O MW 268.07 (CID 6096963) = 27.2 g/L; dodecahydrate Na2HPO4*12H2O MW 358.14 (CID 61456) = 36.3 g/L. Weighing a hydrate at the anhydrous figure under-doses phosphate by 47% (heptahydrate) or 60% (dodecahydrate).

components.name: Potassium phosphate monobasic, anhydrous (KH2PO4)
components.note: MW 136.09 (PubChem CID 516951, KH2PO4); 2.4 g/L = 17.6 mM in the 10x = 1.8 mM at 1x.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~800 mL, pH, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl (adjust the 10x stock to pH 7.4; some protocols leave the 10x unadjusted and pH only after dilution)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 20 min at 15 psi on liquid cycle, or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 6-12 months; discard if cloudy or if precipitate forms
recipes.hazards: Concentrated HCl used for the pH adjustment is corrosive - add it dropwise in a fume hood with gloves and eye protection.
notes: Contains no Ca2+ or Mg2+. For the Ca/Mg-containing version add 1.33 g/L CaCl2*2H2O (MW 147.01, PubChem CID 6093260) and 1.0 g/L MgCl2*6H2O (MW 203.30, PubChem CID 24644) to the 10x - but add them AFTER autoclaving and only to a phosphate-free diluent, otherwise calcium and magnesium phosphates precipitate.
The 10x stock may precipitate at 4 C. Warm to room temperature and mix until clear before diluting.
pH rises slightly on dilution; check pH of the 1x working solution if the application is pH-sensitive.
Every weight above was re-derived from the PubChem molecular weight for the exact anhydrous salt named, and each g/L figure reproduces the stated molarity to better than 1%.

recipes.name: PBS (Phosphate-Buffered Saline), 1x, pH 7.4
recipes.summary: Standard isotonic wash and dilution buffer for cells, blots and immunoassays.

components.name: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
components.note: MW 58.44 (PubChem CID 5234); 8.0 g/L = 137 mM.

components.name: Potassium chloride (KCl)
components.note: MW 74.55 (PubChem CID 4873); 0.2 g/L = 2.7 mM.

components.name: Sodium phosphate dibasic, ANHYDROUS (Na2HPO4)
components.note: Anhydrous Na2HPO4, MW 141.96 (PubChem CID 24203); 1.44 g/L = 10.1 mM. Same-molarity hydrate weights: dihydrate (MW 177.99) 1.81 g/L; heptahydrate (MW 268.07) 2.72 g/L; dodecahydrate (MW 358.14) 3.63 g/L.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~800 mL, pH, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl (or NaOH if overshot); pH 7.2 is used for some cell-culture applications
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 20 min at 15 psi on liquid cycle, or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: Room temperature; 4 C once opened for cell-culture use
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months sterile; discard immediately if turbid (microbial growth)
recipes.hazards: Concentrated HCl/NaOH used for pH adjustment are corrosive; wear gloves and eye protection.
notes: Easiest route is a 1:10 dilution of the 10x stock; the direct weigh-out above is for when you want a single-use batch at exactly pH 7.4.
For PBS-T (wash buffer) add Tween-20 to 0.05-0.1% (v/v) after the buffer is made up to volume.

recipes.name: TBS (Tris-Buffered Saline), 10x, pH 7.5
recipes.summary: 10x Tris-buffered saline stock; the preferred wash base for phospho-specific western blots (phosphate-free).

components.name: Tris base (free base, Trizma base)
components.note: 0.2 M x 121.14 = 24.2 g/L. Tris base MW 121.14 confirmed on PubChem CID 6503 (tromethamine, C4H11NO3). Tris-HCl is a different salt, MW 157.59-157.60 (PubChem CID 93573); weighing that instead gives the wrong pH and needs NaOH, not HCl, to reach 7.5.

components.name: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
components.form: anhydrous NaCl (sodium chloride forms no stable hydrate at room temperature)

recipes.solvent: deionized water (combine in ~800 mL, pH, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min (optional for blotting use)
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 6-12 months
recipes.hazards: Concentrated HCl used for pH adjustment is corrosive; add dropwise with gloves and eye protection.
notes: 1x working solution = 20 mM Tris, 150 mM NaCl, pH 7.5.
A pH 7.6 variant (24 g Tris base + 88 g NaCl per litre) is equally common and interchangeable for western blotting.
Tris pH is strongly temperature-dependent (approx. -0.028 pH units per degree C). pH the stock at the temperature at which it will be used, or at least record the temperature.
Tris base 121.14 g/mol and NaCl 58.44 g/mol both confirmed against PubChem; 0.2 M and 1.5 M give 24.2 g/L and 87.7 g/L respectively.

recipes.name: TBS-T (Tris-Buffered Saline + Tween-20), 1x
recipes.summary: Standard western blot wash buffer: 20 mM Tris, 150 mM NaCl, 0.1% Tween-20, pH 7.5.

components.name: Tween-20 (polysorbate 20)
components.note: Viscous. Cut the pipette tip or use a positive-displacement pipette, and rinse the tip in the buffer to deliver the full volume.

recipes.adjustPHWith: none required if the 10x TBS was correctly adjusted
recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: Room temperature for up to 1 week; 4 C for longer
recipes.shelfLife: 1-2 weeks; make fresh weekly -- Tween-20 supports microbial growth and oxidises over time
notes: 0.05% Tween-20 is also widely used and gives less stringent washing; 0.1% is the Cell Signaling Technology standard.
Add Tween-20 AFTER diluting the TBS and bringing close to volume -- adding it early causes heavy foaming that makes volume measurement inaccurate.
Do not make a 10x TBST stock and store it long-term; peroxide impurities accumulate in concentrated Tween-20 and can quench HRP signal.
Swap 10x TBS for 10x PBS to make PBS-T; PBS-T is fine for most blots but should NOT be used with phospho-specific antibodies or when the detection chemistry is phosphate-sensitive.

recipes.name: SDS-PAGE Running Buffer (Tris-Glycine-SDS), 10x
recipes.summary: 10x stock of the Laemmli discontinuous running buffer for denaturing SDS-PAGE.

components.name: Tris base (free base)
components.note: Tris base MW 121.14 (PubChem CID 6503); 30.0 g/L = 248 mM in the 10x = ~25 mM at 1x. Do not substitute Tris-HCl (MW 157.59, CID 93573).

components.name: Glycine
components.note: Glycine MW 75.07 (PubChem CID 750); 144.0 g/L = 1.92 M in the 10x = 192 mM at 1x.

components.name: SDS (sodium dodecyl sulfate), electrophoresis grade
components.hazard: Fine powder is a serious respiratory and eye irritant -- weigh in a fume hood or behind a shield and wear a mask.
components.note: Sodium dodecyl sulfate, MW 288.38 (PubChem CID 3423265, C12H25NaO4S); 10.0 g/L = 1% (w/v) in the 10x = 0.1% at 1x = 3.5 mM.

recipes.adjustPHWith: NONE -- do not adjust the pH
recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months (10x stock); discard 1x buffer after 1-2 runs
recipes.hazards: SDS powder: respiratory/eye irritant. Avoid generating dust.
notes: Critical: do NOT titrate this buffer with HCl or NaOH. The correct pH of 8.3 arises from the Tris:glycine ratio; adding chloride destroys the stacking behaviour of the discontinuous system and causes smeared or distorted bands.
1x working buffer = 25 mM Tris, 192 mM glycine, 0.1% (w/v) SDS.
Dissolve the Tris and glycine fully before adding SDS, and stir slowly -- SDS foams badly.
The 10x stock may precipitate SDS at low room temperature. Warm to ~37 C and mix until clear before diluting; do not decant off the precipitate.
For native PAGE use the same recipe but omit the SDS entirely (10x Tris-glycine, pH 8.3).

The 30.0 g/L Tris figure matches the cited Cold Spring Harbor recipe exactly and gives 248 mM (24.8 mM at 1x). Many other protocols -- including the 10x transfer buffer elsewhere in this file -- specify 30.3 g/L, which gives exactly 250 mM. The 0.8% difference has no effect on a run; do not be alarmed by the mismatch between the two stocks.

recipes.name: 1.5 M Tris-Cl, pH 8.8 (Resolving Gel Buffer, 4x)
recipes.summary: Stock buffer for the resolving (lower) layer of a Laemmli SDS-PAGE gel; used as one quarter of the gel volume to give 375 mM final.

components.name: Tris base (free base)
components.form: Tris base, titrated to pH 8.8 with HCl
components.note: MW 121.14 confirmed on PubChem CID 6503 (tromethamine, C4H11NO3). Strict arithmetic: 1.5 M x 121.14 = 181.7 g/L; Cold Spring Harbor publishes 181.5 g/L. The 0.1% difference is immaterial once the buffer is titrated to pH 8.8. Do not substitute Tris-HCl powder (MW 157.59, PubChem CID 93573).

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~850 mL, pH, cool to room temperature, re-check pH, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: 6 M HCl
recipes.sterilization: none (0.22 um filter optional to extend shelf life)
recipes.storage: 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months; discard if cloudy or contaminated
recipes.hazards: 6 M HCl is corrosive and causes severe skin burns and eye damage, and adding it to a concentrated Tris solution releases heat locally. Titrate slowly with stirring, in a fume hood, with gloves and eye protection.
notes: Temperature matters more than the thermal bookkeeping. Tris has a pKa near 8.06 at 25 C, so at pH 8.8 only about 15% of the Tris is protonated and the titration consumes roughly 0.23 mol HCl per litre (about 38 mL of 6 M HCl), not the much larger volume the stacking-gel buffer needs. Dissolving 1.5 mol of Tris base is endothermic and outweighs the heat released by that partial neutralisation, so the solution typically ends up a few degrees BELOW room temperature rather than above it. Either way, Cold Spring Harbor's recipe instructs: cool (equilibrate) the solution to room temperature and readjust the pH to 8.8. Tris pH shifts about -0.028 units per degree C, so a buffer pH-ed at the wrong temperature is off by roughly 0.3 pH units for every 10 C of error -- pH-ed cold it finishes too acidic once it warms, pH-ed warm it finishes too alkaline once it cools.
Some laboratories include 0.4% (w/v) SDS in this buffer (a '4x' buffer that supplies both the Tris and the SDS). If you use that version, omit the separate 10% SDS from the gel mix.

Use this buffer as one QUARTER of the final gel volume (2.5 mL per 10 mL of gel mix), giving 375 mM Tris-Cl pH 8.8 in the cast resolving gel. This is a 1-in-4 dilution, not a 1:4 ratio of buffer to everything else (which would be one fifth, 300 mM).
