recipes.name: 0.5 M Tris-Cl, pH 6.8 (Stacking Gel Buffer, 4x)
recipes.summary: Stock buffer for the stacking (upper) layer of a Laemmli SDS-PAGE gel; used as one quarter of the gel volume to give 125 mM final.

components.name: Tris base (free base)
components.form: Tris base, titrated to pH 6.8 with HCl
components.note: MW 121.14 confirmed on PubChem CID 6503. 0.5 M x 121.14 = 60.57 g/L (60.5-60.6 g/L as published). Do not substitute Tris-HCl powder (MW 157.59, PubChem CID 93573). Reaching pH 6.8 from Tris base requires near-complete protonation (about 95% at 25 C, i.e. roughly 0.47 mol HCl per litre of the 0.5 M stock -- far more acid than the pH 8.8 resolving buffer needs), and that neutralisation warms the solution by a few degrees, so let it return to room temperature before the final pH check.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~850 mL, pH, cool to room temperature, re-check pH, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: 6 M HCl
recipes.sterilization: none (0.22 um filter optional)
recipes.storage: 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months
recipes.hazards: 6 M HCl is corrosive; a large volume is needed to reach pH 6.8 and the titration is exothermic. Work in a fume hood with gloves and eye protection.
notes: pH 6.8 is the single most important number in the Laemmli system -- it sets the glycine ionisation that produces band stacking. Check it carefully at room temperature.
A 1.0 M pH 6.8 stock is also common; if you use 1.0 M, halve the volume in the gel recipe to keep the stacking gel at 125 mM.

Use this buffer as one QUARTER of the final gel volume (0.75 mL per 3 mL of gel mix), giving 125 mM Tris-Cl pH 6.8 in the cast stacking gel. This is a 1-in-4 dilution, not a 1:4 ratio of buffer to everything else.

recipes.name: 10% (w/v) SDS Stock Solution
recipes.summary: Working detergent stock used in gel casting, lysis buffers and running/transfer buffers.

components.name: SDS (sodium dodecyl sulfate), electrophoresis grade
components.hazard: Powder is a respiratory and eye irritant; weigh in a fume hood wearing a dust mask.
components.note: Sodium dodecyl sulfate (sodium lauryl sulfate), C12H25NaO4S, MW 288.38 (PubChem CID 3423265); 10 g per 100 mL = 347 mM.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (heat to ~68 C with gentle stirring to dissolve, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: none normally required; a few protocols adjust to pH 7.2 with HCl
recipes.sterilization: DO NOT AUTOCLAVE. 0.22 um filter if sterility is needed.
recipes.storage: Room temperature -- never 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: 6-12 months
recipes.hazards: SDS dust is harmful if inhaled and irritating to eyes and airways. pH is adjusted with none normally required; a few protocols adjust to pH 7.2 with HCl — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Stir gently. Vigorous stirring produces a persistent foam that makes it impossible to bring the solution accurately to volume.
SDS crystallises out below ~15 C. If the stock is cloudy, warm to 37-50 C and mix until completely clear before pipetting -- otherwise the delivered concentration is wrong.
Autoclaving hydrolyses SDS and is not recommended.

recipes.name: 10% (w/v) Ammonium Persulfate (APS)
recipes.summary: Free-radical initiator for polyacrylamide gel polymerisation; used with TEMED.

components.name: Ammonium persulfate ((NH4)2S2O8)
components.hazard: Strong oxidiser; harmful if swallowed, causes skin/eye irritation and respiratory sensitisation.
components.note: Ammonium peroxydisulfate, (NH4)2S2O8, MW 228.2 (PubChem CID 62648 gives 228.21; supplier sheets round to 228.20); 0.1 g per 1 mL = 438 mM.

recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: Prepare fresh, or aliquot and store at -20 C; the solid powder is stored at room temperature in a desiccator
recipes.shelfLife: Best made fresh on the day; up to 1 week at 4 C, or ~2-3 months as single-use frozen aliquots
recipes.hazards: Strong oxidising agent. Avoid contact with combustible material. Wear gloves and eye protection.
notes: Most gels fail to polymerise because of old APS, not bad acrylamide. If a gel is slow to set, make fresh APS first.
Typical scale is small: 0.1 g in 1 mL of water. Freeze in 100-200 uL single-use aliquots and do not refreeze.
A useful freshness test: fresh 10% APS crackles/fizzes faintly when the powder hits the water.
Used at 0.1% (w/v) final in both resolving and stacking gels -- i.e. 1 part 10% APS per 100 parts gel volume.

recipes.name: Laemmli / SDS Sample (Loading) Buffer, 4x
recipes.summary: Denaturing, reducing loading buffer; mix 1 part with 3 parts protein sample and heat before loading.

components.name: Tris-Cl, pH 6.8
components.note: This previously specified the 0.5 M stacking-gel stock, which at 200 mM final would occupy 40% of the volume; together with 40% glycerol that leaves no room for the SDS, dye and reductant. Use a 1 M Tris-Cl pH 6.8 stock = 20% v/v (2 mL per 10 mL of 4x buffer). If made from powder, use Tris base (MW 121.14) titrated to pH 6.8 with HCl.

components.name: SDS (sodium dodecyl sulfate)
components.hazard: Powder is a respiratory irritant.
components.note: MW 288.38 (PubChem CID 3423265); 8% (w/v) here gives 2% at 1x.

components.name: Glycerol
components.note: Glycerol, C3H8O3, MW 92.09, density ~1.26 g/mL (PubChem CID 753); adds sample density so it sinks into the well. Gives 10% (v/v) at 1x.

components.name: Bromophenol blue (sodium salt)
components.note: Sodium salt MW 691.9 (PubChem CID 3791690); free acid MW 670.0 (PubChem CID 8272). Tracking dye only - see notes, commercial 4x buffers use far less dye.

components.name: DTT (dithiothreitol)
components.form: add as the SOLID, immediately before use
components.hazard: Harmful if swallowed; strong odour. Handle in a fume hood.
components.note: MW 154.25 (PubChem CID 439196, C4H10O2S2; PubChem displays 154.3 at four significant figures).Add DTT as the solid: 0.4 M x 154.25 = 61.7 g/L, i.e. 0.062 g per mL of 4x buffer (0.617 g per 10 mL).

recipes.adjustPHWith: none -- pH is set by the Tris-Cl stock; do not re-titrate the finished buffer
recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: Store WITHOUT reducing agent at room temperature or 4 C; store complete (reduced) buffer in single-use aliquots at -20 C
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year without reducing agent; ~6 months as frozen reduced aliquots (do not refreeze)
recipes.hazards: DTT and 2-mercaptoethanol are toxic and malodorous -- prepare and use in a fume hood. 2-mercaptoethanol in particular is acutely toxic.
notes: UNCERTAINTY -- read before use: the bromophenol blue concentration differs substantially between authorities. Cold Spring Harbor Protocols specifies 0.4% (w/v) in the 4x buffer (the value used above); most commercial 4x Laemmli buffers use only 0.02-0.1% (w/v). 0.4% produces a very dark blue front that can obscure low-molecular-weight bands and interfere with UV/stain-free imaging. If you are following a commercial protocol or imaging small proteins, use 0.02-0.1% instead. The dye is a tracking marker only and does not affect separation.

A 4x buffer is volumetrically crowded: 40% glycerol alone takes nearly half the volume, and 8% SDS plus 400 mM DTT displace roughly another 11%. Any component supplied from a stock dilute enough to need >20% of the volume will not fit. Build it as: 2 mL 1 M Tris-Cl pH 6.8 + 4 mL glycerol + 0.8 g SDS + 40 mg bromophenol blue + water to 10 mL, then add 0.617 g solid DTT to the aliquot immediately before use.

Add the reducing agent immediately before use. Alternatively add 2-mercaptoethanol to 20% (v/v) (= 5% v/v, ~715 mM at 1x). If you add BME to an already-finished aliquot rather than building it into the formulation, everything else is diluted ~20% -- either account for that or make the base buffer at 5x and let the BME bring it to 4x.
Omit the reducing agent entirely to run non-reducing gels and preserve disulfide-linked complexes.
Standard use: mix 1 volume of 4x buffer with 3 volumes of sample, then heat 5 min at 95-100 C (or 10 min at 70 C for membrane proteins, which aggregate irreversibly when boiled).
Order of addition matters: water, then Tris, then glycerol with vigorous stirring, then SDS with SLOW stirring to avoid frothing, then the dye.
Equivalent 2x recipe (also from CSH): 100 mM Tris-Cl pH 6.8, 4% SDS, 20% glycerol, 0.2% bromophenol blue, 200 mM DTT -- mix 1:1 with sample.
DTT 154.25 g/mol (PubChem CID 439196) x 0.4 M = 61.7 g/L, which is the weight quoted above; SDS 288.38, glycerol 92.09 and bromophenol blue sodium salt 691.9 all confirmed against PubChem.

The exact 4x composition here (notably the 0.4% w/v bromophenol blue) traces to Cold Spring Harbor Protocols, which is subscription-access. Openly licensed community formulations (OpenWetWare, protocols.io) agree on the Tris/SDS/glycerol/reductant figures but use much less dye.

recipes.name: SDS Gel-Loading Buffer, 2x
recipes.summary: Denaturing reducing loading buffer mixed 1:1 with sample; convenient for dilute protein samples.

components.name: Tris-Cl, pH 6.8
components.note: From the 0.5 M Tris-Cl pH 6.8 stock (Tris base MW 121.14, titrated with HCl)

components.name: SDS (sodium dodecyl sulfate)
components.hazard: Powder is a respiratory irritant.
components.note: MW 288.38 (PubChem CID 3423265).

components.name: Glycerol
components.note: Glycerol MW 92.09, density ~1.26 g/mL (PubChem CID 753).

components.name: Bromophenol blue (sodium salt)
components.note: Sodium salt MW 691.9 (PubChem CID 3791690); free acid MW 670.0 (CID 8272). 0.01-0.05% is sufficient in practice and gives a cleaner dye front.

components.name: DTT (dithiothreitol)
components.hazard: Harmful if swallowed; use in a fume hood.
components.note: MW 154.25 (PubChem CID 439196). A 1 M DTT stock is 154.25 g/L; 200 mM final needs 20% of the volume, which fits alongside 20% glycerol in a 2x buffer. Add just before use.

recipes.adjustPHWith: none
recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: Store without DTT at room temperature; add DTT fresh from a 1 M stock immediately before use
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year without DTT
recipes.hazards: DTT / 2-mercaptoethanol are toxic and malodorous -- handle in a fume hood.
notes: Cold Spring Harbor explicitly instructs: store the buffer WITHOUT DTT at room temperature and add DTT from a 1 M stock just before the buffer is used. Reduced buffer left at room temperature loses reducing power within days.
200 mM 2-mercaptoethanol (approx. 1.4% v/v) can be substituted for the DTT.
Mix 1:1 with sample, then heat 3-5 min at 95-100 C.
VOLUME CHECK: unlike the 4x buffer, the 2x version has room for a liquid DTT stock - 20% v/v of 1 M DTT plus 20% v/v glycerol leaves 60% of the volume for everything else.
2-mercaptoethanol substitute: BME is 14.3 M neat (MW 78.14, density 1.114 g/mL, PubChem CID 1567), so 200 mM = 1.4% (v/v).
The precise 2x formulation is from Cold Spring Harbor Protocols (subscription). Openly licensed equivalents on OpenWetWare agree on Tris/SDS/glycerol/DTT but vary in dye concentration.

recipes.name: SDS-PAGE Resolving Gel, 8%
recipes.summary: Laemmli resolving gel for large proteins, roughly 40-200 kDa.

components.name: Acrylamide/bis-acrylamide 37.5:1
components.hazard: Unpolymerised acrylamide is a potent neurotoxin and probable human carcinogen. Always wear nitrile gloves, work in a fume hood, and never handle the powder outside a hood -- buy the ready-made 30% solution.
components.note: 29:1 acrylamide:bis is also used and gives slightly better resolution of small proteins. Acrylamide monomer C3H5NO, MW 71.08 (PubChem CID 6579); N,N'-methylenebisacrylamide MW 154.17 (PubChem CID 8041). A 30% (w/v) 37.5:1 stock is 29.2% acrylamide + 0.8% bis.

components.name: Ammonium persulfate (APS)
components.note: ADD LAST, immediately before pouring

components.name: TEMED (N,N,N',N'-tetramethylethylenediamine)
components.hazard: Flammable, corrosive, strong ammoniacal odour -- use in a fume hood.
components.note: ADD LAST with the APS. Increase to 0.1% to speed polymerisation. TEMED C6H16N2, MW 116.20, density ~0.775 g/mL (PubChem CID 8037).

recipes.solvent: deionized water (bring to final volume; add the water FIRST, then acrylamide, Tris and SDS, and only then APS/TEMED)
recipes.adjustPHWith: none -- pH comes from the Tris-Cl stock
recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: Cast and use the same day; a poured gel can be kept overnight at 4 C wrapped in tissue moistened with 1x running buffer
recipes.shelfLife: Use within 24 h of casting; the un-catalysed mix must be poured within minutes of adding APS/TEMED
recipes.hazards: Acrylamide monomer is a neurotoxin and probable carcinogen; TEMED is flammable and corrosive; APS is a strong oxidiser. Gloves, eye protection and a fume hood are mandatory until the gel has fully polymerised.
notes: Approximate resolving range: 40-200 kDa.

Overlay the poured gel with water-saturated butanol, isopropanol or ethanol to exclude oxygen (which inhibits polymerisation) and give a flat interface. Rinse the overlay off thoroughly before casting the stacker.
Polymerisation takes ~30 min at room temperature. Keep the leftover mix in the tube to judge when the gel has set.
Degassing the mix under vacuum for 10 min before adding APS/TEMED improves reproducibility.
Worked example for 10 mL: 2.7 mL 30% acrylamide, 2.5 mL 1.5 M Tris pH 8.8, 0.1 mL 10% SDS, 0.1 mL 10% APS, 4 uL TEMED, water to 10 mL.

Acrylamide 71.08 g/mol, bis-acrylamide 154.17 g/mol, TEMED 116.20 g/mol, ammonium persulfate 228.21 g/mol and SDS 288.38 g/mol all confirmed against PubChem. All gel components here are specified as dilutions of stocks, so no direct weigh-out arithmetic is involved.

recipes.summary: The general-purpose Laemmli resolving gel, roughly 20-150 kDa.

components.name: Acrylamide/bis-acrylamide 37.5:1
components.hazard: Unpolymerised acrylamide is a potent neurotoxin and probable human carcinogen. Wear nitrile gloves and work in a fume hood; use the ready-made 30% solution rather than the powder.
components.note: Acrylamide monomer C3H5NO, MW 71.08 (PubChem CID 6579); N,N'-methylenebisacrylamide MW 154.17 (PubChem CID 8041). A 30% (w/v) 37.5:1 stock is 29.2% acrylamide + 0.8% bis.

components.name: Ammonium persulfate (APS)
components.note: ADD LAST, immediately before pouring

components.name: TEMED
components.hazard: Flammable and corrosive -- use in a fume hood.
components.note: ADD LAST with the APS. TEMED C6H16N2, MW 116.20, density ~0.775 g/mL (PubChem CID 8037).

recipes.adjustPHWith: none
recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: Cast and use the same day; may be stored overnight at 4 C wrapped in buffer-moistened tissue
recipes.hazards: Acrylamide monomer is a neurotoxin and probable carcinogen; TEMED is flammable/corrosive; APS is a strong oxidiser.

Overlay with water-saturated butanol or isopropanol while polymerising.

Acrylamide 71.08 g/mol, bis-acrylamide 154.17 g/mol, TEMED 116.20 g/mol, ammonium persulfate 228.21 g/mol and SDS 288.38 g/mol all confirmed against PubChem. All gel components here are specified as dilutions of stocks, so no direct weigh-out arithmetic is involved.

components.hazard: Unpolymerised acrylamide is a potent neurotoxin and probable human carcinogen. Gloves and fume hood required.

components.name: TEMED
components.hazard: Flammable and corrosive.
components.note: ADD LAST with the APS. TEMED C6H16N2, MW 116.20, density ~0.775 g/mL (PubChem CID 8037).

recipes.adjustPHWith: none
recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: Cast and use the same day
recipes.hazards: Acrylamide monomer is a neurotoxin and probable carcinogen; TEMED is flammable/corrosive; APS is a strong oxidiser.
notes: Approximate resolving range: 10-60 kDa.
Worked example for 10 mL: 5.0 mL 30% acrylamide, 2.5 mL 1.5 M Tris pH 8.8, 0.1 mL 10% SDS, 0.1 mL 10% APS, 4 uL TEMED, water to 10 mL.
For peptides below ~10 kDa, a Tris-tricine gel system (Schagger & von Jagow) resolves far better than 15% Tris-glycine.
15% gels are brittle -- handle carefully when removing from the cassette for transfer or staining.

Acrylamide 71.08 g/mol, bis-acrylamide 154.17 g/mol, TEMED 116.20 g/mol, ammonium persulfate 228.21 g/mol and SDS 288.38 g/mol all confirmed against PubChem. All gel components here are specified as dilutions of stocks, so no direct weigh-out arithmetic is involved.

recipes.name: SDS-PAGE Stacking Gel, 5%
recipes.summary: Low-percentage upper gel at pH 6.8 that concentrates the sample into a sharp band before it enters the resolving gel.

components.name: Acrylamide/bis-acrylamide 37.5:1
components.hazard: Unpolymerised acrylamide is a potent neurotoxin and probable human carcinogen. Gloves and fume hood required.
components.note: Acrylamide monomer C3H5NO, MW 71.08 (PubChem CID 6579); N,N'-methylenebisacrylamide MW 154.17 (PubChem CID 8041). A 30% (w/v) 37.5:1 stock is 29.2% acrylamide + 0.8% bis.

components.name: Tris-Cl, pH 6.8
components.note: If using a 1.0 M pH 6.8 stock, halve the volume

components.name: Ammonium persulfate (APS)
components.note: ADD LAST

components.name: TEMED
components.hazard: Flammable and corrosive.
components.note: Stacking gels use ~2.5x more TEMED than resolving gels because low-percentage gels polymerise slowly. TEMED C6H16N2, MW 116.20, density ~0.775 g/mL (PubChem CID 8037).

recipes.adjustPHWith: none -- pH comes from the Tris-Cl stock
recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: Cast directly onto the set resolving gel and use the same day
recipes.hazards: Acrylamide monomer is a neurotoxin and probable carcinogen; TEMED is flammable/corrosive; APS is a strong oxidiser.
notes: 4% is an equally standard alternative and gives slightly sharper wells for large sample volumes.
The pH difference between the stacker (6.8) and resolver (8.8) is what creates the moving-boundary stacking effect -- do not use the same Tris stock for both.
Dry the cassette above the resolving gel thoroughly before pouring, and pour to within ~5 mm of the top so the comb teeth sit fully in the stacker.
Sets in 10-15 min. Leave at least 30 min before pulling the comb.
Worked example for 3 mL: 0.5 mL 30% acrylamide, 0.75 mL 0.5 M Tris pH 6.8, 30 uL 10% SDS, 30 uL 10% APS, 3 uL TEMED, water to 3 mL.

Acrylamide 71.08 g/mol, bis-acrylamide 154.17 g/mol, TEMED 116.20 g/mol, ammonium persulfate 228.21 g/mol and SDS 288.38 g/mol all confirmed against PubChem. All gel components here are specified as dilutions of stocks, so no direct weigh-out arithmetic is involved.

recipes.name: Wet Transfer Buffer (Towbin), 1x
recipes.summary: Standard wet (tank) transfer buffer for moving proteins from a Tris-glycine gel onto nitrocellulose or PVDF.

components.name: Tris base (free base)
components.note: Tris base MW 121.14 (PubChem CID 6503); 3.03 g/L = 25.0 mM.

components.name: Glycine
components.note: Glycine MW 75.07 (PubChem CID 750); 14.4 g/L = 192 mM.

components.name: Methanol
components.hazard: Flammable and acutely toxic by ingestion, inhalation and skin contact; causes blindness. Handle in a fume hood and collect waste as halogen-free organic solvent.
components.note: Add after the salts have dissolved

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve salts in ~500 mL water, add methanol, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: NONE -- do not adjust the pH
recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: 4 C (pre-chill before use; transfers generate significant heat)
recipes.shelfLife: Make fresh, or reuse a maximum of 2-3 times within a week; buffer conductivity rises with each use
recipes.hazards: Methanol is toxic and flammable. Prepare in a fume hood; do not pour down the drain.
notes: Do NOT pH this buffer. The Tris:glycine ratio sets pH ~8.3; adding acid or base introduces chloride, which massively increases current and heating.

Methanol strips SDS from proteins and improves binding to the membrane, but reduces the transfer efficiency of large (>100 kDa) proteins. For proteins >100 kDa, drop methanol to 10% and/or add SDS to 0.01-0.05-0.1% and contradicted it).
For PVDF, methanol is required (and the membrane must be pre-wetted in 100% methanol). For nitrocellulose, methanol can be reduced or omitted.

Towbin buffer with SDS: add 1-10 mL of 10% SDS per litre (0.01-0.1% final) to aid transfer of large proteins; start at the low end (0.01-0.025%) and increase only if large proteins are still retained in the gel. Note that SDS can reduce binding to nitrocellulose.
Keep the buffer and tank cold (ice pack or 4 C room). Overheating causes gel distortion and poor transfer.
3.03 g/L Tris (121.14 g/mol) = 25.0 mM and 14.4 g/L glycine (75.07 g/mol) = 191.8 mM, both matching the stated 1x concentrations.

recipes.name: Transfer Buffer (Tris-Glycine), 10x, methanol-free stock
recipes.summary: Concentrated methanol-free stock; dilute with water and methanol on the day of use.

components.name: Tris base (free base)
components.note: Tris base MW 121.14 (PubChem CID 6503); 30.3 g/L = 250 mM in the 10x = 25 mM at 1x.

components.name: Glycine
components.note: Glycine MW 75.07 (PubChem CID 750); 144.0 g/L = 1.92 M in the 10x = 192 mM at 1x.

recipes.adjustPHWith: NONE -- do not adjust
recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: Room temperature or 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months
notes: To make 1 L of 1x working transfer buffer: 100 mL of this 10x stock + 200 mL methanol + 700 mL deionized water.
Never store the 10x with methanol already added -- the salts will precipitate.
Note the deliberate difference from the 10x running buffer: this stock contains NO SDS.
The 10x stock may precipitate at 4 C; warm and mix until clear before diluting.
The unadjusted stock sits near pH 8.3 once diluted; finalPH is recorded as null because the buffer is never titrated.

recipes.name: Coomassie Brilliant Blue R-250 Staining Solution
recipes.summary: Classic total-protein stain for polyacrylamide gels; detection limit roughly 50-100 ng per band.

components.name: Coomassie Brilliant Blue R-250
components.note: Coomassie Brilliant Blue R-250, sodium salt C45H44N3NaO7S2, MW 826.0 (PubChem CID 61365; supplier sheets quote 825.97), CAS 6104-59-2. R-250 (not G-250) is the correct dye here; G-250 is used for colloidal and Bradford methods.

components.name: Methanol
components.hazard: Flammable and acutely toxic; causes blindness. Use in a fume hood.

components.name: Acetic acid, glacial
components.hazard: Corrosive; causes severe burns. Always add acid to water, never the reverse.

recipes.sterilization: none (filter through Whatman No. 1 paper to remove undissolved dye)
recipes.storage: Room temperature, in a tightly closed bottle away from light
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year; the solution is reusable several times until staining becomes weak
recipes.hazards: Methanol is toxic and flammable; glacial acetic acid is corrosive. Prepare and use in a fume hood with gloves and eye protection. Dispose of as organic/aqueous chemical waste, never down the drain.
notes: MUST be filtered after the dye dissolves. Undissolved dye particles stick to the gel and produce permanent blue speckles.
Typical protocol: stain 30 min to 2 h with gentle agitation (or overnight), then destain.
The solution is reusable -- keep a dedicated staining tray and return the used stain to the bottle.
Ethanol can be substituted for methanol at the same 40% (v/v) if you want to avoid methanol; sensitivity is comparable.
For mass-spectrometry-compatible staining, use a colloidal Coomassie G-250 method instead -- methanol/acetic acid R-250 stain fixes the gel and can hamper in-gel digestion.

recipes.name: Coomassie Destaining Solution
recipes.summary: Removes unbound Coomassie dye from the gel matrix, leaving blue protein bands on a clear background.

components.name: Methanol
components.hazard: Flammable and acutely toxic; causes blindness. Use in a fume hood.

components.name: Acetic acid, glacial
components.hazard: Corrosive; causes severe burns.

recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: Room temperature in a closed container
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year
recipes.hazards: Methanol is toxic and flammable; glacial acetic acid is corrosive. Fume hood, gloves and eye protection. Collect used destain as chemical waste.
notes: Change the destain several times (every 30-60 min) until the background is clear -- typically 2 h to overnight.

A milder, slower variant used by many labs is 10% methanol / 10% acetic acid; it removes less protein from the gel and is gentler on faint bands. A 30% methanol / 10% acetic acid version is also common. All three work; the higher the alcohol, the faster and more aggressive the destain.
Dropping a folded Kimwipe, a piece of foam or a few Dowex beads into the tray absorbs released dye and speeds destaining considerably.
Prolonged destaining will eventually leach dye from the protein bands themselves -- stop when the background is acceptable.
Methanol-free option: 10% (v/v) acetic acid alone, or 30% ethanol / 10% acetic acid, destains adequately and avoids methanol waste.

recipes.name: Ponceau S Staining Solution
recipes.summary: Rapid, fully reversible total-protein stain for checking transfer efficiency on nitrocellulose or PVDF membranes.

components.name: Ponceau S (tetrasodium salt)
components.note: Ponceau S tetrasodium salt, C22H12N4Na4O13S4, MW 760.6 (PubChem CID 2723873; supplier sheets quote 760.57), CAS 6226-79-5. The free acid form (C22H16N4O13S4, MW 672.7, PubChem CID 408829) is not what is normally supplied.

components.name: Acetic acid, glacial
components.hazard: Corrosive; causes severe burns. Add the acid to the aqueous dye solution, never water to acid.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (CORRECTED: dissolve the dye in ~90% of the final volume of water, then add glacial acetic acid to 5% v/v and bring to final volume. The previous '~95%' left no headroom -- 95% water plus 5% acid is already 100% before the dye's own volume, so the batch would overshoot and end up dilute.)
recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: Room temperature or 4 C, protected from light
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year; reusable many times until staining becomes faint
recipes.hazards: Glacial acetic acid is corrosive and causes severe burns - handle in a fume hood with gloves and eye protection. If 0.1 M NaOH is used to strip the stain, note that NaOH is also corrosive.
notes: Stains in 1-5 min with gentle rocking. Destain with several rinses of deionized water until bands are visible against a pale background.
Fully reversible: wash the membrane in TBS-T or 0.1 M NaOH for a few minutes to remove all dye before blocking. It does not interfere with subsequent immunodetection.
Image or photocopy the stained membrane immediately -- the stain fades and is removed during blocking, and this image is your only record of loading and transfer quality.
The solution is reusable; pour it back into the bottle after each use.
A more sensitive variant adds trichloroacetic acid (e.g. 0.1% Ponceau S in 5% TCA), but TCA fixes proteins to the membrane and can reduce antibody accessibility.
Reversal in 0.1 M NaOH: NaOH is corrosive and causes severe eye damage - wear gloves and eye protection, and prefer a TBS-T wash where possible.

recipes.name: RIPA Lysis Buffer
recipes.summary: Stringent whole-cell lysis buffer that solubilises cytoplasmic, membrane and nuclear proteins for western blotting.

components.name: Tris base (titrated to pH 7.4 with HCl)
components.form: Tris base (free base), NOT Tris-HCl powder
components.note: Tris base MW 121.14 (PubChem CID 6503); 0.05 M = 6.06 g/L. Commonly written '50 mM Tris-HCl pH 7.4', but the standard preparation weighs Tris BASE and titrates with HCl. If you weigh Tris-HCl powder (MW 157.59, PubChem CID 93573) instead, use 7.88 g/L and titrate up with NaOH.

components.name: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
components.note: NaCl MW 58.44 (PubChem CID 5234); 0.15 M = 8.77 g/L.

components.name: NP-40 substitute (Igepal CA-630) or Triton X-100
components.hazard: Both detergents cause serious eye damage and are irritating to skin; Triton X-100 is also very toxic to aquatic life and is a regulated substance in the EU. Wear eye protection and collect waste as chemical waste.
components.note: Genuine Nonidet P-40 is discontinued; Igepal CA-630 is the accepted equivalent. Triton X-100 is a common substitute at the same 1%.

components.name: Sodium deoxycholate
components.note: Sodium deoxycholate C24H39NaO4, MW 414.55-414.6 (PubChem CID 23668196 gives 414.6; supplier sheets 414.55). Precipitates below pH ~7.5 and in the cold - see notes.

components.name: SDS
components.note: SDS MW 288.38 (PubChem CID 3423265); supplied from the 10% (w/v) stock.

components.name: EDTA disodium salt dihydrate (Na2EDTA*2H2O)
components.note: Disodium EDTA dihydrate, C10H14N2Na2O8*2H2O (PubChem records it as C10H18N2Na2O10), MW 372.24, PubChem CID 636371; 1 mM = 0.372 g/L. Free-acid EDTA (MW 292.24, PubChem CID 6049) will not dissolve at neutral pH without NaOH; anhydrous disodium EDTA is MW 336.21.

recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl / NaOH
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter (optional)
recipes.storage: 4 C for the base buffer; aliquot and store at -20 C for long-term
recipes.shelfLife: Up to 6 months at 4 C without inhibitors; add protease/phosphatase inhibitors immediately before use
recipes.hazards: SDS powder is a respiratory irritant; sodium deoxycholate is harmful if swallowed and irritating. Handle detergent powders in a fume hood. pH is adjusted with HCl / NaOH — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: UNCERTAINTY -- there is no single canonical RIPA formulation and published versions differ in ways that matter. The pH is given variously as 7.4 (most common), 7.5, or 8.0; the non-ionic detergent may be NP-40/Igepal or Triton X-100; and EDTA (1-5 mM) and EGTA are present in some recipes and absent in others. The values above are the most widely cited combination. If you are reproducing a specific published experiment, use that paper's exact formulation rather than this one.

ALWAYS add protease inhibitors fresh to the aliquot you are about to use, never to the stock bottle. A typical addition is 1x complete protease inhibitor cocktail plus 1 mM PMSF. For phospho-proteins also add phosphatase inhibitors (e.g. 1 mM Na3VO4, 10 mM NaF, 10 mM sodium pyrophosphate) and keep everything ice-cold.

Sodium deoxycholate precipitates below about pH 7.5 and when cold. Dissolve it at room temperature at the correct pH, and if the chilled buffer goes cloudy, warm briefly and mix -- do not use it cloudy.
RIPA disrupts most protein-protein interactions. For co-immunoprecipitation use a milder buffer (e.g. 1% NP-40 or 0.5% Triton X-100 without deoxycholate and SDS).
The SDS and deoxycholate in RIPA are incompatible with some downstream assays -- use a BCA (not Bradford) protein assay on RIPA lysates.
Tris 121.14, NaCl 58.44, disodium EDTA dihydrate 372.24 and SDS 288.38 all confirmed against PubChem; 50 mM / 150 mM / 1 mM give 6.06 g/L, 8.77 g/L and 0.372 g/L respectively, all sane weigh-outs.

The composite RIPA formulation is supported by Cold Spring Harbor Protocols (subscription) and free manufacturer buffer sheets; the component molecular weights and salt forms above are all from PubChem (public domain), but no single openly licensed protocol pins the whole recipe.

recipes.name: Blocking Buffer, 5% Non-Fat Dry Milk in TBS-T
recipes.summary: General-purpose western blot blocking solution; the default first choice for most antibodies.

components.name: Non-fat dry milk powder (skimmed milk powder)
components.note: Use plain non-fat dry milk with no added sugar, vitamins or cocoa. 7.5 g in 150 mL is the Cell Signaling worked example.

recipes.solvent: 1x TBS-T (20 mM Tris, 150 mM NaCl, 0.1% Tween-20, pH 7.5) -- bring to final volume
recipes.adjustPHWith: none
recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: 4 C; use the same day
recipes.shelfLife: 24 h at 4 C -- milk solutions spoil rapidly and support bacterial growth
notes: DO NOT use milk to block blots probed with phospho-specific antibodies. Milk contains casein, itself a phosphoprotein, which produces high background and can sequester the antibody. Use 5% BSA in TBS-T instead.
Milk also contains biotin and endogenous IgG -- avoid it for biotin/streptavidin detection systems and when using anti-bovine or anti-goat secondary antibodies.
Block for 1 h at room temperature with agitation, or overnight at 4 C.
Mix thoroughly and let stand a few minutes to disperse lumps; filter through cheesecloth or a coffee filter if you see undissolved particles, which cause speckled background.
3% milk is sufficient for many blots and gives slightly better sensitivity at the cost of a little more background.

recipes.name: Blocking / Antibody Dilution Buffer, 5% BSA in TBS-T
recipes.summary: Casein-free blocking buffer required for phospho-specific antibodies and biotin-based detection.

components.name: Bovine serum albumin (BSA), fraction V, protease-free
components.note: 1.0 g in 20 mL is the Cell Signaling worked example for antibody dilution buffer. Use IgG-free/protease-free grade for best results.

components.name: Sodium azide (NaN3)
components.hazard: ACUTELY TOXIC. Forms explosive metal azides in drains and lead/copper plumbing. Do not pour down the sink. Never combine with HRP-based reagents.
components.note: Sodium azide NaN3, MW 65.01 (PubChem CID 33557); 0.02% (w/v) = 3.1 mM. OPTIONAL preservative, for primary-antibody dilutions only - see notes.

recipes.solvent: 1x TBS-T (20 mM Tris, 150 mM NaCl, 0.1% Tween-20, pH 7.5) -- bring to final volume
recipes.adjustPHWith: none
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter (recommended, and required if the solution will be reused)
recipes.storage: 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: 1-2 weeks at 4 C without azide; up to several months with 0.02% azide
recipes.hazards: Sodium azide is acutely toxic and forms explosive heavy-metal azides. Omit it unless you specifically need a long-lived antibody dilution, and dispose of azide-containing waste properly.
notes: This is the required blocking and dilution buffer for phospho-specific antibodies -- milk's casein is a phosphoprotein and will cause high background.

CRITICAL: sodium azide irreversibly inhibits horseradish peroxidase. Never include azide in any solution that contacts an HRP-conjugated secondary antibody or the substrate. It is only acceptable in primary-antibody dilutions, and even then the blot must be washed thoroughly before adding HRP conjugate. Omit the azide entirely if in doubt.
Block for 1 h at room temperature, or overnight at 4 C.
BSA dissolves slowly. Sprinkle it onto the surface of the buffer and let it hydrate without stirring for 10-15 min, then mix gently -- vigorous stirring denatures it and creates foam.
Swap TBS-T for PBS-T if your protocol calls for it, but not for phospho-antibody work.

recipes.name: Sodium phosphate buffer, 0.1 M, pH 7.4
recipes.summary: General-purpose near-neutral buffer for enzyme assays, protein work and fixatives; buffers well over pH 5.8-8.0 via the H2PO4-/HPO4(2-) pair.

components.name: Sodium phosphate dibasic, anhydrous
components.form: Na2HPO4 anhydrous, MW 141.96 (PubChem 141.959). Hydrates have very different masses: dihydrate 177.99, heptahydrate 268.07, dodecahydrate 358.14 - up to a 2.5x difference in weighed mass for the same moles.
components.note: 0.0774 M x 141.96 = 10.99 g/L at pH 7.4. Cold Spring Harbor rounds this to 10.9 g/L.

components.name: Sodium phosphate monobasic monohydrate
components.form: NaH2PO4*H2O monohydrate, MW 137.99. Anhydrous NaH2PO4 is 119.98 (PubChem CID 23672064); the dihydrate is 156.01 (PubChem CID 23673460).
components.note: 0.0226 M x 137.99 = 3.12 g/L at pH 7.4. Cold Spring Harbor rounds this to 3.1 g/L.

recipes.adjustPHWith: 1 M NaOH / 1 M HCl (fine adjustment only - set the pH primarily by the mono/dibasic ratio)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15 min, or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: 4 C (or room temperature if sterile)
recipes.shelfLife: 1 month at 4 C; discard if cloudy or if microbial growth appears
recipes.hazards: Low hazard. The 1 M NaOH and 1 M HCl used for fine pH adjustment are corrosive - wear eye protection.
notes: MW/FORM AUDIT [VERIFIED]: both salts checked against PubChem. Na2HPO4 anhydrous = 141.96 (CID 24203); NaH2PO4*H2O = 137.99 (CID 516949). Molarity x MW reproduces the stated g/L in each case.
PKa values of phosphoric acid at 25 C: pKa1 = 2.15, pKa2 = 7.20, pKa3 = 12.35. Only pKa2 is relevant to the physiological buffer.

Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = 7.20 + log10([HPO4 2-]/[H2PO4 -]). Using the ideal pKa2 of 7.20 this predicts only a ~1.6:1 dibasic:monobasic ratio at pH 7.4 - do NOT mix 1.6:1. The amounts here use 3.4:1 (77.4/22.6) because the apparent pKa2 drops to ~6.87 at the ionic strength of a 0.1 M buffer. The whole table below is internally consistent with an apparent pKa2 of ~6.865. Follow the table, then confirm on a calibrated meter.

MIXING TABLE (Cold Spring Harbor). Combine x mL of 1 M Na2HPO4 with y mL of 1 M NaH2PO4, then dilute to 1 L for a 0.1 M buffer: pH 5.8 = 7.9/92.1; 6.0 = 12.0/88.0; 6.2 = 17.8/82.2; 6.4 = 25.5/74.5; 6.6 = 35.2/64.8; 6.8 = 46.3/53.7; 7.0 = 57.7/42.3; 7.2 = 68.4/31.6; 7.4 = 77.4/22.6; 7.6 = 84.5/15.5; 7.8 = 89.6/10.4; 8.0 = 93.2/6.8. [All twelve rows re-read on this pass and confirmed; Cold Spring Harbor pdb.rec8303 and the Fisher PAGE728 sheet print an identical table.]
1 M stock solutions: 138 g/L NaH2PO4*H2O (monobasic) and 142 g/L Na2HPO4 anhydrous (dibasic).
For any other target pH at 0.1 M, scale the two molarities from the table: molarity_dibasic = 0.1 * (x/100), molarity_monobasic = 0.1 * (y/100).
Phosphate precipitates with Ca2+ and Mg2+ and inhibits many enzymes (kinases, DNA polymerases, carboxylases). Use HEPES or Tris where free divalent cations are needed.
The pH of a phosphate buffer is only weakly temperature dependent (dpKa/dT ~ -0.0028 per C), which is its main advantage over Tris.
Freezing concentrated phosphate buffer causes a large pH drop (to roughly pH 3-4) as Na2HPO4*12H2O crystallises out - do not freeze protein samples in concentrated phosphate.

recipes.name: Potassium phosphate buffer, 0.1 M, pH 7.0
recipes.summary: Near-neutral buffer preferred over sodium phosphate when K+ is required or Na+ interferes (many enzymology and chromatography applications).

components.name: Potassium phosphate dibasic, anhydrous
components.form: K2HPO4 anhydrous, MW 174.18 (PubChem 174.176). The trihydrate is 228.22 (PubChem CID 16217523) - recalculate if you substitute it.

components.name: Potassium phosphate monobasic
components.form: KH2PO4 anhydrous, MW 136.09 (PubChem 136.086). No common hydrate is sold.

recipes.adjustPHWith: 1 M KOH / 1 M HCl (fine adjustment only)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15 min, or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: 4 C; 1 M stocks are stable for ~3 months at room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 1 month at 4 C at working strength
recipes.hazards: Low hazard. 1 M KOH and 1 M HCl used for pH adjustment are corrosive.
notes: MW/FORM AUDIT [VERIFIED]: K2HPO4 anhydrous = 174.18 (PubChem CID 24450); KH2PO4 = 136.09 (PubChem CID 516951). Both molarity x MW products reproduce the stated g/L.
PKa2 of phosphate = 7.20 at 25 C. pH = 7.20 + log10([HPO4 2-]/[H2PO4 -]).

TABLE STATUS: all twelve rows were re-read directly from the Fisher Scientific sheet (PAGE728) on this pass and every one matches. Every row sums to 100 mL. The implied apparent pKa2 lies between 6.79 and 6.83 across the table, declining as the pH rises except that the pH 7.8 and 8.0 rows come back up to about 6.81; the spread is the expected ionic-strength effect in a 0.1 M buffer. Confirm the final pH on a meter.
1 M stock solutions: 174.18 g/L K2HPO4 anhydrous; 136.09 g/L KH2PO4. Equivalently 43.55 g K2HPO4 or 34.02 g KH2PO4 per 250 mL.
Potassium phosphate is more soluble than sodium phosphate at low temperature, so 1 M stocks are less prone to crystallising at 4 C.
Avoid with perchlorate, and avoid mixing with SDS-containing solutions in the cold (potassium dodecyl sulfate precipitates).

recipes.name: Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), 1x, pH 7.4
recipes.summary: Isotonic, near-physiological wash and dilution buffer for cells, tissues and immunoassays.

components.name: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
components.note: 137 mM final. MW 58.44.

components.name: Potassium chloride (KCl)
components.note: 2.7 mM final. MW 74.55.

components.name: Sodium phosphate dibasic, anhydrous (Na2HPO4)
components.note: 8.0 mM final. MW 141.96 - the ANHYDROUS salt. Molar equivalents: 2.15 g/L of Na2HPO4*7H2O (268.07) or 1.43 g/L of Na2HPO4*2H2O (177.99).

components.name: Potassium phosphate monobasic (KH2PO4)
components.note: 1.5 mM final. MW 136.09.

recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl / NaOH
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15 min
recipes.storage: Room temperature; refrigerate after opening for long-term use
recipes.shelfLife: Up to 1 year sterile at room temperature; discard if turbid
recipes.hazards: Low hazard once made up. The HCl and NaOH used to set the pH are corrosive - wear eye protection and gloves while titrating.
notes: AMOUNTS RE-VERIFIED on this pass directly against Cold Spring Harbor Protocols pdb.rec081240 (2014): 8.0 g NaCl / 137.0 mM, 0.20 g KCl / 2.7 mM, 1.14 g Na2HPO4 / 8.0 mM, 0.20 g KH2PO4 / 1.5 mM per litre, pH 7.4, autoclaved. The g/L and mM columns are mutually consistent with the PubChem molecular weights.

ATTRIBUTION: this is the CSH 2014 formulation, NOT the Sambrook / CSH 2006 one. The Sambrook lineage (pdb.rec8247) is a different and more widely used PBS with 1.44 g/L Na2HPO4 (10 mM) and 0.24 g/L KH2PO4 (1.8 mM). Both are legitimately called 'PBS pH 7.4'; check which your protocol assumes.

Dulbecco's PBS (DPBS) is yet another formulation whose phosphate content (about 1.15 g/L Na2HPO4, 0.20 g/L KH2PO4) is very close to the amounts above, so matching phosphate numbers alone does not identify the recipe. DPBS 'with calcium and magnesium' additionally contains 0.9 mM CaCl2 and 0.5 mM MgCl2 - not interchangeable for cell-adhesion work.
Preparation: dissolve all four salts in roughly 800 mL of water, set the pH to 7.4, then make up to 1 L.
Total phosphate here is 9.5 mM (8.0 mM dibasic + 1.5 mM monobasic). The Sambrook variant is 11.8 mM total (10 mM Na2HPO4 + 1.8 mM KH2PO4).
Autoclaving shifts the pH slightly upward; re-check the pH after sterilisation for pH-critical assays.

recipes.name: Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), 10x stock, pH 7.4
recipes.summary: Concentrated PBS stock; dilute 1:10 with water for working-strength PBS.

components.name: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
components.note: 1.37 M in the stock. MW 58.44.

components.name: Potassium chloride (KCl)
components.note: 26.8 mM in the stock. MW 74.55.

components.name: Sodium phosphate dibasic heptahydrate (Na2HPO4*7H2O)
components.note: 95.5 mM in the stock. MW 268.07 - HEPTAHYDRATE, verified on PubChem. The molar equivalent as the anhydrous salt is 13.6 g/L (MW 141.96).

components.name: Potassium phosphate monobasic (KH2PO4)
components.note: 14.7 mM in the stock. MW 136.09.

recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl / NaOH
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15 min
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: Up to 1 year at room temperature
recipes.hazards: Low hazard once made up. The HCl and NaOH used to set the pH are corrosive - wear eye protection and gloves while titrating.
notes: AMOUNTS RE-VERIFIED on this pass against Cold Spring Harbor Protocols pdb.rec102681 (2018): 25.6 g Na2HPO4*7H2O, 80 g NaCl, 2 g KCl, 2 g KH2PO4 per litre, pH 7.4, autoclave, store up to 1 year at room temperature.
Set the pH of the 10x stock to 7.4; after 1:10 dilution the pH lands close to 7.4 but should be confirmed for pH-critical work.
The 10x stock can precipitate at 4 C. Warm to room temperature and mix to full dissolution before diluting.
HYDRATE MISMATCH: this stock uses the HEPTAHYDRATE of Na2HPO4 while the 1x CSH recipe in this file uses the ANHYDROUS salt - a classic weighing error when substituting one for the other.

THIS 10x IS NOT EXACTLY 10x THE 1x RECIPE IN THIS FILE. 25.6 g/L of Na2HPO4*7H2O is 95.5 mM, so a 1:10 dilution gives ~9.6 mM dibasic phosphate against 8.0 mM in the 1x recipe. NaCl, KCl and KH2PO4 do match at 1:10. Both recipes are faithful to their own Cold Spring Harbor sources (pdb.rec102681 and pdb.rec081240) but differ by ~19% in dibasic phosphate - a real difference in buffer strength, not just hydrate bookkeeping. Pick one lineage and stay on it.

recipes.name: Tris-HCl buffer, 1 M stock (pH 7.4 / 7.6 / 8.0)
recipes.summary: Workhorse buffer for pH 7.0-9.0; the standard stock behind TE, TAE, TBS and most molecular biology buffers.

components.name: Tris base (free base)
components.form: Tris base / Trizma base / tromethamine, free base, C4H11NO3, MW 121.14. Do NOT weigh out Trizma HCl (C4H12ClNO3, MW 157.60; PubChem CID 93573 lists 157.59) in its place - it is a different compound and the same mass gives both the wrong molarity and a far lower pH.
components.note: 121.14 g/L for a 1 M solution. Cold Spring Harbor prints 121.1 g and the Barrick Lab 121 g.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~800 mL, pH, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: concentrated HCl (~12 M): ~70 mL/L for pH 7.4, ~60 mL/L for pH 7.6, ~42 mL/L for pH 8.0
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15 min
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year or more; discard if it develops a yellow colour
recipes.hazards: Concentrated HCl is corrosive and gives off irritating fumes - add it in a fume hood with eye protection and gloves.
notes: MW/FORM AUDIT [VERIFIED]: Tris free base = 121.14 (PubChem CID 6503); Tris hydrochloride = 157.59-157.60 (PubChem CID 93573). The HCl volumes (70 / 60 / 42 mL per litre for pH 7.4 / 7.6 / 8.0) were re-read from Cold Spring Harbor on this pass.
PKa of Tris = 8.06 at 25 C (suppliers also quote 8.08-8.1). Useful buffering range pH 7.0-9.0.

STRONGLY temperature dependent: the pH falls by about 0.03 units per 1 C rise (dpKa/dT ~ -0.028 per C). A 0.05 M Tris solution reads roughly pH 9.5 at 5 C, 8.9 at 25 C and 8.6 at 37 C. Always pH at the temperature of use, or record the pH-ing temperature.
The pH also drops on dilution: a tenfold dilution LOWERS the pH by roughly 0.1 unit (Tris is a cationic buffer).
Alternative preparation without concentrated HCl: blend 1 M Tris base with 1 M Tris-HCl (157.60 g/L) to the target pH; pH = 8.06 + log10([Tris base]/[Tris-HCl]).
Let the solution reach room temperature before the final pH adjustment - dissolving Tris base is endothermic and the solution starts out cold.
Discard yellow solutions and switch to a higher grade of Tris base.

Tris is a primary amine: it reacts with aldehydes (unsuitable with glutaraldehyde or formaldehyde fixation), interferes with Bradford and BCA protein assays at high concentration, and inhibits some enzymes.

OPEN-SOURCE NOTE: the HCl titration volumes come from Cold Spring Harbor (copyrighted) and the Barrick Lab wiki, which states no open license. No openly licensed source for those volumes was found on this pass; the 121.14 g/L figure follows directly from the PubChem molecular weight.

recipes.name: Tris-buffered saline (TBS), 1x, pH 7.5
recipes.summary: Phosphate-free isotonic wash buffer; the standard alternative to PBS for Western blots and for any assay using phosphatases or phospho-specific antibodies.

components.name: Tris base (free base)
components.form: Tris base (tromethamine), free base, MW 121.14 - not Tris-HCl (157.60).
components.note: 0.05 M x 121.14 = 6.06 g/L; Cold Spring Harbor specifies 6.05 g/L.

components.name: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
components.note: 0.15 M x 58.44 = 8.77 g/L; Cold Spring Harbor specifies 8.76 g/L.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~800 mL, pH, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: 1 M HCl
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15 min (or 0.22 um filter)
recipes.storage: 4 C (short-term use at room temperature is acceptable)
recipes.shelfLife: 3 months at 4 C (Cold Spring Harbor); discard if turbid
recipes.hazards: Low hazard once made up. The 1 M HCl used to set the pH is corrosive - wear eye protection and gloves while titrating.
notes: AMOUNTS RE-VERIFIED on this pass: 6.05 g Tris base + 8.76 g NaCl per litre, pH set to 7.5 with 1 M HCl, stable 3 months at 4 C (Cold Spring Harbor pdb.rec11830). Both figures agree with molarity x PubChem MW.
Two near-identical conventions circulate: pH 7.5 (Cold Spring Harbor) and pH 7.6 (Abcam and classic Western blot protocols). Both are 50 mM Tris / 150 mM NaCl.
For TBST (TBS-Tween), add Tween-20 to 0.05-0.1% (v/v) after pH adjustment.
A 10x stock is 60.5 g/L Tris base and 87.6 g/L NaCl, pH 7.5; re-check the pH after diluting.
Because Tris pH is temperature dependent, pH the solution at the temperature at which it will be used.

OPEN-SOURCE NOTE: the 6.05 g / 8.76 g amounts come from Cold Spring Harbor (copyrighted); the Barrick Lab wiki states no open license. The underlying 50 mM Tris / 150 mM NaCl composition is generic and reproducible from the PubChem molecular weights alone.

recipes.name: HEPES buffer, 1 M stock, pH 7.5
recipes.summary: Zwitterionic Good's buffer for pH 6.8-8.2; the standard choice for cell culture and enzyme work where phosphate or Tris interfere.

components.name: HEPES, free acid
components.form: HEPES free acid, C8H18N2O4S, MW 238.30 (PubChem lists 238.31), CAS 7365-45-9. The MONOSODIUM salt is a different compound: C8H17N2NaO4S, MW 260.29, CAS 75277-39-3 (PubChem CID 2724248). A solution of the monosodium salt is strongly ALKALINE (about pH 10-10.5, as expected for the fully deprotonated base of a pKa 7.5 acid), so it is titrated DOWN with HCl rather than up with NaOH. The HEMIsodium salt is the one that lands near pH 7.5 unadjusted.
components.note: 238.30 g/L for a 1 M solution. A 1 M solution of the free acid sits around pH 5.0-6.5 before adjustment. The Barrick Lab makes 100 mL of 1 M HEPES from 23.83 g, the same figure.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~800 mL, pH, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: NaOH (5-10 M for a 1 M stock; KOH if a K+ buffer is wanted)
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter (preferred). Autoclaving is possible but can cause slight yellowing.
recipes.storage: 4 C, protected from light
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months at 4 C; discard if yellow or turbid
recipes.hazards: 5-10 M NaOH (or KOH) used for titration is severely corrosive and the dilution is strongly exothermic - add slowly with stirring, wear eye protection and gloves.
notes: MW/FORM AUDIT [VERIFIED]: HEPES free acid = 238.30-238.31 (PubChem CID 23831); HEPES monosodium salt = 260.29 (PubChem CID 2724248). 1 M x 238.30 = 238.30 g/L, consistent with the Barrick Lab's 23.83 g per 100 mL.
PKa (pKa2) = 7.55 at 20 C; 7.48 at 25 C. Sigma also quotes 7.85 at 0 C and 7.31 at 37 C.
Temperature coefficient dpKa/dT = -0.014 per C - much flatter than Tris, which is a main reason to choose HEPES.
Useful buffering range pH 6.8-8.2. Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = 7.48 + log10([HEPES-]/[HEPES free acid]) at 25 C.
HEPES in cell culture medium under ambient light can generate hydrogen peroxide and cytotoxic photoproducts - store in the dark and do not leave HEPES-buffered media illuminated.
HEPES weakly chelates some metal ions and can interfere with the Lowry protein assay and with some redox chemistry.
Common working strengths: 10-25 mM in cell culture medium, 20-50 mM in enzyme assay buffers.
The Barrick Lab variant is 1 M HEPES-NaOH at pH 7.0 (about 2.0 mL of 6 M NaOH per 100 mL) - the same stock at a different set point.

recipes.name: MOPS buffer, 1 M stock, pH 7.0
recipes.summary: Zwitterionic Good's buffer for pH 6.5-7.9; used for RNA gels, protein chromatography and defined microbial media.

components.name: MOPS, free acid
components.form: MOPS free acid, C7H15NO4S, MW 209.26 (PubChem lists 209.27), CAS 1132-61-2. The sodium salt is C7H14NNaO4S, MW 231.25 (PubChem CID 3859613) - do not substitute at equal mass.
components.note: 209.26 g/L for a 1 M solution.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~800 mL, pH, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: NaOH (5-10 M for a 1 M stock)
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter (preferred - MOPS solutions yellow on autoclaving)
recipes.storage: 4 C, protected from light
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months at 4 C; discard if yellow
recipes.hazards: 5-10 M NaOH used for titration is severely corrosive - add slowly with stirring, wear eye protection and gloves.
notes: MW/FORM AUDIT [VERIFIED]: MOPS free acid = 209.26-209.27 (PubChem CID 70807); MOPS sodium salt = 231.25 (PubChem CID 3859613). 1 M x 209.26 = 209.26 g/L.
PKa = 7.20 at 25 C. Useful buffering range pH 6.5-7.9.
Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = 7.20 + log10([MOPS-]/[MOPS free acid]) at 25 C.
MOPS solutions darken to yellow on autoclaving and on light exposure, and the discoloured buffer can inhibit enzymes. Filter-sterilise and store dark.
10x MOPS RNA gel running buffer is a separate recipe: 0.2 M MOPS, 20 mM sodium acetate, 10 mM EDTA, pH 7.0.
For Bis-Tris polyacrylamide gels, MOPS running buffer suits mid-size to large proteins (roughly 14-260 kDa); MES running buffer, with its lower pKa, is the choice for small proteins.
The temperature coefficient is small (about -0.01 per C in the Good's buffer literature) - check your supplier's data sheet if the application is temperature-critical.

recipes.name: MES buffer, 0.5 M stock, pH 6.0
recipes.summary: Zwitterionic Good's buffer for the mildly acidic range pH 5.5-6.7; used in plant tissue culture, ion-exchange chromatography and EDC/NHS coupling.

components.name: MES monohydrate
components.form: MES monohydrate, C6H13NO4S*H2O, MW 213.25, CAS 145224-94-8 (PubChem CID 16218417). The anhydrous free acid is C6H13NO4S, MW 195.24, CAS 4432-31-9 (PubChem CID 78165) - both are sold simply as 'MES', so read the bottle label.
components.note: 0.5 M x 213.25 = 106.63 g/L of the monohydrate; 0.5 M x 195.24 = 97.62 g/L if using the anhydrous free acid.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~800 mL, pH, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: NaOH (5 M)
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter, or autoclave 121 C, 15 min
recipes.storage: 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months at 4 C
recipes.hazards: 5 M NaOH used for titration is severely corrosive - add slowly with stirring, wear eye protection and gloves.
notes: MW/FORM AUDIT [VERIFIED]: MES monohydrate = 213.25 (PubChem CID 16218417); MES anhydrous free acid = 195.24 (PubChem CID 78165). Both g/L figures follow from molarity x MW.

PKa = 6.15 at 20 C (6.10 at 25 C per Calbiochem). Useful buffering range pH 5.5-6.7. Suppliers disagree on the upper bound - Calbiochem 475893 gives 5.5-6.5 while Sigma and most Good's-buffer tables give 5.5-6.7. This is a judgement call about acceptable capacity ~0.5 units above the pKa, not a factual conflict; treat capacity above pH 6.5 as marginal.
Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = 6.10 + log10([MES-]/[MES free acid]) at 25 C.
The monohydrate MW is printed as 213.24-213.25 by different suppliers; the exact sum 195.24 + 18.02 = 213.26. The spread is rounding and is immaterial at bench precision.
Preferred over citrate or phosphate when metal chelation must be avoided - MES binds metals only very weakly.
Standard buffer for EDC / sulfo-NHS carbodiimide bioconjugation, typically 0.1 M MES with 0.5 M NaCl at pH 6.0.

recipes.name: Citrate buffer, 0.1 M, pH 5.0
recipes.summary: Acidic buffer covering pH 3.0-6.2; used in enzyme assays, antigen retrieval and formulation work.

components.name: Citric acid monohydrate
components.form: Citric acid monohydrate, C6H8O7*H2O, MW 210.14. Anhydrous citric acid is MW 192.12 - a 9% mass difference.
components.note: 0.035 M x 210.14 = 7.35 g/L at pH 5.0 (equivalent to 350 mL of 0.1 M citric acid per litre).

components.name: Trisodium citrate dihydrate
components.form: Trisodium citrate dihydrate, C6H5Na3O7*2H2O, MW 294.10 (some suppliers print 294.12). Anhydrous trisodium citrate is MW 258.07 (PubChem CID 6224).
components.note: 0.065 M x 294.10 = 19.12 g/L at pH 5.0 (equivalent to 650 mL of 0.1 M trisodium citrate per litre).

recipes.adjustPHWith: 1 M HCl / 1 M NaOH (fine adjustment only - set the pH primarily by the acid:citrate ratio)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15 min, or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: 4 C (citrate buffers support microbial growth at room temperature)
recipes.shelfLife: 1-3 months at 4 C; discard if turbid or if growth appears
recipes.hazards: Low hazard once made up. The 1 M HCl and 1 M NaOH used for fine pH adjustment are corrosive - wear eye protection and gloves while titrating.
notes: MW/FORM AUDIT [VERIFIED]: citric acid monohydrate = 210.14 (PubChem CID 22230); trisodium citrate dihydrate = 294.10 (PubChem CID 71474); anhydrous trisodium citrate = 258.07 (PubChem CID 6224). Both molarity x MW products reproduce the stated g/L.
PKa values of citric acid at 25 C: pKa1 = 3.13, pKa2 = 4.76, pKa3 = 6.40.
To make any pH from the table for volume V litres: moles of citric acid monohydrate = 0.1 * (x/100) * V; moles of trisodium citrate dihydrate = 0.1 * (y/100) * V.
Heat-induced epitope retrieval in IHC uses a far more dilute buffer: 10 mM sodium citrate pH 6.0 (2.94 g/L of the dihydrate, adjusted with HCl), often with 0.05% Tween-20.
Citrate strongly chelates Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+ and other di-/trivalent metals - it inhibits metalloenzymes and is unsuitable where free divalent cations are required.
The classic McIlvaine buffer is the related citrate-phosphate system (0.1 M citric acid + 0.2 M Na2HPO4) spanning pH 2.2-8.0.
OPEN-SOURCE NOTE: the mixing table's only support found on this pass is a commercial web reference; the chemistry (MWs, pKa values) is confirmed against PubChem. Verify the final pH on a meter.

recipes.name: Acetate buffer, 0.1 M, pH 5.0
recipes.summary: Simple, cheap acidic buffer for pH 3.6-5.6; standard for many hydrolase assays, ion exchange, and nucleic acid precipitation buffers.

components.name: Sodium acetate trihydrate
components.form: CH3COONa*3H2O, MW 136.08 (PubChem CID 23665404). Anhydrous sodium acetate is MW 82.03 (PubChem CID 517045) - using the wrong form is a 66% mass error.
components.note: 0.0704 M x 136.08 = 9.58 g/L at pH 5.0 (5.78 g/L if using the anhydrous salt).

components.name: Acetic acid, glacial
components.form: Glacial acetic acid, ~99.7% w/w, density 1.049 g/mL at 25 C, ~17.4 M.
components.note: 0.0296 M x 60.05 = 1.78 g/L = 1.70 mL of glacial acetic acid per litre at pH 5.0.
components.hazard: Glacial acetic acid is corrosive and flammable; handle in a fume hood.

recipes.adjustPHWith: glacial acetic acid / 1 M NaOH (fine adjustment only)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15 min, or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: Room temperature (4 C for long-term)
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months
recipes.hazards: Glacial acetic acid: corrosive, flammable, strong odour - use a fume hood and full PPE. 1 M NaOH is corrosive.
notes: MW/FORM AUDIT [VERIFIED]: sodium acetate trihydrate = 136.08 (PubChem CID 23665404); anhydrous sodium acetate = 82.03 (PubChem CID 517045); acetic acid = 60.05 (PubChem CID 176). All molarity x MW products reproduce the stated g/L.
PKa of acetic acid = 4.76 at 25 C (sources give 4.75-4.77). Useful buffering range pH 3.6-5.6.

Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = 4.76 + log10([acetate-]/[acetic acid]). The apparent pKa falls to about 4.6-4.65 at 0.1 M ionic strength, which is why the empirical table below does not match the ideal calculation exactly.

MIXING TABLE (Gomori). Combine x mL of 0.2 M acetic acid with y mL of 0.2 M sodium acetate, then dilute to 100 mL for a 0.1 M buffer: pH 3.6 = 46.3/3.7; 3.8 = 44.0/6.0; 4.0 = 41.0/9.0; 4.2 = 36.8/13.2; 4.4 = 30.5/19.5; 4.6 = 25.5/24.5; 4.8 = 20.0/30.0; 5.0 = 14.8/35.2; 5.2 = 10.5/39.5; 5.4 = 8.8/41.2; 5.6 = 4.8/45.2. Every row sums to 50 mL of 0.2 M stock, i.e. 0.1 M final in 100 mL.

0.2 M STOCK AMOUNTS : 0.2 M sodium acetate is 27.22 g/L of the trihydrate (or 16.41 g/L anhydrous). 0.2 M acetic acid is 12.01 g/L, which is 11.5 mL of glacial acetic acid per litre at density 1.049 g/mL and 99.7% assay. An earlier revision carried 11.55 mL/L, copied from a university SOP; the arithmetic gives 11.5 mL/L. The 0.4% difference is below weighing/pipetting error but the corrected figure is the one that is internally consistent with the MW.

TABLE CAVEAT: the pH 5.0 row (14.8/35.2) has been cross-checked; the other rows are the standard Gomori values. The implied apparent pKa across the table spans about 4.59-4.73 (lowest at the pH 4.4 row, highest at pH 5.4), clustering near 4.62. Confirm the final pH on a calibrated meter.
3 M sodium acetate at pH 5.2 (408.24 g/L of the trihydrate, adjusted with glacial acetic acid) is the standard stock for ethanol precipitation of nucleic acids.
Acetate is volatile at low pH - do not lyophilise or rotary-evaporate an acetate buffer and expect the pH to survive.

recipes.name: Carbonate-bicarbonate buffer, pH 9.6 (ELISA coating buffer)
recipes.summary: Alkaline buffer used almost universally for passive adsorption of proteins onto polystyrene ELISA plates.

components.name: Sodium carbonate, anhydrous
components.form: Na2CO3 anhydrous, MW 105.99 (PubChem 105.988). Sodium carbonate decahydrate (washing soda) is MW 286.14 (PubChem CID 151402) - do not substitute at equal mass.

components.name: Sodium bicarbonate
components.form: NaHCO3, MW 84.01 (PubChem 84.007). No hydrate is sold.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~950 mL, check pH, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: 1 M HCl or 1 M NaOH, added slowly with stirring
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter (do NOT autoclave - CO2 is driven off and the pH shifts)
recipes.storage: 4 C, tightly capped
recipes.shelfLife: No more than 2 weeks at 4 C; many labs make it fresh daily
recipes.hazards: 1 M NaOH and 1 M HCl used for pH adjustment are corrosive - wear eye protection.
notes: MW/FORM AUDIT [VERIFIED]: Na2CO3 anhydrous = 105.99 (PubChem CID 10340); NaHCO3 = 84.01 (PubChem CID 516892); Na2CO3*10H2O = 286.14 (PubChem CID 151402). Both molarity x MW products reproduce the stated g/L.

MOLARITY LABEL MARKED UNCERTAIN. The gram amounts (1.59 g/L Na2CO3 + 2.93 g/L NaHCO3) and the pH 9.6 target are consistent across many sources, but the molarity label is not: by arithmetic this mixture is 0.050 M total carbonate (0.015 + 0.0349), yet many websites label the identical recipe '0.1 M'. Sigma capsule C3041, dissolved per its instructions, is specified as 0.05 M pH 9.6, which supports the 0.05 M reading. Treat total carbonate here as 0.05 M and check what your protocol assumes.
PKa values of carbonic acid at 25 C: pKa1 = 6.35, pKa2 = 10.33. The pH 9.2-10.6 window uses pKa2.

Ideal Henderson-Hasselbalch predicts about pH 9.9-10.0 for this ratio (10.33 + log10(0.015/0.0349)). Real solutions measure lower (~9.5-9.6) because of ionic-strength effects and CO2 uptake. Always set the final pH on a calibrated meter rather than trusting the calculation.
The buffer absorbs atmospheric CO2 and drifts acidic - keep it capped and remake often.
Do not autoclave: heating decomposes bicarbonate and shifts the pH substantially.
Typical use: coat ELISA plates with 1-10 ug/mL antigen in this buffer, 100 uL per well, overnight at 4 C.
A common alternative at the same pH is a genuinely 0.1 M carbonate-bicarbonate buffer: 3.18 g/L Na2CO3 + 5.86 g/L NaHCO3, i.e. double all the amounts above.

recipes.name: Borate buffer, 0.1 M, pH 8.5
recipes.summary: Alkaline buffer for pH 8.0-10.2; standard for amine-reactive labelling (NHS esters, isothiocyanates) and for protein conjugation.

components.name: Boric acid
components.form: H3BO3, orthoboric acid, MW 61.83 (PubChem prints 61.84; the 0.01 spread is the rounding of the boron atomic weight and is immaterial at bench precision).
components.hazard: Boric acid is a category 1B reproductive toxicant (EU H360FD) - avoid dust inhalation, weigh in a fume hood or with a dust mask.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~800 mL, pH, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: 5 M NaOH (titrate the boric acid solution up to the target pH)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15 min, or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 6-12 months
recipes.hazards: Boric acid and borates are reproductive toxicants (EU H360FD) - avoid inhaling the dust and do not use in reproductive- or developmental-toxicology assays. 5 M NaOH used for titration is severely corrosive.
notes: MW/FORM AUDIT [VERIFIED]: boric acid = 61.83-61.84 (PubChem CID 7628); sodium tetraborate decahydrate (borax) = 381.37-381.4 (PubChem CID 16211214). 0.1 M x 61.83 = 6.18 g/L.
PKa of boric acid = 9.24 at 25 C. Useful buffering range pH 8.0-10.2. Capacity at pH 8.5 is modest (0.74 units below the pKa) - use 0.1-0.2 M for adequate capacity.
Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = 9.24 + log10([borate]/[boric acid]).

ALTERNATIVE (borax/boric acid): blend 0.05 M sodium tetraborate decahydrate (borax, Na2B4O7*10H2O, MW 381.37, 19.07 g/L) with 0.2 M boric acid (12.37 g/L) to the target pH. One mole of borax supplies four moles of boron, so 0.05 M borax is 0.2 M in total boron.

Borate complexes cis-diols: it binds sugars, glycoproteins, catecholamines, RNA ribose and Tris-derived polyols. Do not use borate for carbohydrate or glycoprotein work unless the complexation is intended.
For NHS-ester labelling, 0.1 M sodium borate pH 8.5 is a common choice because it contributes no primary amines (unlike Tris) that would quench the reaction.
Boric acid dissolves slowly in cold water - warm gently to about 40 C with stirring.

recipes.name: Glycine-HCl buffer, 0.1 M, pH 3.0
recipes.summary: Low-pH elution buffer for affinity chromatography - the standard way to strip antibodies from Protein A/G resins and antigens from immunoaffinity columns.

components.name: Glycine
components.form: Glycine, free base (aminoacetic acid), C2H5NO2, MW 75.07, CAS 56-40-6. Glycine hydrochloride (MW 111.53) and glycine sodium salt are different materials - use the free base.
components.note: 0.1 M x 75.07 = 7.51 g/L. The unadjusted solution sits near the isoelectric point (about pH 6); titrate down with HCl.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~800 mL, pH, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: concentrated or 1 M HCl (titrate down to the target pH)
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: Room temperature or 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months
recipes.hazards: Concentrated HCl is corrosive and fuming - add it in a fume hood with eye protection and gloves.
notes: MW/FORM AUDIT [VERIFIED]: glycine free base = 75.07 (PubChem CID 750). 0.1 M x 75.07 = 7.51 g/L.
PKa values of glycine at 25 C: pKa1 (alpha-carboxyl) = 2.35, pKa2 (alpha-amino) = 9.78. Glycine-HCl works off pKa1 and buffers usefully over pH 2.2-3.6.
Henderson-Hasselbalch (acidic arm): pH = 2.35 + log10([glycine zwitterion]/[protonated glycine]).
pH 2.5-3.0 is the usual elution window. Start at pH 3.0 and go lower only if elution is incomplete - many antibodies and most enzymes are irreversibly denatured below pH 2.5.
NEUTRALISE IMMEDIATELY: collect eluate into one-tenth volume of 1 M Tris-HCl pH 8.5 (or 1 M Tris base) so each fraction returns to neutral pH within seconds.
Including 0.15 M up to 0.5 M NaCl can improve recovery of some antibodies.
The alkaline counterpart is glycine-NaOH buffer (separate recipe in this category), which uses pKa2 = 9.78.

recipes.name: Glycine-NaOH buffer, 0.1 M, pH 9.5
recipes.summary: Alkaline buffer for pH 8.6-10.6; used for alkaline phosphatase assays, amine chemistry, and as a cheap alternative to CAPS or borate.

components.name: Glycine
components.note: 0.1 M x 75.07 = 7.51 g/L; titrate up with NaOH.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~800 mL, pH, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: 1 M or 5 M NaOH (titrate up to the target pH)
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter, or autoclave 121 C, 15 min
recipes.storage: Room temperature or 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months
recipes.hazards: Concentrated NaOH is severely corrosive and dissolving NaOH pellets is strongly exothermic - add slowly with stirring, wear eye protection and gloves.
notes: MW/FORM AUDIT [VERIFIED]: glycine free base = 75.07 (PubChem CID 750). 0.1 M x 75.07 = 7.51 g/L.
Works off pKa2 (alpha-amino group) = 9.78 at 25 C. Useful buffering range pH 8.6-10.6.
Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = 9.78 + log10([glycinate anion]/[glycine zwitterion]).
Glycine is a primary amine - unsuitable for NHS-ester or aldehyde chemistry, which it quenches. Use borate or carbonate for those.
Alkaline solutions take up CO2 and drift acidic; keep tightly capped.
Classic alkaline phosphatase substrate buffer: 0.1 M glycine, 1 mM MgCl2, 1 mM ZnCl2, pH 10.4.

recipes.name: Normal saline, 0.9% (w/v) NaCl
recipes.summary: Isotonic unbuffered sodium chloride solution for rinsing, dilution, and fluid/electrolyte replacement.

components.name: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
components.note: 9.0 g per litre = 154 mM NaCl (MW 58.44, PubChem CID 5234). Delivers 154 mEq/L Na+ and 154 mEq/L Cl-; calculated osmolarity 308 mOsmol/L.

recipes.solvent: water for injection / deionized water (bring to final volume)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15 min, or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: Up to 2 years sterile and sealed; discard if turbid or if particulates are visible
notes: MATH CHECK [VERIFIED]: 9.0 g/L / 58.44 g/mol = 154 mM, giving 154 mEq/L of each ion and 308 mOsmol/L, matching the FDA labelling for 0.9% Sodium Chloride Injection USP. NaCl MW confirmed on PubChem (CID 5234).
This solution is UNBUFFERED. Freshly made saline sits around pH 5.0-6.5 with essentially no buffering capacity - it is not a substitute for PBS in pH-sensitive work.
0.9% is called 'normal' saline for historical reasons only; it is not 1 N in the chemical sense (1 N NaCl would be 58.44 g/L).
Slightly hypertonic relative to plasma (308 vs about 285-295 mOsmol/L) and it delivers a supraphysiological chloride load; large-volume clinical use can cause hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis.
For any human or animal administration, pharmacopoeial (USP/EP) grade material and validated sterile, non-pyrogenic manufacturing are required. A bench-prepared solution is for laboratory use only.
Related strengths: 0.45% (half-normal, 77 mM), 3% (hypertonic, 513 mM), 5% (hypertonic, 856 mM).

Microbiology labs often use a slightly weaker sterile saline for dilution series - the Barrick Lab uses 0.85% w/v (8.5 g/L, 145 mM), autoclaved. Do not treat 0.85% and 0.9% as interchangeable where osmolarity matters.

recipes.name: 5 M NaCl
recipes.summary: Universal salt stock for buffers, hybridization, lysis and chromatography.

components.name: Sodium chloride
components.note: 5 M x 58.44 = 292.2 g/L. MW confirmed against PubChem.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~0.8 vol, then bring to final volume)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min (or 0.22 um filter)
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: Indefinite if sterile and not contaminated
notes: NaCl is anhydrous, FW 58.44 (PubChem CID 5234). No hydrate forms are in routine lab use, so there is no hydrate trap here.
MATH CHECK: 5 mol/L x 58.44 g/mol = 292.2 g/L. A litre of 5 M NaCl therefore needs ~292 g.
5 M is near the practical ceiling. Solubility is roughly 359 g/L at 25 C, i.e. about 6.1 M, so the stock sits at ~82% of saturation.
Dissolution is mildly endothermic - the solution cools and the last grams go into solution slowly. Warm gently and stir.
Add the salt to ~80% of final volume, dissolve fully, then top up. Weighing salt into a full measure of water overshoots the final volume.
May crystallize in a cold room; re-warm and mix before use.

recipes.name: 1 M MgCl2 (from hexahydrate)
recipes.summary: Divalent cation source for PCR, restriction digests, polymerase and ligase buffers.

components.name: Magnesium chloride hexahydrate
components.form: MgCl2 * 6H2O (FW 203.30, formula Cl2H12MgO6) - NOT anhydrous MgCl2 (FW 95.21)
components.note: 1 M x 203.30 = 203.30 g/L, i.e. 20.33 g per 100 mL. Both the hexahydrate MW and the anhydrous MW were checked on PubChem.

recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min (or 0.22 um filter)
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year; discard if cloudy
notes: Hexahydrate 203.30 (PubChem CID 24644) vs anhydrous 95.21 (PubChem CID 5360315). The stated form and the stated MW agree.
HYDRATE TRAP: weighing the hexahydrate solid but calculating with the anhydrous MW gives a solution about 2.1x too dilute. Nearly all bench-stock MgCl2 is the hexahydrate.
MgCl2 * 6H2O is strongly hygroscopic and deliquescent. Weigh quickly, keep the bottle tightly closed, and reject a jar that has caked or gone wet - its water content is no longer exactly 6H2O.
Autoclaved stocks can lose water by evaporation and drift up in concentration; filter sterilization avoids this.
For PCR, keep small single-use aliquots. Repeated pipetting into one shared tube is a classic cause of drifting Mg2+ and failed reactions.

recipes.name: 1 M MgSO4 (from heptahydrate)
recipes.summary: Mg2+ source for SOB/SOC medium, phage buffers and minimal media.

components.name: Magnesium sulfate heptahydrate
components.form: MgSO4 * 7H2O (FW 246.48, formula H14MgO11S) - NOT anhydrous MgSO4 (FW 120.37)

recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min (or 0.22 um filter)
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year
notes: Heptahydrate 246.48 (PubChem CID 24843). Anhydrous MgSO4 is 120.37, so the two differ by ~2.05x.
Bottles labelled only 'magnesium sulfate' on a molecular-biology bench are usually the heptahydrate - confirm on the label before weighing.
The heptahydrate effloresces (loses water) in dry air, which slowly raises the effective Mg content of an old bottle. Buy fresh for quantitative work.
SOB is commonly made with 10 mM MgSO4 plus 10 mM MgCl2; the Barrick Lab SOB variant uses 20 mM MgSO4 alone. SOC is SOB with ~20 mM glucose added after autoclaving.
Barrick Lab keeps Mg as a separately autoclaved 10% (w/v) MgSO4 * 7H2O stock and adds it to defined medium after autoclaving.
Do not autoclave MgSO4 together with phosphate-containing media - magnesium phosphate precipitates. Add it as a sterile stock after autoclaving.

recipes.name: 1 M CaCl2 (from dihydrate)
recipes.summary: Ca2+ source for chemical competent-cell prep, phage buffers, nuclease and protease buffers.

components.name: Calcium chloride dihydrate
components.form: CaCl2 * 2H2O (FW 147.01, formula CaCl2H4O2); anhydrous CaCl2 = 110.98; hexahydrate CaCl2 * 6H2O = 219.07
components.note: 1 M x 147.01 = 147.01 g/L using the dihydrate. Dihydrate and anhydrous MWs both confirmed on PubChem.
components.hazard: Irritant; anhydrous CaCl2 dissolves strongly exothermically - add solid to water, never the reverse.

recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter (preferred)
recipes.storage: 4 C in aliquots; room temperature acceptable for non-sterile use
recipes.shelfLife: 6-12 months
recipes.hazards: Calcium chloride is an eye and skin irritant. The anhydrous form releases substantial heat on dissolving; use eye protection and dissolve slowly.
notes: Dihydrate 147.01 (PubChem CID 6093260), anhydrous 110.98 (PubChem CID 5284359), hexahydrate 219.07 (PubChem CID 6093252; = 110.98 + 6 x 18.015). The stated form matches the stated MW.
HYDRATE TRAP: three forms are commonly stocked. Choosing the wrong MW is the most frequent CaCl2 error - read the bottle before you weigh.
Anhydrous CaCl2 is an aggressive desiccant: it takes up water fast, so a part-used anhydrous bottle no longer weighs out accurately. The dihydrate is the better routine choice.
Filter sterilize rather than autoclave - heating drives out insoluble carbonate and phosphate precipitates, especially in hard water.
Do not add concentrated CaCl2 to a phosphate-containing buffer; calcium phosphate precipitates immediately.
For the classic chemical-competence method, cells are washed and held in ice-cold 100 mM (0.1 M) CaCl2 - dilute this stock 1:10.

A 2.5 M stock made from ~11 g CaCl2 * 6H2O in 20 mL final (0.0502 mol / 0.020 L = 2.51 M), 0.22 um filtered and stored in 1 mL aliquots at 4 C, is the other common format. The arithmetic checks out; the published version of that variant is in a subscription source only.

recipes.name: 2 M KCl
recipes.summary: Monovalent salt stock for PCR buffers, ionic-strength adjustment and protein purification.

components.name: Potassium chloride
components.note: 2 M x 74.55 = 149.10 g/L. MW confirmed against PubChem.

recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min (or 0.22 um filter)
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: Indefinite if sterile
notes: KCl anhydrous, 74.55 (PubChem CID 4873). No hydrate to confuse.
MATH CHECK: 2 mol/L x 74.55 g/mol = 149.1 g/L.
KCl is much less soluble than NaCl: about 34 g per 100 mL at 20 C (~4.6 M), falling to roughly 3.7 M at 4 C. A 2 M stock stays clear in a cold room.
Standard Taq PCR buffer is 50 mM KCl - dilute this stock 1:40.
Do not substitute KCl for NaCl in SDS-containing buffers: potassium dodecyl sulfate is insoluble and will precipitate.

recipes.name: 1 M DTT (dithiothreitol)
recipes.summary: Reducing agent stock for keeping protein cysteines reduced and for SDS-PAGE sample buffer.

components.name: DL-Dithiothreitol
components.form: DTT free dithiol, anhydrous solid, C4H10O2S2 (FW 154.25)
components.note: 1 M x 154.25 = 154.25 g/L, i.e. 3.09 g in 20 mL. PubChem reports the molecular weight rounded to 154.3; the four-figure value 154.25 is the one used on labels and in recipes.
components.hazard: Irritant; strong thiol odour.

components.name: Sodium acetate buffer, pH 5.2
components.note: Final 10 mM sodium acetate pH 5.2 - 3.33 mL of the 3 M pH 5.2 stock per litre. The mildly acidic pH is what keeps the stock stable.

recipes.adjustPHWith: supplied by the 0.01 M sodium acetate; do not re-titrate
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter - NEVER autoclave (DTT is heat-labile)
recipes.storage: 20 C in single-use aliquots (e.g. 0.5-1 mL); keep on ice while in use
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year at -20 C as unopened aliquots; use a thawed aliquot the same day and discard
recipes.hazards: Irritant; harmful if swallowed. The powder has a strong, persistent thiol odour - weigh it in a fume hood.
notes: DTT free dithiol, C4H10O2S2, 154.25 (PubChem CID 448795). There is no hydrate or salt form in routine use.
MATH CHECK: 1 M x 154.25 = 154.25 g/L, consistent with the common 3.09 g / 20 mL preparation.

Make it up in 0.01 M sodium acetate pH 5.2 rather than plain water. At neutral or alkaline pH the thiolate is oxidized quickly by dissolved oxygen; the acidic buffer suppresses that, which is why the stock keeps.
Do not autoclave and do not leave at room temperature. Repeated freeze-thaw of one aliquot is the usual reason a DTT stock stops working.
Loss of the sharp sulfur smell is a practical sign the stock has oxidized. If it does not smell, do not trust it.
Typical working concentrations: 1-10 mM in protein buffers; 50-100 mM in SDS-PAGE sample buffer.
The thiolate is the reducing species, so DTT works poorly below about pH 7. Storing at pH 5.2 is fine - it reduces normally once diluted into a neutral working buffer.
TCEP is a more stable, odourless alternative that tolerates low pH and does not need -20 C aliquoting.

OPEN SOURCE GAP: the specific acetate-buffered 1 M formulation and its stability claim are documented in subscription protocol collections; the chemistry (MW, oxidation behaviour) is confirmed openly on PubChem.
