Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - Dithiothreitol (DTT) (1 M)

recipes.name: 0.5 M EDTA, pH 8.0
recipes.summary: Divalent-cation chelator; component of TE, TAE and TBE, and a general nuclease inhibitor.

components.name: EDTA disodium salt dihydrate
components.form: Na2EDTA * 2H2O, C10H18N2Na2O10 (FW 372.24) - NOT free-acid EDTA (292.24) and NOT the tetrasodium salt
components.note: 0.5 M x 372.24 = 186.12 g/L. Both the disodium dihydrate and the free acid MWs were checked on PubChem.

components.name: NaOH pellets
components.note: About 20 g of NaOH pellets per litre are needed to reach pH 8.0. This is a titration, not a stoichiometric component - add until the solid dissolves and the meter reads 8.0.
components.hazard: Corrosive. Causes severe burns; dissolving pellets releases heat. Wear eye protection and gloves.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~0.8 vol, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: NaOH (pellets or 10 N)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: Indefinite if sterile
recipes.hazards: Preparation involves solid NaOH, which is corrosive and generates heat on dissolution. Wear goggles and gloves; add pellets slowly to stirring solution.
notes: Na2EDTA * 2H2O, 372.24 (PubChem CID 636371, formula C10H18N2Na2O10) vs EDTA free acid 292.24 (PubChem CID 6049, C10H16N2O8). The stated form matches the stated MW.
MATH CHECK: 0.5 mol/L x 372.24 g/mol = 186.12 g/L - the expected figure for 0.5 M EDTA.
The disodium salt will not dissolve until the pH is raised toward 8.0 with NaOH. A persistent white slurry means not enough NaOH has gone in - keep adding base and stirring, do not add more water.
FORM TRAP: free-acid EDTA (292.24) needs far more NaOH for the same molarity. The tetrasodium salt dissolves readily but arrives near pH 11 and must be titrated down with HCl.
Use a magnetic stirrer; a litre typically takes 20-40 min. Warming to 40-50 C helps.
Working concentrations: 1 mM in TE, 2 mM in TAE, 10 mM in many lysis and resuspension buffers.
EDTA carryover strips Mg2+ from PCR and restriction reactions. Elute into low-EDTA TE (0.1 mM) or water if an enzymatic step follows.

Addgene - Protocol: Purify plasmid DNA (EDTA in resuspension and TE buffers)

recipes.name: 20% (w/v) SDS
recipes.summary: Anionic detergent stock for lysis buffers, SDS-PAGE and hybridization washes.

components.name: Sodium dodecyl sulfate, electrophoresis grade
components.hazard: The dry powder is a serious respiratory and eye irritant - weigh in a fume hood or balance enclosure and wear a mask.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~0.9 vol at 68 C, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: a few drops of concentrated HCl, only if necessary
recipes.sterilization: none - sterilization is not necessary. DO NOT AUTOCLAVE.
recipes.storage: Room temperature; do NOT refrigerate
recipes.shelfLife: 6-12 months
recipes.hazards: Dry SDS powder is harmful if inhaled and irritating to eyes and airways. Weigh it in a hood. Do not brush spilled powder off the bench dry - wipe it up damp. pH is adjusted with a few drops of concentrated HCl, only if necessary — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Sodium dodecyl sulfate, C12H25NaO4S, 288.38 (PubChem CID 3423265). The recipe is stated as mass percent, so the MW matters only when converting to molarity.

Preparation: weigh 200 g per litre, dissolve in about 900 mL water with warming to ~68 C and stirring, correct to pH 7.2 with a few drops of concentrated HCl only if the solution reads off, then make up to 1 L. Sterilization is unnecessary and autoclaving is not appropriate.
SDS crystallizes below roughly 15-20 C. Keep the stock at room temperature and re-dissolve at 37-68 C if it clouds. Never store SDS stocks in a fridge or cold room.
SDS precipitates with potassium as insoluble potassium dodecyl sulfate. Do not mix SDS stocks with KCl or potassium acetate buffers - alkaline-lysis Solution III exploits exactly this.
Many labs keep 10% (w/v) instead; 10% is the same recipe at 100 g/L. Halve the mass.
OPEN SOURCE GAP: the 68 C dissolution and pH 7.2 details come from a subscription protocol collection. The chemical identity, MW and hazard data are open (PubChem).

sources.title: PubChem CID 3423265 - Sodium lauryl sulfate / sodium dodecyl sulfate (288.38, C12H25NaO4S)
Addgene - Protocol: Purify plasmid DNA (1% SDS in alkaline lysis Solution II)
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - SDS (20% w/v) recipe

recipes.name: 1 M Tris-Cl, pH 7.5
recipes.summary: Workhorse buffer stock for DNA/protein work at near-neutral pH (TE, restriction buffers, lysis buffers).

components.name: Tris base (tromethamine, free base)
components.form: Tris base / Trizma base, C4H11NO3 (FW 121.14) - NOT Tris-HCl salt (FW 157.59)
components.note: 1 M x 121.14 = 121.14 g/L. Both the free base and the hydrochloride MWs were checked on PubChem. Titrate with HCl to the target pH.

components.name: Concentrated HCl (~12 N)
components.note: About 65 mL per litre to reach pH 7.5. [REVISED from 6.2% to 6.5%: the standard table gives 70 mL/L at pH 7.4 and 60 mL/L at pH 7.6, so pH 7.5 interpolates to ~65 mL/L. Henderson-Hasselbalch with pKa 8.06 independently predicts ~64 mL/L.] Titrate to the meter reading - never add a fixed volume blind.
components.hazard: Corrosive and fuming - add in a fume hood, wear eye protection and gloves.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in 0.8 vol, titrate, cool, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: concentrated HCl
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min (or 0.22 um filter)
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year; discard if it yellows or grows anything
recipes.hazards: Titration uses concentrated hydrochloric acid: corrosive, fuming, causes severe burns. Work in a fume hood with eye protection.
notes: Tris base C4H11NO3, 121.14 (PubChem CID 6503) vs tromethamine hydrochloride C4H12ClNO3, 157.59 (PubChem CID 93573). The stated form matches the stated MW.
MATH CHECK: 1 M x 121.14 = 121.14 g/L.

TEMPERATURE: Tris pH shifts by roughly -0.028 pH units per degree C. A buffer set to pH 7.5 at 25 C reads about pH 8.1 at 4 C and about pH 7.1 at 37 C. Calibrate and titrate at the temperature the buffer will be used at, or at minimum record the titration temperature.
Let the solution reach room temperature before the final reading - dissolving Tris base is endothermic and a cold solution reads high.
FORM TRAP: Tris base (121.14) is titrated DOWN with HCl; the Tris-HCl salt (157.59) starts near pH 4.7 and is titrated UP with NaOH. Starting from Tris base is standard.
Use a pH electrode that tolerates Tris (glass-body or double-junction Ag/AgCl); some electrodes read Tris poorly.
Tris is a primary amine and reacts with aldehydes, NHS esters and isothiocyanates. Do not use it for amine-reactive labelling or with formaldehyde/glutaraldehyde fixation.
Buffering range is pH 7.0-9.0 (pKa 8.06 at 25 C). pH 7.5 is at the low end of that range but is standard practice.
OPEN SOURCE GAP: the HCl-volume-per-litre table is published in subscription references. The MW, pKa and temperature coefficient are available openly.

sources.title: PubChem CID 6503 - Tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane / tromethamine (121.14, C4H11NO3)
PubChem CID 93573 - Tromethamine hydrochloride, Tris-HCl (157.59, C4H12ClNO3)
Sigma-Aldrich T1503 - Trizma base technical data (FW 121.14, pKa 8.06 at 25 C, dpKa/dT ~ -0.028/C)
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - Tris-Cl (1 M) recipe (HCl volumes tabulated for pH 7.4, 7.6, 8.0)

recipes.name: 1 M Tris-Cl, pH 8.0
recipes.summary: Standard buffer stock for TE, plasmid preps, PCR and most cloning buffers.

components.name: Tris base (tromethamine, free base)
components.form: Tris base / Trizma base, C4H11NO3 (FW 121.14) - NOT Tris-HCl salt (FW 157.59)

components.name: Concentrated HCl (~12 N)
components.note: About 42 mL per litre to reach pH 8.0 (the tabulated value). Henderson-Hasselbalch with pKa 8.06 predicts ~44 mL/L, so the tabulated figure is internally consistent. Titrate to the meter, do not add blind.
components.hazard: Corrosive and fuming - add in a fume hood.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in 0.8 vol, titrate, cool, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: concentrated HCl
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min (or 0.22 um filter)
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year
recipes.hazards: Titration uses concentrated hydrochloric acid: corrosive and fuming. Fume hood and eye protection.
notes: Tris base 121.14 (PubChem CID 6503); Tris-HCl salt 157.59 (PubChem CID 93573) is a different reagent with a different MW.
MATH CHECK: 1 M x 121.14 = 121.14 g/L. About 0.53 mol of the Tris is protonated at pH 8.0, matching ~42-44 mL of 12 N HCl per litre.
pH 8.0 sits essentially on the Tris pKa (8.06 at 25 C) - maximum buffering capacity and the most forgiving point to titrate to.
Same temperature caveat as all Tris buffers: about -0.028 pH units per degree C. Titrate at the working temperature for cold-room chromatography.
TE buffer is 10 mM Tris-Cl pH 8.0 plus 1 mM EDTA pH 8.0 - dilute this stock 1:100. Addgene's miniprep resuspension buffer uses 25 mM Tris-HCl pH 8.
Autoclaved Tris can go faintly yellow; a pale tint is cosmetic, a strong colour means remake it.
Avoid Tris where a nitrogen-free buffer is needed, or with amine-reactive chemistry.
OPEN SOURCE GAP: the exact HCl volume per litre is tabulated in subscription references; the underlying pKa and MW are open.

sources.title: PubChem CID 6503 - Tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane / tromethamine (121.14, C4H11NO3)
Addgene - Protocol: Purify plasmid DNA (25 mM Tris-HCl pH 8 Solution I; TE for elution)
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - Tris-Cl (1 M) recipe

recipes.name: 1 M Tris-Cl, pH 9.0
recipes.summary: Alkaline Tris stock for alkaline phosphatase reactions, some ligations and high-pH elutions.

components.name: Tris base (tromethamine, free base)
components.note: 1 M x 121.14 = 121.14 g/L. The mass is certain; the HCl volume is not tabulated in the standard reference.

components.name: Concentrated HCl (~12 N)
components.note: UNVERIFIED VOLUME - roughly 8-11 mL per litre. Add HCl dropwise to the meter; do not rely on a fixed volume. Henderson-Hasselbalch with pKa 8.06 gives 10.3% of 1 M protonated at pH 9.0 = 0.103 mol = ~8.6 mL of 12 N acid. The same model predicts 44 mL at pH 8.0 and 68 mL at pH 7.4 against tabulated 42 and 70 mL, so it is accurate to a few percent - hence a range topping out near 11 mL.
components.hazard: Corrosive and fuming.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in 0.8 vol, titrate, cool, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: concentrated HCl
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min (or 0.22 um filter)
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 6-12 months
recipes.hazards: Titration uses concentrated hydrochloric acid: corrosive and fuming. Fume hood and eye protection.
notes: Tris base 121.14 (PubChem CID 6503). Mass per litre is solid.

MARKED UNCERTAIN: the standard Tris table only lists HCl volumes for pH 7.4, 7.6 and 8.0. The volume given here for pH 9.0 is a calculation, not a published figure. Titrate to the meter and treat the stated volume as a starting estimate.

pH 9.0 is about one unit above the Tris pKa (8.06), so buffering capacity is roughly 37% of the maximum at the pKa (buffer capacity scales as alpha x (1 - alpha); with alpha = 0.103 at pH 9.0, 0.0924 vs 0.25). Expect the pH to drift more readily. For strong buffering above pH 9, consider CHES (pKa ~9.3) or glycine-NaOH.
Temperature dependence bites hardest here: at about -0.028 pH units per degree C, a pH 9.0 buffer titrated at 25 C reads near pH 9.6 in a 4 C cold room. Titrate at the working temperature.
Because so little acid is needed, overshooting is easy. Switch to 1 N HCl for the final approach.
OPEN SOURCE GAP: no open source tabulates the HCl requirement at pH 9.0; the value here is derived from the openly published pKa.

Sigma-Aldrich T1503 - Trizma base technical data (buffering range pH 7.0-9.0, pKa 8.06 at 25 C, dpKa/dT ~ -0.028/C)
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - Tris-Cl (1 M) recipe (tabulates pH 7.4, 7.6, 8.0 only)

recipes.name: 3 M Potassium acetate, pH 5.5
recipes.summary: Acidic salt stock for nucleic-acid precipitation and neutralization steps.

components.name: Potassium acetate
components.note: 3 M x 98.14 = 294.42 g/L. MW confirmed against PubChem.

components.name: Glacial acetic acid
components.note: Roughly 20-30 mL per litre - this is a real addition, not a few drops. Glacial acetic acid is ~17.4 M (density 1.049 g/mL, FW 60.05). Ideal Henderson-Hasselbalch (pKa 4.76) predicts ~31 mL/L; at 3 M ionic strength the apparent pKa falls and the true requirement is lower, around 20 mL/L. Titrate to the meter.
components.hazard: Corrosive and pungent - dispense in a fume hood, wear eye protection.

recipes.adjustPHWith: glacial acetic acid (to lower pH) / KOH (to raise)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min (or 0.22 um filter)
recipes.storage: Room temperature; chill to 4 C for use in plasmid preps
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year
recipes.hazards: Prepared with glacial acetic acid, which is corrosive and has a strong vapour. Use a fume hood.
notes: Potassium acetate, C2H3KO2, 98.14 (PubChem CID 517044), anhydrous - the hydrate is not in normal lab use.
MATH CHECK: 3 mol/L x 98.14 g/mol = 294.4 g/L. Adding acetic acid raises total acetate above 3 M while potassium stays at 3 M.
This is NOT the Sambrook/Addgene alkaline-lysis 'Solution III', which is 3 M in potassium but ~5 M in acetate - see the separate entry.
Potassium acetate precipitates SDS as insoluble potassium dodecyl sulfate; that is exactly what clears an alkaline lysate.
For ethanol precipitation of nucleic acids, sodium acetate is the more common choice; potassium acetate is used where K+ is wanted or Na+ must be avoided.
Potassium acetate is hygroscopic - keep the bottle sealed and weigh promptly.
OPEN SOURCE GAP: the acid volume required to reach pH 5.5 at 3 M is not tabulated in an open source; the figure here is calculated from the openly published acetic acid pKa and density.

sources.title: PubChem CID 517044 - Potassium acetate (98.14, C2H3KO2)
PubChem CID 176 - Acetic acid (60.05, density 1.049 g/mL, pKa 4.76)
Addgene - Protocol: Purify plasmid DNA (potassium acetate renaturing solution)

recipes.name: Alkaline Lysis Solution III (3 M potassium, 5 M acetate)
recipes.summary: Neutralization/precipitation buffer for plasmid miniprep by alkaline lysis.

components.name: Potassium acetate
components.note: 60 mL of 5 M potassium acetate per 100 mL final. The 5 M stock itself is 5 x 98.14 = 490.7 g/L.

components.name: Glacial acetic acid
components.note: 11.5 mL per 100 mL final volume (~17.4 M glacial acid, so ~0.20 mol acetic acid per 100 mL).
components.hazard: Corrosive and pungent - dispense in a fume hood.

recipes.solvent: deionized water to final volume (28.5 mL water per 100 mL)
recipes.adjustPHWith: no titration needed - the acetic acid sets the pH
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min
recipes.storage: Room temperature; chill to 4 C before use
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year
recipes.hazards: Contains glacial acetic acid - corrosive, prepare in a fume hood with eye protection.
notes: COMPOSITION VERIFIED against an open source: Addgene's kit-free alkaline lysis miniprep specifies 120 mL of 5 M potassium acetate + 23 mL glacial acetic acid + 57 mL water, i.e. exactly the same 60 : 11.5 : 28.5 ratio scaled to 200 mL. The resulting solution is 3 M in potassium and ~5 M in acetate.
MATH CHECK: per 100 mL, 0.30 mol K+ and 0.30 mol acetate come from the KOAc, plus ~0.20 mol acetic acid = ~0.50 mol total acetate. That is 3.0 M potassium and 5.0 M acetate as claimed.
This is NOT the same reagent as '3 M potassium acetate pH 5.5'. The acetate is in large excess here. Use whichever one your protocol actually names.
Use ice-cold. The cold plus the high K+ drives quantitative precipitation of SDS, protein and chromosomal DNA while plasmid stays in solution.
Calculator note: the 'fromStock' entry gives the 5 M potassium acetate volume; the acetic acid is a straight 11.5% v/v; water makes up the rest.

MARKED UNCERTAIN (pH only): the composition is exact and now openly confirmed, but the pH is not a specified target - the solution is mixed and used without titration. Henderson-Hasselbalch on ~0.30 mol acetate vs ~0.20 mol acetic acid gives pH ~4.9; allowing for ionic-strength effects, pH ~4.7-4.9. Do not titrate to hit any printed number - mix the three volumes as given.
Addgene stores Solution III at 4 C and adds 150 uL to a lysate of 100 uL Solution I + 200 uL Solution II.

sources.title: Addgene - Protocol: Purify plasmid DNA, kit-free alkaline lysis miniprep (Solution III: 120 mL 5 M potassium acetate + 23 mL glacial acetic acid + 57 mL water)
PubChem CID 517044 - Potassium acetate (98.14, C2H3KO2)
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - Alkaline lysis Solution III

recipes.name: 1 M Imidazole, pH 8.0
recipes.summary: Competitive eluent stock for Ni-NTA / IMAC purification of His-tagged proteins.

components.name: Imidazole
components.note: 1 M x 68.08 = 68.08 g/L. MW confirmed against PubChem. Use high-purity, low-A280 grade for IMAC.
components.hazard: Suspected reproductive toxin - avoid dust inhalation and skin contact.

components.name: Concentrated HCl (~12 N)
components.note: About 7 mL of concentrated HCl per litre brings the free base from pH ~10 down to 8.0. With a conjugate-acid pKa of ~6.95, 8.2% of a 1 M solution is protonated at pH 8.0 = 0.082 mol = ~6.8 mL of 12 N acid. Titrate to the meter.
components.hazard: Corrosive and fuming.

recipes.solvent: deionized water, or directly into the running buffer (e.g. 50 mM sodium phosphate + 300 mM NaCl) so the eluent matches the column buffer
recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl (to lower) / NaOH (to raise)
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter (also removes particulates before loading on a column)
recipes.storage: 4 C, protected from light
recipes.shelfLife: 1-3 months; discard when it yellows
recipes.hazards: Imidazole is a suspected reproductive toxin. Wear gloves, avoid inhaling the dust, and weigh in a hood. Titration uses concentrated HCl, which is corrosive and fuming.
notes: Imidazole free base, C3H4N2, 68.08 (PubChem CID 795). No salt or hydrate form is implied here.
MATH CHECK: 1 M x 68.08 = 68.08 g/L, and the calculated HCl requirement (~6.8 mL of 12 N per litre) matches the stated ~7 mL.
Imidazole free base dissolves to about pH 10 and MUST be titrated down. Preparing 'pH 8.0 imidazole' without checking the meter is a common way to strip nickel off a column with an alkaline eluent.
Buy low-absorbance grade. Cheap imidazole absorbs strongly at 280 nm and ruins A280 quantification and chromatograms.
Make the stock up IN the purification buffer (phosphate or Tris plus NaCl) rather than water, so elution does not dilute the salt and drop the protein out of solution.
The conjugate-acid pKa is ~6.95, so at pH 8.0 imidazole contributes little buffering of its own - the phosphate or Tris in the running buffer does the work. That is expected.
Typical usage: 10-20 mM in binding/wash buffer, 250-500 mM to elute.
Imidazole slowly strips Ni2+ from resin at high concentration; do not store columns in imidazole.

sources.title: PubChem CID 795 - Imidazole (68.08, C3H4N2; conjugate-acid pKa ~7)
QIAGEN - Ni-NTA Superflow cartridge handbook (imidazole binding/wash/elution concentrations)
Cytiva/GE Healthcare - His SpinTrap instructions (imidazole stock titrated with HCl; high-purity imidazole for low A280)

recipes.name: 80% (v/v) Glycerol
recipes.summary: Sterile cryoprotectant stock for making bacterial glycerol stocks and for enzyme storage buffers.

components.name: Glycerol
components.note: MW 92.09 (C3H8O3), density ~1.261 g/mL at 20 C. 80% v/v = 800 mL per litre = ~1009 g/L = ~11.0 M.

recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min (glycerol is heat-stable)
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: Indefinite if sterile
notes: Glycerol, C3H8O3, 92.09 (PubChem CID 753). This recipe is volumetric, so the MW only matters for molarity conversions.
Neat glycerol is very viscous. Weigh it rather than pipette it where accuracy matters. If pipetting, use a cut tip and let it drain fully.
Dispense the glycerol FIRST, then add water and mix thoroughly. Otherwise it sits as a bottom layer and never mixes - incomplete mixing is the usual reason a 'glycerol stock' fails to protect cells.
Glycerol and water mix with volume contraction, so 800 mL glycerol plus 200 mL water gives slightly under 1 L. For strict v/v, add glycerol then top up to the mark.
For bacterial glycerol stocks aim for 15-25% glycerol final (Addgene): 250 uL of this 80% stock plus 750 uL culture gives 20%. Freeze at -80 C and shake well before freezing.
Addgene's own instruction is the 50% (v/v) format mixed 1:1 with culture for 25% final. Both are standard; 80% is handier when you want a high culture-to-stock ratio.
Glycerol is hygroscopic - an old open bottle of 'anhydrous' glycerol may already be dilute. Keep it capped.
Recover from a stock by scraping frozen material with a sterile loop or tip; do not thaw the whole vial.

sources.title: Addgene - Protocol: How to create a bacterial glycerol stock (15-25% final glycerol; 50% stock mixed 1:1; store at -80 C)
PubChem CID 753 - Glycerol (92.09, C3H8O3, density ~1.26 g/cm3)
Sigma-Aldrich G5516 - Glycerol, molecular biology grade (FW 92.09, density 1.25-1.26 g/mL)

recipes.name: 1 M Glucose
recipes.summary: Sterile carbon-source stock added to media after autoclaving (e.g. SOC, M9, DM).

components.name: D-Glucose, anhydrous
components.form: D-Glucose (dextrose), anhydrous, C6H12O6 (FW 180.16); glucose monohydrate C6H14O7 = 198.17
components.note: 1 M x 180.16 = 180.16 g/L, i.e. 18.02 g per 100 mL. Anhydrous and monohydrate MWs both confirmed on PubChem.

recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter preferred. A dilute glucose stock (e.g. 10% w/v) can be autoclaved ON ITS OWN - never together with peptone, phosphate or agar.
recipes.storage: Room temperature or 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: 3-6 months; discard at the first sign of turbidity or fungal growth
notes: D-glucose anhydrous 180.16 (PubChem CID 5793) vs glucose monohydrate 198.17 (PubChem CID 21909914) - a 10% error if confused. Most molecular-biology grade dextrose is anhydrous; check the label.
MATH CHECK: 1 M x 180.16 = 180.16 g/L. For reference, 20% (w/v) = 200 g/L = 1.11 M and 10% (w/v) = 100 g/L = 0.555 M.

STERILIZATION NUANCE : filtering is the safest route for concentrated stocks, but separately autoclaving a dilute glucose stock is standard practice - Barrick Lab autoclaves 10% (w/v) glucose on its own and adds it after the medium has cooled. The rule that matters is never to autoclave glucose together with a nitrogen source, phosphate or agar: Maillard and caramelization products inhibit growth.

Barrick Lab: for SOC, make SOB to 960 mL, autoclave, cool to ~50 C, then add 40 mL of sterile 10% (w/v) glucose - about 0.4% w/v, i.e. ~20 mM final. Using this 1 M stock instead, add 20 mL per litre for the same 20 mM.
1 M is well below saturation - D-glucose dissolves to roughly 900 g/L (~5 M) at 25 C, so 1 M is only about 20% of saturation. It still dissolves slowly; stir and warm gently to 37 C if needed.
Glucose solutions are excellent growth media in their own right; anything that enters the bottle will bloom. Aliquot, and keep the working bottle small.
Glucose plus phosphate autoclaved together also produces inhibitory compounds, which is why minimal-glucose plates are made from three separately autoclaved parts (salts, agar, sugar).

sources.title: PubChem CID 5793 - D-Glucose, anhydrous (180.16, C6H12O6)
PubChem CID 21909914 - D-Glucose monohydrate (198.17, C6H14O7)
Barrick Lab - Media and buffer recipes (separately autoclaved 10% w/v glucose stock; SOC at ~20 mM glucose; do not autoclave sugar with agar or phosphate)
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - SOC medium recipe (sterile 1 M glucose added after autoclaving)

recipes.name: 100 mM PMSF (in isopropanol)
recipes.summary: Irreversible serine-protease inhibitor stock added to lysis buffers immediately before use.

components.name: Phenylmethylsulfonyl fluoride
components.note: 0.1 M x 174.19 = 17.42 g/L = 17.4 mg per mL of isopropanol. MW confirmed against PubChem.
components.hazard: ACUTELY TOXIC - a potent cholinesterase inhibitor. Corrosive; fatal if inhaled or absorbed.

recipes.sterilization: none (organic solvent stock)
recipes.storage: 20 C in tightly sealed aliquots
recipes.shelfLife: Up to 1 year at -20 C in isopropanol; hours at most once diluted into aqueous buffer
recipes.hazards: PMSF is highly toxic - it inhibits acetylcholinesterase. The dry powder is a severe respiratory and contact hazard. Always weigh in a fume hood; wear nitrile gloves and eye protection. Do not weigh it on an open balance.
notes: PMSF, C7H7FO2S, 174.19 (PubChem CID 4784), CAS 329-98-6. No salt or hydrate form.
MATH CHECK: 0.1 M x 174.19 = 17.42 g/L, i.e. 17.4 mg/mL - the standard figure.
SOLVENT IS THE POINT: PMSF must be dissolved in an anhydrous organic solvent (isopropanol, ethanol or 1-propanol). It hydrolyzes rapidly in water and an aqueous stock is dead within hours.

STABILITY: the half-life of dilute (micromolar) aqueous PMSF is on the order of 30-40 min at pH 8.0, and inactivation accelerates at higher pH and temperature. Consequences: add PMSF to buffer IMMEDIATELY before use, never store PMSF-containing aqueous buffers, and re-dose during long preparations.
Typical working concentration 0.1-1 mM (dilute 1:1000 to 1:100). Keep the carried-over isopropanol below about 1% v/v; more can denature some proteins.
The cold isopropanol stock may cloud when it hits cold aqueous buffer - add it dropwise with vigorous stirring.
PMSF does not inhibit cysteine, aspartic or metalloproteases. Use a cocktail (or EDTA plus leupeptin and pepstatin) for broad coverage.
DISPOSAL: PMSF is destroyed by alkali. Aqueous waste taken above pH ~8.6 and left several hours at room temperature is largely inactivated; still dispose of it as chemical waste per local rules.
AEBSF (Pefabloc SC) is a less toxic, aqueous-stable substitute worth considering.
OPEN SOURCE GAP: the quantitative aqueous half-life figure is published in subscription protocol collections and vendor sheets; MW, formula and hazard classification are open on PubChem.

sources.title: PubChem CID 4784 - Phenylmethylsulfonyl fluoride (174.19, C7H7FO2S, CAS 329-98-6; hazard classification)
Sigma-Aldrich / Roche - PMSF product page and safety data (FW 174.19, CAS 329-98-6)
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - PMSF recipe (17.4 mg/mL isopropanol; aqueous half-life at pH 8.0)

recipes.name: beta-Mercaptoethanol (BME) - neat reagent and 1 M working stock
recipes.summary: Reducing agent supplied as a neat ~14.3 M liquid; handling rules and dilution to a 1 M aqueous working stock.

components.name: 2-Mercaptoethanol (neat liquid)
components.note: Neat BME is ~14.3 M (FW 78.14, density 1.114 g/mL at 25 C). 1000 / 14.3 = 69.9 mL neat BME per litre of water for a 1 M working stock.
components.hazard: Toxic if inhaled or swallowed; corrosive; overwhelming stench. FUME HOOD ONLY.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (bring to final volume) - prepare and cap inside a fume hood
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter if sterility is needed - do NOT autoclave
recipes.storage: Neat reagent: 4 C, tightly sealed, in a ventilated cabinet with secondary containment. 1 M aqueous working stock: 4 C, use within weeks.
recipes.shelfLife: Neat: 1-2 years (respect the vendor expiry - it oxidizes). Aqueous 1 M: 2-4 weeks at 4 C; make fresh for critical work.
recipes.hazards: HIGH HAZARD. 2-Mercaptoethanol is acutely toxic by inhalation, ingestion and skin absorption, and is corrosive. The odour is extreme and persistent; a single open handling on the bench can clear a floor. Always open the bottle, pipette and cap inside a working fume hood. Wear nitrile gloves (double-glove - BME permeates thin gloves) and eye protection.
notes: 2-mercaptoethanol, C2H6OS, 78.14 (PubChem CID 1567).
MATH CHECK: 1.114 g/mL / 78.14 g/mol = 14.26 mol/L, so ~14.3 M for the neat reagent (~14.1 M at 99% purity). Use 14.3 M for C1V1 dilutions from the neat bottle; 69.9 mL per litre gives 1 M.
Common working concentrations: 1-10 mM in protein buffers (dilute neat 1:14300 to 1:1430); 5% (v/v) in Laemmli SDS-PAGE sample buffer, which is ~0.7 M; 50-100 mM for full disulfide reduction.

DECONTAMINATION: keep a beaker of dilute bleach in the hood and rinse tips and used tubes into it before disposal - oxidizing the thiol kills the smell. Never put BME-wetted tips in an open bench waste container.
Dispose of BME waste as hazardous chemical waste in a sealed container, in the hood. Do not pour it down the drain.
BME is volatile and air-oxidizing; buffers containing it lose reducing power over hours. Add it just before use.

DTT or TCEP are preferable where the smell or toxicity is a problem; TCEP is odourless, non-volatile and stable in aqueous buffer. BME remains standard in SDS-PAGE sample buffer and some enzyme buffers.
The 1 M aqueous working stock exists so routine dilutions can be done without repeatedly opening the neat bottle - but the neat bottle remains the reference standard.

sources.title: PubChem CID 1567 - 2-Mercaptoethanol (78.14, C2H6OS; properties and hazard classification)
Sigma-Aldrich M3148 - 2-Mercaptoethanol (FW 78.13, density 1.114 g/mL, ~14.3 M)

recipes.name: 3 M Sodium acetate, pH 5.2
recipes.summary: Standard salt for ethanol/isopropanol precipitation of DNA and RNA.

components.name: Sodium acetate trihydrate
components.form: CH3COONa * 3H2O, C2H9NaO5 (FW 136.08) - NOT anhydrous sodium acetate (FW 82.03)
components.note: 3 M x 136.08 = 408.24 g/L using the trihydrate; the same 3 M from anhydrous salt would be 246.09 g/L. Both MWs confirmed on PubChem.

components.name: Glacial acetic acid
components.note: Roughly 40-60 mL per litre - a substantial addition, not a dropwise one. Glacial acetic acid is ~17.4 M. Ideal Henderson-Hasselbalch (pKa 4.76) predicts ~62 mL/L; the apparent pKa drops at 3 M ionic strength, which lowers the real requirement toward ~40 mL/L. Titrate to the meter.
components.hazard: Corrosive and pungent - use a fume hood, wear eye protection.

recipes.adjustPHWith: glacial acetic acid
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min (or 0.22 um filter)
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: Indefinite if sterile
recipes.hazards: Prepared with glacial acetic acid, which is corrosive with a strong vapour. Use a fume hood.
notes: Sodium acetate trihydrate, C2H9NaO5, 136.08 (PubChem CID 23665404) vs anhydrous sodium acetate 82.03 (PubChem CID 517045) - a 1.66x error if confused. The stated form matches the stated MW.
MATH CHECK: 3 mol/L x 136.08 g/mol = 408.2 g/L. That is a lot of solid for a litre; dissolve in ~700 mL and top up.

Standard use: add 1/10 volume of 3 M sodium acetate, then 2-2.5 volumes of ethanol, to precipitate nucleic acids. Addgene's generic DNA purification protocol specifies the same 1/10 volume with 2-2.5 volumes of 95-100% ethanol.
pH VARIANTS: pH 5.2 is the long-standing convention for DNA; Addgene specifies pH 4.8 for the same step; pH 5.5 is sometimes used for RNA. All three work - follow the protocol you are running.
Chilling at -20 C overnight or -80 C for ~30 min improves recovery of small or dilute samples.
Where sodium must be avoided, substitute ammonium acetate (but note ammonium ions inhibit T4 polynucleotide kinase) or potassium acetate.

OPEN SOURCE GAP: the acetic acid volume needed to reach pH 5.2 in a 3 M acetate solution is not tabulated in an open source; the range here is calculated from the openly published acetic acid pKa and density, with an explicit ionic-strength caveat.

sources.title: PubChem CID 23665404 - Sodium acetate trihydrate (136.08, C2H9NaO5)
PubChem CID 517045 - Sodium acetate, anhydrous (82.03, C2H3NaO2)
Addgene - Protocol: Purify plasmid DNA (1/10 volume 3 M Na-acetate plus 2-2.5 volumes ethanol; cold incubation)
Sigma-Aldrich S7899 - Sodium acetate trihydrate, molecular biology grade (FW 136.08)

recipes.name: 0.1 M CaCl2 Competent Cell Buffer
recipes.summary: Ice-cold calcium chloride buffer for preparing chemically competent E. coli by the classical CaCl2 heat-shock method.

components.name: Calcium chloride dihydrate
components.note: 0.1 M x 147.01 = 14.70 g/L. If using ANHYDROUS CaCl2 use MW 110.98 (11.10 g/L); if using the hexahydrate CaCl2*6H2O use MW 219.07 (21.91 g/L). The dihydrate is the most commonly stocked form. MWs confirmed against PubChem CID 6093260 (dihydrate), 5284359 (anhydrous), 6093252 (hexahydrate).

components.name: Glycerol
components.note: OMIT for the two ice-cold wash steps. Include ONLY in the final resuspension buffer if you intend to freeze aliquots at -80 C. Freshly-made (unfrozen) competent cells need no glycerol.

recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15 min
recipes.storage: Sterile buffer at 4 C for up to 6 months; must be chilled to 0-4 C before use
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months at 4 C
recipes.hazards: CaCl2 dissolution is exothermic; the solid is an eye and respiratory irritant. Low overall risk.
notes: Typical yield: 10^5 - 10^7 cfu/ug pUC19 - lower than the Inoue method but far simpler and adequate for routine subcloning and re-transformation of intact plasmid.
Keep everything ice-cold from the first spin onward. Grow cells to OD600 0.35-0.5, chill on ice 20-30 min, pellet at ~3000 x g for 10 min at 4 C.
Wash the pellet in 0.5 culture-volume of ice-cold buffer, re-pellet, then resuspend in 1/25 to 1/50 culture volume.
Competence increases markedly if cells are left in CaCl2 on ice for 12-24 h before use or freezing.
A widely used variant adds 70 mM CaCl2 + 20 mM MgCl2 (from MgCl2*6H2O, MW 203.30), or pre-washes with 0.1 M MgCl2 before the CaCl2 step; both give modestly higher efficiency.
0.1 M CaCl2*2H2O at MW 147.01 = 14.70 g/L, which is the mass every published version of this buffer calls for. Salt form and number agree.

recipes.name: TSS (Transformation and Storage Solution)
recipes.summary: Single-solution buffer that makes E. coli competent and doubles as the cryostorage medium - cells are made competent and frozen in the same tube.

components.name: PEG 8000 (polyethylene glycol, MW 8000)
components.note: PEG 3350 works nearly as well and is a common substitute. Dissolves slowly - stir with gentle warming.

components.name: DMSO (dimethyl sulfoxide)
components.hazard: DMSO is a potent skin-penetration enhancer - it will carry dissolved solutes through gloves and skin. Use fresh, anhydrous, cell-culture-grade DMSO and nitrile gloves.

components.name: Magnesium chloride hexahydrate
components.note: 0.05 M x 203.30 = 10.17 g/L. Anhydrous MgCl2 is MW 95.21 (4.76 g/L) - the hexahydrate is what is normally stocked. Chung et al. 1989 permits 50 mM MgCl2 OR 50 mM MgSO4 (MgSO4*7H2O, MW 246.48). MWs confirmed against PubChem CID 24644 (hexahydrate), 5360315 (anhydrous), 24843 (MgSO4*7H2O).

recipes.solvent: LB (Lysogeny Broth) medium - bring to final volume; LB makes up the remaining ~85% of the solution
recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl / NaOH
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter (do NOT autoclave after adding DMSO)
recipes.storage: 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: Less than 2 weeks at 4 C - make fresh for best efficiency
recipes.hazards: DMSO penetrates skin and carries solutes with it. Handle with nitrile gloves. pH is adjusted with HCl / NaOH — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Grow cells in LB at 37 C to OD600 ~0.5 (about 2-2.5 h), chill on ice 20 min, pellet at low speed (~1500-3000 x g, 5-10 min, 4 C), and resuspend in 1/10 culture volume of ice-cold TSS.
Typical efficiency 10^6 - 10^7 cfu/ug pUC19 - comparable to CaCl2, with the advantage that cells can be snap-frozen in TSS at -80 C directly and retain competence.

Transformation: mix DNA with 100 uL competent cells, hold on ice 30 min, then either heat-shock 42 C for 45-60 s or (per Chung) simply add SOC and outgrow at 37 C - TSS transformations work without heat shock.
Autoclave the LB/PEG/MgCl2 portion first, cool fully, then add DMSO and filter-sterilise. Adding DMSO before autoclaving destroys it.

sources.title: Chung C.T., Niemela S.L., Miller R.H. (1989) PNAS 86:2172-2175 - One-step preparation of competent E. coli: transformation and storage of bacterial cells in the same solution
protocols.io - TSS Competent Cells and Transformation
PubChem (NLM/NCBI) - Magnesium chloride hexahydrate, CID 24644 (molecular weight, formula, hazard classification)

corrections: CORRECTED 2026-07-20: Rice University Bennett Lab wiki URL no longer resolves; replaced with a live protocols.io TSS protocol. Composition re-verified against Chung et al. 1989 (LB + 10% w/v PEG, 5% v/v DMSO, 50 mM Mg2+, pH 6.5) - unchanged.
CORRECTED 2026-07-21: MgSO4*7H2O molecular weight corrected 246.47 -> 246.48 g/mol (PubChem CID 24843).

recipes.name: Inoue Transformation Buffer (TB)
recipes.summary: High-manganese PIPES buffer used to make 'ultracompetent' E. coli (10^8-10^9 cfu/ug) for library construction and difficult ligations.

components.name: Manganese(II) chloride tetrahydrate
components.note: 0.055 M x 197.90 = 10.88 g/L. CRITICAL: anhydrous MnCl2 is MW 125.84 - weighing 10.88 g of the anhydrous salt delivers 0.0865 M, a 57% overdose. Always confirm the hydrate printed on the bottle. MW confirmed against PubChem CID 643989 (tetrahydrate).

components.name: Calcium chloride dihydrate
components.note: 0.015 M x 147.01 = 2.21 g/L. Anhydrous CaCl2 is MW 110.98 (PubChem CID 5284359).

components.name: Potassium chloride
components.note: 0.25 M x 74.55 = 18.64 g/L (protocols usually print 18.65 g).

components.name: PIPES
components.form: PIPES free acid, MW 302.37 - prepare a 0.5 M stock (151.19 g/L), adjust to pH 6.7 with 5 M KOH, then 0.22 um filter
components.note: Add 20 mL of the 0.5 M pH-6.7 stock per litre of buffer. PIPES free acid will not dissolve until the pH is raised toward neutrality - add KOH gradually while stirring.

recipes.adjustPHWith: KOH (pH the 0.5 M PIPES stock to 6.7 BEFORE adding the salts)
recipes.sterilization: 0.45 um filter (Nalgene, pre-rinsed) - do NOT autoclave
recipes.storage: Aliquots at -20 C; thaw and chill to 0 C immediately before use
recipes.shelfLife: At least 1 year at -20 C
recipes.hazards: Manganese(II) chloride is harmful if swallowed and is a suspected reproductive/neurological toxin (CNS effects on chronic exposure). Weigh with gloves; avoid dust. pH is adjusted with KOH (pH the 0.5 M PIPES stock to 6.7 BEFORE adding the salts) — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: DO NOT AUTOCLAVE - the buffer turns brown/orange and precipitates, and competence is lost. Filter-sterilise only.
Order of addition matters: pH the PIPES stock to 6.7 first, then add it to the water, then dissolve the salts. Adding KOH to the complete mix precipitates Mn(OH)2.
Grow cells in SOB at 18-22 C (NOT 37 C) to OD600 0.55 - the low growth temperature is the single most important variable for reaching 10^8-10^9 cfu/ug.

Chill on ice 10 min, pellet at 2500 x g for 10 min at 4 C, resuspend in 80 mL ice-cold Inoue TB per 250 mL culture, re-pellet, resuspend in 20 mL TB, then add DMSO to 7% (v/v) final and snap-freeze in liquid nitrogen.
Discard the buffer if it develops any colour or precipitate.

recipes.name: SOB Medium (Super Optimal Broth)
recipes.summary: Magnesium-rich rich medium used to grow E. coli for competent-cell preparation; base medium for SOC.

components.name: Sodium chloride
components.note: = 0.584 g/L. Many published SOB recipes round this to 0.5 g/L (8.6 mM); either is acceptable.

components.name: Potassium chloride
components.note: = 0.186 g/L. Usually added as 10 mL of a 250 mM KCl stock per litre.

components.name: Magnesium chloride
components.form: 1 M MgCl2 stock from MgCl2*6H2O, MW 203.30 (203.30 g/L); anhydrous MgCl2 is MW 95.21
components.note: ADD AFTER AUTOCLAVING from a separately sterilised stock - autoclaving Mg with the medium causes precipitation.

components.name: Magnesium sulfate
components.form: 1 M MgSO4 stock from MgSO4*7H2O, MW 246.48 (246.48 g/L); anhydrous MgSO4 is MW 120.37
components.note: ADD AFTER AUTOCLAVING. Many labs substitute a single 2 M Mg2+ stock (1 M MgCl2 + 1 M MgSO4) and add 10 mL/L.

recipes.adjustPHWith: NaOH / HCl
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 20 min (base only); add Mg stocks after cooling
recipes.storage: Room temperature or 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months
recipes.hazards: pH is adjusted with NaOH / HCl — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: SOB differs from LB chiefly in its high Mg2+ and low Na+ - the Mg2+ is what supports the very high competence achieved by the Hanahan and Inoue methods.
For competent-cell prep, grow in SOB at 18-22 C with vigorous aeration to OD600 0.5-0.6.
Add 20 mM glucose after autoclaving to convert SOB into SOC.
For plates add 15 g/L agar.

Formulation variants are all in current use and all acceptable: the Hanahan/CSH form used here is 10 mM MgCl2 + 10 mM MgSO4 at pH 7.0; the Barrick Lab wiki version uses a single 20 mM MgSO4 (2.4 g/L anhydrous) and adjusts to pH 7.5. Total Mg2+ is 20 mM either way.

recipes.name: SOC Medium (Super Optimal broth with Catabolite repression)
recipes.summary: Recovery/outgrowth medium added immediately after heat-shock transformation to maximise transformation efficiency.

components.name: Magnesium chloride
components.note: Add after autoclaving.

components.name: Magnesium sulfate
components.note: Add after autoclaving.

components.name: D-Glucose (dextrose)
components.note: 0.02 M x 180.16 = 3.60 g/L, i.e. 0.36% (w/v). Glucose monohydrate is MW 198.17 (3.96 g/L, PubChem CID 22814120). ADD AFTER AUTOCLAVING as a filter-sterilised stock - autoclaving glucose with the amino-acid-rich base causes Maillard browning and loss of transformation efficiency. Conveniently added as 40 mL of sterile 10% (w/v) glucose per 960 mL of SOB.

recipes.adjustPHWith: NaOH / HCl
recipes.sterilization: autoclave the base 121 C, 20 min; add Mg and glucose stocks after cooling, then 0.22 um filter the finished medium
recipes.storage: Room temperature (sterile, sealed) or 4 C; convenient as 1 mL sterile aliquots
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year sealed and sterile; discard any cloudy aliquot
recipes.hazards: pH is adjusted with NaOH / HCl — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Warm to room temperature or 37 C before adding to heat-shocked cells - cold SOC costs efficiency.
Add 250-950 uL SOC per transformation and shake at 37 C, 225 rpm for 1 h before plating.
The 1-h outgrowth is essential for kanamycin, chloramphenicol, tetracycline and gentamicin resistance (these need time to express). For ampicillin/carbenicillin it can be shortened to ~20-30 min.
SOC is simply SOB plus 20 mM glucose - if you keep SOB on hand, add sterile glucose on the day of use.

recipes.name: 50% (v/v) Glycerol Stock Solution for Bacterial Cryostocks
recipes.summary: Sterile glycerol solution mixed 1:1 with an overnight culture to make -80 C bacterial cryostocks.

components.name: Glycerol
components.note: Glycerol MW 92.09, density 1.26 g/mL. Glycerol is extremely viscous - pipette slowly with a cut tip or weigh it (50 mL = 63.0 g). 'Molecular biology grade' / >=99% is standard.
components.hazard: Low hazard. Glycerol is hygroscopic; keep the bottle closed so the concentration does not drift.

recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15 min in a loosely capped screw-cap glass bottle
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year (indefinite if it remains sterile and clear)
recipes.hazards: Low hazard - glycerol is not GHS-classified as hazardous. It is hygroscopic, and freshly autoclaved solution is hot enough to scald. Handle with normal sterile technique.
notes: Standard use: mix 500 uL of dense overnight culture with 500 uL of this 50% solution in a cryovial, vortex, and freeze immediately at -80 C. Final glycerol concentration = 25%.
If your lab prefers 15% final glycerol (also very common and arguably better for long-term viability), make a 30% (v/v) solution instead and mix 1:1, or add 150 uL of 50% glycerol to 350 uL culture.

Commonly used final glycerol concentrations span roughly 10-25% (v/v): Addgene's protocol lands at 25% from a 1:1 mix with 50% stock, and the Barrick Lab wiki uses an 80% (v/v) stock added at one volume per five volumes of culture, giving 13.3% final. Below about 10% ice crystals damage cells; above about 30% glycerol itself becomes inhibitory on thaw.
NEVER thaw a cryostock to sample it. Scrape frozen chips off the surface with a sterile tip or loop and return the vial to -80 C immediately - repeated freeze/thaw is the main cause of dead stocks.
80 C stocks last many years. -20 C storage is NOT equivalent; viability falls off within months because -20 C freezers cycle through the glycerol/water eutectic.
Autoclaving is non-negotiable - glycerol is not self-sterilising and unautoclaved stock solution is a classic source of contaminated cryostocks.
