recipes.name: Mammalian Cell Freezing Medium (10% DMSO in FBS)
recipes.summary: Standard cryoprotectant medium for banking adherent and suspension mammalian cell lines in liquid nitrogen.

components.name: DMSO (dimethyl sulfoxide)
components.hazard: DMSO carries dissolved compounds straight through skin and most glove materials. Use fresh, unopened or well-sealed stock (it absorbs water), handle in the hood with nitrile gloves. DMSO is also cytotoxic at room temperature - do not leave cells sitting in it.

recipes.solvent: fetal bovine serum (FBS), heat-inactivated if that is your lab standard - bring to final volume
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter using a DMSO-compatible membrane - PTFE or nylon. Do NOT reach for PES or cellulose acetate, which are the usual cell-culture default but are rated for aqueous/mild-organic use only and are not DMSO-compatible. (At 10% DMSO in serum a PES membrane will usually survive the contact time; PTFE/nylon is the correct choice and is mandatory if you ever filter neat DMSO.) DMSO also attacks polystyrene filter housings and leaches plasticiser - use a polypropylene-housed unit and glass or polypropylene receiving vessels, never polystyrene.
recipes.storage: 4 C, protected from light
recipes.shelfLife: Prepare fresh on the day of freezing where possible; up to 1 month at 4 C
recipes.hazards: DMSO is a powerful skin-penetration enhancer. Never handle it with bare hands near other chemicals.
notes: Alternative that is equally standard: complete growth medium + 20% FBS + 10% DMSO. Use this when FBS is scarce or the line is serum-sensitive.
Serum-free lines: use a commercial serum-free cryomedium, or the base medium + 10% DMSO. Some sensitive lines do better at 5% DMSO (see Frontiers 2021 on Treg products).
Target 1 x 10^6 to 5 x 10^6 cells/mL for routine lines (ATCC/Gibco guidance goes up to 1 x 10^7 for serum-containing medium).
Add the freezing medium ICE-COLD, aliquot fast, and get vials into the controlled-rate cooler within 10 minutes - DMSO is toxic to cells at room temperature.

Cool at 1 C/min (isopropanol 'Mr Frosty' style container or a controlled-rate freezer) to -80 C overnight, then transfer to liquid-nitrogen vapour phase for long-term storage. Cells left at -80 C degrade over months.
Thaw FAST (37 C water bath, ~1-2 min) and dilute out the DMSO immediately in 9-10 volumes of pre-warmed medium; pellet and resuspend in fresh medium.

sources.title: Thermo Fisher / Gibco Cell Culture Basics - Freezing Cells protocol
Abcam - Cryopreservation of mammalian cell lines protocol
ATCC - Animal Cell Culture Guide, cryopreservation section
PubChem (NLM/NCBI) - Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), MW 78.13, CID 679 (molecular weight, formula, hazard classification)

corrections: CORRECTED 2026-07-20: filter membrane guidance previously described PES as DMSO-compatible. PES and cellulose acetate are rated for aqueous/mild-organic use and are not DMSO-compatible; PTFE and nylon are. Guidance rewritten, with a note that PES generally survives dilute (10%) DMSO but must not be used for neat DMSO.

recipes.name: Phosphate-Buffered Saline (PBS), 1x
aka: Dulbecco's PBS without calcium and magnesium
recipes.summary: The default isotonic wash and dilution buffer for cell biology, immunostaining and fixation.

components.name: Disodium hydrogen phosphate
components.note: 0.01 M x 141.96 = 1.42 g/L. CRITICAL hydrate check, all confirmed on PubChem: Na2HPO4 anhydrous 141.96 (CID 24203); Na2HPO4*2H2O 177.99 (CID 6451167); Na2HPO4*7H2O 268.07 (CID 6096963); Na2HPO4*12H2O 358.14 (CID 61456). The Cold Spring Harbor 10x PBS recipe specifies the HEPTAHYDRATE at 25.6 g/L for the 10x stock, which works out to 9.55 mM in the 1x working solution.

components.name: Potassium dihydrogen phosphate
components.form: KH2PO4 (anhydrous - KH2PO4 has no common hydrate)
components.note: 0.0018 M x 136.09 = 0.245 g/L. KH2PO4 has no common hydrate.

recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl / NaOH (usually needs almost nothing)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 20 min, or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: Room temperature (sterile) or 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months; discard if cloudy or if any precipitate or growth appears
recipes.hazards: pH is adjusted with HCl / NaOH (usually needs almost nothing) — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: For a 10x stock, multiply every mass by 10: 80 g NaCl, 2 g KCl, 14.2 g Na2HPO4 (anhydrous), 2.45 g KH2PO4 per litre. Do NOT pH the 10x stock to 7.4 - it will sit near pH 6.8 and comes to 7.4 on dilution. pHing the concentrate then diluting overshoots.
The 10x stock precipitates at 4 C. Store it at room temperature and re-dissolve any crystals by warming before diluting.

This is CALCIUM- and MAGNESIUM-FREE (like DPBS -/-), which is what you want for trypsinisation washes. For DPBS +/+ add 0.9 mM CaCl2 (from CaCl2*2H2O, MW 147.01) and 0.5 mM MgCl2 (from MgCl2*6H2O, MW 203.30) - but never add Ca/Mg to a concentrated stock, they precipitate with phosphate.
PBS has essentially no buffering capacity above pH 8 and is a poor choice for reactions that generate acid.
Autoclaved PBS can drop ~0.2 pH units; re-check if the application is pH-critical.

sources.title: Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - 10X PBS recipe
Sambrook & Russell, Molecular Cloning 3rd ed., Appendix 1 - Buffers and Solutions
Sigma-Aldrich - 10X Phosphate-Buffered Saline preparation
PubChem (NLM/NCBI) - Sodium phosphate, dibasic (anhydrous), CID 24203 (molecular weight, formula, hazard classification)
PubChem (NLM/NCBI) - Monobasic potassium phosphate, CID 516951 (molecular weight, formula, hazard classification)

recipes.name: 4% Paraformaldehyde (PFA) in PBS
recipes.summary: Standard crosslinking fixative for immunofluorescence, immunohistochemistry and cell/tissue morphology preservation.

components.name: Paraformaldehyde
components.form: EM-grade paraformaldehyde powder, (CH2O)n - a polymer that depolymerises to formaldehyde on heating in alkali
components.hazard: IARC Group 1 human carcinogen and respiratory sensitiser. Weigh and dissolve ONLY in a chemical fume hood. Do not use methanol-stabilised formalin as a substitute where methanol interferes (e.g. many phospho-epitopes and GFP).

components.name: Sodium hydroxide
components.note: = 1 mL of 1 M NaOH per 100 mL. This is the depolymerising catalyst - the powder will not dissolve without it. Add before heating.
components.hazard: Solid NaOH and 1 M NaOH are corrosive - they cause deep, initially painless eye and skin burns, and dissolution is strongly exothermic. Add NaOH to water, never the reverse, and wear splash goggles.

components.name: PBS
components.note: = 10 mL of 10x PBS per 100 mL. Add ONLY after the PFA has fully dissolved and the solution has cooled - adding PBS to hot alkaline PFA causes phosphate precipitation.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve PFA in ~half the final volume, then bring to final volume after pH adjustment)
recipes.adjustPHWith: 1 M HCl (roughly 1 mL per 100 mL to come back down from the NaOH)
recipes.sterilization: 0.45 um filter (removes undissolved particulate; this is clarification, not sterilisation)
recipes.storage: Aliquots at -20 C, protected from light; 4 C for no more than about 1 week
recipes.shelfLife: Several months at -20 C. Avoid repeated freeze/thaw - re-polymerisation gives cloudy solution and poor, patchy fixation
recipes.hazards: Formaldehyde is an IARC Group 1 carcinogen (nasopharyngeal cancer; strong evidence for leukemia) and a respiratory/skin sensitiser. Fume hood mandatory for weighing and heating. Nitrile gloves, safety glasses, lab coat. Formaldehyde-contaminated waste must go to hazardous waste - never down the drain. OSHA PEL 0.75 ppm 8-h TWA, STEL 2 ppm. pH is adjusted with 1 M HCl (roughly 1 mL per 100 mL to come back down from the NaOH) — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Procedure for 100 mL: add 4 g PFA to ~50 mL water in a fume hood; add 1 mL 1 M NaOH; stir on a 60 C heat block/stirrer until the milky suspension goes clear (10-60 min). DO NOT EXCEED 60 C.
Then add 10 mL 10x PBS, cool to room temperature, adjust to pH 7.4 with ~1 mL 1 M HCl, bring to 100 mL with water and filter through 0.45 um.
If the solution never fully clears, add NaOH dropwise - but a stubbornly cloudy prep usually means old, oxidised PFA powder. Buy small bottles.
Typical fixation: 10-20 min at room temperature for cultured cells; longer/overnight at 4 C for tissue. Over-fixation masks epitopes and may require antigen retrieval.

Formalin (37% formaldehyde with 10-15% methanol) diluted 1:10 gives '10% neutral buffered formalin' = ~4% formaldehyde and is a legitimate substitute for histology, but the methanol disrupts some epitopes, GFP fluorescence and membrane structure - freshly depolymerised PFA is preferred for immunofluorescence.
8% PFA stock is convenient: make at double strength and dilute 1:1 with 2x buffer or medium for live-cell fixation.

sources.title: Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - Paraformaldehyde Stock Solution (4% in PBS)
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - Paraformaldehyde in PBS
R&D Systems - Protocol for Making a 4% Formaldehyde Solution in PBS
OSHA - Formaldehyde Hazard Recognition
PubChem (NLM/NCBI) - Formaldehyde - IARC Group 1 carcinogen classification and physical data, CID 712 (molecular weight, formula, hazard classification)

corrections: CORRECTED 2026-07-21: Added an explicit corrosivity hazard to the NaOH component; the recipe previously carried a hazard note for the formaldehyde only.

recipes.name: 70% (v/v) Ethanol Disinfectant
recipes.summary: General-purpose surface and glove disinfectant for benches, biosafety cabinets and equipment.

components.name: Ethanol
components.note: IF YOUR STOCK IS 95% (190 proof), change stockConc to 95 - the calculator then gives 736.8 mL of 95% per litre instead of 700 mL. Reagent-alcohol/denatured stock is fine for disinfection and much cheaper.
components.hazard: Highly flammable, flash point ~13 C. Keep away from Bunsen burners and hot plates. Never spray ethanol near an open flame or into a running biosafety cabinet with an ignition source.

recipes.solvent: deionized or distilled water (bring to final volume)
recipes.sterilization: none required for surface use; 0.22 um filter if it will contact sterile material
recipes.storage: Room temperature in a closed, labelled, flammables-rated container away from ignition sources
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year in a sealed container; spray bottles evaporate and drift in concentration - top up or remake every few months
recipes.hazards: Highly flammable liquid and vapour (GHS H225). Store in a flammables cabinet if you keep more than a working quantity. Ethanol vapour accumulates inside a closed biosafety cabinet - do not flame immediately after spraying.
notes: MEASURE BY VOLUME AND BRING TO VOLUME - do not add 300 mL of water to 700 mL of ethanol. Ethanol/water mixing CONTRACTS by ~3-4%, so 700 mL ethanol + 300 mL water settles at roughly 965 mL, and the same 700 mL of ethanol in that smaller volume works out to about 72-73% (v/v), not 70%. Contraction makes the additive shortcut too STRONG, not too weak. It still lands inside the effective 60-90% band, so it disinfects, but it is not the stated concentration - bring to volume in a graduated cylinder.

70% works better than 95% or absolute ethanol because the water is required: it slows evaporation (giving real contact time) and lets alcohol penetrate the cell before proteins coagulate into a protective shell. CDC recommends the 60-90% band.
Allow at least 1 minute of visible wet contact time. Ethanol that flashes off in 5 seconds has not disinfected anything.
NOT sporicidal - ethanol does not kill Bacillus or Clostridioides difficile spores, and is unreliable against non-enveloped viruses. Use fresh 1:10 bleach for spore or blood-borne spills.
Isopropanol at 70% (v/v) is an accepted equivalent for surfaces and is often preferred for electronics; it is a poorer choice where residue matters.

sources.title: CDC - Guideline for Disinfection and Sterilization in Healthcare Facilities: Chemical Disinfectants (Alcohol)
WHO Guidelines on Hand Hygiene in Health Care - alcohol-based formulations
PubChem (NLM/NCBI) - Ethanol - MW 46.07, GHS flammability classification, CID 702 (molecular weight, formula, hazard classification)

corrections: CORRECTED 2026-07-20: the volume-contraction note had the direction of the error backwards. It previously claimed that adding 300 mL water to 700 mL ethanol yields ~68%. Contraction shrinks the final volume to ~965 mL, so the same 700 mL of ethanol is at ~72-73%, i.e. too strong, not too weak. The practical instruction (bring to volume) is unchanged.

recipes.name: 10% Bleach (1:10 Household Bleach) Disinfectant
recipes.summary: Broad-spectrum sporicidal disinfectant for biological spills, liquid waste decontamination and BSL-2 surface decon.

components.name: Sodium hypochlorite (household bleach)
components.form: unscented household bleach, 5.25% sodium hypochlorite (NaOCl, MW 74.44)
components.note: CHECK YOUR BOTTLE. Household bleach is sold at 5-9% NaOCl and concentrated versions at 8.25% are now common. Set stockConc to the label value - 1 part bleach + 9 parts water is only 'a 1:10' by volume, and the resulting ppm depends entirely on the starting strength. At 5.25% stock, a 1:10 dilution = 5250 ppm NaOCl (~5000 ppm available chlorine).
components.hazard: Corrosive to skin, eyes and metal. NEVER mix with acids (releases chlorine gas) or with ammonia/quaternary ammonium cleaners (releases chloramine gas). Never add bleach to formaldehyde- or azide-containing waste.

recipes.solvent: tap or deionized water (bring to final volume)
recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: Opaque or closed container, room temperature, away from light and metal
recipes.shelfLife: MAKE FRESH DAILY. Diluted hypochlorite in a closed opaque container loses 40-50% of its free available chlorine in a month; in an open tray or clear bottle it decays far faster. Undiluted stock bleach itself loses ~20% potency per year.
recipes.hazards: Corrosive (GHS H314). Wear gloves and eye protection. Generates toxic chlorine gas with acids and chloramine with ammonia - never combine waste streams. Corrodes stainless steel: rinse centrifuge rotors, biosafety cabinet surfaces and instruments with water or 70% ethanol after the contact time.
notes: 1:10 (~5000 ppm) is the concentration for spills, blood, high organic load and liquid biological waste. 1:100 (~500 ppm) is adequate for routine low-soil surface wiping.
Contact time matters more than concentration: allow 10 minutes for spills and biohazard decontamination (CDC allows >=1 min for routine surface disinfection of a cleaned surface).
Organic material (serum, blood, agar, culture medium) rapidly quenches free chlorine. Absorb and remove bulk spill material first, then apply fresh bleach.
For liquid culture waste, add bleach to a final ~10% of the total volume and hold >=30 min before drain disposal (check local rules - many institutions prohibit bleach down the drain).

Bleach is one of the few routinely available reagents that reliably kills bacterial spores and non-enveloped viruses, and it destroys nucleic acid - it is the standard choice for decontaminating PCR workspaces (10% bleach, then water, then 70% ethanol).
Do not autoclave bleach-containing waste - it releases chlorine gas and destroys the autoclave chamber.

sources.title: CDC - Guideline for Disinfection and Sterilization in Healthcare Facilities: Chlorine and Chlorine Compounds
CDC - Cleaning and Disinfecting with Bleach (WASH)
CDC/NIH - Biosafety in Microbiological and Biomedical Laboratories (BMBL), 6th ed., Appendix B: Decontamination and Disinfection
PubChem (NLM/NCBI) - Sodium hypochlorite - MW 74.44, GHS corrosivity classification, CID 23665760 (molecular weight, formula, hazard classification)

recipes.name: Gram Stain Reagent 1 - Crystal Violet (Hucker's Modification)
recipes.summary: Primary stain of the Gram stain; ammonium oxalate stabilises the dye and prevents precipitation.

components.name: Crystal violet
components.form: certified crystal violet (hexamethyl pararosaniline chloride), C25H30ClN3, MW 407.98, CI 42555
components.note: 2.0 g per 100 mL final. FDA BAM R32 specifies crystal violet of 90% DYE CONTENT - dye content, not merely the word 'certified', is what the 2.0 g figure assumes, and a lot of different dye content will stain differently. Use a Biological Stain Commission-certified lot. Dissolve in the ethanol portion (Solution A). MW 407.98 confirmed against PubChem CID 11057 (C25H30ClN3).
components.hazard: Crystal violet is a suspected mutagen and stains skin and clothing persistently. Gloves; avoid dust.

components.name: Ammonium oxalate monohydrate
components.note: 0.8 g per 100 mL final. Published Hucker recipes say simply 'ammonium oxalate'; the monohydrate is the normal commercial form and is what the 0.8 g figure assumes. MWs confirmed on PubChem: monohydrate 142.11 (CID 62600), anhydrous 124.10 (CID 14213). Dissolve in the water portion (Solution B).
components.hazard: Oxalate salts are harmful if swallowed (hypocalcaemia, renal oxalate deposition).

components.name: Ethanol, 95% (v/v)
components.note: = 20 mL of 95% ethanol per 100 mL final. This is the Solution A solvent.
components.hazard: Flammable.

recipes.solvent: distilled water (bring to final volume; ~80 mL per 100 mL forms Solution B)
recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: Room temperature in a tightly stoppered amber or opaque bottle
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year (Hucker's formulation is notably stable). Filter before use if any precipitate or metallic sheen develops
recipes.hazards: Crystal violet is a suspected mutagen and an irreversible stain on skin and fabric. Ammonium oxalate is harmful if swallowed. Ethanol is flammable.
notes: Preparation for 100 mL: Solution A = 2.0 g certified crystal violet in 20 mL 95% ethanol. Solution B = 0.8 g ammonium oxalate in 80 mL distilled water. Combine A and B, then LET IT STAND 24 HOURS BEFORE USE and filter through paper.
The 24-h stand is not optional folklore - it lets undissolved dye and oxalate complex fully, and skipping it is a common cause of dye crystals on the slide that look like Gram-positive cocci.
Stain a heat-fixed smear for 1 minute, then rinse gently with water.
The ammonium oxalate is the entire point of Hucker's modification versus a plain aqueous crystal violet - it prevents dye precipitation and gives more consistent Gram-positive retention.
Store away from light; crystal violet photodegrades.

sources.title: FDA Bacteriological Analytical Manual (BAM) R32: Gram Stain
American Society for Microbiology (ASM) - Gram Stain Protocol

Kopeloff N. & Cohen P. (1928) Stain Technology 3(2):64-69 - Further Studies on a Modification of the Gram Stain. NOTE: this paper describes the Kopeloff modification, not Hucker's; it is cited here for context on Gram-stain modifications. The ammonium-oxalate crystal violet used in this recipe originates with Hucker G.J. & Conn H.J., NY State Agric Exp Stn Tech Bull 93 (1923), which is not available online
PubChem (NLM/NCBI) - Crystal violet, CID 11057 (molecular weight, formula, hazard classification)
PubChem (NLM/NCBI) - Ammonium oxalate monohydrate, CID 62600 (molecular weight, formula, hazard classification)

corrections: CORRECTED 2026-07-20: citation title/URL mismatch. The title cited Hucker & Conn 1923 (NY State Agric Exp Stn Tech Bull 93) but the URL resolves to their 1928 Stain Technology paper "Further Studies on a Modification of the Gram Stain". Title corrected to describe the linked article and to keep the 1923 bulletin as the named origin.
CORRECTED 2026-07-21: Added the FDA BAM R32 '90% dye content' specification for the crystal violet lot - the 2 g/100 mL figure assumes it, and dye content varies between suppliers.

CORRECTED 2026-07-21 (audit): AUTHOR MISATTRIBUTION. The 2026-07-20 pass re-titled DOI 10.3109/10520292809115682 as 'Hucker G.J. & Conn H.J. (1928)'. Crossref publisher metadata and Taylor & Francis both give the authors as Nicholas Kopeloff and Peter Cohen, Stain Technology 3(2):64-69 - this is the Kopeloff modification, a different formulation from Hucker's, and it is the origin of the 1:1 acetone-alcohol decolorizer rather than of the ammonium-oxalate crystal violet. Title corrected to the true authors and the Hucker & Conn 1923 bulletin retained as the named origin of this recipe.

recipes.name: Gram Stain Reagent 2 - Gram's Iodine (Mordant)
recipes.summary: Iodine/iodide mordant that forms an insoluble crystal violet-iodine complex inside the cell, trapping the primary stain.

components.name: Iodine
components.note: = 1.0 g per 300 mL, the classical Hucker/BAM proportion. Iodine is essentially insoluble in water alone - it only dissolves because the iodide forms the triiodide ion.
components.hazard: Iodine vapour is corrosive to the respiratory tract and eyes; sublimes at room temperature. Weigh in a fume hood and keep the bottle tightly closed. Stains skin brown.

components.name: Potassium iodide
components.note: = 2.0 g per 300 mL. The 1:2 I2:KI mass ratio is essential - halving the KI leaves undissolved iodine and a weak mordant.

recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: AMBER or opaque tightly-stoppered bottle, room temperature, away from light and metal
recipes.shelfLife: Discard after 30 days once in routine use, or immediately if the deep brown colour fades toward yellow - a pale solution is a failed mordant and is the single most common cause of Gram-positive organisms reading as Gram-negative
recipes.hazards: Iodine is corrosive and sublimes; harmful if inhaled or swallowed. Stains everything brown. Incompatible with ammonia (forms shock-sensitive nitrogen triiodide) and with metals.
notes: Preparation: grind the iodine crystals TOGETHER WITH the KI in a dry mortar first, then add water slowly with continuous grinding until fully dissolved, then bring to volume. Adding water first leaves iodine floating undissolved for days.
Alternative equally valid preparation: dissolve the KI in a small volume of water first, add the iodine to that concentrated iodide solution (it dissolves readily), then dilute to volume.
Apply for 1 minute after the crystal violet rinse, then rinse with water.
Many clinical labs use a stabilised PVP-iodine commercial formulation, which resists the fading problem; the free-iodine recipe above is the classical one and must be monitored for colour.
Never store in a metal-capped bottle - iodine attacks the cap and the corrosion products ruin the stain.

sources.title: FDA Bacteriological Analytical Manual (BAM) R32: Gram Stain
American Society for Microbiology (ASM) - Gram Stain Protocol
Canton City Health District - Gram Stain Reagent Preparation SOP 400-001-01-03-P
PubChem (NLM/NCBI) - Iodine (I2) - MW 253.81, sublimation and hazard data, CID 807 (molecular weight, formula, hazard classification)

recipes.name: Gram Stain Reagent 3 - Decolorizer (95% Ethanol)
recipes.summary: Differentiating step - removes the crystal violet-iodine complex from thin-peptidoglycan Gram-negative cells but not from Gram-positives.

components.name: Ethanol
components.form: ethanol, 95% (v/v) - the FDA BAM standard decolorizer
components.note: If you buy 95% (190 proof) ethanol you can use it neat; set stockConc to 95 and finalConc to 95, or simply dispense the stock. For the faster acetone-alcohol variant see the notes.
components.hazard: Highly flammable.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (bring to final volume; not needed if starting from 95% stock)
recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: Room temperature, tightly closed, away from ignition sources
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year sealed; evaporation from a dropper bottle concentrates it over time
recipes.hazards: Highly flammable (GHS H225). Acetone-containing variants are more volatile still and are also eye irritants. Keep away from flames - Gram staining is often done next to a Bunsen burner used for heat fixing.
notes: THIS IS THE STEP THAT DECIDES THE RESULT. Over-decolorization turns Gram-positives pink; under-decolorization turns Gram-negatives purple. It is timing-critical and thickness-critical, not recipe-critical.
With 95% ethanol: apply drop-by-drop down a tilted slide until the runoff is no longer blue - typically 15-30 seconds - then rinse IMMEDIATELY with water to stop the reaction.

Common faster alternative: acetone-alcohol, equal parts 100% acetone and 95% ethanol (50:50 v/v). This decolorizes in about 5-10 seconds. It is more forgiving than pure acetone and faster than pure ethanol, and is the formulation most clinical labs prefer.
Pure acetone alone is too aggressive - it strips Gram-positives - and is not recommended.

Always run a known Gram-positive and a known Gram-negative control (e.g. Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli) alongside unknowns; the control smear is what tells you your decolorization timing is right.
Thick smears over-retain dye and read falsely Gram-positive. Make thin, evenly spread, fully air-dried smears.
Note that old (>18-24 h) Gram-positive cultures decolorize more readily and can read Gram-variable - always Gram stain fresh growth.

sources.title: FDA Bacteriological Analytical Manual (BAM) R32: Gram Stain
American Society for Microbiology (ASM) - Gram Stain Protocol
Bartholomew J.W. (1962) Stain Technology 37(3):139-155 - Variables influencing results, and the precise definition of steps in Gram staining as a means of standardizing the results obtained
PubChem (NLM/NCBI) - Ethanol - MW 46.07, GHS flammability classification, CID 702 (molecular weight, formula, hazard classification)

corrections: CORRECTED 2026-07-20: the Bartholomew 1962 citation pointed at PMID 52916, which is actually Adams E. (1975) "Studies in gram staining", Stain Technol 50:227-31. Replaced with the correct Bartholomew 1962 article, Stain Technology 37(3):139-155, DOI 10.3109/10520296209117723.

recipes.name: Gram Stain Reagent 4 - Safranin Counterstain (Working Solution)
recipes.summary: Counterstain that renders decolorized (Gram-negative) cells pink-red; Gram-positives remain purple.

components.name: Safranin O
components.form: certified Safranin O, C20H19ClN4, MW 350.84, CI 50240
components.note: = 0.25 g per 100 mL of WORKING solution. Prepared in practice as a stock (2.5 g Safranin O in 100 mL 95% ethanol) diluted 1:10 in water, which lands at exactly this concentration.
components.hazard: Suspected mutagen; stains skin and fabric.

components.name: Ethanol, 95% (v/v)
components.note: = 10 mL of 95% ethanol per 100 mL working solution, carried over from the 1:10 dilution of the alcoholic stock.
components.hazard: Flammable.

recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: Room temperature, tightly stoppered bottle, away from light
recipes.shelfLife: Stock solution 1 year; working solution 6 months. Filter if precipitate appears
recipes.hazards: Safranin O is a suspected mutagen and a persistent stain. Ethanol content makes it flammable.
notes: Two-step preparation as published: STOCK = 2.5 g certified Safranin O dissolved in 100 mL 95% ethanol. WORKING = 10 mL stock + 90 mL distilled water. The component list above already describes the finished working solution, so a calculator scaling to any volume gives the working strength directly.

Apply after the water rinse that follows decolorization, then rinse and blot (do not rub) dry. Published contact times differ: FDA BAM R32 calls for 10-30 s, ASM and most teaching protocols for 30-60 s. Start at 30 s and adjust against a known Gram-negative control.

Some labs use 0.1% basic fuchsin instead of safranin as the counterstain. Basic fuchsin stains faint Gram-negative organisms (Haemophilus, Legionella, Bacteroides, Campylobacter) far better and is preferred in clinical microbiology for those targets.
Do not over-counterstain - excessive safranin can bleed into Gram-positive cells and make them look Gram-variable.

sources.title: FDA Bacteriological Analytical Manual (BAM) R32: Gram Stain
American Society for Microbiology (ASM) - Gram Stain Protocol
Ricca Chemical - Gram Staining (Hucker Modification) reagent formulations
PubChem (NLM/NCBI) - Safranin O (C.I. Basic Red 2) - MW 350.84, CID 2723800 (molecular weight, formula, hazard classification)

corrections: CORRECTED 2026-07-21: Counterstain contact time widened from '30 s to 1 min' to '10-60 s' - FDA BAM R32 specifies 10-30 s while ASM specifies 30-60 s; both are in routine use.

recipes.name: Loeffler's Methylene Blue Stain
recipes.summary: Simple metachromatic stain for bacterial morphology, Corynebacterium metachromatic granules, and as a counterstain in acid-fast and capsule staining.

components.name: Methylene blue (trihydrate)
components.form: methylene blue chloride trihydrate, C16H18ClN3S*3H2O, MW 373.90, CI 52015
components.note: 0.30 g per 130 mL of finished stain. FDA BAM R45 specifies methylene blue of 90% DYE CONTENT for this 0.3 g figure. If your bottle is ANHYDROUS methylene blue (MW 319.85, PubChem CID 6099) use 1.98 g/L instead of 2.31 g/L for the same molar dye content. Most Biological Stain Commission-certified product is the trihydrate, MW 373.90 (PubChem CID 104827).
components.hazard: Harmful if swallowed; stains skin and fabric intensely. Methylene blue is a MAO inhibitor and a serotonin-syndrome risk in clinical contexts - not a bench issue at these amounts, but treat as harmful.

components.name: Ethanol, 95% (v/v)
components.note: = 30 mL of 95% ethanol per 130 mL of finished stain. Dissolve the methylene blue in this ethanol first - it does not dissolve well directly in the dilute KOH.
components.hazard: Flammable.

components.name: Potassium hydroxide
components.note: = 0.01 g per 130 mL, i.e. the 100 mL of 0.01% (w/v) aqueous KOH called for in the classical recipe. This is a very small mass - weigh it as 10 mL of a 0.1% (1 g/L) KOH solution rather than trying to weigh 10 mg.
components.hazard: KOH solid is corrosive; at 0.01% the finished stain is not.

recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: Room temperature, tightly stoppered bottle
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year or more - Loeffler's is very stable and reportedly improves with age as the dye slowly oxidises to azure B, which improves metachromasia
recipes.hazards: Harmful if swallowed. Stains skin, clothing and benchtops permanently. Ethanol content makes it flammable.
notes: Classical preparation: dissolve 0.3 g methylene blue in 30 mL 95% ethanol; separately prepare 100 mL of 0.01% (w/v) aqueous KOH; combine. Final volume 130 mL. The component list above is scaled to any final volume you like.
Use: flood a heat-fixed smear for 1 minute, rinse gently with tap water, blot dry, examine under oil immersion.
Chief clinical use is demonstrating the metachromatic (Babes-Ernst) volutin granules of Corynebacterium diphtheriae, which stain reddish-purple against the blue cell body.
Also standard as the counterstain in the Ziehl-Neelsen / Kinyoun acid-fast stains and for capsule (negative) staining backgrounds.
A plain 1% aqueous methylene blue (no alcohol, no KOH) is a different reagent - fine for simple morphology, but it does NOT give reliable metachromasia. Do not substitute.
Methylene blue at ~0.1% is also the standard stain for haemocytometer yeast viability counts (methylene blue reduction test) - dead yeast stain blue.

sources.title: FDA Bacteriological Analytical Manual (BAM) R45: Methylene Blue Stain (Loeffler's)
Hardy Diagnostics - Methylene Blue, Loeffler's, technical data sheet
Pro-Lab Diagnostics - Loeffler's Methylene Blue instructions for use
PubChem (NLM/NCBI) - Methylene blue trihydrate, CID 104827 (molecular weight, formula, hazard classification)
PubChem (NLM/NCBI) - Methylene blue (anhydrous), CID 6099 (molecular weight, formula, hazard classification)

corrections: CORRECTED 2026-07-21: Added the FDA BAM R45 '90% dye content' specification for the methylene blue lot.

recipes.name: Trypan Blue Solution, 0.4% (w/v)
recipes.summary: Dye-exclusion viability stain for haemocytometer and automated cell counting - dead cells with compromised membranes stain blue.

components.name: Trypan blue
components.form: trypan blue, tetrasodium salt, C34H24N6Na4O14S4, MW 960.81, CAS 72-57-1, CI 23850
components.note: MW 960.81 is the TETRASODIUM SALT, which is what CAS 72-57-1 / Sigma T6146 / T8154 supply. The figure 872.88 that circulates in some recipe tables is the free acid (C34H28N6O14S4) and does not match the tetrasodium formula. The recipe is weighed as % (w/v), so the MW is reference-only and no mass changes.
components.hazard: Trypan blue is a suspected teratogen and possible carcinogen (it is a benzidine-derived azo dye and can release o-tolidine). Wear gloves; avoid weighing the dry powder outside a hood or balance enclosure. Dispose as chemical waste.

components.name: Sodium chloride
components.note: = 8.1 g/L, i.e. 139 mM - the solution is deliberately isotonic so that cells do not lyse or shrink during the count.

components.name: Potassium phosphate, dibasic
components.note: 0.6 g/L. MWs confirmed on PubChem: K2HPO4 anhydrous 174.18 (CID 24450); K2HPO4*3H2O 228.22 (CID 16217523) - if using the trihydrate weigh 0.786 g/L for the same molar amount.

recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter (essential - trypan blue readily forms dye aggregates that look exactly like small dead cells under the microscope)
recipes.storage: Room temperature or 4 C, protected from light
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year; RE-FILTER before use if you see any particulate - dye precipitate is the number-one cause of falsely low viability readings
recipes.hazards: Suspected teratogen and possible carcinogen (benzidine-derived azo dye). Gloves required. Collect as chemical waste; do not pour down the drain.
notes: This is the Sigma T8154 / standard cell-culture formulation: 0.4% trypan blue in 0.81% NaCl and 0.06% dibasic potassium phosphate. A 0.4% solution simply made up in PBS is a widely used and acceptable substitute.

Standard count: mix cell suspension 1:1 with 0.4% trypan blue (i.e. dilution factor 2), load the haemocytometer, and count within 3-5 MINUTES. Trypan blue is itself cytotoxic - viability falls visibly if you leave the mix sitting, and a 15-minute-old mix will read artificially low.
Viability (%) = (unstained cells / total cells) x 100. Cells/mL = (mean count per 1 mm square) x dilution factor x 10^4.
Serum proteins bind trypan blue and cause high background staining. Wash cells into PBS or serum-free medium before counting if the background is heavy.

Dye exclusion measures MEMBRANE INTEGRITY, not metabolic health. Early-apoptotic cells still exclude trypan blue and will be scored as viable. For apoptosis use annexin V/PI; for metabolic viability use MTT/resazurin.
A 0.2% working strength is sometimes used for fragile primary cells to reduce dye toxicity.

recipes.name: MTT Stock Solution, 5 mg/mL
recipes.summary: Tetrazolium stock for the MTT colorimetric cell viability / proliferation / cytotoxicity assay.

components.name: MTT (thiazolyl blue tetrazolium bromide)
components.note: = 5 mg/mL = 12.07 mM. Dissolve by vortexing or brief sonication; it goes into PBS readily but can be slow.
components.hazard: MTT is acutely toxic if swallowed and is mutagenic in bacterial assays (Ames-positive). Formazan-containing waste and DMSO solubilisation waste must go to chemical hazardous waste.

recipes.solvent: sterile PBS, pH 7.4 (phenol-red-free serum-free medium or HBSS also work; avoid phenol red, which absorbs near the read wavelength)
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter AFTER dissolving (removes undissolved dye and sterilises; the stock goes into live cultures)
recipes.storage: 20 C in single-use amber or foil-wrapped aliquots, PROTECTED FROM LIGHT
recipes.shelfLife: At least 6 months at -20 C. At 4 C use within a few days only. Discard if the yellow solution develops any blue/purple tinge - that is spontaneous formazan and it wrecks the assay baseline
recipes.hazards: Acutely toxic if swallowed (GHS H301) and mutagenic in vitro. Handle powder in a hood or enclosed balance; gloves. Collect all MTT and formazan/DMSO waste as hazardous chemical waste.
notes: Working use: add MTT stock to 10% of the culture volume - i.e. 10 uL of 5 mg/mL stock per 100 uL well - giving a final concentration of ~0.5 mg/mL. Incubate 2-4 h at 37 C.

Then remove the medium and dissolve the purple formazan crystals in 100-150 uL DMSO (or acidified isopropanol: 0.04 M HCl in isopropanol, or 10% SDS in 0.01 M HCl for overnight solubilisation without aspirating).
Read absorbance at 570 nm with a reference wavelength of 630-690 nm to subtract plate/debris background.
ALWAYS include medium-only (no cell) blanks - MTT reduces slowly and non-enzymatically in medium, and the blank subtraction matters.
Phenol red absorbs around 560 nm. Use phenol-red-free medium for the MTT incubation, or make sure blanks contain identical medium.

Known interferences: ascorbate, polyphenols, N-acetylcysteine and other reductants reduce MTT chemically and give false 'viability'. Compounds that alter mitochondrial activity without killing cells also distort the readout - MTT reports metabolic activity, not cell number.
MTT formazan is water-insoluble (unlike MTS/XTT/WST-1), which is why the solubilisation step is required; the newer tetrazolium salts avoid it but cost more.

recipes.name: Butterfield's Phosphate-Buffered Dilution Water (Dilution Blanks)
recipes.summary: Standard non-nutritive diluent for 10-fold serial dilutions in viable plate counts and OD-to-cfu calibration.

components.name: Potassium dihydrogen phosphate
components.form: KH2PO4, MW 136.09 - stock is 34.0 g/L (0.25 M), adjusted to pH 7.2 with 1 N NaOH
components.note: = 1.25 mL of the 0.25 M pH-7.2 stock per litre of working dilution water. The working solution is deliberately extremely dilute: it buffers pH without providing nutrients or osmotic shock.

recipes.solvent: distilled or deionized water (bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: 1 N NaOH (pH the 0.25 M STOCK to 7.2, not the working dilution)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15 min after dispensing into bottles/tubes
recipes.storage: Room temperature; refrigerate for long storage
recipes.shelfLife: Stock solution: 1 year at 4 C. Dispensed sterile blanks: 6 months, but discard any blank whose volume has changed by more than 1 mL from evaporation - the dilution factor is the whole point
recipes.hazards: pH is adjusted with 1 N NaOH (pH the 0.25 M STOCK to 7.2, not the working dilution) — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Stock: dissolve 34.0 g KH2PO4 in 500 mL water, adjust to pH 7.2 with 1 N NaOH, bring to 1 litre. Store at 4 C.
Working dilution water: 1.25 mL stock per litre of water. FDA BAM R11 dispenses into bottles at 90 OR 99 +/- 1 mL; 9 +/- 0.2 mL tubes are the common lab-scale equivalent. Autoclave after dispensing.

Standard 10-fold series: 1 mL sample into 9 mL blank = 10^-1; carry 1 mL forward each time. Alternatively 11 mL into 99 mL = 10^-2 per step. VORTEX 25 times through a 30 cm arc (or 7 s vortex) between every step - incomplete mixing is the dominant error in plate counts.
Use a FRESH sterile pipette tip for every transfer. Reusing a tip carries over cells on the outside and inflates the count by up to a full log.
Plate 0.1 mL (spread plate) or 1 mL (pour plate) and count plates bearing 25-250 colonies (FDA BAM) or 30-300 (Standard Methods). cfu/mL = (colony count) / (volume plated in mL x dilution factor).

COMMON ALTERNATIVES, all acceptable: 0.85% (w/v) NaCl saline (8.5 g/L NaCl, MW 58.44); 0.1% (w/v) buffered peptone water; 1x PBS. Butterfield's is the FDA/AOAC reference diluent for food microbiology. Plain distilled water is NOT acceptable - it osmotically lyses cells and undercounts by 20-50% within minutes.

OD-to-cfu rule of thumb for E. coli: OD600 of 1.0 is roughly 8 x 10^8 cfu/mL, but this is strain- and spectrophotometer-path-length-specific and MUST be calibrated for your own instrument by plate count. Keep readings below OD600 0.6 or dilute - the Beer-Lambert relationship goes non-linear above that.
Hold total elapsed time from first dilution to plating to under 20 minutes, and work at room temperature.

recipes.name: P1 / Resuspension buffer (alkaline lysis)
recipes.summary: First alkaline-lysis buffer: resuspends the cell pellet; RNase A degrades host RNA.

components.name: Tris
components.note: Weigh as the free base and titrate down to pH 8.0 with concentrated HCl; the buffer is then 50 mM Tris-Cl. If you weigh Tris-HCl salt instead (MW 157.59, PubChem CID 93573) you must titrate UP with NaOH.

components.name: EDTA
components.note: 0.01 M x 372.24 = 3.72 g/L; usually added from a 0.5 M pH 8.0 stock. EDTA free acid (MW 292.24, CID 6049) is a different solid and will not dissolve below pH 8.

components.name: RNase A
components.note: Add after autoclaving and cooling; 1 mL of 10 mg/mL stock per litre

recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl
recipes.sterilization: autoclave the Tris/EDTA base, cool, then add RNase A
recipes.storage: 2-8 C once RNase A is added
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months at 2-8 C
recipes.hazards: pH is adjusted with HCl — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Composition matches the published QIAGEN Buffer P1: 50 mM Tris-Cl pH 8.0, 10 mM EDTA, 100 ug/mL RNase A.
EDTA chelates divalent cations, which destabilises the outer membrane and inhibits Mg2+-dependent nucleases.
Resuspend the pellet completely; residual clumps are a common cause of low yield.
Without RNase A the prep carries RNA and A260-based quantification reads high.

recipes.name: P2 / Lysis buffer (alkaline lysis)
recipes.summary: Alkaline SDS lysis: denatures chromosomal DNA and protein while plasmid stays annealed.

components.name: Sodium hydroxide (NaOH)
components.hazard: Caustic; causes severe skin burns and eye damage
components.note: 0.2 M x 40.00 = 8.0 g/L; normally diluted from a 10 M stock (20 mL of 10 M per litre)

components.name: SDS
components.hazard: Powder is a respiratory and eye irritant - weigh in a fume hood or use a 20% solution
components.note: 10 g/L; normally diluted from a 20% (w/v) stock (50 mL per litre). PubChem CID 3423265.

recipes.sterilization: none - prepare from filter-sterilised or autoclaved stocks; do not autoclave the finished buffer
recipes.storage: Room temperature, tightly capped
recipes.shelfLife: Prepare fresh; discard after about 1 week
recipes.hazards: NaOH is caustic and causes severe burns; SDS powder is a respiratory irritant - weigh in a hood. Wear eye protection.
notes: Composition matches the published QIAGEN Buffer P2 (200 mM NaOH, 1% w/v SDS) and Addgene Solution II (0.2 N NaOH, 1% SDS).
Make fresh from 10 M NaOH and 20% (w/v) SDS stocks; NaOH takes up atmospheric CO2 and loses titre in an open bottle.
Invert gently 4-6 times - do not vortex, which shears chromosomal DNA into the plasmid fraction.
Do not leave cells in P2 longer than about 5 minutes or the plasmid becomes irreversibly denatured.
SDS crystallises out below roughly 15 C; warm the bottle gently to redissolve before use.

recipes.name: P3 / N3 Neutralisation buffer (3 M potassium acetate, pH 5.5)
recipes.summary: Neutralises the lysate and precipitates SDS, protein and chromosomal DNA as a potassium dodecyl sulfate complex.

components.name: Potassium acetate
components.note: 3.0 M x 98.14 = 294.4 g/L; well below the room-temperature solubility of potassium acetate

components.name: Glacial acetic acid
components.hazard: Corrosive; causes severe burns - dispense in a fume hood
components.note: 11.5 mL per 100 mL of finished buffer in the classic Sambrook/Addgene recipe; confirm the final pH is 5.5 and top up if needed. PubChem CID 176.

recipes.adjustPHWith: glacial acetic acid
recipes.sterilization: autoclave or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: Room temperature; chill on ice before use
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year
recipes.hazards: Glacial acetic acid is corrosive and volatile - use a fume hood and eye protection.
notes: Identical to Alkaline Lysis Solution III in the stock-solutions pack.
Made the classic way (60 mL of 5 M potassium acetate + 11.5 mL glacial acetic acid + 28.5 mL water per 100 mL) the solution is 3 M in potassium but 5 M in acetate.
QIAGEN does not disclose the composition of its Buffer P3 or Buffer N3; 3 M potassium acetate pH 5.5 is the classic open equivalent.
Use ice-cold: chilling gives a tighter precipitate of genomic DNA, protein and potassium dodecyl sulfate.

recipes.name: PB / Binding buffer - silica column (community equivalent)
recipes.summary: Chaotropic buffer that makes DNA bind a silica membrane. Community equivalent of the proprietary kit buffer.

components.name: Guanidine hydrochloride
components.hazard: Harmful if swallowed; irritant. Never mix guanidine waste with bleach.
components.note: 5.0 M x 95.53 = 477.7 g/L; dissolution is strongly endothermic, so the solution goes cold and the volume contracts - top up to the mark after it re-equilibrates

components.name: Isopropanol
components.hazard: Flammable
components.note: PubChem CID 3776

recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter - do not autoclave (guanidine salts decompose on heating)
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months
recipes.hazards: Guanidine hydrochloride is harmful and irritant. NEVER add bleach or acid to guanidine-containing waste: guanidine salts form highly reactive compounds with hypochlorite, releasing chlorine and chloramine gas (and hydrogen cyanide from thiocyanate salts such as guanidinium thiocyanate). Collect guanidine waste separately.
notes: Kit vendors keep their binding buffers confidential; 5 M guanidine hydrochloride with 30% isopropanol is the widely published open equivalent.
Standard silica-column ratio is 5 volumes of binding buffer per 1 volume of PCR reaction; for a miniprep lysate roughly 1 volume is used.
Silica binding needs an acidic-to-neutral pH; if binding is poor, check that the lysate was fully neutralised by P3.
Hydrogen cyanide is released from the thiocyanate salt, not from guanidine hydrochloride - but both must be kept away from bleach.

recipes.name: PE / Wash buffer - silica column (community equivalent)
recipes.summary: Ethanolic wash that strips salt and protein while DNA stays bound to the silica membrane.

components.name: Tris
components.note: 1.21 g/L; dissolve in the water portion and titrate to pH 7.5 with HCl BEFORE adding the ethanol - pH readings in 80% ethanol are meaningless

components.name: Ethanol
components.hazard: Highly flammable
components.note: PubChem CID 702; add ethanol before first use

recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl
recipes.sterilization: none required (80% ethanol is self-preserving)
recipes.storage: Room temperature, tightly capped
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year (ethanol evaporates - re-cap tightly)
recipes.hazards: Highly flammable (80% ethanol) - keep away from ignition sources and store in a flammables cabinet. pH is adjusted with HCl — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: QIAGEN states that the composition of Buffer PE is confidential; 10 mM Tris-Cl pH 7.5 with 80% ethanol is the standard published open equivalent.
The kit concentrate ships ethanol-free - the ethanol must be added before first use.
Spin the empty column an extra 1 min after the wash: carry-over ethanol inhibits ligation, PCR and sequencing.
80% is the practical optimum: less ethanol starts to elute DNA, more leaves salt behind.

recipes.name: EB / Elution buffer (10 mM Tris-Cl, pH 8.5)
recipes.summary: Low-salt, slightly alkaline buffer for eluting DNA from a silica column.

components.name: Tris
components.note: 1.21 g/L, titrated to pH 8.5 with HCl

recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl
recipes.sterilization: autoclave or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year
recipes.hazards: pH is adjusted with HCl — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: QIAGEN publishes Buffer EB as 10 mM Tris-Cl, pH 8.5.
pH 8.5 maximises elution efficiency; unbuffered water (often pH 5-6 after standing) elutes less well and accelerates depurination on storage.
EDTA-free by design so the eluate can go straight into enzymatic reactions - use TE only for long-term storage.
The pH of Tris buffers is temperature dependent (about -0.03 pH units per degree C); calibrate and adjust at the temperature of use.

recipes.name: CTAB Extraction Buffer, 2% (genomic DNA)
recipes.summary: Detergent lysis buffer for genomic DNA from plants, fungi and bacteria; keeps polysaccharides away from the DNA.

components.name: CTAB (hexadecyltrimethylammonium bromide)
components.hazard: Harmful if swallowed; causes skin and serious eye irritation - avoid raising dust
components.note: 20 g/L. PubChem CID 5974, MW 364.45 (C19H42BrN). Dissolves only with warming to about 65 C.

components.name: Tris
components.note: 12.11 g/L, titrated to pH 8.0 with HCl

components.name: EDTA
components.note: 7.44 g/L, or 40 mL of 0.5 M pH 8.0 stock per litre

components.name: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
components.note: 1.4 M x 58.44 = 81.8 g/L; well below saturation (about 360 g/L)

components.name: 2-Mercaptoethanol
components.hazard: Toxic if swallowed or inhaled; strong stench - fume hood only
components.note: 2 mL per litre; PubChem CID 1567. Add fresh immediately before use.

recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl
recipes.sterilization: autoclave or 0.22 um filter, without 2-mercaptoethanol
recipes.storage: Room temperature (some protocols store at -20 C); pre-warm to 65 C before use
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year without 2-mercaptoethanol; use the same day once BME is added
recipes.hazards: 2-Mercaptoethanol is toxic and foul-smelling - handle in a fume hood only. CTAB dust is a skin and eye irritant and is harmful if swallowed. pH is adjusted with HCl — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Composition (2% w/v CTAB, 100 mM Tris pH 8.0, 20 mM EDTA pH 8.0, 1.4 M NaCl, 0.2% v/v 2-mercaptoethanol) matches the open protocols.io 2x CTAB protocol.
The 1.4 M NaCl keeps polysaccharides soluble so they do not co-precipitate with the DNA.
CTAB comes out of solution when cold (its Krafft point is near room temperature) - warm the buffer to 65 C before use and after any frozen storage.
Add 1% (w/v) PVP-40 for tissues high in polyphenols.
2-Mercaptoethanol is added after sterilisation because it is volatile and oxidises.

recipes.summary: Sucrose/Triton lysis buffer for the rapid boiling-prep plasmid method.

components.name: Sucrose
components.note: 80 g/L; PubChem CID 5988, MW 342.30

components.name: Triton X-100
components.hazard: Causes serious eye damage; very toxic to aquatic life - do not pour down the drain
components.note: 50 mL/L; viscous - cut the pipette tip and rinse it out

components.name: EDTA
components.note: 18.6 g/L, or 100 mL of 0.5 M pH 8.0 stock per litre

components.name: Tris
components.note: 6.06 g/L, titrated to pH 8.0 with HCl

recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl

recipes.storage: Room temperature

recipes.hazards: Triton X-100 causes serious eye damage and is very toxic to aquatic organisms - wear eye protection and dispose of as chemical waste. pH is adjusted with HCl — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Standard composition: 8% (w/v) sucrose, 5% (v/v) Triton X-100, 50 mM EDTA, 50 mM Tris-Cl pH 8.0.
Used with lysozyme (roughly 0.5-1 mg/mL added fresh) and a 45-60 s boil; fast but lower purity than column preps.
Boiling denatures chromosomal DNA and protein into a pellet that can be lifted out with a toothpick.
Do not autoclave with lysozyme present; lysozyme is added to an aliquot immediately before use.

recipes.name: Phenol : Chloroform : Isoamyl alcohol, 25:24:1 (pH 8.0)
recipes.summary: Organic extraction mix that strips protein from nucleic acid samples.

components.name: Buffered phenol (pH 8.0)
components.hazard: SEVERE - corrosive, acutely toxic by skin contact; causes deep chemical burns that may be painless at first. PubChem CID 996.
components.note: 25 parts of 25:24:1

components.name: Chloroform
components.hazard: Suspected carcinogen, target-organ toxicant (liver, kidney); volatile - fume hood only. PubChem CID 6212.
components.note: 24 parts of 25:24:1

components.name: Isoamyl alcohol
components.hazard: Flammable, irritant
components.note: 1 part of 25:24:1; PubChem CID 31260

recipes.solvent: none - mix the three components directly
recipes.adjustPHWith: equilibrate against 0.1 M Tris-Cl pH 8.0 until the aqueous layer reads pH 7.8-8.0
recipes.sterilization: none
recipes.storage: 4 C, amber bottle, under a layer of Tris-Cl pH 8.0
recipes.shelfLife: Use while colourless - discard if pink, yellow or brown (oxidised to quinones)
recipes.hazards: SEVERE. Phenol causes deep chemical burns and is absorbed through intact skin, with systemic toxicity; chloroform is a suspected carcinogen and CNS depressant. Fume hood, double nitrile gloves (change immediately on contact), lab coat and face protection. Keep polyethylene glycol 300/400 on hand for skin splashes and know the location of the nearest safety shower. Halogenated organic waste stream.
notes: pH 8.0 keeps DNA in the aqueous phase; acidic phenol at pH 4.5-5.0 drives DNA into the organic/interphase and is used to recover RNA.
Isoamyl alcohol suppresses foaming and sharpens the interface.
Always follow with a chloroform:isoamyl alcohol 24:1 back-extraction to remove residual phenol, which inhibits enzymes and skews A260/A280.
Nitrile gloves give only short-term protection against phenol - change them at the first sign of contact.
Ratio check: 25:24:1 by volume equals 50% : 48% : 2%.

recipes.name: Proteinase K stock, 20 mg/mL
recipes.summary: Broad-specificity serine protease that digests nucleases and protein during extraction.

components.name: Proteinase K
components.hazard: Inhaling the powder can cause respiratory sensitisation - weigh carefully or buy as a solution

components.name: Tris
components.note: 1.21 g/L, titrated to pH 7.5 with HCl

components.name: Calcium chloride (CaCl2)
components.note: 0.001 M x 147.01 = 0.147 g/L. Ca2+ stabilises the enzyme against autolysis. Anhydrous CaCl2 is MW 110.98 (PubChem CID 5284359) - do not swap the numbers.

recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl
recipes.sterilization: do NOT autoclave (it would destroy the enzyme); 0.22 um filter if sterility is needed
recipes.storage: 20 C in single-use aliquots
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year at -20 C
recipes.hazards: Proteinase K powder is a respiratory sensitiser - weigh in a balance enclosure or buy a ready-made solution. pH is adjusted with HCl — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Typical working concentration 50-200 ug/mL, digesting at 50-56 C.
Adding glycerol to 50% (v/v) gives a stock that stays liquid at -20 C and survives repeated sampling; the common commercial storage buffer is 50 mM Tris pH 8.0, 3 mM CaCl2, 50% glycerol.
The enzyme stays active in 0.5-1% SDS, in 2 M urea and in EDTA, which is why it is used with harsh lysis buffers.
Inactivate with PMSF or by heating, and remove it before ligation or PCR - carry-over protease will digest polymerases and ligases.

recipes.name: RNase A stock, 10 mg/mL (DNase-free)
recipes.summary: Removes RNA from plasmid and genomic preps; heated to destroy contaminating DNase.

components.name: RNase A
components.hazard: The dry powder is a potential respiratory allergen - weigh with care

components.name: Tris
components.note: 1.21 g/L, titrated to pH 7.5 with HCl

recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl
recipes.sterilization: heat at 100 C for 15 min to inactivate DNases, then cool slowly to room temperature; do not autoclave
recipes.storage: 20 C in aliquots (working aliquots keep at 4 C)
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year at -20 C
recipes.hazards: RNase A powder is a potential respiratory allergen; weigh it in an enclosure. Contamination risk rather than a chemical hazard. pH is adjusted with HCl — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: The 10 mM Tris-Cl pH 7.5 / 15 mM NaCl formulation is the standard DNase-free RNase A recipe.
The heat step is essential - untreated RNase A preparations carry DNase activity; slow cooling lets RNase A refold while DNases stay denatured.
Working concentrations run from about 2-10 ug/mL for gentle RNA removal up to 100 ug/mL in miniprep P1.
RNase A is extremely persistent on labware; keep dedicated tips, tubes and pipettes away from any RNA work.

QIAGEN Plasmid Buffers - Buffer P1 uses RNase A at 100 ug/mL

recipes.name: Lysozyme stock, 50 mg/mL
recipes.summary: Digests peptidoglycan to lyse Gram-positive cells and stubborn Gram-negative pellets.

components.name: Lysozyme
components.hazard: Hen egg-white lysozyme powder is a recognised respiratory sensitiser and allergen - avoid raising dust

components.name: Tris
components.note: 1.21 g/L, titrated to pH 8.0 with HCl

recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter - do NOT autoclave
recipes.storage: 20 C in aliquots; make fresh where possible
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months at -20 C
recipes.hazards: Lysozyme powder is a respiratory sensitiser - weigh in a balance enclosure and avoid inhaling dust. pH is adjusted with HCl — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Stock solutions of 10-50 mg/mL in 10 mM Tris-Cl pH 8.0 are the standard preparation.
Typical working concentration 1-5 mg/mL, 37 C for 15-30 min.
Adding EDTA to 1-10 mM permeabilises the Gram-negative outer membrane so lysozyme can reach the peptidoglycan.
Activity drops with repeated freeze-thaw; aliquot and use each tube once.

sources.title: Sigma-Aldrich - preparing lysozyme solution from powder (10-50 mg/mL in 10 mM Tris pH 8.0)
Barrick Lab - phenol/chloroform bacterial genome extraction (lysozyme lysis step)

categories.title: Advanced phage work: gradients, precipitation, dialysis & EM staining

recipes.name: CsCl Density Solution, 1.30 g/mL (light step)
recipes.summary: Lightest layer of a discontinuous CsCl step gradient for phage purification; sits above the 1.50 and 1.70 g/mL steps and traps low-density debris.

components.name: Cesium chloride
components.note: 404.0 g CsCl brought to 1 L FINAL volume (not added to 1 L of buffer). For a 100 mL batch: 40.4 g.
components.hazard: CsCl is harmful if swallowed and is an expensive heavy-alkali salt; avoid dust and collect rinse waste rather than discarding down the drain where local rules require.

components.name: Magnesium sulfate
components.note: From 1 M MgSO4 made with MgSO4*7H2O.

recipes.solvent: phage buffer base (10 mM Tris-HCl pH 7.5 + 10 mM MgSO4) - dissolve CsCl in roughly 60% of the final volume, then bring to the final volume in a volumetric cylinder
recipes.adjustPHWith: pH is set by the Tris-HCl stock; CsCl itself is close to pH-neutral
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter (do not autoclave - repeated autoclaving of concentrated CsCl is unnecessary and wastes an expensive salt)
recipes.storage: Room temperature in a tightly capped bottle
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year; re-check density with a refractometer or by weighing before each run
recipes.hazards: Cesium chloride is harmful if swallowed. It is not radioactive (Cs-133 is the stable natural isotope), so the hazard is chemical, not radiological. Concentrated solutions are dense and heavy - support tubes properly. pH is adjusted with pH is set by the Tris-HCl stock; CsCl itself is close to pH-neutral — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: MAKE-UP RULE used across this set: g CsCl per litre of FINAL solution = (target density - 1.000) / 0.7425 x 1000. This reproduces the classic step-gradient table (1.30 -> 404 g/L, 1.40 -> 539 g/L, 1.50 -> 673 g/L, 1.70 -> 943 g/L) to within a fraction of a percent.
The 0.7425 term is (1 - partial specific volume of CsCl in water, ~0.2575 mL/g). It is why you must bring to a final volume rather than adding CsCl to a fixed volume of buffer.

VERIFY every batch: weigh exactly 1.000 mL at 20-25 C on an analytical balance. The mass in grams is the density in g/mL. A refractometer reading works too (see the 1.50 g/mL entry for the conversion).
Typical use: layer heaviest at the bottom. A common three-step stack is 1.70, then 1.50, then 1.30, with the concentrated phage sample layered on top.
Make all steps in the same buffer so the only variable between layers is CsCl concentration.
Buffer choice is flexible - SM buffer, phage buffer or 50 mM Tris pH 7.6 with MgCl2 are all used. Avoid phosphate buffers here if you plan to add Ca2+ later.

sources.title: PubChem CID 24293 - Cesium chloride (formula ClCs, MW 168.36)
King Lab Cookbook (MIT) - CsCl gradient for phage: grams of CsCl per 25/50/100 mL for densities 1.3, 1.4, 1.5 and 1.7
University of Pittsburgh - Density and refractive index table for cesium chloride at 25 C

recipes.name: CsCl Density Solution, 1.50 g/mL (middle step / phage band)
recipes.summary: Middle layer of a CsCl step gradient; most tailed phages band at or just above this density, so the interface above it is where you usually pull the phage.

components.name: Cesium chloride
components.note: 673.4 g CsCl brought to 1 L FINAL volume. For a 100 mL batch: 67.3 g.

recipes.solvent: phage buffer base (10 mM Tris-HCl pH 7.5 + 10 mM MgSO4), brought to final volume
recipes.adjustPHWith: set by the Tris-HCl stock
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: Room temperature, tightly capped
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year; verify density before use
recipes.hazards: Cesium chloride is harmful if swallowed. Not radioactive. Very dense solution - balance ultracentrifuge tubes to the tolerance stated by the rotor manufacturer, not by eye. pH is adjusted with set by the Tris-HCl stock — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: REFRACTOMETER CHECK: for CsCl at 25 C, density (g/mL) is approximately 10.8601 x n - 13.4974, where n is the refractive index. This linear fit reproduces the published table across roughly 1.25-1.90 g/mL. Reference points from that table: n 1.3619 -> 1.298 g/mL; n 1.3807 -> 1.4969; n 1.3992 -> 1.699.
Target refractive index for this solution: about 1.3810.

Many Caudoviricetes phages have buoyant densities in the 1.45-1.55 g/mL range, so they collect at the 1.30/1.50 interface of a step gradient. Densities outside that range do occur - do not assume, look for the band.
Collect the band by side-puncture with a needle, or from the top with a wide-bore tip. Do not vortex, and do not push phage through a narrow needle repeatedly - tails shear.
DIALYSE PROMPTLY. Phage sitting in 4 M CsCl loses infectivity over days, and CsCl carryover ruins downstream enzymology and EM staining.
For an equilibrium (isopycnic) run rather than a step gradient, see the separate isopycnic entry - you dissolve CsCl directly into the phage suspension instead of layering.

sources.title: PubChem CID 24293 - Cesium chloride
King Lab Cookbook (MIT) - CsCl gradient for phage
University of Pittsburgh - Density and refractive index for cesium chloride at 25 C (paired density/RI values)
OpenWetWare - Gill lab: CsCl step gradient for phage K

recipes.name: CsCl Density Solution, 1.70 g/mL (heavy step / cushion)
recipes.summary: Bottom cushion of a CsCl step gradient; almost nothing biological is denser, so it stops phage and DNA-protein aggregates from pelleting onto the tube wall.

components.name: Cesium chloride
components.note: 942.8 g CsCl brought to 1 L FINAL volume. For a 100 mL batch: 94.3 g. This is near the practical solubility ceiling - dissolution takes time and the solution cools as it dissolves.

recipes.solvent: phage buffer base (10 mM Tris-HCl pH 7.5 + 10 mM MgSO4), brought to final volume
recipes.adjustPHWith: set by the Tris-HCl stock
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter (filtering this solution is slow - use a 0.45 um prefilter or a large-area membrane)
recipes.storage: Room temperature, tightly capped; do not refrigerate - CsCl can crystallise out near saturation in the cold
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year; if crystals appear, warm to 30-37 C and redissolve before use, then re-check density
recipes.hazards: Cesium chloride is harmful if swallowed. Not radioactive. Near-saturated CsCl is very dense (1.7 g/mL) - a full ultracentrifuge tube weighs almost twice what water would, so rotor load limits and speed derating may apply. Check the rotor manual. pH is adjusted with set by the Tris-HCl stock — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Target refractive index at 25 C: about 1.3993 (density ~ 10.8601 x n - 13.4974).
MANY ROTORS ARE SPEED-DERATED ABOVE ~1.2 g/mL AVERAGE TUBE DENSITY. Look up the derating table for your rotor before spinning a full CsCl gradient at maximum speed.
This layer is a cushion, not a separation step. It usually only needs to be 1-2 mL in a swinging-bucket tube.
Dissolving takes 10-20 min with stirring; the endothermic dissolution drops the temperature, so let the solution re-equilibrate to room temperature before measuring density.
If you cannot get to 1.70, check that you are bringing to final volume rather than adding to a fixed buffer volume - that is the most common error.

sources.title: PubChem CID 24293 - Cesium chloride
King Lab Cookbook (MIT) - CsCl gradient for phage (1.7 g/mL = 94.29 g per 100 mL)
University of Pittsburgh - Density and refractive index for cesium chloride at 25 C

recipes.name: CsCl Isopycnic (Equilibrium) Gradient - solid CsCl added to phage suspension
recipes.summary: Instead of layering pre-made steps, solid CsCl is dissolved directly into the concentrated phage suspension; the gradient forms itself during a long spin and the phage bands at its own buoyant density.

components.name: Cesium chloride (solid, added to the phage suspension)
components.note: 0.75 g of solid CsCl per 1.0 mL of phage suspension. Because CsCl occupies volume (~0.2575 mL/g), 1.0 mL of suspension plus 0.75 g CsCl becomes ~1.19 mL at a density of about 1.47 g/mL. To hit 1.50 g/mL exactly use 0.81 g per mL of suspension.
components.hazard: Harmful if swallowed; avoid weighing dust.

components.name: Concentrated phage suspension (in SM, phage buffer or TM)
components.note: The suspension IS the solvent here. Titre should be high (>=1e10 PFU/mL is comfortable) or the band will be invisible.

recipes.solvent: the phage suspension itself - no additional water is added
recipes.adjustPHWith: not adjusted; inherited from the phage buffer
recipes.sterilization: none after mixing - the input phage suspension should already be clarified and, if desired, 0.45 um filtered
recipes.storage: Run the gradient immediately; do not store phage in CsCl
recipes.shelfLife: Hours - dialyse the recovered band the same day
recipes.hazards: Cesium chloride is harmful if swallowed and is not radioactive. Long high-speed runs: confirm rotor speed derating for solutions above ~1.2 g/mL average density.
notes: ARITHMETIC: adding m grams of CsCl to 1 mL of aqueous suspension gives density = (1 + m) / (1 + 0.2575 x m). At m = 0.75 this is 1.47 g/mL; at m = 0.81 it is 1.50 g/mL.

Equilibrium gradients need a long spin (typically overnight at high g in a fixed-angle or vertical rotor). Step gradients are faster (2-4 h) but give a less pure product; equilibrium gradients give the cleanest phage.
The phage forms a visible bluish-white opalescent band if the titre is high enough. Mark the tube position before puncturing.
Empty capsids band lighter than full virions - if you see two bands, the upper one is usually DNA-free particles.
MEASURE THE INPUT VOLUME ACCURATELY. The whole calculation depends on knowing the starting suspension volume, and PEG pellets carry over more liquid than you expect.
Adjust the density downwards for phages known to band light and upwards for unusually DNA-dense phages; 1.45-1.55 g/mL covers most tailed phages.
This entry is written as a make-up rule rather than a fixed litre recipe because the volume is set by how much lysate you have.

sources.title: PubChem CID 24293 - Cesium chloride (MW 168.36)
University of Pittsburgh - Density and refractive index for cesium chloride at 25 C (used to check the make-up rule)
protocols.io - Cesium Chloride Gradients

recipes.name: Phage Dialysis Buffer, High-Salt (stage 1, CsCl removal)
recipes.summary: First-stage dialysis buffer for getting CsCl out of a banded phage preparation without osmotically shocking the particles; a second stage into ordinary SM or phage buffer follows.

components.name: Magnesium sulfate heptahydrate
components.note: 1.97 g/L. If using anhydrous MgSO4 (MW 120.37) use 0.96 g/L.

components.name: Tris-HCl, pH 7.5
components.note: 25 mL of 1 M Tris-HCl pH 7.5 per litre

recipes.solvent: deionized water, brought to final volume
recipes.adjustPHWith: set by the 1 M Tris-HCl pH 7.5 stock
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min, or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: 4 C (dialysis is normally run cold)
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months if sterile
recipes.hazards: pH is adjusted with set by the 1 M Tris-HCl pH 7.5 stock — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: WHY HIGH SALT FIRST: a band pulled from a 1.5 g/mL CsCl gradient is at very high ionic strength. Dropping straight into a 100 mM salt buffer is a large osmotic step and can crack capsids of some phages. Dialysing first against ~1 M NaCl and then against normal-strength buffer is gentler.

Practical scheme: dialyse against about 1000 volumes of this buffer for 12-18 h at 4 C, then change into standard SM buffer or phage buffer for a further 3-4 h. Two changes of the second buffer are usual.
Choose a membrane with a molecular weight cut-off far below the particle size - 12-14 kDa cut-off tubing is standard and retains phage completely while letting Cs+ and Cl- through freely.
PRE-TREAT DIALYSIS TUBING if your supplier specifies it (boiling in EDTA/bicarbonate); untreated tubing can carry sulfides and heavy metals.
Alternative to dialysis: buffer exchange on a desalting/gel-filtration column or a 100 kDa centrifugal concentrator. Both are faster but concentrators can lose long-tailed phage to the membrane.
Check for complete CsCl removal by refractive index or conductivity if the downstream step is EM or an enzymatic reaction; residual Cs+ produces stain artefacts on grids.
Do not add gelatin to the stage-1 buffer if the preparation is going onto EM grids - gelatin gives a background film.

sources.title: PubChem CID 5234 - Sodium chloride (MW 58.44)
PubChem CID 24843 - Magnesium sulfate heptahydrate (MW 246.48)
Frontiers in Microbiology - Genome, biology and stability of the Thurquoise phage (CsCl band dialysed first against SM with 1 M NaCl, 18 h at 4 C, then standard SM)

recipes.name: PEG Precipitation, Direct-Addition Variant (10% w/v PEG-8000 / 1 M NaCl final)
recipes.summary: Higher-PEG, higher-salt precipitation performed by dissolving solid PEG-8000 and NaCl straight into the clarified lysate; recovers small and low-titre phages that the milder 4% PEG / 0.5 M NaCl method leaves behind.

components.name: Polyethylene glycol 8000
components.note: 100 g per litre of clarified lysate. PEG-8000 is a polydisperse polymer - it has an average molecular weight, not a single MW, so it is specified by % w/v and never by molarity.
components.hazard: PEG powder is a fine-dust inhalation nuisance; weigh in a hood or behind a shield.

components.name: NaCl
components.note: 58.44 g per litre of lysate, added as solid or from a 5 M stock

recipes.solvent: the clarified phage lysate itself (dissolve NaCl first, then add PEG in portions with slow stirring at 4 C)
recipes.adjustPHWith: not adjusted; the lysate's own medium sets the pH
recipes.sterilization: not applicable - performed on an already-clarified lysate
recipes.storage: Hold the PEG/lysate mix at 4 C during precipitation; do not store long term in PEG
recipes.shelfLife: Precipitation step only - proceed to centrifugation within 24 h
recipes.hazards: PEG dust is an inhalation irritant. Solutions above ~8% PEG are viscous - pipette slowly, and expect significant hold-up volume in tips.
notes: HOW THIS DIFFERS from the 5x PEG/NaCl stock method: there you dilute a premade 20% PEG / 2.5 M NaCl stock 1:5 into lysate to reach 4% PEG / 0.5 M NaCl. Here the final condition is 10% PEG / 1 M NaCl, which is a stronger crowding condition, and no lysate dilution occurs.

WHEN TO USE THE STRONGER CONDITION: small-genome or small-capsid phages, filamentous phages, low-titre lysates (<1e9 PFU/mL), and any prep going onto a CsCl gradient where you want maximum recovery in the smallest volume.
WHEN NOT TO: if you plan to use the pellet directly in an enzymatic reaction. Higher PEG means more PEG carryover, and PEG inhibits restriction enzymes, ligase and PCR.
PEG dissolves slowly and clumps. Sprinkle it in over several minutes with gentle stirring; do not dump it in. Warming to 37 C speeds dissolution but let the mixture return to 4 C before the hold.
Hold 1 h to overnight at 4 C, then centrifuge 10,000-11,000 x g for 20-30 min at 4 C. Longer holds increase yield but also co-precipitate more host protein and DNA.
Drain the tube upside down for several minutes and give a brief second spin to collect and remove residual PEG solution before resuspending.
Resuspend gently in SM, phage buffer or a high-salt TMN-type buffer. Do not vortex tailed phage - flick, or leave at 4 C overnight to resuspend passively.

A common intermediate is 8% PEG-8000 / 1 M NaCl, reached by adding a 25% PEG-8000 / 1 M NaCl stock at a ratio that gives 8% final. All three conditions (4%, 8%, 10%) are in routine use; higher is not automatically better.
PEG-6000 is used interchangeably at the same % w/v for phage. PEG-4000 and below precipitate phage poorly.

sources.title: PubChem CID 5234 - Sodium chloride (MW 58.44)
Barrick Lab protocols - Isolating Phage Genomic DNA (20% PEG-8000 / 2.5 M NaCl precipitation solution; 1 M NaCl / 10 mM Tris pH 7.5 / 0.1 mM EDTA resuspension buffer)
Bio-protocol - Phage purification via PEG precipitation and phage DNA isolation for Illumina sequencing (10% PEG-8000 / 1 M NaCl final)

recipes.name: Uranyl Acetate Negative Stain, 2% (w/v)
recipes.summary: The default negative stain for TEM of phage; gives high contrast and fine grain, but is acidic, radioactive (depleted uranium) and regulated in most institutions.

components.name: Uranyl acetate dihydrate
components.note: 20 g/L, i.e. 0.20 g in 10 mL. As a molarity this is 46.9 mM (MW 426.16 for the dihydrate, UO2(CH3COO)2*2H2O).
components.hazard: RADIOACTIVE AND CHEMICALLY TOXIC. Commercial uranyl acetate is made from depleted uranium with a specific activity of roughly 0.37-0.51 uCi/g (14-19 kBq/g). The dominant risks are inhalation of the powder and ingestion; uranium is a heavy-metal nephrotoxin and an internal alpha emitter.

recipes.adjustPHWith: usually not adjusted. Freshly dissolved uranyl acetate self-buffers to roughly pH 4.2-4.9 depending on concentration. If a slightly higher pH is needed, add 0.1 M KOH DROPWISE - above about pH 5.0 insoluble uranyl hydroxide forms and the stain is ruined.
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um syringe filter immediately before use (filtration removes microcrystals, which are the commonest cause of stain artefacts)
recipes.storage: 4 C in the dark, in a foil-wrapped or amber container, inside a designated radioactive-materials area
recipes.shelfLife: Weeks to a few months; discard as soon as a precipitate or turbidity appears. Re-filter before every session.
recipes.hazards: RADIOACTIVE HEAVY METAL. Uranyl acetate requires a radioactive materials licence or a specific exemption in many jurisdictions - check with your radiation safety office BEFORE ordering. Weigh the powder in a fume hood or glove box; the powder, not the solution, is the main inhalation risk. Wear nitrile gloves, safety glasses and a lab coat, and work in a marked designated area. Collect ALL waste, including grids, blotting papers, pipette tips and rinse water, as combined radioactive and chemical waste - never down the drain. Uranium damages kidneys and bone; alpha emission is only a hazard if the material gets inside the body. Do not use if you are pregnant without consulting your safety office. pH is adjusted with usually not adjusted. Freshly dissolved uranyl acetate self-buffers to roughly pH 4.2-4.9 depending on concentration. If a slightly higher pH is needed, add 0.1 M KOH DROPWISE - above about pH 5.0 insoluble uranyl hydroxide forms and the stain is ruined. — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: TYPICAL USE ON PHAGE: adsorb a few microlitres of phage suspension to a glow-discharged carbon-coated grid for 30-60 s, wick away the excess with filter paper, wash briefly on a drop of water to remove buffer salts, then float on a drop of stain for 20-60 s and wick dry.
WASH THE GRID before staining if the sample is in phosphate or high salt. Phosphate precipitates uranyl ions immediately, and CsCl carryover leaves crystalline debris.

The acidic pH (~4.5) is the main biological drawback: it can collapse osmotically fragile or lipid-containing phages and can contract contractile tails. If your phage looks wrong, try a neutral stain (phosphotungstate or ammonium molybdate) before concluding anything about morphology.
Uranyl ions also act as a mild fixative for nucleic acid and protein, which is why UA-stained specimens often look better preserved than those in neutral stains.
Adding 1 mM EDTA allows the pH to be raised towards neutrality, at the cost of a coarser stain grain.
Do not prepare uranyl acetate in a shared bench area or a shared balance. Powder contamination of a balance is a reportable event in many institutions.
If your institution will not permit uranium, the practical substitutes are 1-2% phosphotungstate at pH 7.0 or 1-2% ammonium molybdate at pH 7.0 (separate entries).

sources.title: PubChem CID 114927 - Uranyl acetate dihydrate (formula C4H12O8U, MW 426.16)
New Jersey Department of Health - Hazardous Substance Fact Sheet: Uranyl acetate
Columbia University Research - Uranyl compound use in laboratories (specific activity, designated-area and waste requirements)
Ted Pella technical note 19481 - Uranium salts for staining (pH of aqueous uranyl acetate 0.5-3%, KOH adjustment limit, EDTA effect)

recipes.name: Uranyl Formate Negative Stain, 0.75% (w/v)
recipes.summary: Finer-grained uranyl stain than uranyl acetate, used when the goal is structural detail (capsid symmetry, tail fibres, single-particle averaging); must be made fresh, kept dark, and carries the same uranium hazards.

components.name: Uranyl formate
components.note: 7.5 g/L. The standard small-batch prep is 37.5 mg in 5 mL. As a molarity this is 20.8 mM (MW 360.06 for anhydrous uranyl formate, UO2(HCOO)2).
components.hazard: RADIOACTIVE AND CHEMICALLY TOXIC - same depleted-uranium hazard class as uranyl acetate. Additionally, the powder is strongly light-sensitive.

components.name: Sodium hydroxide (pH adjustment)
components.note: Roughly 10 uL of 5 M NaOH per 5 mL, added dropwise until the solution darkens slightly to a deeper yellow. This is an endpoint judged by colour, not a fixed volume - published preps use anywhere from a few microlitres to ~6.4 uL of 4 M NaOH per mL.
components.hazard: 5 M NaOH is corrosive; causes severe eye and skin burns.

recipes.solvent: boiling deionized water (the powder does not dissolve well in cold water)
