recipes.adjustPHWith: 5 M NaOH added dropwise. About pH 4.5 is the practical ceiling - above that uranyl formate precipitates.
recipes.sterilization: 0.2 um syringe filter into a foil-wrapped tube
recipes.storage: In the dark, wrapped in foil. Some labs freeze small aliquots at -80 C; the original method specifies making it fresh for each session.
recipes.shelfLife: Same day is safest. Frozen aliquots are used by some labs but stain quality degrades once precipitate appears.
recipes.hazards: RADIOACTIVE HEAVY METAL (depleted uranium), same licensing, designated-area, PPE and combined radioactive/chemical waste requirements as uranyl acetate. Additionally: STRONGLY LIGHT-SENSITIVE, especially to UV - it precipitates on exposure, so work under foil and avoid bench lamps. Preparation involves boiling water plus a uranium salt: do the boiling step in a fume hood with the vessel covered, and never boil the uranyl solution to dryness. pH is adjusted with 5 M NaOH added dropwise. About pH 4.5 is the practical ceiling - above that uranyl formate precipitates. — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: PREPARATION SEQUENCE that labs use: weigh 37.5 mg into a small vessel, add 5 mL of water that has just been brought to a boil, stir in the dark ~5 min, add 5 M NaOH dropwise until the colour deepens slightly, stir a further ~5 min, then filter through 0.2 um into a foil-wrapped tube. Top back up to 5 mL if filtration lost volume.

Why it is preferred for fine structure: the stain grain is smaller than uranyl acetate, which matters when you are averaging particles or resolving capsid facets. For simply confirming that you have a myovirus versus a podovirus, uranyl acetate is easier and cheaper.
pH check with paper strips is adequate - do not put a glass pH electrode into a uranium solution you then have to decontaminate.
A 2% (w/v) uranyl formate variant is also published for single-particle work, neutralised with about 6.4 uL of 4 M NaOH per mL of stain and filtered at 0.22 um.
The same grid-washing rule applies as for uranyl acetate: rinse off phosphate and residual CsCl before staining or you will get precipitate over the whole grid.
Uranyl formate is markedly more expensive and less shelf-stable than uranyl acetate. Do not switch to it unless the extra resolution is actually needed.

sources.title: PubChem CID 22227334 - Uranyl formate (formula C2H2O6U, MW 360.06)
Grigorieff Lab (UMass Chan Medical School) - Preparation of uranyl formate stain (37.5 mg in 5 mL boiling water, 5 M NaOH to a slightly darker yellow, 0.2 um filter, foil-wrapped, pH ceiling ~4.5)

Revisiting sodium phosphotungstate and ammonium molybdate as nonradioactive negative-staining agents for single-particle analysis (2% uranyl formate neutralised with 6.4 uL 4 M NaOH per mL, 0.22 um filtered, aliquots frozen)
New Jersey Department of Health - Hazardous Substance Fact Sheet: uranyl compounds

recipes.name: Phosphotungstate Negative Stain, 2% (w/v), pH 7.0
recipes.summary: Uranium-free negative stain used at neutral pH; the standard choice for osmotically sensitive or lipid-containing phages and for anything that looks distorted in acidic uranyl stains.

components.name: Phosphotungstic acid
components.note: 20 g/L. Formula of the anhydrous acid is H3PW12O40, MW 2880.1. Commercial PTA is normally sold as a hydrate (H3PW12O40*xH2O, x commonly around 24, MW near 3313), so weigh by mass percent and do NOT try to work in molarity unless the exact hydrate is stated on the bottle.
components.hazard: Phosphotungstic acid is a strong acid and a tungsten heavy-metal compound; corrosive to skin and eyes as the solid and as the unneutralised solution.

recipes.solvent: deionized water; dissolve the PTA fully, then titrate to pH 7.0, then bring to final volume
recipes.adjustPHWith: 1 M NaOH or 1 M KOH, added dropwise with stirring to pH 7.0. The volume needed depends on the hydrate and the lot, so titrate with a pH meter rather than measuring a fixed volume. Neutralising the acid converts it to sodium (or potassium) phosphotungstate, which is the form actually used as a stain. 1 M NaOH/KOH is corrosive.
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um syringe filter before use
recipes.storage: 4 C in a closed container; small frozen aliquots (-20 or -80 C) are also used
recipes.shelfLife: Several months at 4 C; discard if it turns blue-grey (that colour change indicates reduction of the tungstate)
recipes.hazards: Phosphotungstic acid solid and the unneutralised solution are corrosive - wear gloves and eye protection and add the acid to water, never the reverse. Tungsten compounds are heavy-metal waste: collect for chemical waste disposal, do not pour down the drain. Once neutralised to pH 7 the solution is much less aggressive but is still heavy-metal waste. It is NOT radioactive - this is the main practical advantage over uranyl stains.
notes: CONCENTRATION AND pH IN THE LITERATURE VARY: 1% at pH 7.2 and 2% at pH 7.0 are both standard, and pH 6.0-7.5 is all in routine use. Higher pH gives lower contrast; lower pH approaches the problems of uranyl stains.

WHY NEUTRAL MATTERS FOR PHAGE: contractile tails (myoviruses) can be triggered to contract by acid stains, and lipid-containing phages (for example Cystoviridae, Tectiviridae) are distorted. If your uranyl-acetate images show all-contracted sheaths, repeat in phosphotungstate before reporting it as a biological observation.
PTA gives lower contrast and a coarser grain than uranyl acetate. Expect to increase exposure/defocus slightly.
PTA penetrates some particles rather than staying strictly outside them, which can produce a mixed positive/negative appearance. This is normal, not a failure.
Never mix phosphotungstate with uranyl solutions - the two form an immediate precipitate.
As with all negative stains, wash phosphate buffer off the grid first. Sodium phosphotungstate itself tolerates residual salt better than uranyl stains do.
Published as sodium phosphotungstate at 2% w/v adjusted to pH 7.0 with sodium hydroxide, filtered and stored frozen in small aliquots.

sources.title: PubChem CID 90478944 - Phosphotungstic acid / tungsten hydroxide oxide phosphate (formula H3O40PW12, MW 2880.1)
Revisiting sodium phosphotungstate and ammonium molybdate as nonradioactive negative-staining agents for single-particle analysis (2% w/v SPT set to pH 7.0 with NaOH; aliquots frozen)
IntechOpen - Negative and positive staining in transmission electron microscopy for virus diagnosis (aqueous phosphotungstic acid ~1%, pH 7.2 as the standard virus stain)
Indiana University Electron Microscopy Center - Negative staining procedures (stain concentrations and pH ranges)

recipes.name: Ammonium Molybdate Negative Stain, 1-2% (w/v), pH 7.0
recipes.summary: Neutral, uranium-free negative stain that is gentler on osmotically fragile particles than either uranyl or phosphotungstate; the trade-off is noticeably lower contrast.

components.name: Ammonium heptamolybdate tetrahydrate
components.note: 20 g/L for a 2% stain, 10 g/L for 1%. Formula (NH4)6Mo7O24*4H2O, MW 1235.9. Both 1% and 2% are in routine use; start at 2% for phage and drop to 1% if the stain is too heavy.
components.hazard: Molybdate salts are harmful if swallowed and irritating to eyes and respiratory tract as a dust.

recipes.adjustPHWith: ammonium hydroxide (preferred, because it introduces no foreign cation) or 1 M NaOH, added dropwise to pH 7.0. The volume required is lot-dependent - titrate with a meter. Ammonium hydroxide releases pungent ammonia vapour, so adjust in a fume hood.
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um syringe filter immediately before use
recipes.storage: 4 C protected from light; small aliquots can be frozen
recipes.shelfLife: Months at 4 C; discard if crystals or a blue tint appear (blue indicates reduced molybdenum)
recipes.hazards: Ammonium molybdate is harmful if swallowed and irritating as a dust; weigh carefully. Molybdenum is a heavy metal - collect solutions as chemical waste. pH adjustment with ammonium hydroxide must be done in a fume hood. NOT radioactive. pH is adjusted with ammonium hydroxide (preferred, because it introduces no foreign cation) or 1 M NaOH, added dropwise to pH 7.0. The volume required is lot-dependent - titrate with a meter. Ammonium hydroxide releases pungent ammonia vapour, so adjust in a fume hood. — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: WHERE THIS ONE WINS: phages with envelopes or internal lipid membranes, and any particle that visibly swells, shrinks or collapses in uranyl acetate. Because the stain is at neutral pH and near-isotonic strengths can be used, osmotic damage is minimal.
WHERE IT LOSES: contrast. Molybdenum (Z = 42) scatters electrons much less than tungsten (Z = 74) or uranium (Z = 92), so images look flat by comparison. Budget for longer exposures.
Some protocols add trehalose or add the stain at 1% rather than 2% to reduce background; test on a spare grid before committing a precious sample.
The published single-particle comparison used 1% (w/v) ammonium molybdate set to pH 7.0 with ammonium hydroxide, stored as frozen aliquots.
Do not mix with uranyl or phosphotungstate stains; heteropolyacid precipitates form.
Filter every time. Molybdate solutions throw microcrystals on standing and those crystals are the usual cause of speckled grids.

sources.title: PubChem CID 16211167 - Ammonium paramolybdate tetrahydrate (formula H32Mo7N6O28, MW 1235.9)
Revisiting sodium phosphotungstate and ammonium molybdate as nonradioactive negative-staining agents for single-particle analysis (1% w/v ammonium molybdate, pH 7.0 with ammonium hydroxide)
Indiana University Electron Microscopy Center - Negative staining procedures (ammonium molybdate 1-2% at pH 7.0 for osmotically sensitive specimens)

recipes.name: T7 Phage Dilution / Extraction Buffer (20 mM Tris, 100 mM NaCl, 6 mM MgSO4)
recipes.summary: The Tris/NaCl/Mg buffer supplied with T7 display systems and used generally for diluting, eluting and storing T7 and other podoviruses; higher ionic strength than SM, lower Mg2+.

components.name: Tris-HCl, pH 8.0
components.note: 20 mL of 1 M Tris-HCl pH 8.0 per litre. Some published versions of the same buffer use pH 7.4 or 7.5 - see notes.

components.name: Magnesium sulfate heptahydrate
components.note: 1.48 g/L. From a 1 M MgSO4 stock: 6 mL per litre.

recipes.solvent: deionized water, brought to final volume
recipes.adjustPHWith: set by the 1 M Tris-HCl stock
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min, or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: Room temperature or 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: 6-12 months; discard if cloudy
recipes.hazards: pH is adjusted with set by the 1 M Tris-HCl stock — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: COMPOSITION VARIANTS: 20 mM Tris-HCl pH 8.0 / 100 mM NaCl / 6 mM MgSO4 is the form distributed with T7 display kits. A closely related version at 20 mM Tris-HCl pH 7.4 / 100 mM NaCl / 20 mM MgSO4 is also published. Both work; check which one your protocol assumes before comparing titres between labs.
Its main use beyond dilution is plate elution: flooding a plate lysate with this buffer and holding at 4 C for 2 h to overnight recovers phage from the top agar.
T7 is a lytic podovirus with a short non-contractile tail. It does not need the Ca2+ that many mycobacteriophages and some other systems require, so there is no CaCl2 in this buffer.

For long-term 4 C storage of a T7 lysate some labs add a drop of chloroform to keep it sterile. Do not do this if the preparation is destined for animal or clinical work, or for any lipid-containing phage.
Add gelatin to 0.01% (w/v) if you are diluting to very low titre - adsorption losses to plastic tubes are the dominant error at 1e2 PFU/mL and below.
Do not autoclave with the Mg2+ present if you also intend to add phosphate later; magnesium phosphate precipitates.

sources.title: PubChem CID 5234 - Sodium chloride (MW 58.44)
PubChem CID 24843 - Magnesium sulfate heptahydrate (MW 246.48)
MIT Synthetic Biology Group (Lu Lab) wiki - Phage display: amplifying T7 and fd-tet (phage extraction buffer 20 mM Tris-HCl pH 8.0, 100 mM NaCl, 6 mM MgSO4)

recipes.name: T4 Phage Buffer (Tris / NaCl / gelatin, magnesium-free)
recipes.summary: Simple magnesium-free Tris/NaCl/gelatin diluent used for T4 stocks and titrations; the absence of divalent cations is deliberate and distinguishes it from SM and from Ca2+-containing phage buffers.

components.name: Tris-HCl, pH 7.6
components.note: 10 mL of 1 M Tris pH 7.6 per litre

components.name: NaCl
components.note: 4 g/L, which is 68.5 mM

components.name: Gelatin
components.note: 0.1 g/L, i.e. 5 mL of a 2% (w/v) gelatin stock per litre. Gelatin coats glass and plastic and prevents phage from adsorbing out of dilute suspensions.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (about 990 mL, then bring to 1 L)
recipes.adjustPHWith: pH balanced to 7.6 measured at 25 C before autoclaving (Tris pH is strongly temperature dependent - always specify the measurement temperature)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min. The source protocol boils the buffer to dissolve the gelatin before pH adjustment and autoclaving.
recipes.storage: Room temperature in sterile aliquots
recipes.shelfLife: 6-12 months; discard any aliquot that becomes cloudy - gelatin supports microbial growth once contaminated
notes: NO MAGNESIUM, NO CALCIUM. T4 adsorption to E. coli does not require added divalent cations under normal plating conditions, and leaving them out avoids precipitates and simplifies downstream chemistry. If you are working with a phage of unknown requirements, use SM or a Ca2+-containing phage buffer instead.
This is a diluent and short-term storage buffer. For archiving T4, add glycerol and freeze at -80 C, or keep a chloroform-protected lysate at 4 C.
The gelatin is doing real work in serial dilutions: without it, titres from the last two or three dilution steps read systematically low because phage stick to tube walls.
Gelatin makes the buffer unsuitable for EM grid preparation (it leaves a background film) and for some protein assays. Use a gelatin-free buffer for those.
T4 is a myovirus with a contractile tail. Vigorous vortexing and repeated freeze-thaw both cause tail sheath contraction and loss of infectivity - mix by inversion.
Composition as published in an open-access T4 physiology study from the Kutter lab: 10 mL 1 M Tris pH 7.6, 4 g NaCl, 0.1 g gelatin, water to 1 L.

sources.title: PubChem CID 5234 - Sodium chloride (MW 58.44)
Frontiers in Microbiology - Bacteriophage T4 infection of stationary phase E. coli (phage buffer: 10 mL 1 M Tris pH 7.6, 4 g NaCl, 0.1 g gelatin, 990 mL water, pH 7.6 at 25 C, autoclaved)
Refardt phage cookbook - media and buffers

recipes.name: Peptone-Saline Diluent (Maximum Recovery Diluent) for host range and EOP
recipes.summary: Buffer-free isotonic peptone/saline diluent for serial dilutions in host-range and efficiency-of-plating work, where Tris or phosphate in the diluent would confound comparisons across bacterial hosts.

components.name: Peptone (casein peptone / tryptone)
components.note: Deliberately low. Enough to protect cells and phage from dilution stress, not enough to support measurable growth over the 1-2 h a dilution series is held.

components.name: NaCl
components.note: 8.5 g/L is 145 mM, close to isotonic for most bacteria. As a molarity: 0.145 M (MW 58.44, anhydrous NaCl).

recipes.adjustPHWith: 1 M NaOH or 1 M HCl to pH 7.0 +/- 0.2 at 25 C (the diluent is intentionally unbuffered, so adjust before autoclaving and do not expect it to hold pH against an acidic sample)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15 min. For dilution blanks, dispense 9.0 mL per tube BEFORE autoclaving and verify the post-autoclave volume - evaporation of a few hundred microlitres shifts every dilution factor in the series.
recipes.storage: Room temperature or 4 C in capped tubes
recipes.shelfLife: Up to about 3 months in sealed tubes; discard tubes whose fill level has visibly dropped or that show any turbidity
recipes.hazards: pH is adjusted with 1 M NaOH or 1 M HCl to pH 7.0 +/- 0.2 at 25 C (the diluent is intentionally unbuffered, so adjust before autoclaving and do not expect it to hold pH against an acidic sample) — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: WHY NOT SM FOR EOP: efficiency of plating compares a phage's titre on a test host against its titre on the reference host. If the diluent contributes Mg2+, Ca2+ or Tris, it can change adsorption on one host and not the other, and you end up measuring the buffer instead of the host range. A near-inert isotonic diluent removes that variable.
Composition is the ISO 6887 dilution fluid used across food and water microbiology: 1.0 g/L peptone plus 8.5 g/L NaCl, pH about 7.0.

COUNTERPOINT: some phages genuinely need divalent cations to adsorb at all. Those cations belong in the top agar or the adsorption mix, not in the diluent, so that every host in the panel gets the same amount.
Add gelatin to 0.01% (w/v) if you are diluting below about 1e3 PFU/mL and losing titre to plasticware. Note this in your methods, since it is a departure from the standard diluent.
HOLD TIMES MATTER. Even 1 g/L peptone will eventually support growth. Plate dilution series within 30 min where possible, and never leave them overnight.
Use the same diluent lot across a whole host-range panel. Peptone lots vary, and a mid-experiment switch shows up as a systematic titre shift.

sources.title: PubChem CID 5234 - Sodium chloride (MW 58.44)
Sigma-Aldrich product datasheet 07233 - Maximum Recovery Diluent (peptone saline): NaCl 8.5 g/L, peptone 1.0 g/L
Liofilchem instructions for use - Maximum Recovery Diluent (ISO 6887 composition and pH)

recipes.name: Sodium Deoxycholate Stock, 10% (w/v)
recipes.summary: Bile-salt detergent stock used to complete lysis of Gram-negative hosts and release trapped phage from cell debris; a milder, non-halogenated alternative to a chloroform kill step, with its own important limitation.

components.name: Sodium deoxycholate
components.note: 100 g/L, i.e. 5 g in 50 mL. As a molarity this is 241 mM (MW 414.6, sodium salt of deoxycholic acid, C24H39NaO4). Typical working concentration in a lysate is 0.1-1% (w/v).
components.hazard: Irritating to eyes, skin and respiratory tract; the powder is light and disperses easily. Weigh in a hood or behind a shield and avoid raising dust.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolves slowly - stir, do not shake, or you will get a column of foam)
recipes.adjustPHWith: NaOH if required. Deoxycholate solutions are alkaline as prepared. KEEP THE STOCK AT pH 8.0-8.5: below roughly pH 7.5 deoxycholic acid comes out of solution as a gel or precipitate, and the effect is worse in dilute solutions than in concentrated ones.
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter (do not autoclave - heat and the alkaline pH promote hydrolysis)
recipes.storage: Room temperature or 4 C, protected from light. Do not refrigerate below 4 C; the stock can gel.
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months; discard if a precipitate forms that does not redissolve on gentle warming
recipes.hazards: Sodium deoxycholate is a skin, eye and respiratory irritant and is harmful if swallowed. It is a strong detergent - avoid skin contact and wear gloves. Powder generates dust readily. Not a carcinogen or a halogenated waste, which is precisely why some labs prefer it to chloroform. pH is adjusted with NaOH if required. Deoxycholate solutions are alkaline as prepared. KEEP THE STOCK AT pH 8.0-8.5: below roughly pH 7.5 deoxycholic acid comes out of solution as a gel or precipitate, and the effect is worse in dilute solutions than in concentrated ones. — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: CRITICAL LIMITATION - THE SAME ONE CHLOROFORM HAS: deoxycholate is a membrane-active detergent, so it inactivates phages that depend on lipid. Envelope- and internal-membrane-containing phages (for example Cystoviridae, Tectiviridae, Corticoviridae) are destroyed. Most tailed phages (Caudoviricetes) tolerate it. ALWAYS titre a small aliquot before and after treatment on a new phage.
Typical use on a lysate: add stock to 0.1-1% (w/v) final, mix gently, hold 15-30 min at room temperature or 37 C, then clarify by centrifugation and/or 0.45 um filtration.

WHY IT HELPS: deoxycholate permeabilises the Gram-negative outer membrane and disperses membrane fragments that otherwise trap phage in the debris pellet. Yields from a PEG precipitation often improve because less phage is lost with the debris.
It is far less effective against Gram-positive hosts, whose thick peptidoglycan is unaffected. Use lysozyme, mutanolysin or a phage lysin for those.

REMOVE IT BEFORE DOWNSTREAM WORK. Residual deoxycholate interferes with protein quantitation (it is the reason DC-type protein assays exist), inhibits some enzymes, and produces heavy background on EM grids. Dialysis, PEG precipitation and gradient purification all remove it.

Deoxycholate precipitates in the presence of divalent cations at high concentration and at acidic pH. If your lysate is in a high-Mg buffer and you see a cloud on adding DOC, that is the detergent coming out, not the phage.
Do not use deoxycholate on preparations intended for phage therapy, animal dosing or any clinical application without a validated removal and residual-testing step.

sources.title: PubChem CID 23668196 - Sodium deoxycholate (formula C24H39NaO4, MW 414.6)
Bonilla N et al., Phage on tap - a quick and efficient protocol for the preparation of bacteriophage laboratory stocks. PeerJ 4:e2261 (2016)
Abcam - Western blot buffers and stock solutions (10% w/v sodium deoxycholate stock, 5 g in 50 mL, protect from light)

recipes.name: Ni-NTA Native Lysis / Binding Buffer, pH 8.0 (10 mM imidazole)
recipes.summary: Standard non-denaturing sodium-phosphate buffer for resuspending E. coli pellets and loading soluble His-tagged protein onto Ni-NTA. The low imidazole background suppresses binding of native histidine-rich host proteins without stripping the 6xHis tag.

components.name: Sodium phosphate monobasic monohydrate
components.note: 6.90 g per litre. Anhydrous NaH2PO4 (MW 119.98, PubChem CID 23672064) may be substituted at 6.00 g/L; the dihydrate (MW 156.01) at 7.80 g/L.

components.name: Sodium chloride
components.note: 17.53 g per litre. High salt suppresses non-specific ionic binding to the resin.

components.name: Imidazole
components.hazard: Reproductive toxicant and corrosive; weigh in a fume hood with gloves.
components.note: 0.68 g per litre. Weigh the free base and re-adjust pH after it dissolves - imidazole is itself a base and pushes the buffer alkaline.

recipes.adjustPHWith: 10 M NaOH (raise) or concentrated HCl (lower); adjust after all solids have dissolved and at the temperature of use
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um membrane filtration. Do not autoclave - autoclaving discolours imidazole and shifts the pH.
recipes.storage: 4 degrees C, tightly capped
recipes.shelfLife: About 1 month at 4 degrees C. Phosphate/imidazole is a usable carbon and nitrogen source for contaminants; discard if cloudy.
recipes.hazards: Imidazole is classified as harmful if swallowed, causes severe skin burns and serious eye damage, and is a suspected reproductive toxicant (may damage the unborn child). Weigh the powder in a fume hood; wear nitrile gloves and eye protection. Ni-NTA resin itself contains nickel salts, which are respiratory/skin sensitisers and carcinogens - do not handle dried resin or nickel powder outside a hood. pH is adjusted with 10 M NaOH (raise) or concentrated HCl (lower); adjust after all solids have dissolved and at the temperature of use — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Standard three-step native imidazole ladder is 10 mM (bind) / 20-40 mM (wash) / 250 mM (elute) at pH 8.0 in this same base buffer, so it is most economical to make one 4 L batch of 50 mM phosphate + 300 mM NaCl pH 8.0 and split it before adding imidazole.
Keep at least 1 pH unit away from the pI of the target protein to prevent precipitation on the column.

Chelators (EDTA, EGTA, citrate) strip nickel off the NTA chelate and must be left out; likewise keep strong reductants low (DTT or TCEP at 1 mM or less, no beta-mercaptoethanol) or the resin will darken and lose capacity.
Add protease inhibitors and lysozyme to the volume actually used for lysis, not to the bulk buffer - see the protease-inhibitor and lysozyme-lysis entries in this pack.
Chill to 4 degrees C before resuspending the cell pellet; work on ice throughout.
The Barrick Lab Pfu-Sso7d preparation uses this exact buffer at 10 mM imidazole for lysis, 40 mM for washing and 250 mM for elution.

sources.title: Barrick Lab - Protocol for harvesting Pfu-Sso7d polymerase (lysis / wash / elution buffer compositions)
Khin NC, Ganguly D, Yee S, Chan KX. Nickel-NTA Protein Purification. protocols.io (2020), doi:10.17504/protocols.io.bihhkb36
PubChem CID 516949 - Sodium Phosphate, Monobasic, Monohydrate
PubChem CID 795 - Imidazole

recipes.name: Ni-NTA Native Wash Buffer, pH 8.0 (20 mM imidazole)
recipes.summary: Same phosphate/salt base as the native binding buffer with the imidazole raised to 20 mM to displace weakly bound contaminants while leaving the 6xHis-tagged target on the resin.

components.name: Sodium phosphate monobasic monohydrate
components.note: 6.90 g per litre.

components.name: Sodium chloride
components.note: 17.53 g per litre.

components.name: Imidazole
components.hazard: Corrosive; suspected reproductive toxicant.
components.note: 1.36 g per litre.

recipes.adjustPHWith: 10 M NaOH or concentrated HCl
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um membrane filtration; do not autoclave
recipes.storage: 4 degrees C
recipes.shelfLife: About 1 month at 4 degrees C; discard if cloudy
recipes.hazards: Imidazole: harmful if swallowed, causes severe skin burns and serious eye damage, suspected reproductive toxicant. Handle powder in a fume hood. pH is adjusted with 10 M NaOH or concentrated HCl — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: 20 mM is the usual starting stringency. If the eluate carries contaminants, step the wash up: the Barrick Pfu-Sso7d preparation washes at 40 mM and a published mini-G protein preparation also washes at 40 mM imidazole.
If the target itself starts appearing in the wash fractions, drop back toward 10-20 mM and instead wash with more column volumes.
Wash three times with roughly two resin-bed volumes each and keep the fractions separately - running W1/W2/W3 on a gel next to the eluates is the quickest way to diagnose a poor purification.
Washing can be monitored by reading A280 of the flow-through until it returns to baseline.
Imidazole absorbs strongly below 250 nm; blank any A280 reading against the matching buffer rather than against water.

sources.title: Khin NC, Ganguly D, Yee S, Chan KX. Nickel-NTA Protein Purification. protocols.io (2020), doi:10.17504/protocols.io.bihhkb36 (wash buffer at 20 mM imidazole)
Barrick Lab - Protocol for harvesting Pfu-Sso7d polymerase (wash at 40 mM imidazole)
HisPur Purification of His-Tagged Proteins. protocols.io, doi:10.17504/protocols.io.bmg7k3zn (wash at 25 mM imidazole, PBS base)
PubChem CID 795 - Imidazole

recipes.name: Ni-NTA Native Elution Buffer, pH 8.0 (250 mM imidazole)
recipes.summary: High-imidazole buffer that competes the 6xHis tag off the nickel chelate and releases folded protein under native conditions.

components.name: Sodium phosphate monobasic monohydrate
components.note: 6.90 g per litre.

components.name: Sodium chloride
components.note: 17.53 g per litre.

components.name: Imidazole
components.hazard: Corrosive; suspected reproductive toxicant.
components.note: 17.02 g per litre. The pH will climb sharply as this dissolves - titrate back to 8.0 with HCl.

recipes.adjustPHWith: Concentrated HCl (the buffer runs alkaline once 250 mM imidazole is in); 10 M NaOH if overshooting
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um membrane filtration; do not autoclave
recipes.storage: 4 degrees C
recipes.shelfLife: About 1 month at 4 degrees C; discard if cloudy or yellowed
recipes.hazards: Imidazole: harmful if swallowed, causes severe skin burns and serious eye damage, suspected reproductive toxicant. At 250 mM this buffer is a concentrated corrosive base-forming solution - gloves and eye protection.
notes: Elute in several small fractions of about one resin-bed volume each rather than one large one; the protein normally concentrates in fractions 1-2.
A 250 mM imidazole background raises the apparent A280. Blank the spectrophotometer on the elution buffer, and dilute eluates at least 1:2 before a Bradford assay.
Imidazole must be removed before most downstream work (activity assays, crystallography, labelling, storage) - dialyse or buffer-exchange into the dialysis buffer in this pack.
Alternative elution regimes: a step gradient of 50/100/250/500 mM improves purity for stubborn contaminants, and a published mini-G protein preparation elutes at 500 mM imidazole.
Do not freeze protein in this buffer without first exchanging it; high imidazole plus freeze-thaw promotes aggregation.

sources.title: Khin NC, Ganguly D, Yee S, Chan KX. Nickel-NTA Protein Purification. protocols.io (2020), doi:10.17504/protocols.io.bihhkb36 (elute buffer at 250 mM imidazole)
Barrick Lab - Protocol for harvesting Pfu-Sso7d polymerase (elution at 250 mM imidazole)
PubChem CID 795 - Imidazole

recipes.name: Ni-NTA Binding Buffer, high-salt with Triton X-100 and glycerol, pH 8.0
recipes.summary: A stronger-stringency native binding/lysis buffer for proteins that carry hitchhikers or aggregate: 500 mM NaCl plus non-ionic detergent breaks hydrophobic and ionic non-specific interactions, and glycerol stabilises the target.

components.name: Sodium phosphate buffer (from monobasic + dibasic sodium phosphate)
components.form: NaH2PO4 - H2O titrated to pH 8.0 with Na2HPO4 (MW 141.96, PubChem CID 24203) or NaOH
components.note: Total phosphate 50 mM. Easiest route: dissolve 6.90 g/L NaH2PO4 - H2O and bring to pH 8.0 with NaOH.

components.name: Sodium chloride
components.note: 29.22 g per litre.

components.name: Triton X-100
components.hazard: Causes serious eye damage; very toxic to aquatic life. Do not pour down the drain.
components.note: Polydisperse octylphenol ethoxylate - no single molecular weight applies, so it is specified by volume percent. PubChem CID 5590 lists the idealised monomer (C16H26O2, 250.38 g/mol) only; the commercial average is roughly 625 g/mol.

components.name: Glycerol
components.note: MW 92.09; anhydrous glycerol, at least 99%.

components.name: Imidazole
components.hazard: Corrosive; suspected reproductive toxicant.
components.note: 0.68 g per litre.

recipes.adjustPHWith: 10 M NaOH or concentrated HCl, set before adding detergent and glycerol
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um membrane filtration (filter slowly - Triton and glycerol are viscous); do not autoclave
recipes.storage: 4 degrees C; let it warm slightly and mix before use because Triton can phase-separate in the cold
recipes.shelfLife: About 1 month at 4 degrees C
recipes.hazards: Imidazole is corrosive and a suspected reproductive toxicant. Triton X-100 causes serious eye damage and is very toxic to aquatic organisms - collect waste for chemical disposal rather than drain disposal. Glycerol is low hazard but makes floors slippery.
notes: Matching wash and elution buffers are identical except for imidazole at 20 mM and 250 mM respectively.
Keep the working pH at least 1 unit away from the pI of the target so it stays soluble on the column.
Non-ionic detergent is compatible with Ni-NTA; ionic detergents such as SDS are not and will strip the resin and denature the protein.
If DTT is needed, cap it at 1 mM - higher concentrations reduce and leach the nickel.
Triton X-100 has a strong absorbance around 280 nm from its phenol ring, so quantify protein by Bradford or BCA rather than by A280 when this buffer is present.
Detergent also interferes with some downstream assays and with crystallisation; remove it during dialysis or on a desalting column.

sources.title: Khin NC, Ganguly D, Yee S, Chan KX. Nickel-NTA Protein Purification. protocols.io (2020), doi:10.17504/protocols.io.bihhkb36 (1X binding / wash / elute buffers with 0.5% Triton X-100 and 10% glycerol)
PubChem CID 5590 - Triton X-100
PubChem CID 753 - Glycerol

recipes.name: Denaturing Ni-NTA Buffer B, 8 M urea, pH 8.0 (solubilisation / binding)
recipes.summary: Chaotropic solubilisation and binding buffer for His-tagged protein trapped in inclusion bodies. The 6xHis tag still binds Ni-NTA when the protein is fully unfolded, so the whole purification can be run in 8 M urea and the protein refolded afterwards.

components.name: Urea
components.hazard: Low acute toxicity, but 8 M solutions slowly generate cyanate that carbamylates lysines and protein N-termini.
components.note: 480.5 g per litre of final solution. Urea dissolves endothermically - the solution gets very cold and takes 20-30 min of stirring; warm gently to no more than 37 degrees C if needed.

components.name: Sodium phosphate monobasic monohydrate
components.note: 13.80 g per litre.

components.name: Tris base
components.note: 1.21 g per litre. Can equally be added as 10 mL/L of 1 M Tris-Cl pH 8.0.

recipes.adjustPHWith: NaOH, immediately before use
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um membrane filtration only. Never autoclave a urea buffer - heat decomposes urea to ammonium and isocyanate.
recipes.storage: Room temperature (urea crystallises out of 8 M solutions at 4 degrees C)
recipes.shelfLife: Make fresh; use within about 1 week. Re-check and re-adjust pH on the day of use.
recipes.hazards: Urea itself is low-hazard, but concentrated urea solutions release cyanate on standing or heating, which carbamylates proteins and blocks N-terminal sequencing and some proteolytic digests. Do not warm above 37 degrees C and do not autoclave. Handle powder to avoid dust inhalation. pH is adjusted with NaOH, immediately before use — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Batch trick that makes the pH series easy: prepare one un-pH-adjusted stock of 100 mM NaH2PO4 + 10 mM Tris + 8 M urea, make it up to only 900 mL per intended litre to leave room for titrant, then split it and titrate aliquots to pH 8.0 (Buffer B), 6.3 (Buffer C), 5.9 (Buffer D) and 4.5 (Buffer E).
The pH of urea buffers drifts with temperature and with standing - measure and correct on the day of use, not when the stock was made.

6 M guanidine hydrochloride (MW 95.53, PubChem CID 5742) in PBS with 10 mM imidazole at pH 7.4 is the common alternative denaturant and gives more complete solubilisation, but guanidinium precipitates with SDS so samples must be exchanged into urea before SDS-PAGE. Guanidine HCl must never be mixed with bleach.
Do not add imidazole to Buffer B if a pH-step elution is planned; imidazole is only used if the denaturing purification is run as an imidazole-gradient instead of a pH-gradient.
Reducing agents and chelators are still forbidden - DTT above about 1 mM and any EDTA will damage the nickel chelate.
Standard urea removal is by stepwise dialysis (8 -> 6 -> 4 -> 2 -> 0 M) so the protein has a chance to refold at each step.

sources.title: Mace K. His-tagged protein purification under denaturing conditions (University of Manchester) - stock buffer 100 mM NaH2PO4 / 10 mM Tris-Cl / 8 M urea with the gram amounts and MWs stated

HisPur Purification of His-Tagged Proteins. protocols.io, doi:10.17504/protocols.io.bmg7k3zn (inclusion bodies solubilised in 8 M urea or 6 M guanidine; denaturant must be present throughout; guanidine samples must be dialysed into urea before SDS-PAGE)
PubChem CID 1176 - Urea
PubChem CID 6503 - Tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane

recipes.name: Denaturing Ni-NTA Buffer C, 8 M urea, pH 6.3 (wash)
recipes.summary: Same 8 M urea base as Buffer B, titrated to pH 6.3. Mild protonation of the histidine imidazole side chains releases contaminants while most 6xHis tags stay bound.

components.name: Urea
components.hazard: Generates carbamylating cyanate on standing or heating.
components.note: 480.5 g per litre.

components.name: Sodium phosphate monobasic monohydrate
components.note: 13.80 g per litre.

components.name: Tris base
components.note: 1.21 g per litre.

recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl, immediately before use
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um membrane filtration; never autoclave
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: Make fresh; use within about 1 week and re-check the pH on the day of use
recipes.hazards: Urea solutions release cyanate that carbamylates lysine side chains and free N-termini; do not heat above 37 degrees C. Avoid dust when weighing the large mass of urea. pH is adjusted with HCl, immediately before use — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Poured from the same un-adjusted 8 M urea stock as Buffers B, D and E - only the titration differs.
A pH 6.8 wash is also commonly used and is slightly gentler; drop to 6.3 only if contaminants persist.
Two washes of roughly two resin-bed volumes each, collected separately, gives an interpretable gel.
Tris is a poor buffer below pH 7, so it is the 100 mM phosphate that does the buffering here; verify the pH of each batch rather than assuming it.
If the target itself begins to come off in this wash, use pH 6.8 or switch to an imidazole gradient under denaturing conditions instead of a pH gradient.

sources.title: Mace K. His-tagged protein purification under denaturing conditions (University of Manchester) - shared 8 M urea stock buffer split and titrated for lysis (pH 8.0), wash (pH 6.8) and elution (pH 5.9 and 4.5)
HisPur Purification of His-Tagged Proteins. protocols.io, doi:10.17504/protocols.io.bmg7k3zn (denaturant must be maintained in every buffer)
PubChem CID 1176 - Urea

recipes.name: Denaturing Ni-NTA Buffer E, 8 M urea, pH 4.5 (elution)
recipes.summary: Acidic 8 M urea buffer that fully protonates the histidine tag and strips the target off the nickel resin without needing imidazole. Buffer D at pH 5.9 is the gentler first elution step.

components.name: Urea
components.hazard: Generates carbamylating cyanate on standing or heating.
components.note: 480.5 g per litre.

components.name: Sodium phosphate monobasic monohydrate
components.note: 13.80 g per litre.

components.name: Tris base
components.note: 1.21 g per litre.

recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl, immediately before use
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um membrane filtration; never autoclave
recipes.storage: Room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: Make fresh; use within about 1 week and re-check the pH on the day of use
recipes.hazards: Urea solutions release cyanate that carbamylates proteins; do not heat above 37 degrees C. Acidified urea plus concentrated phosphate is an irritant - gloves and eye protection. pH is adjusted with HCl, immediately before use — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Standard two-step denaturing elution: first elute twice with the pH 5.9 buffer (Buffer D), which releases most 6xHis-tagged proteins, then twice more with this pH 4.5 buffer to recover whatever is left.
Neutralise fractions promptly (add 1/20 volume of 1 M Tris-Cl pH 8.5, or dialyse) if the protein is acid-labile.
Poured from the same un-adjusted 8 M urea stock as Buffers B, C and D.

Protein recovered in 8 M urea is unfolded. Refold by stepwise dialysis down the urea concentration, or by rapid dilution into a large volume of refolding buffer; refolding efficiency is protein-specific and usually needs optimisation.

If the material is only wanted for antibody production, gels or mass spectrometry, refolding may be unnecessary - but carbamylation from long urea exposure will shift masses by +43 Da per modified amine.

sources.title: Mace K. His-tagged protein purification under denaturing conditions (University of Manchester) - elution buffers at pH 5.9 and pH 4.5 from a shared 8 M urea stock
PubChem CID 1176 - Urea
PubChem CID 516949 - Sodium Phosphate, Monobasic, Monohydrate

recipes.name: Bacterial Lysis Buffer with Lysozyme, pH 8.0 (IMAC-compatible)
recipes.summary: Native binding buffer supplemented with lysozyme, a nuclease and PMSF, used to resuspend an E. coli pellet before sonication or freeze-thaw. Lysozyme digests the peptidoglycan, the nuclease drops the viscosity from released chromosomal DNA, and PMSF blunts serine proteases.

components.name: Sodium phosphate monobasic monohydrate
components.note: 6.90 g per litre; titrate to pH 8.0.

components.name: Sodium chloride
components.note: 17.53 g per litre.

components.name: Imidazole
components.hazard: Corrosive; suspected reproductive toxicant.
components.note: 0.68 g per litre; keeps the lysate at binding stringency so it can be loaded straight onto the resin.

components.name: Lysozyme (hen egg white)
components.note: 1 mg/mL is a common working strength; published protocols also run as low as 50 ug/mL. Add from a filtered stock immediately before use and hold 30-60 min on ice.

components.name: DNase I (or Benzonase)
components.note: Needs divalent Mg2+ (add MgCl2 to about 2 mM). Skip the Mg2+ if it would compromise the target and rely on sonication shearing instead.

components.name: PMSF
components.hazard: Acutely toxic and corrosive cholinesterase inhibitor; add from an isopropanol stock in a fume hood.
components.note: MW 174.19. PMSF hydrolyses fast at this pH (half-life about 35 min at pH 8.0), so add it immediately before lysis and re-dose after long steps.

recipes.solvent: Deionised or Milli-Q water; the base buffer is the native Ni-NTA lysis/binding buffer
recipes.adjustPHWith: 10 M NaOH or concentrated HCl (set the base buffer before adding enzymes)
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter the base buffer; add enzyme and inhibitor stocks aseptically after filtration
recipes.storage: Base buffer at 4 degrees C; complete buffer is made fresh and used within the hour
recipes.shelfLife: Complete buffer: use immediately. Base buffer: about 1 month at 4 degrees C.
recipes.hazards: PMSF is fatal if swallowed and causes severe skin and eye burns; it inhibits acetylcholinesterase. Weigh and dissolve it in a fume hood with gloves, and never handle the powder on the open bench. Imidazole is corrosive and a suspected reproductive toxicant. Sonication generates aerosols of live-cell lysate and high noise - use a sound enclosure and appropriate containment.
notes: Typical resuspension ratio is 2-5 mL of buffer per gram of wet cell paste; too little buffer makes the lysate viscous and hard to clarify.

Order of operations: resuspend cold, add lysozyme and hold 30-60 min on ice, add nuclease plus Mg2+, then sonicate in short pulses (for example 15 x 5 s with 15 s of cooling between pulses) keeping the sample on ice at all times.
Clarify by centrifuging at high speed (about 14,000 x g, 10-20 min, 4 degrees C) and pass the supernatant through a 0.45 um syringe filter before loading the resin.

Always save a small pre-induction sample, a whole-lysate sample and the insoluble pellet - if the target is absent from the soluble fraction the problem is expression or inclusion bodies, not chromatography.
Do not add EDTA-containing protease-inhibitor cocktails to an IMAC lysate; they strip the nickel. Use an EDTA-free cocktail.
Lysozyme is about 14.3 kDa and will show as a band on the gel; it mostly stays in the flow-through.
Freeze-thaw lysis (one to three cycles at -80 degrees C) works as a gentler alternative or supplement to sonication.

sources.title: Nehme R, Carpenter B, Tehan BG, et al. Expression and Purification of Mini G Proteins from Escherichia coli. Bio-protocol 7(9):e2235 (2017), PMC5419499 - lysis additive final concentrations: PMSF 1 mM, pepstatin A 2.5 uM, leupeptin 10 uM, DNase I 50 ug/mL, lysozyme 50 ug/mL, DTT 100 uM

HisPur Purification of His-Tagged Proteins. protocols.io, doi:10.17504/protocols.io.bmg7k3zn - lysis optimisation; avoid chelators such as EDTA and strong reductants such as DTT or beta-mercaptoethanol with nickel resin
Sigma-Aldrich Product Information: Phenylmethanesulfonyl fluoride (P7626) - working concentration 0.1-1 mM; aqueous half-life about 35 min at pH 8.0
PubChem CID 4784 - Phenylmethylsulfonyl Fluoride

recipes.name: Protease Inhibitor Supplement for Protein Lysates (EDTA-free, IMAC-compatible)
recipes.summary: Working concentrations for the four inhibitors most often added to a bacterial or eukaryotic lysate, chosen so the mixture stays compatible with Ni-NTA. This is an additive note rather than a stand-alone solution: each component is spiked into the lysis buffer immediately before lysis.

components.name: PMSF (serine protease inhibitor)
components.hazard: Fatal if swallowed; causes severe burns; cholinesterase inhibitor. Fume hood, gloves, eye protection.
components.note: MW 174.19, C7H7FO2S. A 100 mM stock is 17.4 mg/mL in 100% isopropanol or anhydrous ethanol; isopropanol stocks are stable for months at 15-25 degrees C. Effective range 0.1-1 mM.

components.name: Leupeptin (serine and cysteine protease inhibitor)
components.note: MW 426.6 for the free base; usually supplied as the hemisulfate. Dissolve in water or DMSO; store aliquots at -20 degrees C. 10 uM is about 4.3 ug/mL.

components.name: Pepstatin A (aspartic protease inhibitor)
components.note: MW 685.9. Practically insoluble in water - make the stock in methanol or DMSO. 2.5 uM is about 1.7 ug/mL.

components.name: Benzamidine hydrochloride (trypsin-like serine protease inhibitor)
components.note: MW 156.61 for benzamidine hydrochloride (C7H9ClN2); the hydrate form is also sold, so check the label before weighing. Water-soluble, reversible.

recipes.solvent: Added into the working lysis buffer; individual stocks are made in isopropanol (PMSF), water or DMSO (leupeptin, benzamidine) and methanol or DMSO (pepstatin A)
recipes.adjustPHWith: Not applicable - the lysis buffer sets the pH
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter aqueous stocks; organic-solvent stocks are self-preserving
recipes.storage: PMSF stock 15-25 degrees C in isopropanol; leupeptin, pepstatin A and benzamidine stocks in single-use aliquots at -20 degrees C, protected from light
recipes.shelfLife: PMSF in isopropanol at least 9 months. Aliquoted peptide inhibitors about 6 months at -20 degrees C. Once diluted into aqueous buffer, PMSF is spent within roughly 2 hours.
recipes.hazards: PMSF is acutely toxic by the oral route, corrosive to skin and eyes, and inhibits acetylcholinesterase - it is the most dangerous reagent on most protein-prep benches. Never weigh it outside a fume hood, never make aqueous stocks, and dispose of PMSF waste as toxic chemical waste (it self-quenches slowly in dilute alkali). Pepstatin A and leupeptin stocks contain DMSO or methanol, which carry dissolved solutes through skin - double-glove. DMSO and methanol are flammable.
notes: PMSF hydrolyses quickly in water: reported aqueous half-lives at 25 degrees C are about 110 min at pH 7.0, 55 min at pH 7.5 and 35 min at pH 8.0. Add it right before lysis and re-dose before long incubations or after buffer exchange.

Do NOT include EDTA or EGTA when the lysate is destined for Ni-NTA or any other IMAC resin - chelators strip the metal off the chelate. Use an EDTA-free cocktail. EDTA at 1 mM is fine for lysates going to anion exchange, size exclusion or immunoprecipitation.
Also avoid strong reductants (DTT, TCEP or beta-mercaptoethanol above about 1 mM) in IMAC lysates for the same reason.
Working concentrations here follow a published mini-G protein preparation: PMSF 1 mM, pepstatin A 2.5 uM, leupeptin 10 uM.
Commercial EDTA-free tablet cocktails are a convenient one-tablet-per-50-mL substitute for the whole mixture; their exact composition is proprietary.
Keep everything at 4 degrees C - low temperature is itself one of the most effective protease controls.
For proteins with a known problem protease, a single targeted inhibitor is preferable to a shotgun cocktail because every additive is a potential downstream interference.

sources.title: Sigma-Aldrich Product Information: Phenylmethanesulfonyl fluoride (P7626) - working concentration 0.1-1 mM (17-170 ug/mL); isopropanol stocks stable at least 9 months at 15-25 degrees C; aqueous half-lives 110 min (pH 7.0), 55 min (pH 7.5), 35 min (pH 8.0)

Nehme R, Carpenter B, Tehan BG, et al. Expression and Purification of Mini G Proteins from Escherichia coli. Bio-protocol 7(9):e2235 (2017), PMC5419499 - PMSF 1 mM, pepstatin A 2.5 uM, leupeptin 10 uM final

HisPur Purification of His-Tagged Proteins. protocols.io, doi:10.17504/protocols.io.bmg7k3zn - avoid protease inhibitors or additives containing chelators such as EDTA, and strong reducing agents such as DTT or beta-mercaptoethanol, with nickel resin
PubChem CID 72429 - Leupeptin; CID 5478883 - Pepstatin; CID 80289 - Benzamidine hydrochloride

recipes.name: Dialysis / Storage Buffer, 50 mM Tris-Cl pH 8.0 + 150 mM NaCl
recipes.summary: General-purpose buffer for dialysing imidazole, urea or guanidinium out of a purified protein and for holding it afterwards. The same composition doubles as the digestion buffer for TEV, HRV 3C or thrombin tag cleavage; DTT and glycerol are added only for storage.

components.name: Tris-Cl
components.form: tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane hydrochloride (Tris-HCl); or Tris base MW 121.14 titrated with HCl
components.note: 7.88 g/L of Tris-HCl, or 6.06 g/L of Tris base then titrated to pH 8.0 with HCl. Most labs make it as 50 mL/L of a 1 M Tris-Cl pH 8.0 stock.

components.name: Sodium chloride
components.note: 8.77 g per litre.

components.name: DTT (storage only - omit for dialysis feeding back onto Ni-NTA)
components.hazard: Irritant with a strong odour; add in a fume hood.
components.note: MW 154.25, C4H10O2S2. Add fresh - DTT oxidises within hours in aerated buffer at pH 8.

components.name: Glycerol (storage only)
components.note: MW 92.09. 10% is enough to protect against a single freeze-thaw; 20% suppresses freezing at -20 degrees C and is preferred for enzymes stored there.

recipes.adjustPHWith: Concentrated HCl (Tris base route) or 10 M NaOH; the pH of Tris shifts by about -0.03 per degree C, so titrate at the temperature of use
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um membrane filtration (autoclavable if glycerol and DTT are added afterwards)
recipes.storage: 4 degrees C without DTT; add DTT immediately before use
recipes.shelfLife: Base buffer about 3 months at 4 degrees C; DTT-containing buffer is single-use
recipes.hazards: Low hazard overall. DTT has a strong sulfurous odour and is a skin and eye irritant. Concentrated HCl used for titration is corrosive and must be handled in a fume hood. Glycerol solutions are slippery when spilled.
notes: Standard exchange: dialyse against at least 100 volumes of buffer, change the buffer twice with a minimum of 2 h between changes (or one change plus overnight at 4 degrees C). Two 100-fold changes give roughly a 10,000-fold reduction in imidazole.
Choose a dialysis membrane with a molecular-weight cut-off about one third of the protein mass or lower.

For refolding out of 8 M urea, step the urea down rather than removing it in one jump: dialyse sequentially against 6, 4, 2 and then 0 M urea in this buffer, several hours per step at 4 degrees C, and centrifuge out any precipitate before proceeding.
Spin concentrators are a faster alternative for imidazole removal, but concentrate slowly (about 3,500-5,000 x g) to avoid precipitating protein at the membrane.
Tris is a poor buffer below pH 7.0 and reacts with primary-amine chemistry (NHS esters, some crosslinkers) - switch to HEPES or phosphate for those applications.

As a digestion buffer for tag removal, use 50 mM Tris-Cl pH 8.0 + 150 mM NaCl without glycerol, then pass the digest back over Ni-NTA to remove the cleaved tag, the uncut protein and any His-tagged protease.
For long-term storage, aliquot, snap-freeze in liquid nitrogen and hold at -80 degrees C; repeated freeze-thaw is the most common cause of lost activity.

sources.title: Khin NC, Ganguly D, Yee S, Chan KX. Nickel-NTA Protein Purification. protocols.io (2020), doi:10.17504/protocols.io.bihhkb36 - digestion buffer 50 mM Tris-HCl pH 8.0 + 150 mM NaCl, supplemented with 1 mM DTT and 20% glycerol for storage; spin-concentrate at 3,500-5,000 rcf
Barrick Lab - Protocol for harvesting Pfu-Sso7d polymerase (storage buffer with 50% glycerol, 100 mM Tris-HCl pH 8.0, 0.2 mM EDTA, 2 mM DTT added immediately before use)
PubChem CID 93573 - Tromethamine hydrochloride (Tris-HCl)
PubChem CID 439196 - Dithiothreitol

recipes.name: IPTG Induction of a T7 / lac Expression Culture (working concentrations)
recipes.summary: How much IPTG to add and when, for inducing His-tagged protein from a lac-operator-controlled vector in E. coli. This is a usage note for the existing 1 M IPTG stock, not a new solution.

components.name: IPTG (isopropyl beta-D-1-thiogalactopyranoside)
components.note: MW 238.30, C9H18O5S. 1 mM final = 1 mL of 1 M stock per litre of culture (a 1:1000 addition). Lower-temperature, lower-inducer regimes commonly use 0.1-0.5 mM, and some protocols go as low as 50 uM.

components.name: D-glucose (optional, added to the growth medium before induction)
components.note: MW 180.16. Catabolite repression from 0.2% glucose reduces leaky pre-induction expression from lac-based promoters. Glucose must be exhausted or the culture washed before IPTG is effective in some systems.

recipes.solvent: Expression medium (LB, 2xYT or Terrific Broth) with the appropriate selection antibiotic
recipes.adjustPHWith: Not applicable
recipes.sterilization: IPTG is added from a 0.22 um-filtered 1 M aqueous stock; never autoclave IPTG
recipes.storage: 1 M IPTG stock in aliquots at -20 degrees C
recipes.shelfLife: 1 M IPTG stock about 1 year at -20 degrees C; thawed aliquots about 1 month at 4 degrees C
recipes.hazards: IPTG is not classified as acutely hazardous but is a non-metabolisable gratuitous inducer and should be handled as a research chemical; avoid dust when weighing. The real hazards of an induction are biological (live recombinant E. coli - decontaminate all culture waste) and mechanical (hot shaking incubators, glass flasks).
notes: Standard high-yield regime: grow at 37 degrees C to OD600 0.4-0.6, add IPTG to 1 mM, and continue shaking at 37 degrees C for about 4 h (Barrick Lab large-scale expression).

Solubility-rescue regime: induce at OD600 0.4-0.6 with 0.1-0.5 mM IPTG and shift to 16-25 degrees C for 16-20 h. Slower synthesis at low temperature is the single most effective way to keep a protein out of inclusion bodies.
Documented working points across open protocols: 1 mM at 37 degrees C for 4 h; 0.5 mM (Barrick Pfu-Sso7d); 50 uM at 25 degrees C for 16-20 h (mini-G proteins).
2xYT is richer than LB and gives higher cell density; adding 0.2% glucose before induction reduces leakiness of lac-based systems.
Always keep a 50 uL to 1 mL pre-induction sample and a matched post-induction sample - running them side by side on a gel is the fastest diagnostic when nothing purifies.
IPTG concentration mainly changes the rate of expression, not the total yield; if the protein is going into inclusion bodies, lower the temperature before lowering the inducer.
Harvest by centrifugation, discard the supernatant, and freeze the pellet at -80 degrees C if not lysing the same day.

sources.title: Barrick Lab - Large-scale Protein Expression in E. coli: induce at OD600 0.4-0.6 with 1/100 volume of IPTG to 1 mM final, 4 h at 37 degrees C, 275 rpm; 2xYT richer than LB; optional 0.2% glucose to reduce leakiness
Barrick Lab - Protocol for harvesting Pfu-Sso7d polymerase (IPTG 0.5 mM final)
Nehme R, et al. Expression and Purification of Mini G Proteins from Escherichia coli. Bio-protocol 7(9):e2235 (2017), PMC5419499 - induction with 50 uM IPTG at 25 degrees C for 16-20 h
PubChem CID 656894 - Isopropyl beta-D-thiogalactopyranoside

recipes.name: Bradford Protein Assay Reagent (Coomassie G-250, acidic)
recipes.summary: Acidic Coomassie Brilliant Blue G-250 solution for colorimetric total-protein quantitation. Dye binding to protein shifts the absorbance maximum from about 470 nm to 595 nm within minutes.

components.name: Coomassie Brilliant Blue G-250
components.note: MW 854.0 (sodium salt, C47H48N3NaO7S2). 100 mg per litre = 0.01% (w/v). Dissolve it in the ethanol first, before any acid or water is added.
components.hazard: Dye powder stains everything; avoid raising dust.

components.name: Ethanol, 95%
components.note: 50 mL of 95% ethanol per litre, which is about 4.75% (v/v) absolute ethanol in the finished reagent. Methanol may be substituted but is more toxic.
components.hazard: Highly flammable.

components.name: Phosphoric acid, 85%
components.note: 100 mL of 85% phosphoric acid per litre, which is about 8.5% (w/v) H3PO4 in the finished reagent. Add slowly and with stirring after the dye has dissolved in ethanol.
components.hazard: Corrosive - causes severe skin burns and eye damage.

recipes.adjustPHWith: Not adjusted - the reagent is deliberately strongly acidic and no titration is performed
recipes.sterilization: Filter through Whatman No. 1 (or equivalent) paper to remove undissolved dye; sterility is not required
recipes.storage: 4 degrees C in an amber or foil-wrapped glass bottle
recipes.shelfLife: Several months at 4 degrees C. Re-filter if a precipitate forms. Discard if the blank reading at 595 nm drifts upward or the reagent looks blue rather than red-brown.
recipes.hazards: 85% phosphoric acid is corrosive and causes severe burns - add acid to the alcoholic dye solution slowly in a fume hood with gloves, a lab coat and face protection, never the reverse. Ethanol (or methanol, if substituted) is highly flammable; keep away from ignition sources. Methanol is additionally acutely toxic and can cause blindness. The finished reagent is acidic and stains skin, clothing and plasticware; it also etches quartz over time, so use disposable plastic or glass cuvettes.
notes: Order of addition matters: dissolve the dye in the ethanol first, then add the acid with stirring, then dilute to volume with water. Adding water first leaves undissolved dye.
Microplate format: 10 uL of standard or sample plus 180-200 uL of reagent per well, mix, hold 10 min at room temperature, read at 595 nm.
Cuvette format: about 1 part sample to 50 parts reagent; the same 5-60 min reading window applies but read all samples at a consistent time.

Build the standard curve with BSA (a 0.1 ug/uL working standard covering roughly 0-10 ug per well is convenient). The Bradford response is strongly protein-dependent - BSA and IgG give noticeably different slopes - so report which standard was used.

Detergents are the main interferent: SDS, Triton X-100 and NP-40 at even fractions of a percent will inflate or destroy the reading. Buffers containing detergent need matched-blank correction, dilution, or a BCA assay instead.
High imidazole and reducing agents are tolerated far better by Bradford than by BCA, which makes Bradford the convenient choice for IMAC fractions - dilute the eluate at least 1:2 first.
The dye-protein complex is not a true end point and continues to drift; read all wells within the same short window and include a reagent-only blank on every plate.
The assay is originally from Bradford (1976, Anal Biochem 72:248), a closed-access paper; the working recipe recorded here is taken from open protocol sources.

sources.title: iGEM Marburg 2018 - Bradford Assay protocol: dissolve 100 mg Coomassie Brilliant Blue G-250 in 50 mL 95% ethanol, add 100 mL 85% phosphoric acid, dilute to 1 L, filter (Whatman), store at 4 degrees C in a dark bottle; 10 uL sample + 180 uL reagent, 10 min at RT, read 595 nm
PubChem CID 6324599 - Coomassie Brilliant Blue G-250
PubChem CID 1004 - Phosphoric Acid
HisPur Purification of His-Tagged Proteins. protocols.io, doi:10.17504/protocols.io.bmg7k3zn - dilute elution fractions at least 1:2 before a Bradford assay

recipes.name: BCA Protein Assay Working Reagent (50:1 Reagent A : Reagent B)
recipes.summary: Alkaline copper reagent mixed fresh from two kit components. Protein reduces Cu(II) to Cu(I) in the biuret reaction, and two bicinchoninic acid molecules chelate each Cu(I) to give a purple complex read at 562 nm.

components.name: BCA Reagent A (sodium carbonate, sodium bicarbonate, bicinchoninic acid and sodium tartrate in 0.1 M sodium hydroxide)
components.hazard: Strongly alkaline (0.1 M NaOH) - corrosive to skin and eyes.
components.note: 50 parts. Bicinchoninic acid MW 344.3, C20H12N2O4 (PubChem CID 71068); sodium carbonate monohydrate MW 124.00 (CID 2735133); sodium tartrate dihydrate MW 230.08 (CID 12598458). The manufacturer does not publish the individual concentrations, so this reagent is specified by volume ratio rather than molarity.

components.name: BCA Reagent B (4% w/v cupric sulfate)
components.hazard: Copper salts are harmful if swallowed and very toxic to aquatic life.
components.note: 1 part. 4% (w/v) copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate, CuSO4 - 5H2O, MW 249.69.

recipes.solvent: Not applicable - the working reagent is the two kit reagents combined, used undiluted
recipes.adjustPHWith: Not adjusted - the alkalinity is built into Reagent A
recipes.sterilization: Not required
recipes.storage: Kit reagents at room temperature. Working reagent at room temperature in a closed container.
recipes.shelfLife: Working reagent is stable for several days at room temperature in a closed container, but is best made the day of use. Discard any reagent that is discoloured or shows microbial growth.
recipes.hazards: Reagent A contains 0.1 M sodium hydroxide and is corrosive - it causes severe skin and eye damage; wear gloves and eye protection. Copper sulfate in Reagent B is harmful if swallowed and highly toxic to aquatic organisms; collect assay waste for chemical disposal rather than pouring it down the sink. The supplied BSA standard ampules contain 0.05% sodium azide - azide forms shock-sensitive metal azides in lead and copper drain plumbing, so azide-containing waste must never go down the drain.
notes: Mixing ratio is 50 parts Reagent A to 1 part Reagent B; brief turbidity on mixing clears to a green working reagent.
Volume needed: 2.0 mL of working reagent per sample in the test-tube format, 200 uL per well in the microplate format.

Microplate procedure: 25 uL of standard or sample plus 200 uL of working reagent (a 1:8 ratio), shake 30 s, incubate 37 degrees C for 30 min, cool to room temperature, read at or near 562 nm. Wavelengths from 540-590 nm work.

Test-tube procedure: 0.1 mL of sample plus 2.0 mL of working reagent (1:20), 37 degrees C for 30 min (working range 20-2,000 ug/mL), or room temperature for 2 h, or 60 degrees C for 30 min for the enhanced protocol (working range 5-250 ug/mL).
Colour development never truly stops, so read all tubes or wells within about 10 minutes of each other and always subtract a blank.
Standard curve is built from BSA; the kit standard is supplied at 2.0 mg/mL in 0.9% saline with 0.05% sodium azide.
BCA tolerates detergents (including SDS and Triton X-100) far better than Bradford, which is why it is preferred for RIPA and membrane-protein lysates.

BCA is destroyed by reducing agents and chelators: DTT, beta-mercaptoethanol, TCEP, EDTA and even high imidazole will give false highs or kill the signal. For IMAC eluates containing 250 mM imidazole, either dilute heavily and blank against matched buffer, or use Bradford instead.
The chemistry is from Smith et al. (1985, Anal Biochem 150:76), a closed-access paper; the working ratios and conditions recorded here come from the freely published kit instructions.

sources.title: Thermo Scientific Pierce BCA Protein Assay Kit instructions (23225 / 23227): Reagent A contains sodium carbonate, sodium bicarbonate, bicinchoninic acid and sodium tartrate in 0.1 M sodium hydroxide; Reagent B is 4% cupric sulfate; working reagent 50:1; 37 degrees C 30 min; read 562 nm; range 20-2,000 ug/mL; BSA standard 2 mg/mL in 0.9% saline with 0.05% sodium azide
PubChem CID 71068 - Bicinchoninic acid
PubChem CID 24463 - Copper sulfate pentahydrate

recipes.name: Laemmli SDS Sample Buffer, 2x (Bio-Rad formulation, reducing)
recipes.summary: Two-fold concentrated denaturing sample buffer mixed 1:1 with a protein sample before SDS-PAGE. This is the Bio-Rad #1610737 formulation, which is slightly leaner in Tris and richer in glycerol than the 4x buffer already in the library, and is the convenient choice for dilute samples such as IMAC flow-through and wash fractions.

components.name: Tris-Cl, pH 6.8
components.form: tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane hydrochloride; or Tris base MW 121.14 titrated to pH 6.8 with HCl
components.note: 65.8 mM = 10.37 g/L of Tris-HCl. Easiest route: 8.2 mL of 0.5 M Tris-Cl pH 6.8 stock per 62.5 mL of buffer, or simply 13.2 mL of 0.5 M Tris-Cl pH 6.8 per 100 mL.

components.name: SDS (sodium dodecyl sulfate)
components.hazard: Powder is harmful if inhaled and irritating to the respiratory tract; weigh in a hood or use a pre-made 10-20% solution.
components.note: MW 288.38, C12H25NaO4S. 21 g/L; equivalent to 105 mL of a 20% (w/v) SDS stock per litre.

components.name: Glycerol
components.note: MW 92.09. Specified by the manufacturer as % w/v; at a density of 1.26 g/mL this is about 20.9% v/v. Glycerol is what makes the sample sink into the well.

components.name: Bromophenol blue
components.note: MW 670.0 for the free acid; the water-soluble sodium salt (MW about 691.9) is what is usually stocked. 0.1 g per litre - tracking dye only.

components.name: 2-Mercaptoethanol (added immediately before use)
components.hazard: Acutely toxic by inhalation, ingestion and skin contact; foul-smelling. Dispense only in a fume hood.
components.note: MW 78.13. 50 uL of neat 2-mercaptoethanol per 950 uL of 2x buffer, which gives 355 mM in the final 1x sample. Neat BME is about 14.3 M.

recipes.adjustPHWith: Set by the 0.5 M Tris-Cl pH 6.8 stock; titrate that stock with HCl before use rather than pH-ing the finished sample buffer (SDS interferes with pH electrodes)
recipes.sterilization: None required; do not autoclave (SDS foams and bromophenol blue degrades)
recipes.storage: Without reductant: room temperature or 4 degrees C. With BME or DTT added: 4 degrees C short-term, or aliquot and store at -20 degrees C.
recipes.shelfLife: Non-reducing buffer at least 1 year at room temperature. Reducing buffer about 1 month at 4 degrees C or 6 months at -20 degrees C; the thiol oxidises out.
recipes.hazards: 2-Mercaptoethanol is acutely toxic if inhaled, swallowed or absorbed through skin, and its odour is detectable far below the hazardous level - always dispense in a fume hood, cap tubes before removing them, and collect waste separately. SDS powder is a respiratory and eye irritant; use a pre-made stock solution where possible. Heating sealed tubes builds pressure - use screw-cap tubes or lid locks when boiling samples. pH is adjusted with Set by the 0.5 M Tris-Cl pH 6.8 stock; titrate that stock with HCl before use rather than pH-ing the finished sample buffer (SDS interferes with pH electrodes) — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Use: mix sample 1:1 with 2x buffer, then heat. 95-100 degrees C for 5 min is standard; 70 degrees C for 10 min is safer for membrane proteins, which aggregate when boiled.
Never boil a sample containing 8 M urea - heat drives urea to cyanate and carbamylates the protein. Hold urea samples at 37 degrees C or below.
DTT at 50-100 mM final is an odourless substitute for BME and is preferred for mass-spectrometry samples, but is less effective at fully reducing some disulfide-rich proteins.
Omit the reductant entirely for non-reducing gels (native disulfide analysis, some antibody work) and label those aliquots clearly.
If a sample is too dilute to halve with 2x buffer, concentrate it first or switch to the 4x or 6x buffer already in the library.
Samples in guanidine hydrochloride must be exchanged into urea or dialysed before adding SDS sample buffer - guanidinium and dodecyl sulfate form an insoluble precipitate.
A sample that runs as a smeared or streaked lane is usually under-reduced, over-loaded or still carrying chromosomal DNA; nuclease treatment during lysis fixes the last case.

sources.title: Bio-Rad - 2x Laemmli Sample Buffer (#1610737) instruction sheet 4006028: 65.8 mM Tris-HCl pH 6.8, 2.1% SDS, 26.3% (w/v) glycerol, 0.01% bromophenol blue; add 50 uL 2-mercaptoethanol per 950 uL to give 355 mM in the 1x sample
PubChem CID 3423265 - Sodium Lauryl Sulfate (SDS)
PubChem CID 1567 - 2-Mercaptoethanol
PubChem CID 8272 - Bromophenol Blue

categories.title: RNA work: extraction, denaturing gels, storage, DNase & reverse transcription

recipes.name: Solution D - Guanidinium Thiocyanate Denaturing Solution (4 M)
recipes.summary: Chaotropic lysis solution that dissolves cells and inactivates RNases in one step; the aqueous half of the acid guanidinium-phenol-chloroform (AGPC) total-RNA method.

components.name: Guanidine thiocyanate
components.form: guanidine thiocyanate (guanidinium isothiocyanate, GITC), C2H6N4S, CAS 593-84-0 - anhydrous solid, no hydrate
components.note: 472.6 g per litre. Dissolves slowly and endothermically; warm to about 60-65 C with stirring. The solid is hygroscopic - keep the bottle closed.
components.hazard: Harmful if swallowed. Contact with acids or with hypochlorite bleach liberates toxic gases including hydrogen cyanide. Never put guanidinium waste into a bleach-containing waste stream.

components.name: Trisodium citrate dihydrate
components.note: 7.35 g per litre. Anhydrous trisodium citrate is MW 258.07 (PubChem CID 6224) and would be 6.45 g per litre - the two forms are not interchangeable by mass. Usually added as 33.3 mL of a 0.75 M pH 7.0 citrate stock per litre.

components.name: N-Lauroylsarcosine sodium salt (sarkosyl)
components.form: sodium N-dodecanoyl-N-methylglycinate (sodium lauroyl sarcosinate), C15H28NNaO3, CAS 137-16-6
components.note: 5 g per litre, most easily added as 50 mL of a 10% (w/v) sarkosyl stock per litre.
components.hazard: Anionic detergent - irritating to eyes and skin; the dry powder is a respiratory irritant.

components.name: 2-Mercaptoethanol
components.form: 2-mercaptoethanol (beta-mercaptoethanol, BME), C2H6OS, CAS 60-24-2, neat liquid, density about 1.114 g/mL (neat is roughly 14.3 M)
components.note: 7.81 g per litre, i.e. about 7.0 mL of neat BME per litre (0.70 mL per 100 mL). ADD FRESH - prepare the other three components as a stock and add BME to the aliquot you are about to use.
components.hazard: Toxic by inhalation and skin contact; foul odour. Fume hood only. Do not return BME-containing solution to a shared stock bottle.

recipes.adjustPHWith: the citrate is added as a pH 7.0 sodium citrate stock; do not titrate the finished solution
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filtration only - DO NOT AUTOCLAVE (thiocyanate solutions must not be heated in a sealed vessel)
recipes.storage: Room temperature in an amber or foil-wrapped bottle, without 2-mercaptoethanol; add BME to the working aliquot
recipes.shelfLife: About 3 months at room temperature without BME; use BME-containing working solution within about 1 month at 4 C
recipes.hazards: Guanidine thiocyanate is the dominant hazard: acidification or contact with bleach releases hydrogen cyanide gas. Segregate guanidinium waste from all halogen/hypochlorite waste and label the container. 2-Mercaptoethanol is toxic and malodorous - dispense in a fume hood. Wear nitrile gloves and eye protection.
notes: Working composition: 4 M guanidine thiocyanate, 25 mM sodium citrate pH 7.0, 0.5% (w/v) sarkosyl, 0.1 M 2-mercaptoethanol.

One convenient batch, scaled from the original single-step description: 250 g guanidine thiocyanate + 293 mL water + 17.6 mL of 0.75 M sodium citrate pH 7.0 + 26.4 mL of 10% sarkosyl, dissolved at about 65 C, gives roughly 530 mL of stock; add 0.36 mL BME per 50 mL of that stock immediately before use.

Downstream in the AGPC method (see the Addgene kit-free protocol): per 1 mL of lysate add 0.1 mL of 2 M sodium acetate pH 4, then water-saturated acid phenol, then 0.2 mL chloroform:isoamyl alcohol 49:1. The low pH keeps DNA and protein in the organic/interphase and leaves RNA in the aqueous phase.
The acidic step is what makes this method RNA-selective. At pH 8 (ordinary buffered phenol) DNA partitions into the aqueous phase with the RNA - that is the single most common way this method fails.
Guanidinium carryover inhibits reverse transcriptase and most polymerases. Wash the pellet with 75% ethanol at least twice.
Thiocyanate also attacks stainless steel and some centrifuge rotors over time - rinse spills promptly with water.

sources.title: Addgene - Kit Free RNA Extraction (Solution D composition, acid sodium acetate, chloroform:isoamyl 49:1, 75% ethanol, LiCl option)
Back to Old Books Toward Affordable Research: Homemade Phenol-Based Reagent for Triphasic RNA Purification (Biochem Genet, PMC11186942)
PubChem CID 65046 - Guanidine thiocyanate, C2H6N4S, MW 118.16

recipes.name: Acid Guanidinium-Phenol Monophasic Reagent (TRIzol-style, laboratory-made)
recipes.summary: Single-bottle acid phenol plus guanidinium/ammonium thiocyanate reagent for one-tube total RNA extraction; chloroform is added afterwards to split the phases.

components.name: Phenol, acid (water-saturated, unbuffered or citrate-buffered to about pH 4.2)
components.form: phenol, C6H6O, CAS 108-95-2, MW 94.11, density about 1.07 g/mL; melt crystals at about 50 C. Must be ACID phenol (pH 4.2-4.5), NOT the pH 8.0 Tris-buffered phenol used for DNA.
components.note: 380 mL of molten/water-saturated phenol per litre. 500 g of phenol crystals is about 467 mL. Add 8-hydroxyquinoline to 0.1% (w/v) as an antioxidant and yellow indicator if your phenol is not already stabilised.
components.hazard: SEVERE: phenol causes rapidly penetrating chemical burns and is absorbed through intact skin with systemic toxicity. Fume hood, double nitrile gloves, face shield. Keep PEG-300/400 on hand for skin decontamination - water alone is not sufficient.

components.name: Guanidine thiocyanate
components.form: guanidine thiocyanate (guanidinium isothiocyanate), C2H6N4S, CAS 593-84-0, anhydrous
components.note: 94.5 g per litre.
components.hazard: Liberates hydrogen cyanide on contact with acid or bleach. Never combine this reagent's waste with hypochlorite.

components.name: Ammonium thiocyanate
components.note: 30.4 g per litre.
components.hazard: Harmful if swallowed; also releases HCN with acid. Same waste-segregation rule as guanidine thiocyanate.

components.name: Sodium acetate trihydrate
components.note: 13.61 g per litre. If using ANHYDROUS sodium acetate (MW 82.03, PubChem CID 517045) use 8.20 g per litre instead.

components.name: Glycerol
components.note: 50 mL per litre. Helps keep the reagent one phase and stabilises it on storage.

recipes.solvent: nuclease-free water - dissolve the salts and glycerol in water first, then add the molten/saturated phenol and mix
recipes.adjustPHWith: sodium acetate / acetic acid (the phenol itself is pre-equilibrated to about pH 4.2 with citrate buffer)
recipes.sterilization: none - filtration is not practical and heating a thiocyanate/phenol mixture is unsafe. Use nuclease-free water and clean glassware.
recipes.storage: 4 C in an amber glass bottle with a phenol-resistant (PTFE-lined) cap, away from light
recipes.shelfLife: Up to about 1 year at 4 C while it remains colourless to pale straw; discard if pink, red or dark yellow (oxidised phenol nicks RNA)
recipes.hazards: This reagent combines two of the worst hazards in a molecular biology lab. Phenol: deep chemical burns and dermal systemic toxicity - fume hood, face shield, double gloves, PEG-400 decontamination on hand. Thiocyanates: hydrogen cyanide evolution on acidification or bleach contact - dedicated, clearly labelled halogen-free waste container. Chloroform added later is a suspected carcinogen. Do not autoclave any of it. pH is adjusted with sodium acetate / acetic acid (the phenol itself is pre-equilibrated to about pH 4.2 with citrate buffer) — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Published composition of the laboratory-made reagent: 38% acid phenol, 0.8 M guanidine thiocyanate, 0.4 M ammonium thiocyanate, 0.1 M sodium acetate, 5% glycerol, with the phenol adjusted to about pH 4.2 with citrate buffer.

This is a facts-only formulation record for a home-made equivalent of the commercial monophasic reagents. Commercial products are proprietary mixtures and their exact composition is not published; their safety data sheets confirm the two active ingredients are phenol and a guanidinium salt.

Typical use ratios reported for this class of reagent: 1 mL per 50-100 mg tissue or per about 1e7 cultured cells; then 0.2 mL chloroform per 1 mL of reagent for phase separation; then isopropanol (about 0.5 mL per 1 mL reagent) to precipitate RNA from the aqueous phase; then two washes with 75% ethanol.

ACID PHENOL IS THE CRITICAL DETAIL. Tris-buffered phenol at pH 8.0 (the standard DNA reagent, already in this library as PCI 25:24:1) will carry genomic DNA into the aqueous phase and ruin the preparation.

Phenol/chloroform extraction leaves trace phenol in the aqueous phase. Phenol carryover shows as a depressed A260/A230 ratio and inhibits reverse transcription; a back-extraction with chloroform:isoamyl alcohol 24:1 fixes it.
Acid phenol:chloroform 5:1 at pH 4.5-4.7 is the ready-made alternative to preparing acid phenol yourself; it is a different reagent from the pH 8.0 phenol:chloroform:isoamyl 25:24:1 used for DNA.
Do not use polystyrene tubes or serological pipettes with this reagent - phenol dissolves them. Polypropylene and glass only.

sources.title: Back to Old Books Toward Affordable Research: Homemade Phenol-Based Reagent for Triphasic RNA Purification (Biochem Genet, PMC11186942) - 38% acidic phenol, 0.8 M guanidine thiocyanate, 0.4 M ammonium thiocyanate, 0.1 M sodium acetate, 5% glycerol, phenol at pH 4.2
Addgene - Kit Free RNA Extraction (acid extraction ratios, 75% ethanol washes)
PubChem CID 996 - Phenol, C6H6O, MW 94.11
PubChem CID 15666 - Ammonium thiocyanate, CH4N2S, MW 76.12

recipes.name: 2 M Sodium Acetate, pH 4.0 (RNA / AGPC acidification)
recipes.summary: Low-pH acetate used to acidify guanidinium lysates so that DNA and protein stay in the phenol phase and RNA stays aqueous; not the same reagent as 3 M sodium acetate pH 5.2.

components.name: Sodium acetate trihydrate
components.note: 272.2 g per litre. With ANHYDROUS sodium acetate (MW 82.03, PubChem CID 517045) use 164.1 g per litre. Reaching pH 4.0 takes a large volume of glacial acetic acid - allow for it when making up to volume.

components.name: Glacial acetic acid
components.note: Titrant only. pH 4.0 is well below the acetate pKa of 4.76, so the solution is mostly free acetic acid at this point and needs a lot of acid.
components.hazard: Corrosive and flammable; add in a fume hood.

recipes.solvent: nuclease-free water (dissolve in about 700 mL, titrate to pH 4.0, then bring to 1 L)
recipes.adjustPHWith: glacial acetic acid
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filtration (preferred for RNA work) or autoclave 121 C, 20 min
recipes.storage: Room temperature in a dedicated RNA-only bottle
