recipes.shelfLife: 1 year or more; discard if cloudy or if anything has been pipetted into the bottle with a non-filter tip
recipes.hazards: Glacial acetic acid is corrosive and flammable. The finished solution is a mild irritant only.
notes: Use: 0.1 mL of 2 M sodium acetate pH 4 per 1 mL of guanidinium lysate, before the acid phenol and chloroform additions.

DISTINCT FROM the 3 M sodium acetate pH 5.2 already in this library. pH 5.2 acetate is the alcohol-precipitation salt; pH 4.0 acetate is the phase-partition acidifier. Swapping them defeats the RNA/DNA separation.

Some published variants use 2 M sodium acetate at pH 4.0-4.5, or add the acid as a sodium acetate/acetic acid mixture. Anything in that window gives an aqueous phase acidic enough to retain DNA in the organic phase.
Prepare with nuclease-free water and keep the bottle RNA-dedicated - acetate stocks are a classic route for RNase contamination to enter an otherwise clean workflow.

sources.title: Addgene - Kit Free RNA Extraction (2 M sodium acetate pH 4, 0.1 mL per 1 mL lysate)
PubChem CID 23665404 - Sodium acetate trihydrate, MW 136.08
PubChem CID 517045 - Sodium acetate (anhydrous), MW 82.03

recipes.name: 75% (v/v) Ethanol, RNase-free (RNA pellet wash)
recipes.summary: Salt-removal wash for precipitated RNA pellets; 75% rather than 70% because RNA is slightly more soluble in aqueous ethanol than DNA and the extra alcohol keeps small RNAs on the pellet.

components.name: Ethanol
components.form: absolute (200 proof) molecular-biology-grade ethanol, C2H6O, CAS 64-17-5, MW 46.07. Do NOT use denatured/reagent alcohol here - the denaturants are not defined and can carry nucleases or inhibitors.
components.note: 750 mL of absolute ethanol per litre. If your stock is 95% (190 proof), use 789.5 mL per litre instead.
components.hazard: Highly flammable (GHS H225), flash point about 13 C.

recipes.solvent: nuclease-free water - bring to final volume in a graduated cylinder
recipes.sterilization: none required; 0.22 um filter into a sterile RNase-free bottle if you want extra assurance
recipes.storage: 20 C in a tightly capped bottle, dedicated to RNA work (ice-cold ethanol reduces pellet loss)
recipes.shelfLife: Make fresh monthly - repeated opening of a cold bottle condenses atmospheric water and drifts the concentration down
recipes.hazards: Highly flammable liquid and vapour. Keep away from ignition sources. Ethanol/water mixing contracts by a few percent, so measure by bringing to volume, not by adding 750 mL ethanol to 250 mL water.
notes: Standard use: two washes of the RNA pellet with 0.5-1 mL of cold 75% ethanol per 1 mL of original lysis reagent, with a brief spin between washes.

DIFFERENT REAGENT from the 70% ethanol bench disinfectant already in this library. That one may be denatured alcohol made with tap-quality water; this one must be nuclease-free, and the two bottles should never be interchanged.
Air-dry the pellet only until the ethanol sheen is gone (a few minutes). An over-dried RNA pellet becomes very hard to redissolve - do not use a vacuum concentrator to completion.
Ethanol carryover is a strong inhibitor of reverse transcription and of downstream enzymatic steps; it also shows up as an odd absorbance profile on a spectrophotometer.
For RNA, 75% is the widely used figure (Addgene kit-free protocol and the commercial monophasic-reagent protocols). 70% is used for DNA. The difference is small but real for short RNAs.
Chilled 75% ethanol reduces the amount of RNA that redissolves during the wash - store the bottle at -20 C.

sources.title: Addgene - Kit Free RNA Extraction (75% ethanol wash)
PubChem CID 702 - Ethanol, C2H6O, MW 46.07

recipes.name: Lithium Chloride Precipitation Solution, 7.5 M LiCl / 50 mM EDTA
recipes.summary: Selective RNA precipitant: at 2.5 M final it drops high-molecular-weight RNA while leaving DNA, tRNA, most 5S RNA, protein and free nucleotides in solution.

components.name: Lithium chloride
components.form: lithium chloride, LiCl, CAS 7447-41-8, anhydrous (PubChem lists MW 42.39; the salt is deliquescent and gains water on the shelf)
components.note: 317.9 g per litre of anhydrous LiCl. Dissolution is strongly exothermic - add the solid in portions to cold water and let it cool before making to volume, or your final concentration will read low.
components.hazard: Harmful if swallowed. Lithium salts are reproductive/developmental hazards - avoid dust inhalation.

components.name: EDTA disodium salt dihydrate
components.form: ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt dihydrate (Na2EDTA*2H2O), C10H14N2Na2O8*2H2O, CAS 6381-92-6
components.note: 18.61 g per litre, or 100 mL of 0.5 M EDTA pH 8.0 per litre. EDTA will not dissolve until the pH is raised to about 8.

recipes.adjustPHWith: NaOH (needed to dissolve the EDTA)
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filtration (preferred) or autoclave 121 C, 20 min
recipes.storage: Room temperature; a dedicated RNA-only bottle
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year or more; LiCl may crystallise out if the bottle is stored cold - rewarm and redissolve before use
recipes.hazards: Lithium chloride is harmful if swallowed and is classed as a reproductive toxicant in some jurisdictions; handle the dry salt with gloves and avoid generating dust. The heat of solution is enough to make a large batch uncomfortably hot. pH is adjusted with NaOH (needed to dissolve the EDTA) — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Use: add LiCl solution to the RNA sample to 2.5 M final (i.e. 1 volume of 7.5 M stock per 2 volumes of sample), incubate at -20 C for several hours to overnight, then centrifuge.
LiCl precipitation is a cleanup, not a primary extraction. It is the standard way to remove residual DNA, tRNA and nucleotides after an AGPC/phenol prep or an in vitro transcription reaction.

LiCl does NOT efficiently precipitate DNA, tRNA, small RNAs (5S, most of the sub-200 nt fraction) or free nucleotides. If you need small RNAs or miRNAs, use ethanol/isopropanol precipitation instead - LiCl will throw them away.
Lithium carryover inhibits reverse transcription and in vitro translation. Always wash the pellet with 70-75% ethanol before resuspending.
Do not use LiCl-precipitated RNA directly in reactions where the salt has not been washed out - this is the most common complaint about the method.
The Addgene kit-free RNA protocol lists the 7.5 M LiCl stock and the 2.5 M working concentration as an alternative to isopropanol precipitation.

sources.title: Addgene - Kit Free RNA Extraction (7.5 M LiCl stock, 2.5 M final)
PubChem CID 433294 - Lithium chloride, LiCl, MW 42.39
PubChem CID 636371 - Edetate disodium dihydrate, MW 372.24

recipes.name: RNA Storage / Resuspension Solution (1 mM Sodium Citrate, pH 6.4)
recipes.summary: Very dilute, mildly acidic chelating buffer for dissolving and storing purified RNA; slows base-catalysed hydrolysis better than TE or water.

components.name: Trisodium citrate dihydrate
components.note: 0.294 g per litre - too little to weigh accurately for small batches. Prepare a 1 M sodium citrate stock (294.1 g/L) and dilute 1 mL into 1 L.

components.name: HCl (or citric acid)
components.note: Titrant only, to bring the dilute citrate to pH 6.4. Because the solution is only 1 mM its buffering capacity is negligible - measure the pH with a freshly calibrated meter on a stirred sample and titrate very slowly.
components.hazard: Concentrated HCl is corrosive; dilute to 1 M or 0.1 M before titrating.

recipes.adjustPHWith: dilute HCl (or citric acid)
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filtration into RNase-free single-use aliquots (autoclaving is acceptable but risks reintroducing particulates)
recipes.storage: Room temperature or 4 C as unopened aliquots; RNA dissolved in it is stored at -80 C
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year as sealed single-use aliquots; discard any aliquot that has been pipetted into more than once
recipes.hazards: None significant for the finished solution. pH is adjusted with dilute HCl (or citric acid) — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Rationale, in short: RNA is degraded by base-catalysed transesterification via the 2'-OH, so a mildly acidic pH slows it, and citrate chelates the divalent metals (especially Mg2+) that catalyse the same reaction.
The commercial equivalents are specified as 1 mM sodium citrate, pH 6.4 +/- 0.2 (50 mL bottle format) or pH 6.5 +/- 0.1 (1 mL tube format). Either is fine.
Better than water: unbuffered water often sits at pH 5-6 from dissolved CO2 but has no chelator and no buffering, so a trace of alkali or a trace of Mg2+ can be enough to nick long RNA.

Better than TE for RNA: TE is pH 8.0, and the EDTA carries over into reactions where it will chelate the Mg2+ the enzyme needs. 1 mM citrate at 1/50 the concentration of TE's EDTA is much less inhibitory.
Store purified RNA at -80 C in single-use aliquots. Every freeze-thaw cycle costs integrity, and the RIN/28S:18S ratio is what you will see it in.
For long-term archival of precious RNA, storing the pellet under 75% ethanol at -80 C is an alternative to storing it in solution.

sources.title: Invitrogen (Ambion) THE RNA Storage Solution AM7000/AM7001 - 1 mM sodium citrate, pH 6.4 +/- 0.2 (50 mL) and pH 6.5 +/- 0.1 (1 mL tubes); nuclease-free
Gene Link - RNA Reconstitution Solution (1 mM sodium citrate pH 6.4)
PubChem CID 71474 - Trisodium citrate dihydrate, MW 294.10

recipes.name: RNA Stabilization / Tissue Storage Solution (RNAlater-style, ammonium sulfate)
recipes.summary: Saturated ammonium sulfate solution that permeates fresh tissue, precipitates and inactivates RNases, and holds RNA intact for days at room temperature or months refrigerated.

components.name: Ammonium sulfate
components.form: ammonium sulfate, (NH4)2SO4, CAS 7783-20-2, MW 132.14, anhydrous - molecular biology grade
components.note: 700 g PER LITRE OF THE AQUEOUS BASE, not per litre of finished solution. Adding 700 g of solid to 1 L of buffer gives noticeably more than 1 L of product; that is intended - the point is a saturated solution. Dissolve on a stirrer with gentle warming, adding the salt in ~100 g portions.
components.hazard: Low hazard; dust is a mild respiratory irritant.

components.name: Trisodium citrate dihydrate
components.note: 7.35 g per litre of aqueous base, or 25 mL of a 1 M sodium citrate stock (294 g/L) per litre.

components.name: EDTA disodium salt dihydrate
components.note: 7.44 g per litre of aqueous base, or 40 mL of 0.5 M EDTA pH 8.0 per litre.

components.name: Sulfuric acid (dilute, 1 M)
components.note: Titrant only, to bring the finished solution down to pH 5.2.
components.hazard: Corrosive. Add acid to solution, never the reverse.

recipes.solvent: nuclease-free water (make the citrate/EDTA base first, then dissolve the ammonium sulfate into it)
recipes.adjustPHWith: 1 M H2SO4 (the solution comes out near pH 7 as mixed; lower it with sulfuric acid)
recipes.sterilization: 0.2 um filtration to remove undissolved crystals and particulates (do not autoclave - ammonium salts release ammonia on heating)
recipes.storage: Room temperature or 4 C in a capped bottle
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year or more; crystals may form at 4 C - rewarm and redissolve, this is not spoilage
recipes.hazards: Low chemical hazard. The main practical risk is dilute sulfuric acid during pH adjustment. Note that this solution preserves RNA but does NOT inactivate infectious agents - treat tissue in it as biologically active.
notes: Published composition: 700 g/L ammonium sulfate, 25 mM sodium citrate, 20 mM EDTA, pH 5.2. The original patent describes a workable pH range of about 4-8; the commercial product is at pH 5.2.

One verified batch recipe (protocols.io, CC-BY): mix 1400 mL water + 60 mL of 0.5 M EDTA pH 8.0 + 37.5 mL of 1 M sodium citrate, then stir in 1050 g ammonium sulfate in 100 g portions, about 30 min on low heat, cool, filter through 0.2 um. A small amount of undissolved salt is acceptable - saturation is the goal.

That author notes the recipe as written lands near pH 7 and that pH can be lowered with H2SO4; use pH 5.2 to match the commercial formulation, or leave it near neutral if that is what your downstream method was validated on.

Tissue handling: use about 5-10 volumes of solution per volume of tissue, and cut tissue into pieces no more than about 0.5 cm thick so the solution can penetrate. Under-dosed or over-thick pieces are the usual cause of degraded RNA.
Do not freeze tissue in this solution before it has fully permeated (allow overnight at 4 C first); freezing a partly-penetrated block leaves an unprotected core.

The solution is a storage medium, not an extraction reagent - remove/blot the tissue before adding lysis reagent, since carried-over ammonium sulfate will interfere with column binding and with some lysis chemistries.
It is also used as a general RNase-inactivating soak for tissue collected in the field, which is why the recipe is widely reproduced.

sources.title: Malmstrom R (2015) RNAlater Recipe. protocols.io, DOI 10.17504/protocols.io.c56y9d
PubChem CID 6097028 - Ammonium sulfate, (NH4)2SO4, MW 132.14
PubChem CID 71474 - Trisodium citrate dihydrate, MW 294.10

recipes.name: MOPS RNA Gel Running Buffer, 10x (0.2 M MOPS / 20 mM NaOAc / 10 mM EDTA, pH 7.0)
recipes.summary: The running and gel buffer for denaturing formaldehyde-agarose RNA gels and northern blots; a near-neutral Good's buffer that keeps RNA from base hydrolysis during the run.

components.name: MOPS, free acid
components.note: 41.85 g per litre. The SODIUM SALT is MW 231.25 and cannot be substituted at equal mass - if you use the sodium salt you also will not need as much NaOH.

components.name: Sodium acetate, anhydrous
components.note: 1.64 g per litre. With sodium acetate TRIHYDRATE (MW 136.08, PubChem CID 23665404) use 2.72 g per litre instead.

components.name: EDTA disodium salt dihydrate
components.note: 3.72 g per litre, or 20 mL of 0.5 M EDTA pH 8.0 per litre.

components.name: NaOH
components.note: Titrant only - roughly 1 M to 5 M NaOH to bring the MOPS free acid to pH 7.0.
components.hazard: Corrosive; concentrated NaOH generates heat on dilution.

recipes.solvent: nuclease-free water (DEPC-treated-and-autoclaved water is traditional; certified nuclease-free water is equally good and safer to make)
recipes.adjustPHWith: NaOH
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filtration - DO NOT AUTOCLAVE if avoidable (autoclaved MOPS turns yellow and the coloured degradation products can inhibit enzymes)
recipes.storage: Room temperature or 4 C, protected from light in an amber or foil-wrapped bottle
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months; discard when the buffer has darkened past pale straw - yellowing means it has degraded
recipes.hazards: Sodium hydroxide during preparation is corrosive. The finished buffer is not hazardous.
notes: 1x working buffer (dilute 1:10): 20 mM MOPS, 2 mM sodium acetate, 1 mM EDTA, pH 7.0.

A widely used commercial variant is 0.2 M MOPS / 50 mM sodium acetate / 10 mM EDTA pH 7.0 (4.1 g anhydrous sodium acetate per litre instead of 1.64 g). Both run RNA fine; the acetate concentration mainly affects conductivity and therefore heating. Use whichever your northern-blot protocol specifies and stay consistent.

MOPS buffer yellows on light exposure and autoclaving. A dark bottle is a signal to remake it - the discolouration correlates with breakdown products that inhibit downstream enzymes on blotted material.

Recirculate or replace the running buffer during long runs. The buffer is only weakly buffered at 1x and the anode/cathode reservoirs drift apart in pH, which shows up as smeared, curved bands at the top of the gel.
This is a different reagent from the 1 M MOPS pH 7.0 general buffer stock already in this library, and different again from the MOPS SDS running buffer used for Bis-Tris protein gels.
MOPS pKa is 7.20 at 25 C, so pH 7.0 sits just inside the useful range. Do not use MOPS below about pH 6.5.

Do not add DEPC to a finished MOPS buffer: DEPC reacts with amines, and MOPS is a morpholine. Make the buffer with water that was already DEPC-treated and autoclaved, or with certified nuclease-free water.

sources.title: Gold Biotechnology - MOPS Running Buffer Preparation, 10X (200 mM) pH 7.0, 1 L: 41.85 g MOPS (MW 209.26), sodium acetate, 3.72 g EDTA (MW 372.24), pH to 7.0 with 1 M NaOH, filter rather than autoclave, store dark
Separation of long RNA by agarose-formaldehyde gel electrophoresis (Anal Biochem, PMC3755752) - 10x running buffer stated as 0.2 M MOPS-NaOH pH 7.0, 20 mM NaOAc, 10 mM EDTA
PubChem CID 70807 - MOPS, C7H15NO4S, MW 209.27

recipes.name: Denaturing Formaldehyde-Agarose Gel, 1.2% (100 mL)
recipes.summary: Denaturing agarose gel in which formaldehyde keeps RNA linear so that migration reflects length; the standard gel for RNA integrity checks and northern blots.

components.name: Agarose (molecular biology grade)
components.note: 1.2 g per 100 mL. Use 1.0% for transcripts above about 4 kb and 1.5% for short transcripts. Melt the agarose in the water FIRST, without buffer or formaldehyde.

components.name: MOPS RNA gel running buffer, 10x
components.note: 10 mL of 10x MOPS per 100 mL of gel. Add after the molten agarose has cooled to about 60-65 C.

components.name: Formaldehyde
components.form: 37% (w/v) formaldehyde solution (formalin), CH2O, CAS 50-00-0, MW 30.03, usually stabilised with 10-15% methanol; a 37% solution is about 12.3 M
components.note: 3 mL of 37% formaldehyde per 100 mL of gel. Add in a fume hood, to agarose that has cooled to 60-65 C, and pour the gel in the hood.
components.hazard: Formaldehyde is a category 1B carcinogen (GHS H350), a respiratory and skin sensitiser, and acutely irritating to eyes and airway. Handle and pour ONLY in a fume hood. Older bottles can contain formic acid and paraformaldehyde precipitate.

recipes.adjustPHWith: set by the 1x MOPS buffer; do not titrate the gel
recipes.sterilization: not applicable - use nuclease-free water, a clean gel tray and a comb wiped with an RNase decontamination solution
recipes.storage: Pour and use the same day; do not store poured formaldehyde gels
recipes.shelfLife: Same day
recipes.hazards: FORMALDEHYDE IS THE HAZARD HERE. It is a carcinogen and a sensitiser, and hot agarose releases it as vapour. Melt, mix, pour and set the gel inside a fume hood, and run the gel with the tank lid closed. Dispose of the gel and the running buffer as formaldehyde-contaminated waste, not down the sink, unless your institution's rules say otherwise.
notes: Recipe as written (100 mL): 1.2 g agarose + about 87 mL nuclease-free water, melt; cool to 60-65 C; add 10 mL 10x MOPS and 3 mL of 37% formaldehyde in the hood; pour.

A verified 50 mL version from the Barrick lab: 0.5 g agarose in 43.5 mL DEPC-treated water, then 1.5 mL of 37% formaldehyde and 5 mL of 10x MOPS, added in the hood. That is the same 3% (v/v) formaldehyde, about 0.37 M.

REDUCED VS CLASSIC FORMALDEHYDE. The classic Sambrook-era gel uses 2.2 M formaldehyde (about 18 mL of 37% per 100 mL). Modern practice commonly runs 5- to 6-fold less formaldehyde with equivalent resolution and far lower exposure. Both are in use; the low-formaldehyde version is the better default.

Running conditions: 1x MOPS as running buffer, about 5-6 V/cm (roughly 80-100 V on a mini gel). Run slowly - a hot gel gives fuzzy, smiling bands. Barrick lab runs at 80 V specifically to limit heating.
Recirculate or swap the running buffer on runs longer than about 2 h.
Denature samples at 65-70 C for 5-15 min in formaldehyde/formamide loading buffer, then chill on ice before loading.

Staining: ethidium bromide in the loading dye or a post-run SYBR-type stain both work. Barrick lab post-stains in 1x SYBR Gold in 1x TAE for 30 min. Formaldehyde in the gel quenches ethidium somewhat, so intact-RNA gels often look dimmer than DNA gels.

What good total RNA looks like on this gel: two sharp ribosomal bands with the larger (28S in eukaryotes, 23S in bacteria) roughly twice the intensity of the smaller. A smear below them, or a 1:1 ratio, means degradation.
Glyoxal/DMSO denaturing gels are the common formaldehyde-free alternative; they need a different buffer system (phosphate or BPTE) and a pH below 8 throughout.

sources.title: Barrick Lab - Denaturing Formaldehyde Gels for Verifying RNA size (0.5 g agarose in 43.5 mL DEPC water + 1.5 mL 37% formaldehyde + 5 mL 10x MOPS; 65 C 15 min denaturation then ice; run at 80 V; post-stain 1x SYBR Gold in 1x TAE 30 min)
Separation of long RNA by agarose-formaldehyde gel electrophoresis (Anal Biochem, PMC3755752) - traditional gel at 2.2 M formaldehyde, 6 V/cm, and a reduced-formaldehyde alternative
PubChem CID 712 - Formaldehyde, CH2O, MW 30.03

recipes.name: Formaldehyde/Formamide RNA Sample Loading Buffer (for denaturing agarose gels)
recipes.summary: Denaturing sample buffer that unfolds RNA secondary structure with formamide plus formaldehyde before loading on a formaldehyde-agarose gel.

components.name: Formamide (deionized)
components.form: formamide, CH3NO, CAS 75-12-7, MW 45.04 - molecular biology grade, deionized
components.note: 500 uL per mL. Deionize by stirring with mixed-bed ion-exchange resin for 30 min and filtering, or buy pre-deionized. Undeionized formamide accumulates formic acid and ammonium formate, which hydrolyse RNA.
components.hazard: Reproductive toxicant, GHS H360D - may damage the unborn child. Fume hood, nitrile gloves. This is the reason formamide is a controlled reagent in many institutions.

components.name: Formaldehyde
components.note: About 220 uL of 37% formaldehyde per mL of loading buffer.
components.hazard: Category 1B carcinogen and respiratory sensitiser - dispense in a fume hood.

components.name: MOPS RNA gel running buffer, 10x
components.note: 100 uL of 10x MOPS per mL.

components.name: Glycerol
components.note: 50 uL per mL - density agent so the sample sinks into the well.

components.name: EDTA (from 0.5 M, pH 8.0)
components.note: 2 uL of 0.5 M EDTA pH 8.0 per mL. Chelates Mg2+ so metal-dependent nucleases cannot act during the heat step.

components.name: Bromophenol blue
components.form: bromophenol blue, free acid C19H10Br4O5S, MW 670.0 (the sodium salt commonly sold is MW about 691.9)
components.note: Usually added as 25 uL of a 1% (w/v) aqueous stock per mL. Tracks roughly with small RNA - it will run off the gel long before large transcripts arrive.

components.name: Xylene cyanol FF
components.form: xylene cyanol FF, sodium salt, C25H27N2NaO6S2 (PubChem lists the cation form C25H28N2NaO7S2+, MW 555.6), CAS 2650-17-1
components.note: The slower of the two dyes; keep it if you need a marker still visible late in a long run. Optional - some formulations omit it.
components.hazard: Dye powder stains skin and clothing; avoid raising dust.

recipes.adjustPHWith: set by the 1x MOPS content; do not titrate
recipes.sterilization: none - prepare from nuclease-free components in a clean, RNase-free tube
recipes.storage: 20 C in small single-use aliquots, protected from light
recipes.shelfLife: Up to about 3 months at -20 C; make fresh if the formamide has yellowed or the buffer smells strongly of formic acid
recipes.hazards: TWO SERIOUS REAGENTS IN ONE TUBE. Formamide is a reproductive toxicant (H360D) - many institutions require a specific risk assessment and prohibit its use by pregnant workers. Formaldehyde is a carcinogen and sensitiser (H350). Prepare and dispense in a fume hood, wear nitrile gloves, and treat spent buffer and the gel as hazardous waste.
notes: Working composition: 50% formamide, 2.7 M formaldehyde, 1x MOPS running buffer, 5% glycerol, 1 mM EDTA, 0.025% bromophenol blue, 0.025% xylene cyanol FF.

A convenient 1.5 mL batch equivalent to the classic recipe: 720 uL deionized formamide, 260 uL of 37% formaldehyde, 160 uL of 10x MOPS, 100 uL glycerol, 80 uL of 10% (w/v) bromophenol blue, 180 uL nuclease-free water. Marked uncertain only in the sense that different published versions round these volumes differently - the concentrations above are what matters.

Typical use: mix RNA (up to a few ug in a small volume) with 2-3 volumes of loading buffer, heat 65-70 C for 5-15 min, then move IMMEDIATELY to ice. Letting denatured samples cool slowly on the bench lets secondary structure re-form and produces spurious bands.

Ethidium bromide can be added to the loading buffer (about 0.1 volume of a 1 mg/mL stock, or to about 40 ug/mL) so bands are visible without post-staining. Post-staining with a SYBR-type dye avoids adding a mutagen to every sample and generally gives cleaner ribosomal bands.

Deionized formamide matters. Formamide hydrolyses on storage to formic acid and ammonium formate; acidic formamide will chew up the RNA you are trying to visualise. Store formamide at 4 C in the dark and aliquot it.
Formamide has a melting point near 2-3 C, so a -20 C aliquot needs to be fully thawed and mixed before use, not just softened.
This buffer is for FORMALDEHYDE-AGAROSE gels. For denaturing polyacrylamide/urea gels use the 2x formamide RNA loading dye instead - it has no formaldehyde and much more formamide.

sources.title: Separation of long RNA by agarose-formaldehyde gel electrophoresis (Anal Biochem, PMC3755752) - sample loading buffer stated as 50% formamide, 2.7 M formaldehyde, 1x running buffer, 5% glycerol, 1 mM EDTA, 0.025% bromophenol blue, 0.025% xylene cyanol FF; denature 70 C, 5 min
Barrick Lab - Denaturing Formaldehyde Gels (denature 65 C 15 min, then immediately to ice)
PubChem CID 713 - Formamide, CH3NO, MW 45.04

recipes.name: RNA Loading Dye, 2x (formamide-based, formaldehyde-free)
recipes.summary: High-formamide denaturing loading dye for urea-PAGE and quick denaturing agarose checks of RNA; no formaldehyde, so it can be made and used outside a fume hood.

components.name: Formamide (deionized)
components.form: formamide, CH3NO, CAS 75-12-7, MW 45.04, deionized molecular-biology grade
components.note: 950 uL per mL. At 95% there is essentially no water in this buffer - it is the formamide that does the denaturing.
components.hazard: Reproductive toxicant (GHS H360D). Gloves; prepare in a fume hood; follow local rules for formamide use.

components.name: SDS
components.form: sodium dodecyl sulfate (sodium lauryl sulfate), C12H25NaO4S, CAS 151-21-3, MW 288.38
components.note: Add as 2.5 uL of a 10% (w/v) SDS stock per mL. Helps strip residual protein off the RNA.
components.hazard: Dry SDS powder is a serious respiratory irritant - weigh in a hood or buy the 10% or 20% solution.

components.name: Bromophenol blue
components.form: bromophenol blue, free acid C19H10Br4O5S, MW 670.0 (sodium salt about 691.9)
components.note: Fast dye. On a 6-8% urea gel it runs roughly with 20-30 nt RNA.

components.name: Xylene cyanol FF
components.form: xylene cyanol FF, sodium salt, CAS 2650-17-1 (PubChem cation form MW 555.6)
components.note: Slow dye. On a 6-8% urea gel it runs roughly with 100-130 nt RNA. The two dyes together bracket the small-RNA size range.

components.name: EDTA (from 0.5 M, pH 8.0)
components.note: 1 uL of 0.5 M EDTA pH 8.0 per mL. Low, deliberately - just enough to chelate trace Mg2+ during the heat step.

recipes.solvent: nuclease-free water (only about 5% of the volume) - add the aqueous stocks to the formamide, not the reverse
recipes.sterilization: none - prepare from nuclease-free components
recipes.storage: 20 C in single-use aliquots, protected from light
recipes.shelfLife: 6-12 months at -20 C; discard if the formamide has yellowed
recipes.hazards: Formamide is a reproductive toxicant (H360D) and is the main hazard. Handle with gloves in a fume hood and dispose of as chemical waste. SDS powder, if you weigh it yourself, is a respiratory irritant.
notes: Published composition of the 95%-formamide commercial dye: 95% formamide, 0.025% SDS, 0.025% bromophenol blue, 0.025% xylene cyanol FF, 0.5 mM EDTA. Some commercial versions also contain 0.025% ethidium bromide - omit it unless you specifically want in-dye staining, and remember it is a mutagen.

A lower-formamide commercial alternative is 47.5% formamide, 0.01% SDS, 0.01% bromophenol blue, 0.005% xylene cyanol FF, 0.5 mM EDTA. Both are sold as 2x; the 47.5% version is easier on the wells of an agarose gel.
Use: mix 1 volume of RNA sample with 1 volume of 2x dye, heat 65-70 C for 5-10 min, then transfer directly to ice and load promptly.
This dye is formaldehyde-free, so it is the practical choice for urea-polyacrylamide gels, for small RNA/miRNA work, and for a fast integrity check when you do not want to pour a formaldehyde gel.
At 95% formamide the sample is barely denser than the running buffer even with the dyes present; load slowly, or use the 47.5% version for agarose wells.
Deionize the formamide (mixed-bed resin, 30 min stirring, filter) or buy it pre-deionized. Old formamide is acidic and will degrade RNA during the heat step.
Do not use this buffer on a native gel where you want to preserve RNA structure or an RNA-protein complex - it is fully denaturing.

sources.title: Thermo Scientific 2X RNA Loading Dye R0641 user guide (MAN0013155) - 95% formamide, 0.025% SDS, 0.025% bromophenol blue, 0.025% xylene cyanol FF, 0.025% ethidium bromide, 0.5 mM EDTA
New England Biolabs B0363 RNA Loading Dye (2X) - 47.5% formamide, 0.01% SDS, 0.01% bromophenol blue, 0.005% xylene cyanol, 0.5 mM EDTA
PubChem CID 3423265 - Sodium dodecyl sulfate, MW 288.38

recipes.name: DNase I Reaction Buffer, 10x (RNase-free)
recipes.summary: Mg2+/Ca2+ Tris buffer for RNase-free DNase I digestion of genomic DNA carryover in RNA preps, before reverse transcription or northern analysis.

components.name: Tris-Cl, pH 7.6 (from 1 M stock)
components.form: tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane, C4H11NO3, MW 121.14 (free base), titrated to pH 7.6 with HCl; the hydrochloride salt is MW 157.59
components.note: 100 mL of 1 M Tris-Cl pH 7.6 per litre. Tris pH is strongly temperature dependent (about -0.028 pH units per degree C) - set the pH at 25 C and note that the reaction at 37 C is nearer pH 7.3.

components.name: MgCl2 (from 1 M stock)
components.form: magnesium chloride hexahydrate, MgCl2*6H2O, CAS 7791-18-6, MW 203.30 (anhydrous MgCl2 is MW 95.21 - do not substitute by mass)
components.note: 25 mL of 1 M MgCl2 per litre. A 1 M stock is 203.3 g of the hexahydrate per litre. Mg2+ is the catalytic metal for DNase I.

components.name: CaCl2 (from 1 M stock)
components.form: calcium chloride dihydrate, CaCl2*2H2O, CAS 10035-04-8, MW 147.01 (anhydrous CaCl2 is MW 110.98)
components.note: 5 mL of 1 M CaCl2 per litre. A 1 M stock is 147.0 g of the dihydrate per litre. Ca2+ stabilises DNase I against proteolysis and self-inactivation - it is not optional.

recipes.adjustPHWith: set by the Tris-Cl stock (pH measured at 25 C); do not re-titrate after adding the divalent salts or you will precipitate them
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filtration into RNase-free aliquots (do not autoclave - avoid concentrating the divalent salts)
recipes.storage: 20 C in single-use aliquots; short-term 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year at -20 C; discard any aliquot that has become cloudy (precipitated Mg/Ca salts)
recipes.hazards: None significant. The enzyme itself is a protein and a potential sensitiser as an aerosol.
notes: Working (1x) composition: 10 mM Tris-Cl pH 7.6, 2.5 mM MgCl2, 0.5 mM CaCl2.

A common alternative supplier formulation is 100 mM Tris-Cl pH 7.5, 25 mM MgCl2, 1 mM CaCl2 at 10x. Both work; match whichever buffer shipped with your enzyme, because DNase I unit definitions are tied to a specific buffer.
Typical digestion: about 1 unit of RNase-free DNase I per ug of RNA, 10-30 min at 37 C (some formulations are run at room temperature for 10 min to limit RNA nicking).

STOPPING THE REACTION IS THE STEP PEOPLE GET WRONG. Heat-inactivating DNase I while Mg2+ is still present causes metal-catalysed hydrolysis of the RNA. Add EDTA to about 5 mM final BEFORE heating, then 10 min at 75 C. Alternatively use a resin-based inactivation reagent or a fresh column cleanup.
Excess EDTA carried into the reverse transcription reaction will chelate the Mg2+ that RT needs. Keep the DNase step small relative to the RT volume, or clean up afterwards.
DNase I is inhibited by EDTA and by chelators generally, and is denatured by guanidinium - do the digest after your RNA is out of the lysis reagent, not in it.
Confirm the digest worked with a no-RT control in your qPCR. A signal in the minus-RT well means DNA is still there, regardless of what the gel looks like.
DNase I is also sold in an engineered thermostable/EDTA-free format with its own buffer; that is a different reagent with different inactivation conditions.

sources.title: New England Biolabs B0303 DNase I Reaction Buffer (10X) - 100 mM Tris-HCl pH 7.6 at 25 C, 25 mM MgCl2, 5 mM CaCl2
Thermo Scientific 10X Reaction Buffer with MgCl2 for DNase I, RNase-free (MAN0011941) - 100 mM Tris-HCl pH 7.5, 25 mM MgCl2, 1 mM CaCl2
PubChem CID 24644 - Magnesium chloride hexahydrate, MW 203.30
PubChem CID 6093260 - Calcium chloride dihydrate, MW 147.01

recipes.name: Reverse Transcription First-Strand Buffer, 5x (M-MLV type)
recipes.summary: The standard 5x reaction buffer supplied with M-MLV-derived reverse transcriptases: Tris pH 8.3, potassium chloride and magnesium chloride, with DTT added separately.

components.name: Tris-Cl, pH 8.3 (from 1 M stock)
components.form: tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane, C4H11NO3, MW 121.14 (free base), titrated to pH 8.3 with HCl
components.note: 250 mL of 1 M Tris-Cl pH 8.3 per litre. Set and record the pH at 25 C. Tris drifts about -0.028 pH units per degree C, so a buffer at pH 8.3 at 25 C is nearer pH 7.8 at 42 C and about 7.6 at 50 C - which is the pH the enzyme actually sees, and is why 8.3 at room temperature is the specification.

components.name: Potassium chloride
components.note: 27.96 g per litre, or 187.5 mL of a 2 M KCl stock per litre. Gives 75 mM KCl in the 1x reaction.

components.name: MgCl2 (from 1 M stock)
components.form: magnesium chloride hexahydrate, MgCl2*6H2O, MW 203.30 (anhydrous MgCl2 is MW 95.21)
components.note: 15 mL of 1 M MgCl2 per litre. Gives 3 mM MgCl2 in the 1x reaction.

recipes.adjustPHWith: set by the Tris-Cl stock at 25 C; do not re-titrate after adding MgCl2
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filtration into RNase-free single-use aliquots
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year at -20 C; avoid repeated freeze-thaw - aliquot into single-reaction-set volumes
recipes.hazards: None significant.
notes: Published composition: 250 mM Tris-HCl (pH 8.3), 375 mM KCl, 15 mM MgCl2. In the 1x reaction that is 50 mM Tris pH 8.3, 75 mM KCl, 3 mM MgCl2.

DTT IS NOT IN THIS BUFFER and neither are dNTPs. DTT is supplied separately as a 0.1 M stock and added to 5-10 mM in the final reaction; it oxidises in solution, so keep it frozen in aliquots and add it fresh.

An AMV-type 5x buffer is a different formulation - typically 250 mM Tris-HCl pH 8.3, 250 mM KCl, 50 mM MgCl2, 50 mM DTT and 2.5 mM spermidine, with the DTT already included. Do not mix an AMV buffer with an M-MLV enzyme and expect the stated Mg2+.

Magnesium is the parameter to tune if a reverse transcription is failing on a structured template. 3 mM is the default; some GC-rich or highly structured RNAs do better at 4-6 mM, and too much Mg2+ raises mispriming.
Potassium chloride at 75 mM stabilises primer annealing. Higher KCl inhibits M-MLV RT noticeably above about 100 mM.

This buffer is compatible with the whole M-MLV-derived family (native M-MLV and the RNase H-minus engineered versions used for full-length cDNA). The RNase H-minus enzymes are what let you run at 50 C or above, which is the usual fix for structured templates.
Do not autoclave. Filter-sterilise and treat the bottle as RNA-dedicated.

sources.title: Invitrogen M-MLV Reverse Transcriptase Buffer, Cat. 18057-018 product manual - 5X buffer: 250 mM Tris-HCl (pH 8.3), 375 mM KCl, 15 mM MgCl2; does not contain DTT, nucleotides or actinomycin D
PubChem CID 4873 - Potassium chloride, KCl, MW 74.55
PubChem CID 6503 - Tris (tromethamine), C4H11NO3, MW 121.14

recipes.name: First-Strand cDNA Synthesis Reaction Mix (20 uL)
recipes.summary: Assembly sheet for a standard 20 uL reverse transcription: buffer, dNTPs, DTT, primer, RNase inhibitor, reverse transcriptase and template RNA, expressed as stock-to-final dilutions.

components.name: First-strand buffer, 5x
components.note: 4 uL per 20 uL reaction. 1x = 50 mM Tris pH 8.3, 75 mM KCl, 3 mM MgCl2.

components.name: dNTP mix (10 mM each dNTP)
components.form: equimolar dATP/dCTP/dGTP/dTTP, supplied as sodium salts at pH 7-7.5; dATP free acid is C10H16N5O12P3, MW 491.18
components.note: 1 uL per 20 uL reaction. Buy the neutralised 10 mM mix rather than making it from acidic individual stocks - unbuffered dNTPs drop the reaction pH and chelate Mg2+. Store at -20 C in small aliquots; dNTPs are the component most often killed by freeze-thaw.

components.name: DTT (from 0.1 M stock)
components.note: 1 uL per 20 uL reaction (2 uL for 10 mM final, which some protocols specify). Keeps the enzyme's cysteines reduced. DTT oxidises in solution and on freeze-thaw - keep single-use aliquots at -20 C.
components.hazard: Irritant; strong sulfurous odour.

components.name: Primer: oligo(dT)20 (from 50 uM stock)
components.note: 1 uL per 20 uL reaction. Alternatives at the same volume: random hexamers at 50-250 ng per reaction, or a gene-specific primer at 0.5-2 uM. Oligo(dT) primes only polyadenylated RNA - it is the wrong choice for bacterial RNA, for most non-coding RNA, and for degraded samples where the poly(A) tail is far from the region of interest.

components.name: RNase inhibitor (from 40 U/uL stock)
components.note: 1 uL per 20 uL reaction. Placental/recombinant RNase inhibitor proteins require reducing conditions - they are inactivated if the DTT is omitted or has oxidised, which is a quiet way to lose an experiment.

components.name: Reverse transcriptase (from 200 U/uL stock)
components.note: 1 uL per 20 uL reaction (200 U). Add last, on ice, and mix gently - do not vortex enzyme-containing mixes.

components.name: Template RNA
components.note: Typically 10 pg to 5 ug of total RNA, or 10 pg to 500 ng of poly(A) RNA, per 20 uL reaction. Must be free of guanidinium, phenol, ethanol and excess EDTA.

components.name: Nuclease-free water
components.note: To 20 uL.

recipes.adjustPHWith: set by the 5x first-strand buffer; never titrate a reaction mix
recipes.sterilization: not applicable - assemble on ice in nuclease-free tubes with filter tips
recipes.storage: Assemble fresh and run immediately; hold finished cDNA at -20 C (short term) or -80 C
recipes.shelfLife: Use the assembled reaction immediately; cDNA is stable for months at -20 C
recipes.hazards: None significant. DTT is an irritant with a strong odour.
notes: Suggested order of assembly: anneal RNA + primer + dNTPs at 65 C for 5 min and snap-cool on ice; then add buffer, DTT and RNase inhibitor; then add the enzyme last.

Incubation: 37-42 C for native M-MLV; 50-55 C for the RNase H-minus engineered enzymes, which is the standard fix for GC-rich or structured templates. If you used random hexamers, add a 10 min 25 C extension step first so the short primers stay bound.
Inactivate at 70 C for 15 min. Do not heat native M-MLV above about 70 C.
Optional cleanup: 2 units of RNase H at 37 C for 20 min removes the RNA strand of the RNA:DNA hybrid. This matters for long-target PCR from the cDNA; it is unnecessary for short qPCR amplicons.

ALWAYS RUN A NO-RT CONTROL - the identical reaction with water in place of the enzyme. Without it you cannot distinguish cDNA signal from genomic DNA carryover, and no amount of DNase treatment substitutes for the control.
Carry no more than about 10% of the reaction volume forward into a qPCR; RT buffer components (KCl, DTT, excess dNTPs) inhibit Taq at higher fractions.
Inhibitor watch-list for RT: guanidinium salts, phenol, ethanol, SDS above about 0.01%, EDTA above about 1 mM, heparin, haem, humic acids and high concentrations of the sample matrix itself.

Reverse transcription is the least reproducible step in most RNA workflows. Fix the input RNA mass across samples, use the same primer strategy throughout an experiment, and make a single master mix for all reactions in a batch.

sources.title: Thermo Scientific First Strand cDNA Synthesis Kit K1612 user guide (MAN0012612) - 20 uL reaction assembly, component volumes, RNA input range, incubation and inactivation conditions
Invitrogen M-MLV Reverse Transcriptase Buffer, Cat. 18057-018 product manual - 5X buffer composition; DTT supplied separately and used at 10 mM final
PubChem CID 439196 - Dithiothreitol, C4H10O2S2, MW 154.25
PubChem CID 15993 - dATP, C10H16N5O12P3, MW 491.18

recipes.name: YPD Broth (Yeast extract - Peptone - Dextrose)
recipes.summary: Standard rich medium for routine growth of Saccharomyces cerevisiae and most other budding/fission yeasts; the yeast equivalent of LB.

components.name: Yeast extract
components.note: 1% (w/v). Autolysate of Saccharomyces; supplies B vitamins, nucleotides and amino acids.

components.name: Peptone
components.note: 2% (w/v). Bacto Peptone (enzymatic digest of animal protein) is the traditional choice; tryptone/casein peptone also works but colony colour of ade2 strains can differ between lots.

components.name: D-Glucose (dextrose)
components.note: 2% (w/v) = 111 mM. Anhydrous D-glucose MW 180.16; if using glucose monohydrate (MW 198.17) weigh 22.0 g/L. Best added after autoclaving as 100 mL of sterile 20% (w/v) or 50 mL of sterile 40% (w/v) stock.

components.name: Adenine hemisulfate
components.note: Form: adenine hemisulfate ((C5H5N5)2 - H2SO4), MW 368.34. OPTIONAL - this is what turns YPD into YPAD. 0.003% (w/v) = 30 mg/L, conveniently added as 15 mL of a 0.2% (w/v) adenine hemisulfate stock per litre. Many labs instead use 40 mg/L or 100-125 mg/L. Adenine hemisulfate survives autoclaving. Free adenine base is MW 135.13 (PubChem CID 190); the commercial hemisulfate is often sold as the dihydrate, MW ~404.4 - check the label before weighing.

recipes.adjustPHWith: 1 N NaOH or 1 N HCl before autoclaving; most labs leave it unadjusted (it lands near 6.0-6.5)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave the yeast extract/peptone base in 900 mL at 121 C for 15-20 min, cool below 60 C, then add 100 mL of separately sterilized 20% glucose
recipes.storage: Sterile broth at room temperature or 4 C, sealed
recipes.shelfLife: Several months if it stays clear; discard on any turbidity or film
recipes.hazards: pH is adjusted with 1 N NaOH or 1 N HCl before autoclaving; most labs leave it unadjusted (it lands near 6.0-6.5) — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Autoclaving glucose together with peptone drives Maillard browning: the medium darkens and performs worse. If you must autoclave in one pot, use 121 C for 15 min only, never longer or hotter, and never re-autoclave.

Ade2 mutants accumulate a red pigment on adenine-limiting medium. Adding adenine (YPAD) relieves the growth lag and keeps colonies white/pink; leaving it out is how you score the red/white colour phenotype.
Typical S. cerevisiae doubling time in YPD at 30 C is roughly 90 min; growth in synthetic (SD) medium is about twice as slow.

Dominant drug-marker selection is done in YPD, not in synthetic medium, because synthetic media antagonise several of these drugs. Working concentrations used with the standard MX cassettes: G418/geneticin 200 mg/L (kanMX), hygromycin B 200 mg/L (hphMX), nourseothricin 100 mg/L (natMX), phleomycin 10 mg/L (bleMX).
For GAL induction replace the glucose with 2% galactose (YPGal) or 2% galactose + 1% raffinose; galactose must be low in contaminating glucose (< 0.01%) or the GAL1/GAL10 promoters stay repressed.
Long-term strain storage: YPD plus 25% (v/v) glycerol at -70 C or below.
Add 2% (w/v) agar for plates - see the ypd-agar recipe.

recipes.name: YPD Agar Plates (2% agar)
recipes.summary: Non-selective rich plates for streaking, colony purification, replica plating and dominant drug-marker selection in yeast.

components.name: Agar
components.note: 2% (w/v) is the yeast standard - higher than the 1.5% normally used for bacteria, because yeast plates are incubated warm for several days and replica-plated. Bacteriological grade agar; do not use agarose.

components.name: D-Glucose (dextrose)
components.note: 2% (w/v). Add as 100 mL of sterile 20% (w/v) glucose per litre after the molten agar has cooled to ~55-60 C.

components.name: Adenine hemisulfate
components.note: Form: adenine hemisulfate ((C5H5N5)2 - H2SO4), MW 368.34. OPTIONAL (YPAD plates). 30 mg/L; withhold it if you want to score the red ade2 colony colour.

recipes.adjustPHWith: 1 N NaOH before autoclaving; routinely left unadjusted
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min, liquid cycle; swirl gently (do not shake - bubbles) after autoclaving
recipes.storage: Poured plates inverted at 4 C in a sealed sleeve, in the dark
recipes.shelfLife: About 1 month at 4 C; drug-containing plates 2-4 weeks
recipes.hazards: pH is adjusted with 1 N NaOH before autoclaving; routinely left unadjusted — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: One litre gives roughly 40 plates at 25 mL each in 90-100 mm dishes.
Cool to 55-60 C before adding glucose and any heat-labile drug; G418, hygromycin B and nourseothricin all tolerate 55 C briefly but not autoclaving.
Drug concentrations for the MX marker cassettes in YPD agar: G418 200 mg/L, hygromycin B 200 mg/L, nourseothricin 100 mg/L, phleomycin 10 mg/L.
Dry freshly poured plates lid-ajar in a laminar flow hood for 20-30 min before use if you plan to spread liquid transformations on them.
Do not re-melt and re-pour more than once; repeated melting hydrolyses the agar and the gel gets soft.
Yeast colonies take 2-3 days at 30 C to reach 1-2 mm. Use colonies less than about 4-8 weeks old as working stock.
