Clontech/Takara Yeast Protocols Handbook PT3024-1 - YPD agar, plate storage and handling
Yeast Peptone Dextrose (YPD) medium (protocols.io)

recipes.name: Yeast Nitrogen Base (YNB) with Ammonium Sulfate, 10x stock
recipes.summary: Concentrated salt/vitamin/trace-element base that supplies everything a prototrophic yeast needs except a carbon source and amino acids; the foundation of all SD and SC media.

components.name: Yeast nitrogen base without amino acids and without ammonium sulfate
components.note: 10x. Gives 1.7 g/L at 1x. This is the salts + vitamins + trace elements fraction only.

components.name: Ammonium sulfate
components.note: Form: (NH4)2SO4, anhydrous, MW 132.14. 10x. Gives 5.0 g/L (37.8 mM) at 1x - the sole nitrogen source in standard SD medium.

recipes.adjustPHWith: not adjusted as a concentrate; the finished 1x medium is usually taken to pH 5.8 with NaOH
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter sterilize the 10x concentrate - the vitamins (thiamine, riboflavin, folic acid, PABA) are heat-labile
recipes.storage: 4 C, protected from light
recipes.shelfLife: About 6 months at 4 C; discard if it goes cloudy or throws a precipitate that will not redissolve
recipes.hazards: pH is adjusted with not adjusted as a concentrate; the finished 1x medium is usually taken to pH 5.8 with NaOH — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: The one-bag alternative: 'YNB without amino acids' already contains the ammonium sulfate, and is used at 6.7 g/L (1x) or 67 g/L (10x). 6.7 = 1.7 + 5.0.

Published 1x composition of YNB without amino acids (per litre). Nitrogen source: ammonium sulfate 5.0 g. Salts: potassium phosphate monobasic 1.0 g, magnesium sulfate 0.5 g, sodium chloride 0.1 g, calcium chloride 0.1 g. Vitamins: inositol 2.0 mg, calcium pantothenate 400 ug, nicotinic acid 400 ug, pyridoxine HCl 400 ug, thiamine HCl 400 ug, p-aminobenzoic acid 200 ug, riboflavin 200 ug, biotin 2.0 ug, folic acid 2.0 ug. Trace elements: boric acid 500 ug, manganese sulfate 400 ug, zinc sulfate 400 ug, ferric chloride 200 ug, sodium molybdate 200 ug, potassium iodide 100 ug, copper sulfate 40 ug.

Drop the ammonium sulfate and substitute another nitrogen source (monosodium glutamate, proline, urea, allantoin) when you need to select on nitrogen catabolite repression or when using a marker that is antagonised by ammonium.

For amdSYM (acetamide) or dsdA (D-serine) dominant markers the ammonium sulfate must be replaced entirely - a documented recipe uses 1.7 g/L YNB without amino acids and without ammonium sulfate plus 6.6 g/L potassium sulfate, then 0.06% acetamide or 0.2% D-serine as the nitrogen source.
In practice most labs autoclave YNB along with the rest of the 1x medium and get away with it; the strictly correct route for a concentrate that is stored is filtration.

PubChem CID 516951 = potassium phosphate monobasic (KH2PO4, MW 136.09); CID 24843 = magnesium sulfate heptahydrate (MW 246.48); CID 6093260 = calcium chloride dihydrate (MW 147.01) - relevant only if you are building YNB from scratch.

sources.title: Sigma-Aldrich Y0626 Yeast Nitrogen Base Without Amino Acids - product information sheet with full formulation table
Sigma-Aldrich Y1251 Yeast Nitrogen Base Without Amino Acids and Ammonium Sulfate (1.7 g/L base)
EasyClone 2.0 user manual - YNB-amd / D-ser media using ammonium-free YNB plus potassium sulfate (hosted by Addgene)
PubChem CID 6097028, Ammonium sulfate - molecular weight 132.14

recipes.name: SD Minimal Medium (Synthetic Dextrose), broth or plates
recipes.summary: Defined minimal medium containing only YNB and glucose; supports prototrophs and nothing else, and is the base to which dropout supplements are added for auxotrophic marker selection.

components.name: Yeast nitrogen base without amino acids (with ammonium sulfate)
components.note: Or 100 mL/L of the 10x YNB+AS stock. Equivalent to 1.7 g/L YNB base + 5.0 g/L ammonium sulfate.

components.name: D-Glucose (dextrose)
components.note: 2% (w/v) = 111 mM. Add as 50 mL of sterile 40% (w/v) glucose per litre after autoclaving.

components.name: Agar
components.note: OPTIONAL - plates only. 2% (w/v).

recipes.adjustPHWith: 1 N NaOH (YNB alone sits near pH 5.4); adjust before autoclaving
recipes.sterilization: autoclave the YNB (+/- agar) in 850-950 mL at 121 C for 15 min, cool to ~55 C, then add sterile glucose and any supplements
recipes.storage: Broth at room temperature or 4 C; plates inverted at 4 C in a sleeve
recipes.shelfLife: Broth 2-3 months; plates 1-2 months at 4 C
recipes.hazards: pH is adjusted with 1 N NaOH (YNB alone sits near pH 5.4); adjust before autoclaving — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Nearly all laboratory S. cerevisiae strains are auxotrophic (ura3, leu2, his3, trp1, lys2, ade2, met15...) and will NOT grow on plain SD. Add a dropout supplement - see the sd-dropout-selective-medium recipe.
Do not autoclave the sugar with the medium for longer or hotter than 121 C/15 min; caramelised sugar depresses growth and darkens the plates.
Substitute 2% galactose (+/- 1% raffinose) for glucose when inducing from GAL promoters; substitute 2% glycerol + 2% ethanol for non-fermentable carbon (respiration/petite testing).
Doubling time on SD is roughly twice that on YPD. Plan on 3-5 days at 30 C for transformant colonies to appear on selective SD plates.
SD plates are much more prone to drying out than YPD; sleeve them.
A high-phosphate variant (10x BU salts: 70 g/L Na2HPO4-7H2O + 30 g/L NaH2PO4, pH 7, 100 mL/L) is added when the medium must sit near pH 7 for in-plate beta-galactosidase assays.

sources.title: Clontech/Takara Yeast Protocols Handbook PT3024-1 - SD medium (6.7 g/L YNB, pH 5.8, 2% carbon source) and BU salts
Sigma-Aldrich Y0626 Yeast Nitrogen Base Without Amino Acids - use at 6.7 g/L with 2% carbon source
EasyClone 2.0 user manual - SC/SD selection media (hosted by Addgene)
PubChem CID 5793, D-Glucose - molecular weight 180.16

recipes.name: 10x Dropout (DO) Solution / Synthetic Complete supplement
recipes.summary: Aqueous 10x mixture of amino acids and nucleobases added to SD base; omit one or more components to select for the corresponding auxotrophic marker (URA3, LEU2, HIS3, TRP1, LYS2, ADE2, MET15).

components.name: Adenine hemisulfate
components.note: Form: adenine hemisulfate ((C5H5N5)2 - H2SO4), MW 368.34. 10x; 20 mg/L at 1x. OMIT for SD/-Ade (ADE2 selection). Commercial material is often the dihydrate (MW ~404.4).

components.name: L-Arginine monohydrochloride
components.note: Form: L-arginine - HCl, MW 210.66. 10x; 20 mg/L at 1x. Free base MW 174.20 (PubChem CID 6322). Omitted only for canavanine-resistance selection.

components.name: L-Histidine monohydrochloride monohydrate
components.note: Form: L-histidine - HCl - H2O, MW 209.63. 10x; 20 mg/L at 1x. OMIT for SD/-His (HIS3 selection). Free base MW 155.15.

components.name: L-Isoleucine
components.note: Form: free amino acid, MW 131.17. 10x; 30 mg/L at 1x.

components.name: L-Leucine
components.note: Form: free amino acid, MW 131.17. 10x; 100 mg/L at 1x. OMIT for SD/-Leu (LEU2 selection). Leucine is deliberately the highest amino acid because leu2 strains take it up poorly; Sherman's classic SC table uses 30 mg/L instead - the higher 100 mg/L figure is the two-hybrid/Clontech convention. Both are in common use, so state which you used.

components.name: L-Lysine monohydrochloride
components.note: Form: L-lysine - HCl, MW 182.65. 10x; 30 mg/L at 1x. OMIT for SD/-Lys (LYS2 selection).

components.name: L-Methionine
components.note: Form: free amino acid, MW 149.21. 10x; 20 mg/L at 1x. OMIT for SD/-Met (MET15/MET17 selection).

components.name: L-Phenylalanine
components.note: Form: free amino acid, MW 165.19. 10x; 50 mg/L at 1x.

components.name: L-Threonine
components.note: Form: free amino acid, MW 119.12. 10x; 200 mg/L at 1x.

components.name: L-Tryptophan
components.note: Form: free amino acid, MW 204.22. 10x; 20 mg/L at 1x. OMIT for SD/-Trp (TRP1 selection). Light-sensitive and degrades on prolonged autoclaving - keep the stock dark.

components.name: L-Tyrosine
components.note: Form: free amino acid, MW 181.19. 10x; 30 mg/L at 1x. Poorly soluble in neutral water - it is the component that makes the 10x stock look hazy. Dissolve it first with gentle heat or a few drops of 1 N NaOH.

components.name: Uracil
components.note: Form: uracil, MW 112.09. 10x; 20 mg/L at 1x. OMIT for SD/-Ura (URA3 selection). Aqueous solubility is only about 3.6 g/L at 25 C, so 0.2 g/L dissolves easily but a 100x uracil stock is close to the limit.

components.name: L-Valine
components.note: Form: free amino acid, MW 117.15. 10x; 150 mg/L at 1x.

components.name: L-Aspartic acid
components.note: Form: free amino acid, MW 133.10. OPTIONAL - add only when selecting on SD/-Met, where the omission of methionine otherwise limits growth. 10x; 100 mg/L at 1x. Aspartate, glutamate and serine are left out of the standard mix because they acidify the medium and yeast makes them itself.

recipes.adjustPHWith: not adjusted; a few drops of 1 N NaOH may be used to get tyrosine into solution
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C 15 min, or 0.22 um filter (preferred if tryptophan is present)
recipes.storage: 4 C, in the dark
recipes.shelfLife: Up to 1 year at 4 C; discard if microbial growth or an irreversible precipitate appears
recipes.hazards: pH is adjusted with not adjusted; a few drops of 1 N NaOH may be used to get tyrosine into solution — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Use 100 mL of the appropriate 10x DO solution per litre of SD medium.
Naming convention: SD/-Leu/-Trp means the medium contains everything in the list EXCEPT leucine and tryptophan. Anything not named as missing is present.

Make one master 'complete' mix and separate dropout mixes, or - much less error-prone in a small lab - make single 100x stocks of each nutrient and pipette the combination you need (see the yeast-supplement-stocks-100x recipe).

The alternative dry-powder route (Kaiser/CSM style) blends the solids and adds ~2 g of the powder per litre of medium; the dry mix keeps far longer than an aqueous stock but is hard to blend homogeneously without a dedicated dry blender. Dry mixes typically also carry alanine, asparagine, cysteine, glutamine, glycine, proline, serine, myo-inositol and p-aminobenzoic acid, which the aqueous 10x DO above does not.
Do not substitute D-amino acids; yeast cannot use them.
Adenine is included as the hemisulfate because free adenine base dissolves badly.

sources.title: Clontech/Takara Yeast Protocols Handbook PT3024-1 - Appendix C, 10x Dropout (DO) Solution composition table
United States Biological D9515 Drop-out Mix Complete (Kaiser-format dry powder, 2 g per litre) - data sheet

PubChem CID 9449 (adenine hemisulfate, 368.34), CID 66250 (L-arginine HCl, 210.66), CID 165377 (L-histidine HCl monohydrate, 209.63), CID 6106 (L-leucine, 131.17), CID 69568 (L-lysine HCl, 182.65), CID 1174 (uracil, 112.09)

recipes.name: SD Dropout Selective Medium (SD/-Ura, -Leu, -His, -Trp), broth or plates
recipes.summary: SD base plus a dropout supplement lacking one or more nutrients, used to select and maintain plasmids and integrations carrying auxotrophic markers.

components.name: Yeast nitrogen base without amino acids (with ammonium sulfate)
Dropout (DO) supplement
components.note: 100 mL of the appropriate sterile 10x DO solution per litre - see the dropout-solution-10x recipe. Omit the nutrient(s) corresponding to your marker(s).

components.name: D-Glucose (dextrose)
components.note: 2% (w/v). Add as 50 mL of sterile 40% (w/v) stock after the base has cooled to ~55 C.

components.name: Agar
components.note: OPTIONAL - plates only. 2% (w/v).

recipes.solvent: deionized water (850 mL for the base, to 1 L after supplements)
recipes.adjustPHWith: 1 N NaOH, before autoclaving
recipes.sterilization: autoclave the YNB (+/- agar) in 850 mL at 121 C, 15 min; cool to ~55 C; add sterile 10x DO and sterile glucose
recipes.storage: Broth at room temperature or 4 C; plates inverted, sleeved, at 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: Plates 1-2 months at 4 C; broth 2-3 months
recipes.hazards: pH is adjusted with 1 N NaOH, before autoclaving — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Marker-to-medium map: URA3 -> SD/-Ura; LEU2 -> SD/-Leu; HIS3 -> SD/-His; TRP1 -> SD/-Trp; LYS2 -> SD/-Lys; ADE2 -> SD/-Ade; MET15/MET17 -> SD/-Met. Co-transformations use the double or triple dropout.

Add heat-labile additives only below 55 C: 3-amino-1,2,4-triazole (3-AT, from a 1 M aqueous filter-sterilized stock, typically 5-60 mM to suppress leaky HIS3), cycloheximide (from 1 mg/mL, 1000x), X-gal (80 mg/L from a 20 mg/mL DMF stock).

For a lacZ plate assay the medium must be buffered to ~pH 7 with 1x BU salts (100 mL/L of 10x: 70 g/L Na2HPO4-7H2O + 30 g/L NaH2PO4, pH 7) and the carbon source switched to 2% galactose + 1% raffinose if expression runs off a GAL1 promoter.
Extra adenine (15 mL/L of 0.2% adenine hemisulfate, giving ~30 mg/L) improves the growth of ade2 hosts even on media that are not adenine dropouts.
Salt crystals appearing in aged BU-salt plates are cosmetic and do not affect performance.
Selective plates need 3-5 days at 30 C. Do not call a transformation dead before day 4.
Colonies picked from selective plates should be maintained on selective medium; plasmid loss on YPD is rapid for 2-micron vectors.

sources.title: Clontech/Takara Yeast Protocols Handbook PT3024-1 - SD/DO medium assembly, 3-AT, CHX and X-gal additions
EasyClone 2.0 user manual - SC selection media used after yeast transformation (hosted by Addgene)
Sigma-Aldrich Y0626 Yeast Nitrogen Base Without Amino Acids

recipes.name: Yeast Auxotrophic Supplement Stocks, 100x (individual)
recipes.summary: Seven separately bottled 100x solutions of the nutrients that laboratory yeast strains are usually auxotrophic for; mix and match instead of keeping a shelf of pre-made dropout powders.

components.name: Uracil
components.note: Form: uracil, MW 112.09. 100x -> 20 mg/L final. Aqueous solubility is only ~3.6 g/L at 25 C: warm to 50-60 C with stirring to dissolve, and expect it to crash out again at 4 C. Many labs keep this one at 20x (0.4 g/L) or at room temperature for that reason.

components.name: Adenine hemisulfate
components.note: Form: adenine hemisulfate ((C5H5N5)2 - H2SO4), MW 368.34. 0.2% (w/v). Adding 10 mL/L gives 20 mg/L; adding 15 mL/L gives the 30 mg/L (0.003%) used for YPAD and for adenine-supplemented SD. Check whether your bottle is the anhydrous hemisulfate (368.34) or the dihydrate (~404.4).

components.name: L-Histidine monohydrochloride monohydrate
components.note: Form: L-histidine - HCl - H2O, MW 209.63. 100x -> 20 mg/L final.

components.name: L-Leucine
components.note: Form: free amino acid, MW 131.17. 100x -> 100 mg/L final. Use 3.0 g/L instead if your lab follows the 30 mg/L SC convention. Leucine solubility is about 24 g/L at 25 C so 10 g/L dissolves with stirring.

components.name: L-Tryptophan
components.note: Form: free amino acid, MW 204.22. 100x -> 20 mg/L final. Filter, do not autoclave, and store in a foil-wrapped bottle: tryptophan photo-oxidises and its breakdown products are growth-inhibitory.

components.name: L-Lysine monohydrochloride
components.note: Form: L-lysine - HCl, MW 182.65. 100x -> 30 mg/L final.

components.name: L-Methionine
components.note: Form: free amino acid, MW 149.21. 100x -> 20 mg/L final.

recipes.solvent: deionized water - each nutrient in its OWN bottle, not combined
recipes.adjustPHWith: not adjusted
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter sterilize (tryptophan and uracil in particular); adenine hemisulfate and histidine also tolerate 121 C/15 min if you prefer to autoclave
recipes.storage: 4 C; tryptophan in the dark. Keep uracil at room temperature if it precipitates in the cold
recipes.shelfLife: 6-12 months; discard on any turbidity, film or colour change
notes: These are separate stocks, one nutrient per bottle. That is the whole point: to make SD/-Ura you add every stock except uracil.
Add 10 mL of each required 100x stock per litre of SD medium, after autoclaving and after the medium has cooled below 55 C.
The final 1x targets used here match the standard 10x DO table: uracil 20, adenine 20, histidine 20, leucine 100, tryptophan 20, lysine 30, methionine 20 mg/L.
If you also need the non-selective amino acids (Ile 30, Phe 50, Thr 200, Tyr 30, Val 150, Arg 20 mg/L) it is far less work to make a single 10x DO solution - see dropout-solution-10x.
Never autoclave a mixed sugar + amino acid solution; Maillard chemistry between glucose and free amino groups is fast at 121 C.
Warming a stock to redissolve a precipitate is fine; re-filtering it is safer than assuming it is still sterile.

sources.title: Clontech/Takara Yeast Protocols Handbook PT3024-1 - per-nutrient 10x dropout concentrations and 0.2% adenine hemisulfate stock
PubChem CID 1174 (uracil, 112.09) - includes aqueous solubility data
PubChem CID 6106 (L-leucine, 131.17), CID 6305 (L-tryptophan, 204.22), CID 6137 (L-methionine, 149.21), CID 69568 (L-lysine HCl, 182.65), CID 165377 (L-histidine HCl monohydrate, 209.63)

recipes.name: Sporulation Medium (1% Potassium Acetate), plates or liquid
recipes.summary: Nitrogen-poor, non-fermentable acetate medium that pushes diploid S. cerevisiae into meiosis and tetrad formation; the first step of any tetrad dissection or random spore analysis.

components.name: Potassium acetate
components.note: Form: potassium acetate, CH3COOK, anhydrous, MW 98.14. 1% (w/v) = 102 mM. The non-fermentable carbon source and the actual sporulation trigger. Some protocols use 0.3% (w/v) instead.

components.name: Yeast extract
components.note: OPTIONAL enrichment, 0.1% (w/v). Improves sporulation frequency for many strain backgrounds; leave it out if you need a strictly defined medium.

components.name: D-Glucose (dextrose)
components.note: OPTIONAL, 0.05% (w/v) = 2.8 mM. A trace of glucose supports the last division before meiotic commitment; more than this represses sporulation outright.

components.name: Amino acid / nucleobase supplements
components.note: Supply only the nutrients your diploid is auxotrophic for, and only at about 25% of the normal SC concentration (e.g. His, Leu, Lys, Ura). Full-strength amino acids suppress sporulation. 2.5 mL of a 100x stock per litre gives 0.25x.

components.name: Agar
components.note: OPTIONAL - plates only. 2% (w/v).

recipes.adjustPHWith: usually not adjusted - potassium acetate self-buffers near neutrality. Buffering the medium near pH 6 has been reported to raise sporulation efficiency
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min
recipes.storage: Plates inverted at 4 C, sleeved; liquid medium at room temperature or 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: Plates 1-2 months at 4 C; use fresh plates for difficult strains
notes: Only MATa/MATalpha diploids sporulate. Haploids and MATa/MATa or alpha/alpha diploids will just sit there.
Workflow: grow the diploid to late log phase on rich or pre-sporulation medium, wash the cells free of glucose, then patch onto sporulation plates or resuspend in liquid SPM.

Incubate at 25-30 C. Liquid cultures typically give asci in 30-72 h (48 h is the usual sampling point); plates take 2-10 days. Some strains (including many wild isolates) sporulate much better at 18-23 C - if 30 C fails, drop the temperature before changing the medium.
Score by phase-contrast microscopy: mature tetrads are four refractile spores in a tight tetrahedron inside the ascus.
Acetate-based pre-sporulation media (rather than glucose-based) give better liquid sporulation. A common pre-sporulation step is growth on YPD followed by 12-24 h on 1% potassium acetate.
Rich alternatives exist for stubborn strains: 6% (w/v) glucose YPD, or 0.3% potassium acetate + 0.02% raffinose. There is no single medium that works for every background.
Digest the ascus wall with zymolyase or beta-glucuronidase before dissection - see the zymolyase-spheroplasting-buffer recipe.

Reported sporulation efficiencies vary enormously between laboratory backgrounds (SK1 sporulates near-synchronously and almost completely; S288C-derived strains often much less), so treat any single published percentage as strain-specific.

sources.title: Young Lab yeast protocols (University of Washington) - strain sporulation media: 0.3% KAc/0.02% raffinose, 1% KAc, 6% YEPD, supplements and incubation times
Efficient sporulation of yeast in media buffered near pH 6 (Infection and Immunity / PMC free full text)
A method for sporulating budding yeast cells that allows unbiased identification of kinase substrates (PMC open access) - enriched sporulation medium 1% KAc / 0.1% yeast extract / 0.05% glucose
PubChem CID 517044, Potassium acetate - molecular weight 98.14

recipes.name: Lithium Acetate / TE Transformation Solution (1 M stock and 100 mM working)
recipes.summary: The lithium salt solution that permeabilises the yeast cell wall for chemical transformation; used both to wash competent cells and as a component of the PEG transformation mix.

components.name: Lithium acetate
components.note: 10.20 g/L, or more usually 100 mL of sterile 1 M LiAc per litre. Anhydrous lithium acetate is MW 65.99 (PubChem CID 3474584) - 6.60 g/L for the same 0.1 M. Check which form is in the bottle; the two differ by 55%.

components.name: Tris-HCl, pH 7.5
components.note: 10 mL of 1 M Tris-HCl pH 7.5 per litre. Tris base MW 121.14 (PubChem CID 6503).

components.name: EDTA, pH 8.0
components.note: 2 mL of 0.5 M EDTA pH 8.0 per litre. Disodium EDTA dihydrate MW 372.24 (PubChem CID 636371).

recipes.adjustPHWith: dilute acetic acid to bring the 1 M LiAc stock to pH 7.5 before dilution; the Tris component sets the pH of the working solution
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C 15 min, or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: Room temperature or 4 C; keep the 1 M stock separate and dilute fresh
recipes.shelfLife: 1 M LiAc stock: >1 year. Working LiAc/TE: make fresh on the day, or at most keep a few weeks at 4 C
recipes.hazards: Lithium salts are harmful if swallowed and lithium is a reproductive/developmental concern - handle with gloves, do not mouth-pipette, and consult the supplier SDS. Not a severe hazard at bench scale but it is not table salt either. pH is adjusted with dilute acetic acid to bring the 1 M LiAc stock to pH 7.5 before dilution; the Tris component sets the pH of the working solution — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: The 1 M stock is the master reagent: 102.0 g of lithium acetate dihydrate (or 66.0 g anhydrous) per litre, pH adjusted to 7.5 with dilute acetic acid, then autoclaved.

Two conventions coexist. Classic Gietz-style protocols wash and resuspend cells in 100 mM LiAc in water (no Tris/EDTA at all). Two-hybrid-lineage protocols use 100 mM LiAc in 1x TE. Both work; the TE version chelates trace nucleases.
A 5x LiAc/TE concentrate (0.5 M LiAc, 250 mM Tris-Cl pH 7.4, 5 mM EDTA) is convenient - use 70 uL per ~350 uL transformation.
Do not leave cells sitting in LiAc for extended periods before adding PEG; lithium is toxic to yeast over time.
The transformation mix itself ends up at 100 mM LiAc regardless of route - see the peg-liac-ssdna-transformation-mix recipe.

sources.title: Enhancing yeast transformation: a single adjustment to the lithium acetate-PEG method (PMC open access) - 5x LiAc-TE = 0.5 M LiAc, 250 mM Tris-Cl pH 7.4, 5 mM EDTA; cell buffer 0.1 M LiAc, 50 mM Tris-Cl pH 7.4, 1 mM EDTA
EasyClone 2.0 user manual - 1 M lithium acetate in the transformation mix (hosted by Addgene)
PubChem CID 23666338, Lithium acetate dihydrate - molecular weight 102.0
PubChem CID 3474584, Lithium acetate (anhydrous) - molecular weight 66.0

recipes.name: PEG / LiAc / ssDNA Yeast Transformation Mix
recipes.summary: The one-tube cocktail that carries plasmid or linear DNA into lithium-treated yeast; 360 uL per transformation of about 1e8 cells.

components.name: PEG 3350
components.note: 240 uL of a 50% (w/v) PEG 3350 stock per 360 uL reaction. Make the stock by dissolving 50 g PEG 3350 in water to a FINAL VOLUME of 100 mL (not 100 mL of water - the volume difference matters), then autoclave or 0.22 um filter. Keep it tightly capped: evaporation concentrates the PEG and is the single most common cause of a transformation that suddenly stops working. PEG is a polydisperse polymer so it has no single molecular weight - 3350 is a nominal average; PEG 4000 and PEG 8000 are usable substitutes but 3350/4000 give the best efficiencies.

components.name: Lithium acetate
components.note: 36 uL of sterile 1 M LiAc pH 7.5 per 360 uL reaction. See the liac-te-transformation-solution recipe.

components.name: Single-stranded carrier DNA
components.note: 10 uL of a 10 mg/mL boiled/snap-chilled ssDNA stock per 360 uL = 100 ug carrier. The equivalent classic route is 50 uL of a 2 mg/mL stock (also 100 ug). Boil 3-5 min and chill on ice immediately before use - un-denatured carrier DNA costs 10-100x in efficiency.

components.name: Plasmid or linear DNA plus sterile water
components.note: 74 uL per 360 uL reaction, carrying 0.1-1 ug of DNA. For integrations use linearised vector; for episomal work use 100 ng of supercoiled plasmid.

recipes.solvent: the components above are the whole volume - no additional solvent
recipes.adjustPHWith: not adjusted; the pH comes from the LiAc stock
recipes.sterilization: assemble aseptically from separately sterilized stocks; the mix itself is not sterilized
recipes.storage: Assemble immediately before use and keep on ice; do not store the assembled mix
recipes.shelfLife: Use within the hour. The 50% PEG 3350 stock keeps 6-12 months at room temperature if the cap seals
recipes.hazards: PEG 3350 is low-hazard (it is a pharmaceutical laxative). Lithium acetate: see the LiAc recipe. Wear gloves and eye protection as usual.
notes: Order of addition into the tube matters if you are layering: PEG first (it seals the other components away from the cell pellet), then LiAc, then boiled carrier DNA, then the DNA. Vortex hard only after everything is in.
Resuspend the cell pellet completely by vigorous vortexing - clumps transform poorly.

Heat shock: 42 C for 20-40 min is the usual window (40 min in the EasyClone/Gietz-Woods integration protocol; 15-20 min in many episomal protocols). The optimum is strain-dependent - if efficiency is low, titrate it.
A 30 min pre-incubation at 30 C before the heat shock is used by several published variants.
Adding sorbitol (reported at 40 uL of 2 M per reaction) or 10% DMSO has been reported to raise efficiency for some strains; both are optional add-ons, not part of the base mix.
For dominant drug markers (kanMX, hphMX, natMX, bleMX) you must let the cells recover in 1 mL YPD for 2-4 h at 30 C before plating; for auxotrophic markers, plate directly on the selective medium.
Efficiency benchmarks from the original method: about 1e8 cells with 100 ng plasmid and 50-100 ug carrier DNA is the sweet spot; more DNA does not help proportionally.

sources.title: EasyClone 2.0 user manual - transformation mix (240 uL 50% PEG 3350 + 36 uL 1 M LiAc + 10 uL 10 mg/mL ssDNA + 74 uL DNA = 360 uL), 42 C for 40 min (hosted by Addgene; after Gietz & Woods 2002)

Enhancing yeast transformation: a single adjustment to the lithium acetate-PEG method (PMC open access) - 280 uL 50% PEG, 70 uL 5x LiAc-TE, 25 ug ssDNA, 30 C 30 min then 42 C 15 min, sorbitol addition
Yeast Transformation Protocol (protocols.io) - 50% (w/v) PEG 3350 made from 50 g PEG in ddH2O; 2 mg/mL salmon sperm carrier DNA

recipes.name: Single-Stranded Carrier DNA, 2 mg/mL (salmon or herring sperm)
recipes.summary: Denatured, sheared genomic DNA that acts as a carrier in LiAc/PEG yeast transformation; without it transformation efficiency drops by one to two orders of magnitude.

components.name: Salmon (or herring) sperm DNA, sodium salt
components.note: 2 mg/mL. Deoxyribonucleic acid sodium salt from salmon testes (e.g. Sigma D1626) or herring sperm. A commercial pre-sheared 10 mg/mL solution can be diluted 1:5 into TE instead of preparing from powder.

components.name: Tris-HCl, pH 8.0
components.note: The TE component. Tris base MW 121.14 (PubChem CID 6503).

components.name: EDTA, pH 8.0
components.note: Disodium EDTA dihydrate MW 372.24 (PubChem CID 636371). Chelates Mg2+ and keeps contaminating nucleases inactive.

recipes.solvent: TE buffer, pH 8.0 (10 mM Tris-HCl, 1 mM EDTA)
recipes.adjustPHWith: set by the TE buffer; no separate adjustment
recipes.sterilization: dissolve in sterile TE and handle aseptically; do NOT autoclave the DNA
recipes.storage: 20 C in small single-use aliquots
recipes.shelfLife: Years at -20 C. Limit each aliquot to about 3-4 boil/freeze cycles
notes: Preparation: sprinkle the powder onto TE and stir at 4 C - usually overnight - until fully dissolved. Do not vortex a viscous DNA solution to force it.
Shear the DNA to roughly 2-15 kb by sonication, or by passing it 10-12 times through an 18-gauge then a narrower needle. Unsheared DNA is too viscous to pipette accurately and performs worse.
Optional cleanup: phenol/chloroform extract and ethanol precipitate, then redissolve in TE. Phenol is corrosive and toxic - see the hazard note on any phenol-containing recipe in this library.
Immediately before use: boil 3-5 min (cap-locked tube) and plunge into ice water. The DNA must be single-stranded and stay that way. Slow cooling re-anneals it and you lose the benefit.
Aliquot before freezing. Repeated boil-freeze-thaw cycles progressively degrade the carrier.
100 ug of carrier per transformation (50 uL of 2 mg/mL, or 10 uL of 10 mg/mL) is the standard dose.
Herring sperm DNA behaves equivalently to salmon; the important variables are shear length and denaturation, not the species.

sources.title: Yeast Transformation Protocol (protocols.io) - salmon sperm DNA at 2 mg/mL in TE, boiled 5 min with a boiling cap and chilled on ice, reusable 3-4 times from -20 C
Interaction trap / two-hybrid system to identify interacting proteins (PMC free full text) - preparation of sheared, denatured carrier DNA after Schiestl & Gietz
EasyClone 2.0 user manual - ss-carrier DNA 10 mg/mL, boil 3 min before use, keep on ice (hosted by Addgene)

recipes.name: Zymolyase / Lyticase Spheroplasting Buffer (osmotically stabilised)
recipes.summary: Sorbitol-stabilised buffer in which zymolyase or lyticase strips the yeast cell wall to give osmotically fragile spheroplasts, for genomic DNA prep, organelle isolation, immunofluorescence or tetrad dissection.

components.name: D-Sorbitol
components.note: 218.6 g/L. The osmotic support - without it spheroplasts burst as soon as the wall goes. 1.0 M (182.2 g/L) and 1.4 M (255.0 g/L) are equally standard; match whatever your downstream step uses.

components.name: Potassium phosphate buffer, pH 7.5
components.note: 100 mL of 1 M potassium phosphate pH 7.5 per litre. Made from KH2PO4 (MW 136.09, PubChem CID 516951) and K2HPO4 (MW 174.18, PubChem CID 24450). Zymolyase lyses viable cells best at pH 7.5. Sodium phosphate works identically; 10 mM PIPES pH 6.5 (MW 302.4, PubChem CID 79723) is the buffer used in the manufacturer's own S buffer and is preferred when you want to slow endogenous proteases.

components.name: EDTA, pH 8.0
components.note: OPTIONAL. 20 mL of 0.5 M EDTA pH 8.0 per litre. Helps wall digestion and inhibits nucleases, but omit it if the downstream step needs Mg2+ (e.g. intact mitochondria, in vitro transcription).

components.name: 2-Mercaptoethanol
components.hazard: Acutely toxic by inhalation and skin contact, and extremely foul-smelling. Open the bottle and pipette it only in a fume hood; nitrile gloves; collect waste as hazardous.
components.note: 1.4 uL of neat 2-ME per mL of buffer. Required: the sulfhydryl reagent reduces disulfides in the outer mannoprotein layer and is a documented activator of zymolyase. 10 mM DTT (1.54 g/L, MW 154.25, PubChem CID 439196) is a lower-odour substitute. Add fresh on the day - both oxidise.

components.name: Zymolyase 20T
components.hazard: Dry enzyme powder - weigh in a hood or balance enclosure and avoid inhaling the dust; proteolytic/glycolytic enzyme dusts are respiratory sensitisers.
components.note: Usable range is wide, roughly 0.025-1 mg/mL depending on strain, growth phase and how fast you need it. Zymolyase 20T is 20,000 units/g; Zymolyase 100T is 100,000 units/g and is used at about a fifth of the mass. Lyticase from Arthrobacter luteus (e.g. Sigma L2524) is an equivalent beta-1,3-glucanase preparation. Add the enzyme last, after the buffer has cooled.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (bring to 1 L for the base buffer; add 2-ME and enzyme fresh)
recipes.adjustPHWith: the phosphate (or PIPES) buffer sets the pH; adjust the 1 M phosphate stock with KOH/H3PO4 before use
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter the sorbitol/phosphate/EDTA base, or autoclave it; NEVER autoclave the enzyme or the thiol. Use a non-nitrocellulose membrane if you filter the enzyme solution
recipes.storage: Base buffer 4 C; zymolyase powder 2-8 C dry; enzyme stock solution -20 C in aliquots
recipes.shelfLife: Base buffer several months at 4 C. Zymolyase powder loses ~70% of its activity after 3 months at 30 C, so keep it cold. Complete buffer with 2-ME and enzyme: make fresh each time
recipes.hazards: 2-mercaptoethanol is acutely toxic and has an overwhelming odour - fume hood only, and dispose of contaminated tips as hazardous waste. Zymolyase/lyticase powders: avoid inhalation. Spheroplasts themselves are harmless but lyse instantly in water, so keep every wash osmotically supported. pH is adjusted with the phosphate (or PIPES) buffer sets the pH; adjust the 1 M phosphate stock with KOH/H3PO4 before use — concentrated acid/base is corrosive; wear eye protection and add slowly with stirring.
notes: Enzyme stock solution: 20-100 mg/mL zymolyase in water, 10 mM sodium phosphate pH 7.4, or 50 mM Tris-Cl pH 7.5, each containing 5% glucose and 50% glycerol; aliquot and store at -20 C.

Optimum conditions for lysing viable yeast are pH 7.5 at about 35 C; for hydrolysing isolated glucan it is pH 6.5 at 45 C. The enzyme is stable from pH 5 to 10 but loses lytic activity within 5 min at 60 C.

A common two-step version: pre-treat the cell pellet in 100 mM Tris-HCl / 100 mM EDTA pH 8.0 with 2-ME at 30 C (15 min for log phase, 45 min for stationary phase) to strip the outer mannan, then wash into the sorbitol buffer and add enzyme.
Typical digestion is 30-60 min at 30 C with gentle agitation; stationary-phase and wild-type-wall strains take considerably longer. This step is the one that most often needs optimising.

Monitor progress with a two-tube test: dilute 1 uL of the digest into 20 uL of sorbitol buffer (spheroplasts stay intact) and 1 uL into 20 uL of water or 1% SDS (spheroplasts burst). Compare under the microscope, or read OD600 - a drop of 80-90% means the job is done.
Once spheroplasted, handle gently: no vortexing, wide-bore tips, and centrifuge at low speed (about 1000-3000 x g).
For tetrad dissection a far milder digest is used - a brief zymolyase or beta-glucuronidase treatment that just softens the ascus wall, not a full spheroplasting.

Reported buffer variants in the literature include 1.2 M sorbitol / 0.1 M KH2PO4 pH 7.5 with 0.025 mg/mL zymolyase 20T; 1.4 M sorbitol / 50 mM potassium phosphate pH 7.5 with 0.25 mg/mL zymolyase 100T and 40 mM 2-ME; and the manufacturer's S buffer, 1.0 M sorbitol / 10 mM PIPES pH 6.5. All are workable - the sorbitol concentration and the enzyme dose are the levers.

sources.title: MP Biomedicals Zymolyase 20T from Arthrobacter luteus - technical information sheet (20,000 units/g, optimum pH 7.5 / 35 C, S buffer 1.0 M sorbitol + 10 mM PIPES pH 6.5, storage and stock preparation)

High-throughput immunofluorescence microscopy using yeast spheroplast cell-based microarrays (PMC free full text) - spheroplast buffer 1.2 M sorbitol, 0.1 M KH2PO4 pH 7.5 with zymolyase 20T and 2-mercaptoethanol, 30 C for 2 h
PubChem CID 5780, D-Sorbitol - molecular weight 182.17
PubChem CID 1567, 2-Mercaptoethanol - molecular weight 78.13, hazard and toxicity data
PubChem CID 516951 (KH2PO4, 136.09) and CID 24450 (K2HPO4, 174.18)

recipes.name: Potato Dextrose Agar (PDA)
recipes.summary: General-purpose plating medium for yeasts and filamentous fungi; standard for enumerating moulds in food and for maintaining and sporulating fungal isolates.

components.name: Potato infusion
components.note: 200 g of sliced, unpeeled potatoes boiled 30 min in 1 L of distilled water and filtered through cheesecloth; the filtrate is the infusion. Commercial dehydrated PDA supplies this as roughly 4 g/L of potato infusion solids.

components.name: D-Glucose (dextrose)
components.note: Form: anhydrous D-glucose, MW 180.16. 2% (w/v). Autoclaved with the medium in this formulation.

components.name: Agar
components.note: 2% (w/v) per FDA BAM. Commercial dehydrated PDA (39 g/L) contains only 15 g/L agar, so BAM directs adding a further 5 g of agar per litre to reach 20 g/L.

recipes.adjustPHWith: usually not adjusted - the formulation lands at pH 5.6 +/- 0.2. For acidified PDA, add sterile 10% (w/v) tartaric acid to the cooled molten medium
recipes.sterilization: boil to dissolve, then autoclave 121 C for 15 min
recipes.storage: Poured plates inverted at 4 C, sleeved; dehydrated powder tightly closed at room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: Plates 1-2 months at 4 C; dehydrated powder per the manufacturer's expiry
notes: Dispense 20-25 mL per 15 x 100 mm plate.
Do not re-melt the medium more than once - repeated melting hydrolyses the agar and the plates stay soft.
The mildly acidic pH favours fungi over most bacteria but does not exclude them. To suppress bacteria properly, either acidify or add an antibiotic.

Acidified PDA (APDA): add sterile 10% (w/v) tartaric acid (tartaric acid MW 150.09, PubChem CID 444305) to molten medium cooled to about 45-50 C, to roughly pH 3.5. Critically, do NOT autoclave after acidifying - acid hydrolyses agar and the medium will not set. This pH figure is common supplier and laboratory practice rather than a value stated in the FDA BAM M127 entry itself, so verify against your own method before using it for a regulated assay.
Antibiotic alternative used by FDA BAM for cosmetics: cool to 47-50 C and add chlortetracycline HCl to 40 ppm (4 mL of a filter-sterilized 1 g/100 mL stock per litre).
Potato dextrose salt agar for osmophilic/xerophilic fungi: prepare PDA as above and add 75 g of NaCl per litre (NaCl MW 58.44, PubChem CID 5234).
PDA is often preferred over SDA for inducing pigment and conidiation in Fusarium, Penicillium and Aspergillus, because it is nutritionally leaner.

sources.title: FDA Bacteriological Analytical Manual, Media M127: Potato Dextrose Agar - full formula, pH 5.6 +/- 0.2, autoclave 15 min at 121 C, salt and chlortetracycline variants
Sigma-Aldrich P2182 Potato Dextrose Agar - dehydrated medium composition (39 g/L: 4 g potato infusion solids, 20 g dextrose, 15 g agar)
PubChem CID 5793, D-Glucose - molecular weight 180.16
PubChem CID 444305, L-Tartaric acid - molecular weight 150.09

recipes.name: Sabouraud Dextrose Agar and Broth (SDA / SDB)
recipes.summary: High-sugar, mildly acidic peptone medium that selects for yeasts, dermatophytes and other filamentous fungi over most bacteria; the classical clinical mycology medium.

components.name: Polypeptone or neopeptone
components.note: 1% (w/v). Mycological peptone or a polypeptone/neopeptone blend; the nitrogen source.

components.name: D-Glucose (dextrose)
components.note: Form: anhydrous D-glucose, MW 180.16. 4% (w/v) = 222 mM. The high sugar concentration is part of what makes the medium selective. The Emmons modification drops this to 2% (20 g/L) and raises the pH to ~6.9, which suits some dermatophytes better.

components.name: Agar
components.note: For the AGAR form only; FDA BAM specifies 15-20 g/L depending on the gel strength wanted. Omit entirely for Sabouraud dextrose broth.

recipes.adjustPHWith: usually not adjusted; broth lands at pH 5.8, agar at 5.6 +/- 0.2
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 15 min at 118-121 C. Do NOT exceed 121 C - the 4% dextrose caramelises and the medium darkens
recipes.storage: Plates and slants inverted at 4 C, sleeved; broth in screw-cap bottles at room temperature or 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: Plates 1-2 months at 4 C; slants keep longer and are the traditional format for maintaining fungal isolates
notes: Broth version: dissolve the peptone and dextrose only, dispense 40 mL per screw-cap bottle, final pH 5.8.
Agar version: prepare the broth, add 15-20 g of agar, dispense into tubes for slants or flasks for pouring plates.

Selective supplements (added after cooling to ~50 C): chloramphenicol at about 50 mg/L to suppress bacteria (chloramphenicol MW 323.13, PubChem CID 5959), and cycloheximide at about 0.5 g/L to suppress saprophytic moulds when isolating dermatophytes. Cycloheximide also inhibits several clinically important fungi (Cryptococcus neoformans, many Candida and Aspergillus species), so always plate a cycloheximide-free control alongside.
Because it does not exclude every bacterium on its own, SDA in a clinical setting is normally used as the antibiotic-supplemented version.
PDA versus SDA: SDA is richer and grows fungi faster but yields less pigment and less conidiation; PDA is the better choice for morphological identification.
Slants for stock cultures: incubate at 25-30 C until sporulating, then hold at 4 C, or overlay with sterile mineral oil for longer-term maintenance.

sources.title: FDA Bacteriological Analytical Manual, Media M133: Sabouraud's Dextrose Broth and Agar - polypeptone/neopeptone 10 g, dextrose 40 g, water 1 L, pH 5.8 broth / 5.6 +/- 0.2 agar, agar 15-20 g, autoclave 15 min at 118-121 C
Hardy Diagnostics Sabouraud Dextrose (SABDEX) media - technical data sheet including chloramphenicol/cycloheximide selective versions
PubChem CID 5793, D-Glucose - molecular weight 180.16
PubChem CID 5959, Chloramphenicol - molecular weight 323.13; PubChem CID 6197, Cycloheximide - molecular weight 281.35 (toxicity data)
